### NeilBB — 2014-03-18T17:24:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116215>

> For me, I can't fathom spending money on any over priced education for TCM, ND, MD, whatever it is. Aside from the real reasons why I want to get into healing people (which serve as the foundation for why I wanted to become an MD), becoming an MD seemed most feasible because there was a payback (by that I mean I could make a living and pay off my loans) from it.
>
> NDs, functional medicine doctors, TCM, chiros all pay a huge amount of money for school with no guarantee of any payback. These fields all focus on important issues, but I feel that I can serve a purpose helping people heal without a degree if thats the case.

I would say that there is not necessarily a "correct career path" for an individual to pursue. Ultimately, it comes down to: (1) What you want to accomplish, (2) Your natural ability, (3) How much bullshit you can tolerate, (4) How much you want to be compensated for your effort. You have to somehow plug in the variables and do the math.

You have to also realize that when (1) and (2) clash with (3) and (4), which is probably the case since you are here, it ain't gonna be easy no matter what.

### Josh — 2014-03-18T18:21:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116218>

I am not calling you out, just noticing that it is different at 50 than it was at 26. At your age, I had already tried to save the world directly by going into the ghettos, working with refugees, political organizing, AIDS activism, traveling to less developed countries and lamenting their problems. After all of that, medicine was all I could think of to do that gave me something to offer in all of these contexts. In retrospect, I pretty much agree with Marie that the most effective tools for action in this day and age are actually learned in business school. I am now engaged in an exercise that is teaching me those things as well. Exchange is required no matter what one chooses. The currencies vary depending upon the context. Perhaps read " Fourth Uncle in the Mountain" and then go to Manchester, VT and see if you can find him. I met a client of his a few years ago who said he was practicing there. Beings such as him have rare treasures that they may or may not be willing to share. Read carefully and make notes on all of the things that were involved in his training and what he paid for them in time, energy, money, etc..

One does not go on the path to be a doctor/healer for "payback". Even the most avaricious and self-centered person can only handle so many rounds in hospitals filled with decaying people in pain. I have watched the faces of interns and residents faced with my dying mother. I will never forget the smell of the St. Luke's ER on a busy day in the late 80's with everyone from dying AIDS patients to demented old ladies to young children with colds running around. I can not forget the faces of the Cambodian families that had crawled through jungles and sat patiently in horrific refugee camps for years to finally end up in the Bronx with me as their guide to the brave new world. I can not forget my grandmother and her patience with the garment workers in NY. Take a look at the story I put in the "mental illness" thread. How would you respond to that? What is the lesson and blessing there? I have to respond to these things for myself in a way that is meaningful to me. It is a work in progress. I may know how it went by the time I die. Once upon a time it is reputed that before anyone got to learn anything, they were taught how to be present and responsive. They lived in the forest at a school called a "gurukhul" run by someone who had actually managed to finish the basic work before leaving. Somewhere around your age, I realized that I had missed that 20 or so years of training and that I might have some work to do before I could really "choose" anything. I thought I would be there by now....so much for plans.....

Good luck and enjoy every moment, there are no guarantees.....

### NeilBB — 2014-03-18T18:22:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116219>

Regarding making a living, unfortunately the world will pay you handsomely if you smile and feed it comfortable lies and penalize you heavily if you are the disseminator of painful truths. Why?

"Truth" is the subjugation of our thoughts to correspondence with nature.
Civilization promotes "dogmas" which are only very loosely based on truth.
"Conventional wisdom" typically includes outdated info and deliberate falsehoods that serve certain political, economic, and traditional interests.
A "career" is the the subjugation of an individual's time and effort to correspond with the perceived needs of society for remuneration, it actually has very little to do with serving the truth.

It is the story of history. Read about any scientific revolutionary ever. Read "Going Somewhere" by Andrew Marino. Its all there.

### Josh — 2014-03-18T18:52:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116222>

One more note on the "doctor" part. Being called a "doctor" is a mixed blessing and has become down right confusing as there so many kinds of "doctors" in the health world. For me, I could have gotten an OMD (Oriental Medical Doctor) by simply taking the herb test and getting a New Mexico license when I graduated acupuncture school. That door had been open for about 10 years and many practitioners are grandfathered in as OMD's who have half the training I do. Because I am not a "doctor" patients often have trouble putting me in that position and I have felt it keeps us closer to a partnership. On the other hand, in this day and age being a "doctor" of whatever gives some value to one's brand. I am amused by the latest trend of people being called Dr. "first name". To me it feels like kids playing medicine. Perhaps this is appropriate.

Consider that before one can "first, do no harm", one must "heal thyself", I believe it is the law....interesting how they have become separated from each other....

### NeilBB — 2014-03-18T19:08:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116227>

The only hope for humanity is that when society's dogma strays too far from reason, a real scientific revolution typically follows at some point. But it is a long painful process and the revolutionaries are typically tormented or worse. And the old guard never admits it was ever wrong about anything.

But in the meantime, we all still have to try to "earn-a-living" and hope that civilization hasn't strayed too far (committed too many crimes against nature) to recover this time (aka extinction event), which is debatable at this point.

Thats what i meant by you have to do the math and plug in the variables of where you want to fit in to all of that. Maybe you are the next Aristotle or Copernicus. Maybe you'd rather lay low in a remote cave. I don't know.

I hope this helps some. I do know that you probably won't get a speech like that from any premed counselor at your local college. Good luck.

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-18T20:33:51+0200

### Josh — 2014-03-18T21:07:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116259>

I guess this is therapeutic for me because I keep remembering more family/doctor stuff. On the subject of the military, I remember my father's father. He volunteered in 1942 to go to England as a urological surgeon. He was 42 years old. Throughout my childhood, he told me many happy stories of being in England and operating on officers. He gave me his insignia and I kept them in my drawer. Our time together and these stories were my good times with him. Only a few years ago, I was talking with my father and I found out that my grandfather also went to France after D-day and worked in field hospitals for the remainder of the war. He never said a word of this to me before he died when I was 30. For me, this is not a small omission. After the war, my grandfather divided his time between golf, bridge and surgery. In his 70's, he moved to Florida because of his asthma which had not been helped by a lifetime of smoking cigars and pipes. In his 80's, he still flew to NY to see Irving Berlin who was one of his favorite patients. Last night a friend told me how he survived being a door gunner on a Huey in the Marine Corps in 1965. He arrived and his sergeant told him he must learn detachment or he would not survive. I can not imagine more powerful lessons in detachment than the ground war in France 1944-45 or the door of a Huey in Vietnam in 1965. My friend completed his tour unwounded after 13 months. He stills wakes up screaming 2 nights per week. I have been wondering where PTSD fits in our conversations about physiology and brain function?

He arrived and his sergeant told him he must learn detachment or he would not survive. I can not imagine more powerful lessons in detachment than the ground war in France 1944-45 or the door of a Huey in Vietnam in 1965. My friend completed his tour unwounded after 13 months. He stills wakes up screaming 2 nights per week. **I have been wondering where PTSD fits in our conversations about physiology and brain function?**

Josh I've been thinking about this alot of recent. I have PTSD diagnosis, and I have several friends who have it as well. There was an indepth conversation over of FB a week or two ago... that has me researching a future blog post. we have a whole generation of my brothers and sisters in arms returning who need help coping with PTSD, and TBI. having been a responder on 9/11, I have additional experiences that have added to my PTSD. Yet I physically wasn't at the Pentagon for the response. And I worked in the Pentagon for years w/o issues. Yet on 9/11/12 - I physically got so ill I couldn't go in the building. there's definitely something there. I have an idea how I beat it the first time around. and I'm getting closer to beating it the second time... but what works for me may not work for everyone else.

### Josh — 2014-03-18T21:33:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116262>

My dad worked the psychiatric ER at the Lebanon, PA regional VA a year and got really obsessed with the topic. He even wrote a book of sorts that remains unpublished. We are still looking at whether to do anything with his ideas. He is a bit of a character. I am working on some other things in your part of the world that may be related with a partner whose father is a retired Air Force Reserve Brigadier. My dad got bounced out the Army Reserves for playing hooky on a weekend stint to play golf. He almost got deployed in the Berlin Crisis of all things....He recently helped another friend of ours get a PTSD disability for the issues related to his Vietnam service as a pilot. My dad has another couple of years in him and is still interested in participating in something. He might even show up here...

### Hope — 2014-03-18T23:02:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116275>

I often wonder what exactly Americans mean by US medicine being "socialized". I am not sure if there is another country whose ~healthcare~ symptom suppression and disease management system is _more_ based on money and profit than in the US. It seems to be all about control, money and profit......drugs, insurance, everything. (and I've heard some amazing MDs and dentists even say, the system is meant to feed you bad food to make you sick, so you need an MD to give you drugs, so you get worse, may need expensive, cutting-edge tests and possibly surgery and more pieces of the puzzle make money off you). Everything related to American disease management is based on money, as far as I can see, even if it may be very little. I see socialized medicine more as in the failed attempt to create communism, when medical care was _super cheap_, technology _sucked_, but it was affordable for nearly everyone, with _long wait times_ - as it was in Eastern Europe during the Cold War, for instance.

And the original meaning of "doctor"...from docere (Latin), which means to teach.....as in teaching people to live well. The kind of work Jack does. Now that's a real MD if there ever was one. I guess generally, there isn't much money to be made in teaching people to watch their circadian rhythms and the many things we learn here.

And acupuncture - now there is an amazing modality that insurance mostly doesn't cover.....probably because it doesn't pay the other pieces of the symptoms billions of dollars a year. And it may actually heal....not just suppress so you lose clientele = power = money. Christiane Northrup, MD, one of my heroes, talks about how it helps heal/release the emotions that are held in our bodies that we accumulate from the trauma we experience in our lives - and especially in our childhoods.... some MD's do make it big. Dr Northrup is one. Deepak Chopra MD seems to have found a great path for himself too....

I hope I didn't offend anyone with this.....and mostly just embarrassed myself and lol.

Hm. So how has the government here have fundamental control? I see corporations as having the control, the government is just the puppet playing an act, with everything...they can't do a thing really, except cave to the corporations and through them, they keep themselves in power. In Eastern Europe, it was the government - no rich corporations (well, maybe some individuals?).....and in Germany now, I could see it too. But here? ### NeilBB — 2014-03-19T22:29:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116536>

> [Hope said:](/goto/post?id=116520)
>
> Hm. So how has the government here have fundamental control? I see corporations as having the control, the government is just the puppet playing an act, with everything...they can't do a thing really, except cave to the corporations and through them, they keep themselves in power. In Eastern Europe, it was the government - no rich corporations (well, maybe some individuals?).....and in Germany now, I could see it too. But here? I must be missing something....in Germany with insurance, it's not a for profit thing....insurance is supposed to be non-profit and the government has a hand in the budgets etc.
>
> I just am not smart enough. I think I'll go out and hug a tree.

I'm sure you're plenty smart. In essence it's pretty simple. In the U.S., the government decides what medical services are worth and who can get them through its control of coding and pricing at [CMS](http://www.cms.gov/). Corporations are pressured to adopt the same standards that the government sets and it quickly becomes the national model. It is basically a commodity model where everything is lumped into a box and the government decides what the relevant boxes are and what treatments fit with what boxes and how much payment is appropriate for each. Government regulations also control hospitals and physicians through an ever-increasing complexity of regulations. And they control the private insurance industry through regulations and taxation policies which go back to WW2.

### RobH — 2014-03-20T17:01:26+0200

### RobH — 2014-03-20T17:19:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116699>

> [Casey Inman said:](/goto/post?id=116279)
>
> Do you guys have any thoughts about "becoming" a biochemist or biomedical engineer?

Once my friends in engineering school found out I was going to med school I told them (as I was sure myself) that I'd go into designing MRI machines or some field of biomedical engineering.

Out of EE school I had interviews with the big software companies of the time and opportunities. Would have been in early and I'm sure made lots of money as they got bigger and bigger (Think Microsoft in 1991). I know people in those positions. Some are happier than others.

I became an EMT because I wanted to be a ski patrol at my local ski area. Started working night shifts in a local ER.

Once I started getting into the third year of med school and clinical medicine I started realizing how much I enjoyed the patient interactions.

No matter how cynical and frustrated I am by the system that I practice in (I am), no matter how much I complain about it (and I do) I will say this: there is something incredibly rejuvenating and special to me about being a doctor. When I can walk into a room and focus on just that patient, for some limited amount of time (and it is limited and that is frustrating), to apply the knowledge and skills I have learned, for that persons benefit, in a very intimate and personal way, to have them trust me to do that, is tremendously satisfying. (sorry run on sentence)

It all really comes down to that for me. But everything else surrounding it and pressuring it makes it more challenging for sure.

I constantly ask myself if I would do it again. Honestly I don't know. Somedays yes somedays no. Most days no. Knowing what I know now I would have steered a different path through residency and the last 15 years of my life, but of course I didn't know what I know now and never could have. And I will know and perceive things very differently in a few months or years -- of that I'm certain.

I have 3 kids getting to the age of making those sorts of decisions ... well not too close yet but they will be ...

I really don't know what to tell them. I think there is and always will be tremendous opportunity and satisfaction in some fields of medicine. There will always be a need for providers of quality healthcare, and I think as the "system" frays more and more people will be willing and have to pay for what works. It will never be cheap, financially, health wise, or time wise to get there.

But then what rewarding profession will?

Sorry, I doubt this adds any clarity here... just random thoughts drifting through my head after a late shift last evening.

RobH

### Josh — 2014-03-20T17:20:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116700>

I majored in East Asian History. Did all of the most semesters in 2 all-nighter weeks in front of my early IBM PC drinking TAB and Diet Coke and eating bagels to add insult to injury. After graduating, I went to Bryn Mawr College's Post-Bac program and took all of the pre-med courses in 13 months. I loved any class that was well taught. It has left me with an appreciation of Organic Chemistry because of the enthusiasm of the young professor who taught the first semester. They did not keep her because many students complained she was "too hard". I found this a great tragedy. Everyone got better grades the second semester and learned 1/10th of what they had the first semester....This is pretty much the story of my education, I respond to anything interesting and above all, well taught.....

### Josh — 2014-03-20T17:25:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116702>

> No matter how cynical and frustrated I am by the system that I practice in (I am), no matter how much I complain about it (and I do) I will say this: there is something incredibly rejuvenating and special to me about being a doctor. When I can walk into a room and focus on just that patient, for some limited amount of time (and it is limited and that is frustrating), to apply the knowledge and skills I have learned, for that persons benefit, in a very intimate and personal way, to have them trust me to do that, is tremendously satisfying. (sorry run on sentence)

My parents and grandparents have all said essentially this. My father still feels this way at 78. For better or worse, I watched my mother maintain these relationships until she could not get out of bed and I took the phone away 13 days before she died. It is my experience as well as an "alternative" practitioner.

### RobH — 2014-03-20T17:49:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116713>

I absolutely believe you can do the same in "alternative" fields also. So a good question is whether or not the mainstream route is the right one and if not which one is. That I can't answer as I don't have a any experience with the others.

I will say, as I sat through some awesome lectures on functional and anti-aging medicine last weekend I actually thought to myself, perhaps I ought to advise my kids to go this direction (if and when they ask or care)... and I haven't felt like that about medicine in a long time. however... it still remains to be seen how to make it work for me... 8^)

I do think there is hope in mainstream medicine. Most of it is in the hands of the Functional and Age-Management Medicine specialties I believe. And I think there will be an ever-growing need for that personalized and not necessarily pharmaceutical based medicine. But as has been discussed, the barrier to entry (Med School, etc.) is pretty high.

However, I don't say that to discount the various forms of traditional and alternative medicine out there. Some of those things are embraced by functional medicine.

RobH

### Josh — 2014-03-20T19:21:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116733>

Most of the great Traditional practitioners started learning in their families about the time they could walk. Many were from multi-generational lineages such as Yi Tian Ni who was 49th generation:

[http://www.pacificcollege.edu/pcom\_static/publications/audio/categories.php](http://www.pacificcollege.edu/pcom_static/publications/audio/categories.php)

I took some workshops with Koei Kuwahara who shared that after he graduated from a Japanese program that is typically much more rigorous than the US programs, he found he could not get satisfactory results. One day he was talking to a friend who told him to go and talk to Fukushima Kodo. He went to Fukushima's house and was asked if he wanted to intern? He accepted and slept on the floor of Fukushima's room for the next 5 years wherever he went. Fukushima was blind as had been he tradition for acupuncturists for a few hundred years in Japan. This tradition allowed them to work with naked bodies without "issues". Koei described how Fukushima would wake up, sit up in bed and take his own pulse and then treat himself. He then got up and saw and treated an average of 80-120 patients per day. My grandmother never left her house without two briefcases filled with the essentials necessary for being an internist....Compared to these people, my "practice" and "career" looks like a "hobby"....

[http://www.culia.net/culiakiclinicinc/staffs.html](http://www.culia.net/culiakiclinicinc/staffs.html)

[http://www.redwingbooks.com/products/books/MerTheJapHar.cfm](http://www.redwingbooks.com/products/books/MerTheJapHar.cfm)

### Josh — 2014-03-20T20:01:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116738>

From a physicist turned acupuncturist:

> “Do you know about dark energy?” he asked. “Physicists think that the visible universe represents about 4% of what is there. The other 96% is pure energy. As acupuncturists, we can access that energy in our treatments. We can heal by putting ourselves into the space that accesses that energy. A woman came into my clinic, with pain in every joint. I only used 2 needles, but I put myself in that space where you can receive dark energy. After ten minutes, she said all her pain was gone.”
>
> “What did you do?”, I asked. “How did you access that energy?”
>
> “I didn’t do anything”, he replied. “You have to go beyond this world, and just let the energy flow in. If you do anything, nothing will happen. You just have to receive it.”

[http://drjakefratkin.com/articles/dr-moon](http://drjakefratkin.com/articles/dr-moon)

I believe this is true for all healers regardless of the modality or system, formal or informal...now all we have to do is explain it....BOOM

### Josh — 2014-03-27T06:52:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-118217>

A story...tonight I had some friends of friends from the East coast over for dinner. a couple, she is 66, he is 89. She is a 1st generation immigrant who could not decide whether to become a hippie or a nun, so she ended up a nun. After 15 years she left and became a massage therapist. One day she was at a psychic who said "you have not fulfilled what you came for, what did you really want to do?" She replied "I wanted to be a doctor, but I am not smart enough." The psychic said something like "you need to fulfill this or you will not be satisfied". So she went to osteopathic school at 40 and is retiring after 20 years in medicine in a few weeks. Her partner has the redox potential of a bear and started running marathons and riding centuries at 70....

### Josh — 2014-03-27T15:32:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-118270>

So I awoke early stirred up a bit...I began meditating at 4 and some things came so I thought to expand a bit...Well these two wonderful people who came to dinner of course before too long came around to their "health" issues. Once again I was faced with trying to explain how a mechanism more complex than the International Space Station or the Hadrian Collider works....And then on top of that make it relevant to chronic arthritis and HBP..."how can I stop taking my statin and BP Meds and a couple of other things and just do it with food?" and "well I have chronic arthritis..." So being the fool I am I launched into explaining Primate evolution, Rift Valley, Seafood, DHA, Pi Electron Cloud....When their eyes rolled back in their head, I switched to the 3 legged stool...and when I was countered with "everyone is really the same and it is how one lives...." I had to explain methylation and epigenentics...remember I am actually teaching this to a doctor who has practiced for over 20 years who is worried about both them self and the health of their beloved partner that they feel relatively helpless about....Not really an uncommon scenario when one is in health care....After about an hour, I think I got to the point where I was able to make the various threads I had started relevant by describing the neuroimmune model and how it came down to metabolism/redox and that the questioner was essentially experiencing the results of the body adapting to the loss of its ability to maintain the entire system and trying to prioritize the core and brain at the expense of the extremities with collateral effects being higher pressure in the system, reduced nutrition of the periphery and gradual loss of immune and cognitive function...both of these people have already had bouts of cancer as well, breast and prostate. So what to do about it?

In my morning mediation, I picked up the old thread....what to do about it. I believe Tolstoy said something like "what then must we do". I first heard this in high school in the movie "The Year of Living Dangerously" about the Indonesian revolution where a lot of bad stuff happened. The usual hope brutally dashed by the realities of politics and humans story with really great performances by Mel Gibson and Sigourney Weaver "Gin Tonic, no ice..." and "We all become children again when we enter the slums of Asia...." I went to a Quaker college initially called Haverford and the community would spend a lot of time and energy on "honor" "social justice" and other related issues. I came from a suburban high school 3 times the size of Haverford where many of the issues discussed were much more existential than theoretical. I just could not get into it....So i went to the ghettos of Philadelphia to try to "do something" and got involved with the Philadelphia Welfare Rights Organization which operated out of a condemned building and was staffed by retired people on social security. It was the brve new world of the OBRA (Omnibus Reconcilliation Act) and many people were losing what is called "public assistance" by the politically correct and more commonly "welfare". Conceptually this seemed like a good thing to many people. However, the reality was a bit rougher....I was regularly faced with scenarios such as a 15 year old mother of 2 who would say to me something to the effect of "they took my benefits away, my grandmother is putting me and my babies out on the street so I am going to be a prostitute to survive..." Now this seemed pretty extreme to me. However when I questioned the other people there, they explained that in Philadelphia prostitution was not the same as New York where pimps dominated and were rough on the women. They explained that it was unfortunately one of the few choices women such as this had to survive. So I would help these women review what services might still be available to them and their children and wish them the best so to speak....I learned a great deal more there and that may be for another day...After freshman year doing that, I heard from a friend in DC, that he was working with refugee resettlement of Vietnamese and Cambodians and I switched to that and spent the next 2 years working with them in Philadelphia and NYC. The first family I had was an extended Cambodian family that had amazingly managed to escape without losing the majority of the family. They had managed to skip the 2nd stage of the camp process where they would have received a little training for resettlement, etc.. They were assigned to me by Catholic Social Services of Philadelphia and I would visit a few times per week in the apartment that they were given paid for with the resettlement funds provided by the government. They only spoke Khmer and I English so it was really challenging and I do not know if I was very effective. Each time I came, they would sit quietly and look at me as I tried to see what I might help with. I can still see their faces. That year, deeper paradoxes emerged in the ghettos of Philadelphia such as the shooting of Hmong people by local elements...Unfortunately, the traditional poor and disadvantaged of America perceived that the refugees were getting better treatment and routinely decided to take it out on them with physical violence. This apparently is the classic cause of riots, one group perceiving that the other is marginally better off and deciding to do something about it by showing their displeasure through physical violence...After 3 years of this, I realized I could not do much to "save the world directly". I made a half-hearted attempt to work on the AIDS issues in NY, but never found the same passion and commitment I had in those 3 years...Medical School was a default program based on the continued desire to "do something..."

Since then I have had my personal odyssey and the health odysseys of my family....I have trained in what I have trained-also largely accidental rather than conscious choices. Yet, here I find myself still trying to "do something"...

I have a favorite Sufi story that haunts me:

There was a Sufi and when he was young, he thought he could transform the whole world and set out to do so. At middle age, he said to god "well it is impossible to transform the whole world, so I will just focus on my friends and family...." As he aged, he was repeatedly frustrated in his limited ability to help and transform others and finally one day he said to god: "transforming and helping others is so difficult and now I am old, maybe if I am lucky I can transform myself before I die..."

And so here I am....somewhere in this story...

### caroline — 2014-07-23T11:41:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-135291>

I thank all of you for coming to this party ..... for caring....

### Skimo — 2014-07-23T21:10:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-135315>

In highschool, I did consider Med school and my aunt gave me that book to read : The-Case-Dr-Sachs by Martin Winckler. I chose to become a vet instead...
I think I should read it again now...
Practising as a vet required a lot more energy than what I could give, I went on studying pathology, worked in research and now I am working in veterinary public health at a local level...
There are still many possible paths after a career choice, but with american tuitons fees I guess they could be a lot more restricted...

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-23T21:17:55+0300

### Marie — 2014-07-23T23:07:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-135317>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=135316)
>
> vets school is harder to get into than med school now.......people value their pets more than themselves

For the record it has been harder to get into vet school for +25 years. (I was on that track until I worked at an eVet clinic and saw how the vets were treated by nurses and MDs. It wasn't pretty and I decided that my ego wouldn't survive.)

The stats re grad school entrance have more to do with "super bright" young women who want to be vets, and less to do with societal trends. Women have less interest in status and money, so vet school is an option that won't be laughed at by their friends and family. If you don't believe me, go ask your DVM what they take home each year, their income is shockingly low.

Marie

### Skimo — 2014-07-24T07:40:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-135408>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=135316)
>
> vets school is harder to get into than med school now.......people value their pets more than themselves

It is what we hear in France too.
Anyone one can go in the first year of med school here, but only a few make it to the second year, yet there are medicine faculties in a lot of big cities.
While to become a vet you have to be selected first for two years of preparation before to go for the competitive examination alowing 400 students to go into vet school in France each year. We have four vet schools, the two oldest being the first ones founded in the world.

Still in France veterinary prices are considered to be too high by many customers, and the fact than medecine/drugs are refunded by social security and insurances has for consequence that people don't realize the real cost of Health care...
Vets also have lower incomes than other medical professionals with the same studies recquirement...

We don't have much primal food for pets yet in France. Getting food without cereals is already difficult...

### NeilBB — 2014-10-27T23:46:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-145346>

Personally, I consider this a form of child abuse. And I know that's not a subject to joke about. I'm dead serious.

What happens to the kid in Dead Poets Society comes to mind...

### Josh — 2014-10-27T23:57:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-145347>

My mother's parents told her that "she could be any kind of doctor she wanted to be". She of course wanted to be an art historian...not sure how she felt about her 37 years of practice in the end. It was certainly hard to get her to give it up. 13 days before she died....

### Josh — 2014-10-28T00:01:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-145348>

I should have been a UPS driver, actually had the opportunity. Then I would have gotten stock options when they went public as well. I could retire now.

### NeilBB — 2014-10-28T00:02:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-145349>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=145347)
>
> My mother's parents told her that "she could be any kind of doctor she wanted to be". She of course wanted to be an art historian...not sure how she felt about her 37 years of practice in the end. It was certainly hard to get her to give it up. 13 days before she died....

The problem is, when you hear nothing else your whole childhood, you don't have the mental independence to go against your family and you aren't ever able to properly evaluate your real options. Not until much later, at least. It is definitely psychological manipulation. And yep, I know from experience...

It's not that I think parents have to finance their kids higher education. It's not about that. It's more about brainwashing and blackmail that parents often do to kids. The kid in this post knows he doesn't want to go to med school. Especially today. It's a horrible financial idea from any rational perspective. His parents are clueless...

The only people who should be going to med school today are those with a burning passion to do so. Such a person would not be found polling the Internet about his potential career....

### yewwei.tan — 2014-10-28T01:09:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-145357>

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=145346)
>
> Personally, I consider this a form of child abuse. And I know that's not a subject to joke about. I'm dead serious.
>
> What happens to the kid in Dead Poets Society comes to mind...

Heh, reminds me of some of the Tiger Moms I personally know  Lots of my friends back in High School (age 23-25) are currently in med school. I went to a pretty high ranking High School in Singapore, so I happen to know lots of "elites" going down the path of trying to fit into the very perverse medical system over there. Speaking to some of those friends over the years, so many of them are trying to pick "easy" or well-paying jobs. The most sought-after positions always seem to be either a dermatologist, a radiologist, or an anesthesiologist. Some want to become gynaecologists, and almost nobody says "I want to become a brain surgeon".

There's definitely a streak especially so in Asian cultures to encourage "anyone who seems capable" to become a doctor or a lawyer. I definitely got a lot of "oh you're a great musician, those hands would make for a good surgeon" back when I was young. Didn't turn out to be my thing of course.

But even if I did have a passion for that area of expertise, I don't think I would have survived the process. With my current lifestyle, whereby the only real big risk I have is staring at a computer screen (I work from home, and work whenever and wherever I want), I'm already suffering enough health issues to warrant close attention to Quantum Health principles.

So you need someone who is not only motivated to do the job, but who also happen to be able to tunnel enough electrons in every tissue despite the numerous insults along the climb to becoming a top-tier medical professional . Those people are rare indeed.

I feel like my time is better spent following Dr. Kruse and learning from others several hours a day...

### Josh — 2014-10-28T03:24:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-145379>

I watched every day and cared for her over the last 7 months...for the last 12 days, she was no longer a "doctor", it was quite refreshing. Of course, as far as she was concerned she was no longer my mother either....her mother did the same, but it lasted a few years rather than a few days...

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-11-05T10:43:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-146195>

i was having a conversation with a local GP (MD) in a cafe last week and i was banging on about calcium efflux and why everyone is D deficient
when i mentioned the use of calcium channel blockers rather than Exogenous vitamin D, his response was "IS THERE EVIDENCE TO SHOW IT HELPS?"

i had to slap my head...all this training to learn the pathways and the pharmacology, and he couldnt use his own "education" to come up with the solution

it is so sad that medicine selects for the highest 10% of academic brilliance, to make them regurgitate concepts only to have them come out of university and not u

### Da-mo — 2014-11-05T11:36:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-146196>

The solution they were taught came in a pill?

My GP suggested Chantex to help me stop smoking - I asked him how it worked - he said "Oh I cant remember, but its a wonderful drug"

### Da-mo — 2014-11-06T00:17:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-146262>

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=146195)
>
> it is so sad that medicine selects for the highest 10% of academic brilliance, to make them regurgitate concepts only to have them come out of university and not u

The son of a work colleague didn't make the cut for Medicine and Surgery so first started in Genetics then later became a world leading researcher and innovator in the use of superfluids and nanoparticles used in the delivery of transdermal medication.

He studied at Otago University - one of the last, if not the only one left in the world that still uses cadavers to teach their students. There was a documentary film about it called "Donated to Science" in which the donors were filmed and interviewed while still alive and followed the whole process through death, dissection and memorial. It included interviews with the family members of the deceased and the medical students doing the dissections as well. It is one of the most moving films I have ever watched.

### NeilBB — 2014-11-12T17:53:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-147148>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=146272)
>
> We used real people in gross anatomy too.......no idea what they are doing now.

My apartment and clothes smelled like formaldehyde for 6 months that year. There is just no substitute for the experience of live cadavers...

You remember, LSU also used anesthetized live dogs for Harvey Miller's physiology labs back then. I remember PETA would be out in front of school picketing that practice every year. I know they don't do that anymore. We had several people faint in the dog labs and others were "conscientious objectors." They gave them something else to do instead, which I thought was BS--I don't think they should have been allowed to skip it. The experience of intubating and "operating on" a live animal was very different than dissecting a dead cadaver, and I think very helpful in our education...

i studied biology, not medicine. We did some cadavers, but most of the practice was on sedated animals. We did all sorts of small mammals. For the rest freshly killed frogs, fishes etc...that was basically butchering experience. the most interesting part were without any doubts sedated mammals, flowing blood means live.

Yes I did.
It is very hard to get rid of excess EPA.
Year by year I am making progress, but I still have too much EPA and not enough AA.

Yesterday I have given blood for another test, within couple of weeks I should have my latest results.

Until dr Jack publish more specific information, I follow (best I can) dr Patricia Kane when evaluating my results of Fatty Acids Analysis.
Most of what I do is trying to replicate part of her spreadsheet that I am able.
Possibly someone here could help with the other part.

The idea is rather simple.
She assumes that the best level for each analyzed fatty acids is to be in the middle of its range.

She does her blood analysis at [http://www.kennedykrieger.org/](http://www.kennedykrieger.org/)
I can't do that, I do mine at LabCorp.
Actually the analysis is done at Mayo Clinic.
You can see my (10/29/2013) blood analysis, prescription, results and my post-processing here:

### NeilBB — 2014-11-14T22:32:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-147401>

**Neurologist Burnout an Escalating Crisis**

Burnout is a "neurologic crisis," experts say, with the prevalence of burnout currently exceeding 50% among neurologists, higher than among other specialist groups.

But many neurologists suffer burnout during their career and may not even realize it.

"Most physicians, including myself in the past, are unaware that they are burned out," Bruce Sigsbee, MD, from Pen Bay Medical Center, Rockport, Maine, and past president of the American Academy of Neurology (AAN), told _Medscape Medical News_.

In retrospect, he said, there were many over his career that he was burned out. "Given the lack of insight, no active steps were taken or are typically taken to combat burnout. The first step is to recognize the problem," Dr Sigsbee said.

Dr Sigsbee and coauthor James L. Bernat, MD, from Dartmouth-Hitchcock Medical Center, Lebanon, New Hampshire, address physician burnout in an article [published online](http://www.neurology.org/content/early/2014/11/05/WNL.0000000000001077.short) November 5 in _Neurology_.

Dr Sigsbee said, "both the prevalence and the lack of insight into the issue" was the impetus behind their article. Burnout "impacts career satisfaction, but more importantly it has a deleterious impact on patient care," he said. Further, burned-out physicians "lack empathy and make errors." Burnout has been linked to higher rates of depression and suicidal thoughts and behaviors.

Hallmarks of physician burnout are loss of interest and enthusiasm for work (emotional exhaustion), feelings of cynicism (depersonalization), and a diminished sense of personal accomplishment (career dissatisfaction).

**No Choice But to Address It**

"The American Academy of Neurology under the leadership of the President Elect Terry Cascino, MD, is actively working to identify opportunities for the AAN to mitigate the severity and prevalence of burnout amongst its members," Dr Sigsbee said.

"Neurology is the only medical specialty that has both one of the highest rates of burnout and the poorest work–life balance," Neil A. Busis, MD, Department of Neurology, University of Pittsburgh School of Medicine, Pennsylvania, notes in a[linked editorial](http://www.neurology.org/content/early/2014/11/05/WNL.0000000000001087.short).

The AAN Workforce Task Force has predicted a future shortage of neurologists; neurologist burnout could contribute to the shortage but could also result from a shortage, Dr Busis notes.

"If burned out neurologists continue to practice, patient care may suffer due to poor medical judgment and more errors. If neurologists drop out of the workforce, access to care will decrease. If medical students consider neurology undesirable, few will enter the field," he writes.

"We really have no choice but to address burnout," Dr Busis noted in an interview with _Medscape Medical News_. "If we take the nation's health as a priority, we have to have caregivers who are healthy," he said.

Dr Sigsbee and Dr Bernat note that studies of workplace motivational factors suggest several preventive interventions for combating burnout. They include:

-   Providing counseling for physicians either individually or in groups, with a goal of improving adaptive skills to the stress and rapid changes in the healthcare environment;

-   Identifying and eliminating meaningless required hassle factors, such as electronic health record "clicks" or insurance mandates;

-   Redesigning practice to remove pressure to see patients in limited time slots and shift to team-based care; and

-   Creating a culture that promotes career advancement, mentoring, and recognition of accomplishments.

Dr Busis points out that most interventions to help burned out physicians center on the individual and are reactive, focusing on methods to handle stress and better adjust to the current system. "That is not enough. We must be proactive to prevent burnout from developing in the first place," Dr Busis told _Medscape Medical News_.

"To revitalize our specialty we need to advocate for another triple aim: revising the structure, processes, and outcomes of health care systems to recognize, value, and optimize physician wellness and career satisfaction," he notes in his editorial.

"If our efforts are successful, we will be better able to accomplish our professional mission: to increase the quality of neurologic care, decrease health care costs, and improve patient satisfaction," he concludes.

_Neurology_. Published online November 5, 2014.

[http://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/834878?src=stfb](http://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/834878?src=stfb)

### NeilBB — 2014-11-16T23:24:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-147569>

> [Marie said:](/goto/post?id=147568)
>
> 50% of what MDs learn in school is obsolete on their graduation day...

Yeah, but the problem is more that medical school (and much of modern education) indoctrinates you, doesn't educate you. So most have neither the method nor the desire to update their knowledge after graduation.

And the CME system is a complete fraud. A money-grab. Even worse is the re-certification system recently instituted across all American speciality boards. These systems serve 2 purposes: they whitewash the ignorance among practitioners to the public, and they make a lot of money for the organizers of the prepackaged "knowledge." A total fraud.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-11-16T23:48:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-147570>

denise minger's "about" page sums up the problems with formal education as well as any:

_**Who do I think I am, running a health blog without a nutrition PhD? Shouldn’t I be flipping burgers at McDonalds like all those other English majors?**_

_I get this question a lot. It speaks volumes about how we view learning, and why we’ve abandoned personal responsibility for using our own brains when it comes to health. “We can’t possibly understand nutrition if we haven’t paid for a degree! Let’s just trust someone with formal credentials instead of thinking for ourselves.”_

**_First of all, if you believe valid education only happens in a classroom setting, I sure hope you aren’t reading this blog on a computer—since both Steve Jobs and Bill Gates were college dropouts without any credentials to work with technology._**

_I guess I’ll start by explaining my perspective. I have deep respect for formal learning, and a touch of envy for those who thrive in a traditional school system. Most of my family works in higher education (my dad, a college vice president; my mom, a former biologist who did postgraduate immunology research), and my original aspiration was to teach at the university level. Some awesome stuff happens there._

_But I also believe that—for people who are self-motivated, have the time and resources for independent study, and aren’t learning something like dentistry or surgery that requires hands-on training—that a college education can be wildly inefficient and sometimes a barrier to objective thinking. Teachers, after all, come equipped with their own set of biases—ones students must cater to or even adopt if they want a good grade. (My college Women’s History prof comes to mind. Don’t agree that men are the root of all things evil, fattening, and smelly? Then no “A” for you!) At least in my experience, college fostered an atmosphere where the rewards (high marks, scholarships, making the parents proud) were more pertinent than what was actually learned._

_My post-college education strategy has been simple. I approach the field of nutrition like learning a new language: total immersion-style. You didn’t learn your native tongue by sitting in a classroom following grammar lessons; you learned it by jumping into an initially confusing world and feeling your way around until it all started making sense. Every day, I make a conscious effort to surround myself with learning opportunities. I read everything I can get my hands on—from statistics textbooks to scientific papers. I find curricula posted on university websites, copy the lesson plans that look relevant, and acquire the reading material from the library instead of paying thousands of dollars for classroom instruction. If I can’t grasp something on my own, I email or call smart people and ask them to help me. **My goal is to understand. I don’t stop digging until I’ve plowed to the bottom and broken my shovel trying to go even deeper.**_

_I believe anything can be learned. I believe passion is the best fuel for knowledge acquisition. I believe the subjects that have personal relevance are the most enticing, intriguing, and fulfilling ones to study. This is why I blog._

Modern medical training sits you in a classroom and forces you to regurgitate concepts.
When you can remember and regurgitate more concepts, your grade goes up, then you graduate.

My very first lecturer set the stage for my curiosity, the professor said: "no matter what you read, or what you hear from a lecturer, dont believe it. go and read around it until you know the truth."

### BTA — 2014-11-17T01:46:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-147578>

**.
Salt-Water Fish Extinction Seen By 2048**

Already, 29% of edible fish and seafood species have declined by **90%** -- **a drop that means the collapse of these fisheries**.

But the issue isn't just having seafood on our plates. Ocean species filter toxins from the water. They protect shorelines. And they reduce the risks of algae blooms such as the red tide.
"A large and increasing proportion of our population lives close to the coast; thus the loss of services such as flood control and waste detoxification can have disastrous consequences," Worm and colleagues say."

[http://www.cbsnews.com/news/salt-water-fish-extinction-seen-by-2048/](http://www.cbsnews.com/news/salt-water-fish-extinction-seen-by-2048/)

awesome... my first exposure to Rush was in 1981. While learning to ski at Mammoth. my instructor was a huge rush fan. and me in my naivety of youth - asked him why they were so awesome when compared to the beetles and Don Mclean - (my first foray in to music was much different that others). He pulled out a tape - and when we got to the top of the mountain - we went in for coco - he had one of his buddies put the tape on the tape player. I was blown away.... and I began to look for meaning in music from that point forward. I soon stumbled upon Pink floyd.... and from there my downward spiral into music commenced.... I'm currently stuck on Tom Waits right now.....

### Josh — 2014-11-24T21:12:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-148338>

**"The Nature of Excitement**
**The Eleventh of the Twelve-Teachings**

The very nature of excitement is to stimulate the senses and upset the delicate balance between body, mind and spirit. If equilibrium within the body, mind or spirit is upset, either positively or negatively, the fulcrum of the energy field itself shifts and the conscious mind no longer submits to the ‘shining mind’. Instead, real knowledge is obscured due to aberrations that are the dominating influence of the conscious mind.

Excitement and glamour are very powerful distractions
that feed the urge for sensorial stimulation

Given that pleasure is more desirable than pain, it is easy to see why we enjoy this stimulation. However, the thirst for stimulation, when unbalanced, breeds misery. Misery naturally flows from the pursuit of pleasure because the pursuit of pleasure and desire can never be satiated. Left unsatisfied, one can be effortlessly drawn into the pursuit of entertainments and distractions, which can temporarily alleviate dissatisfaction. This is a vicious cycle.

**_We are all prejudiced by our own inclinations and preferences. Real knowledge and work begins in our own back yard. When questioned, the master said: “A rake sweeps leaves. A hoe tills the soil. A shovel digs in the dirt. The human mind is like a back yard where the leaves have fallen, the soil is barren and the earth is waiting for redemption.”_**

**This except is from the late Geoffrey Wilson - respect to him and his legacy"

### Shijin13 — 2014-12-10T19:41:57+0200

I wouldn't want to pull the nights. but I'd be able to manage. seriously. ER Would be easier than running the O'Course in full battle rattle and having blanks fired at you to simulate conflict. Then again I've also been through the crash course of battlefield medicine AKA "Oh Shit, the medics down - now WTF do I do?" Which has come in handy a time or two w/Chaos and Havoc. We've avoided several trips to the ER for things that if I didn't have that training I'd have spent probably $600 easy in ER CoPays. Our house has a stock of Steri strips, iodine, & muprocin.

hell I needed a steri strip for a cut on my toe from dancing the night away at the Ritz on Friday....

### RobH — 2015-01-10T00:20:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-152266>

I think we would all welcome those dissenters and googlers if we had the time to take care of our patients the way she want to.

I live at the hard edge between those administrative folks thinking we whine too much and we should be more efficient, toe the line, and treat each patient like another piece of the assembly line when all of us know that each person is their own N=1. On my other days I play doctor and know that they are all wrong.

Thanks for finding that!

\-rh

### Josh — 2015-01-10T17:46:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-152340>

> [Marie said:](/goto/post?id=152292)
>
> Funny, I've received the BEST care/diagnoses from these "young" docs. One diagnosed my gluten issues WAY before gluten was "in" and the other said I needed surgery... to this day I can't thank them enough.

"An old-time physician whose son had just graduated from medical school decided to pass on some tips about the profession. One day his son accompanied his father while. he was making his hospital rounds. The first patient they called on was advised by his father to cut down on smoking. "How did you manage to come to that conclusion?" asked the son.
"Just look around the room and observe the large number of cigarette ends," was his answer.
The second patient was told to stop eating so much chocolate. Again the new doctor was bewildered. "How come?" he said.
"You don't look," said the father. "If you had you would notice the large number of chocolate boxes all over the place."
"I think I have got the idea now," said the son. "Let me try the next one."
A woman, who was the third patient, was informed by the son to cut down on things involving the church, religion, and priests. The amazed father asked his son what led him to that strange conclusion, since the church had not even been mentioned in the conversation, and you cannot find churches all over the place.
"Well Dad, it was like this," said the son. "Did you notice that I dropped the thermometer? When I bent down to pick it up I could not help noticing the preacher under the bed.""

### BTA — 2015-02-17T02:10:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-155826>

.
According the medical "bible" - JAMA reports the 3rd leading cause of death is .... **Doctors**  yikes............

**Journal of the American Medical Association, 284(4), 483-485. (_2000, July 26_)**

Who has the best healthcare? Japan.... in order: 1 Japan, 2 Sweden, 3 Canada, 4 France, 5 Australia, 6 Spain, 7 Finland, 8 Netherlands, 9 U.K., 10 Denmark, 11 Belgium,12 US, and 13 Germany

The poor performance of the United States was confirmed by the World Health Organization. The press would have you believe that it is the American lifestyle.. smoking, drinking, etc., but the statistics show otherwise. The top ranking best health care statistics are from Japan and 41% of the women smoke and 61% of the men. US stats are 24% of women smoke and 28% men. The data for alcohol is similar.

**_"Unrighteous doctors dealing drugs that will never cure your ills"_**
~**Bob Dylan**

### Sammy — 2015-02-17T13:19:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-155865>

Can any of the smart doctors on this thread recommend any medical textbooks ? It can be biochemistry, physiology, anatomy etc.
I am in first year ^\_^

### Jack Kruse — 2015-02-17T15:39:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-155883>

The books you use in med school wont be a help........Pollack book on water (The fourth phase of water) however will make you realize what is in your biochem book is missing the key ingredient.........Water.

### RobH — 2015-02-19T18:44:28+0200

RobH

### NeilBB — 2015-03-20T15:17:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-159032>

[http://www.ksdk.com/story/life/2015/03/19/dramatic-photo-doctor-grieving-losing-patient/25021031/](http://www.ksdk.com/story/life/2015/03/19/dramatic-photo-doctor-grieving-losing-patient/25021031/)

### Shijin13 — 2015-03-20T15:34:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-159035>

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=159032)
>
> [http://www.ksdk.com/story/life/2015/03/19/dramatic-photo-doctor-grieving-losing-patient/25021031/](http://www.ksdk.com/story/life/2015/03/19/dramatic-photo-doctor-grieving-losing-patient/25021031/)

recently experienced something very similar when a friend lost her baby at 37wks. baby was stillborn, but was revived after 25mins w/o o2 in the womb. baby was transferred to Children's. The neonatal neurologist, was hit very hard by this. 1) he felt the ObGYN could have prevented the outcome when mom arrived at the hospital for low fetal movements and 2) he knew the family personally. He was devastated. One of our friends who stayed with them at Children's over heard a NICUE nurse make the following comment to a brand new NICU nurse. "When you no longer are impacted by death of a patient (child or otherwise) its time to find a new job. "

I imagine that the deaths of any patient hits doc's hard. They're just as human as the rest of us.

### RobH — 2015-03-20T17:24:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-159049>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=159035)
>
> recently experienced something very similar when a friend lost her baby at 37wks. baby was stillborn, but was revived after 25mins w/o o2 in the womb. baby was transferred to Children's. The neonatal neurologist, was hit very hard by this. 1) he felt the ObGYN could have prevented the outcome when mom arrived at the hospital for low fetal movements and 2) he knew the family personally. He was devastated. One of our friends who stayed with them at Children's over heard a NICUE nurse make the following comment to a brand new NICU nurse. "When you no longer are impacted by death of a patient (child or otherwise) its time to find a new job. "
>
> I imagine that the deaths of any patient hits doc's hard. They're just as human as the rest of us.

In my line of work you don't usually get to know the patient well enough when they arrive in extremis. It's the conversations with the families afterwards where I tear up.

In the few cases we see where everybody knows it's time to let go and we do our best (in a less than ideal environment) to make it a good death, even those are tough.

RobH

### Sammy — 2015-04-18T14:32:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-161637>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=155883)
>
> The books you use in med school wont be a help........Pollack book on water (The fourth phase of water) however will make you realize what is in your biochem book is missing the key ingredient.........Water.

Hi Dr. Kruse, I read the book last year. It is one of the reasons why I was hoping that there would be a book out there that would take into account the importance of water in biology.

I had been meaning to ask you this for a long time. I feel kind of lost right now and am not really sure what is the best way I should approach my studies. Because I have been exposed to your blog, I am aware that there are definitely things, important things, that are in medicine's blind spot. How should I go about this? I know I can't rely solely on whatever I am taught at uni and I will need to do extra things. I mean I can read your blogs. But I also don't feel 'satisfied'(?) or too good about myself if I am getting spoonfed with information all the time from you and the fact that you are always pointing out things in our blind spot. For example, if you never pointed out how light was so important to biology, I'd never even suspect that there was anything wrong with blue light at night. Sure, we get taught that radiation can damage DNA and cause cancer etc. But we were really only considering radiation like ionizing radiation etc, we weren't considering how other forms of visible light, infrared can affect the molecules in our bodies as well.

How do you start to see, for yourself, the things that were always in your blindspot without relying on others to point them out? Where does one even start? How do you draw conclusions? How do you decide what to focus on ( e.g. light as opposed to food)? What is important and what is not? I just know time is so precious and I feel that I am not using my time as effectively as I should.

Sorry I am not being very articulate right now. I am just looking for some guidance. When I look to my classmates, I can sense how stressed some of them are becoming and how overwhelmed they feel with exams, assignments, extra curriculars, work, volunteering, spending time with friends and family. And I feel that way too sometimes. I know there must be another way and a better way of getting through medical school while still taking good care of my health........ I want to do well and at the same time I don't want to sacrifice my health

### Josh — 2015-04-18T15:17:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-161638>

Doing well is not sacrificing the last few years of myelination in your brain. Did you ever explore meditation in any form? Start anoptimal health study group to recruit some comrades in the struggle. See Carolyn's GOHWell foundation on facebook to promote these ideas in AUSTRALIA. Be gentle with yourself.

### Sammy — 2015-04-18T17:08:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-161642>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=161638)
>
> **Doing well is not sacrificing the last few years of myelination in your brain.**

I love this!

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=161638)
>
> Did you ever explore meditation in any form? Start anoptimal health study group to recruit some comrades in the struggle. See Carolyn's GOHWell foundation on facebook to promote these ideas in AUSTRALIA. Be gentle with yourself.

I did. I started a very basic qi gong exercise I learnt off youtube and was doing this every night for quite a while. But I stopped when a very experienced tui na masseuse from China told me that I shouldn't be practicising qi gong without the supervision of a sifu because it could really mess up with your qi if you don't know what you are doing and she has seen it happen before. Nowadays, after a long day of uni, I lie down on the grass in my backyard and just relax, watch the clouds, sky, trees, listen to the sounds,the feeling of the grass on my skin, the wind etc...this is how I practice mindfulness.

### Josh — 2015-04-18T17:37:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-161646>

> [Sammy said:](/goto/post?id=161642)
>
> I love this!
>
>
>
> I did. I started a very basic qi gong exercise I learnt off youtube and was doing this every night for quite a while. But I stopped when a very experienced tui na masseuse from China told me that I shouldn't be practicising qi gong without the supervision of a sifu because it could really mess up with your qi if you don't know what you are doing and she has seen it happen before. Nowadays, after a long day of uni, I lie down on the grass in my backyard and just relax, watch the clouds, sky, trees, listen to the sounds,the feeling of the grass on my skin, the wind etc...this is how I practice mindfulness.

Bruce Frantzis does a good job of describing the correct gradient for practice in this book. If you get into trouble, there are teachers in Australia. When in doubt, go slower, less is often more. Lying on the grass is good stuff.

[http://www.amazon.com/Opening-Energy-Gates-Your-Body/dp/1583941460](http://www.amazon.com/Opening-Energy-Gates-Your-Body/dp/1583941460)

Because I have been exposed to your blog, I am aware that there are definitely things, important things, that are in medicine's blind spot. How should I go about this? I know I can't rely solely on whatever I am taught at uni and I will need to do extra things. I mean I can read your blogs. But I also don't feel 'satisfied'(?) or too good about myself if I am getting spoonfed with information all the time from you and the fact that you are always pointing out things in our blind spot. For example, if you never pointed out how light was so important to biology, I'd never even suspect that there was anything wrong with blue light at night. Sure, we get taught that radiation can damage DNA and cause cancer etc. But we were really only considering radiation like ionizing radiation etc, we weren't considering how other forms of visible light, infrared can affect the molecules in our bodies as well.

How do you decide what to focus on ( e.g. light as opposed to food)? What is important and what is not? >

I want to do well and at the same time I don't want to sacrifice my health

Read what you must to do well........but always keep the wisdom your learning from physics and make sure it guides you. I wish I had this advice in residency.......I would have been a rockstar back then and could have affected so many more young minds. People tend not to listen to old NS...........unless I have power of your career. LOL

### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-18T18:00:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-161656>

The wisdom of the universe is crammed into you........remember it. They will teach you all disease manifest from genes and from within........what I have learned from physics is that all disease manifest from outside and our gene have to keep up temporally in time and in environments where the processes can't keep up temporally or spatially, diseases manifest. When faced with a challenging case they can't answer.......you'll know where to look. I do this all the time in the ICU and people are just like.......how did you know to look there? I say why would you where you do? Bad thinking.

No human being itself should be considered impaired innately, instead there are environmental short comings that cause the impairment. Thus, it is incumbent on the on the clinician to recommend treatment of the environment their patient is in. People react to an inferior environment.

### Sammy — 2015-04-22T03:05:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-161992>

Thank you for your words of wisdom Dr Kruse. I always like to listen to what "old NS" have to say! I mean it
It's always about timing, environment and the laws of physics! I really need to make time to get the physics part down pat.

But there are definitely more and more clinicians and researchers that are emphasising the importance of the environment although they don't really know what exactly it is in the environment is affecting us so much.

Just wanted to share what my autoimmune lecturer showed with us last year which really hammers the point that our zip code can be as important as our genetic code, probably even more so!

[http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/v464/n7293/full/nature08990.html](http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/v464/n7293/full/nature08990.html)

Even though the twin girls had identical genomes, and identical epigenomes and trascriptomes in their CD4+ T cells......one had multiple sclerosis and the other didn't. To me, it implies that there there must be something else that is bigger than just the molecules......and that is the field in which the the molecules act.

### yonirv — 2015-05-22T08:07:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-165010>

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=115796)
>
> Pretty good article. But Gladwell and most of my colleagues think it's just a matter of doctors "not having time to deal with the business side" because they have been trained in medicine not business. Blah. Blah. Blah.
>
> In actuality, it's far worse than that IMO. The "business side" of medicine has been hijacked by a socialist style government. This is not a business now. This is a quasi-nationalized extension of the government.
>
> Shijin, I know your are an admirer of Ayn Rand. Have you read any of Peikoff's articles on the the morality of a "right to health care?" He has been opposing government intervention into medicine since 1965 when the King-Anderson bill created Medicare and set us on the path to economic destruction we now find ourselves in.
>

A breath of fresh air. I too think the blah blah of 'the business side of medicine' is just egregiously misunderstanding the effect of arraying virtually thousands of bureaucrats against the essentially unaided lone doctor, who escapes the paperwork for oh say minutes at a time, to actually take care of people. As to the blah blah about "not knowing if the ACA will encourage the bottom up changes needed, and (get this) more physician leaders" is absurd. of course it will not. It cannot. It is and will keep on being top down. thanks.

# Thread: Found this in CT6
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/found-this-in-ct6.10191/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sooperb — 2014-03-16T16:31:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/found-this-in-ct6.10191/post-115781>

"Physical exercise, in cold water, such as swimming causes the body to lose heat at a much faster rate than remaining still in the water."

In all seriousness, does this mean I should and could thrash around a bit in the CT bath?

### Dali Dula — 2014-03-16T17:33:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/found-this-in-ct6.10191/post-115795>

Swimming in really cold water becomes impossible in short order. Ocean swims are brief when water is cold because you have to keep moving. I have learned to stay close to shore both in ocean and ponds when temps get low. I still prefer to move during CT. Just sitting in the tub kind of sucks. I am contemplating getting a large barrel so I can sort of squat and remain mobile.

### Inger — 2014-03-16T17:49:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/found-this-in-ct6.10191/post-115797>

I have started to swim in the river lately as the ice is gone where I use to dip - using no hat or gloves or socks at all anymore. I might be in only a minute but I move and swim around. I love it! The water is about +0,5 degree C
I do not really feel cold after at all! My hands and feet also warm up very fast. I too like this better than sitting still in the water with hat and socks and gloves for 2 minutes lol

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-16T19:10:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/found-this-in-ct6.10191/post-115807>

CT 6 is one of those blogs you need to live to truly get

### prAna303 — 2014-03-16T19:30:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/found-this-in-ct6.10191/post-115811>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=115807)
>
> CT 6 is one of those blogs you need to live to truly get

Yes, this blog was the one that got me stuck in this place... It put so much in place for me, the things i did without knowing why and how. From starting my love with cold when i was around six and nearly drowned when going thru ice. Why i love to run in shorts only , when it is -10C, why i love being in shorts the whole summer so sun can catch me. Why i always slept in freezing cold bedrooms. Why i feel sick when it is more that +18C inside, i have to get outside.

Then adding up with all this quantum stuff, damn you got me hooked.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-16T22:14:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/found-this-in-ct6.10191/post-115831>

Nature has........because you let her play your instrument.

### caroline — 2014-03-16T23:16:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/found-this-in-ct6.10191/post-115846>

I hate sitting in a bathtub so much ...I suppose it would be better/ colder.....but I feel so much better in the ocean - better for my soul....I am hoping the trade off is worth it!

### sooperb — 2014-03-18T22:42:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/found-this-in-ct6.10191/post-116272>

Audrey a thousand times I would prefer open water, more interesting, doesn't smell of chlorine, the sky above, the rest of nature joining in, a bath tub can't compete apart from being right there, all the time and you know you aren't in danger lol. Which reminds me, I need to find a bath ball to stick on the taps to filter the water, off to look.

# Thread: Superfluids=Redox

**Subforum:** Redox Rx
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-16T16:59:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/superfluids-redox.10192/post-115789>

So you are learning water is one superfluid........now here is another.

Remember the universe is fractal. Self similar. Water happens to be life's superfluid and hydrogen and helium happen to be a star's superfluid.

The core of Jupiter is believed to be a type of super fluid.

### nonchalant — 2014-03-17T20:41:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/superfluids-redox.10192/post-115985>

Very similar to Pollack's experiments with water. This film shows experimenters using very cold temps, but what about using higher pressure? Lots of pressure on stars and gas giant planets.

Heh, I liked this quote:

> New radical theories were needed to explain \[the phenomena\].

Not radical, just closer to the truth.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-17T20:45:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/superfluids-redox.10192/post-115988>

yep.......water is life's superfluid.......and the next blog will show.
