# Thread: MY N=1 "I TOLD YOU!"
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-n-1-i-told-you.10201/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kmonty — 2014-03-17T17:41:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-n-1-i-told-you.10201/post-115946>

Early years 1966-1980. Very active BMXing, Martial Arts, all kinds of sports. Looking back my eating habits were not good. Cereal (a lot) and a lifelong candy addiction (that’s a food group..right) Hot Tamale’s, Skittles, Whoppers, Mentos, you get the idea!

I am 48 now and I ate this way all my whole life up until the last 10 years when I became a little more aware of my quality of food.

My health, I believe, has been outstanding in spite of my food quality, eating habits. I consider myself as never getting sick. As I got older I told myself “I don’t have time to be sick” Even when I would get sick I continued on with my daily routine and rub some dirt on it LOL.

I guess around the age of 25 I started to get migraines they weren’t very often; normally it was after working out I just thought/assumed/contributed it to dehydration. Migraines would be from mild to debilitating, crawl in bed and sleep it off. Between 35 – 45 they got more frequently about 2 x per month not very many debilitating ones, I could function, so I continued my daily routine. Since I started doing bulletproof this has improved very much and I get them every couple of months.

In 2000 is when my eating habits started to change, I had moved to Colorado, I was not training as much although still active, Golf, young kids chasing them. We were having food delivered from a farm in Colorado grass fed, range chicken and organic veggies, for the most part.

Although I was feeling good physically I started to notice that I had put on 10 lbs. I don’t want to blow this out of proportion; I have never had weight issues. I have always been thin/lean I am 5’ 11” 135 when I graduated High school in 1984, then most of my adult years 140-145. When I decided that I needed to loose some weight I was 150-155. It was my belief that I felt better at 145, stronger, faster! (Six Million Dollar Man).

In (2002-2003) I did the Body For Life to help me shed the 10 Lbs. I did feel better and dropped the weight that I wanted. The other cool thing at the time is I had no problem cutting out the candy even with the “free day” I still found myself wanting to eat healthy. Not to say that I cut out all foods that had sugars but I did pretty well. (His shake mix that I used had sugar). I followed this protocol for a year and then slowly moved away from it, but keeping some of the principles for another 6 months. I began to eat mostly organic foods, but I went back on the serious sugar candy binging I was used to my whole life.

### kmonty — 2014-03-17T17:43:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-n-1-i-told-you.10201/post-115950>

Foods that are a problem for me.
· Onions, use to give me smelly gas (funny to drop boms and talk of my family) about 8 years ago it began to upset my stomach really bad, not so funny. I just stay away from them now.
· Garlic as well no bad ass gas but lots of belching and upset stomach this is recently started about a year ago.

Work wise since 1989 I have been around computers either sitting in front of one or in an environment that has had them.

My father had a scare with Prostate cancer and unfortunately he had the surgery. My PSA #’s have been kinda high but we heard in Dr. K’s webinar that the PSA number is worthless.

I never drank alcohol, about 3 years ago my primary care physician told me I was pre-diabetic so I cut down on candy intake and that brought my labs back to normal however, I didn’t stop eating the candy until about 7 months ago when I went Bulletproof and I haven’t looked back since at that time I also stopped eating wheat, corn, dairy and all grains (I eat some rice now and again). Now I am eating a lot more seafood because my girlfriend eats epipalio so I eat what she eats.

I hurt my neck on a roller coaster about 10 years ago since then it is always bothering me every day and when I do certain moves surfing it will really hurts.

At the age of 43-44 I had shoulder surgery Labrel ligament tear from skateboarding with my kids and at 46 I had ACL surgery from tae kwon do. I have recovered well from both.

Up until about 1 ½ years ago I always felt my health was pretty good, I felt good had good energy. I keep a really regular schedule up at 4:00am M-F in bed between 8:30 to 10pm.

· Monday, Wednesday, Friday I do core and light cardio. Then I teach an hour Martial Arts class then go to my full time job.
· Tuesday and Thursday ecliptic weights and some cardio followed by my full time job.
· Saturday and Sunday is reserved for Surfing.

I never ate before working out in the past and then 7 months ago I started Bulletproof intermittent fasting and now I don’t eat anything until noon-2:00 everyday. I hoped this would help with the below mentioned heart issues but it hasn’t.

About 1 ½ yrs ago I started getting heart arrhythmias. My heart has never been an issue or even a concern for me until now. I get so many arrhythmias every day I lose count. I also get bouts of light headedness and tingling sensation in my head.

My girlfriend KyraKitty (Betsy) on this forum believes it is probably due to EMF’s. The apt we lived in, for 5 years, had 13 smart meters installed sometime after we moved in. They were located on the wall of our kitchen along the crawlspace area. I would spend hours siting at the kitchen table on my laptop with the 13 smart meters behind and below me.

She also had heart issues really bad 2 ½ years before me, as outlined in her optimal journal, when we lived in that apartment. Hers were as bad as mine are and she got really sick shortly after and couldn’t work but now her heart issues have completely stopped. We recently moved to a new apt on 12/1/2014 the smart meter is nowhere near us now. I also have a computer/phone line server room in a small office I work out of and run in Cupertino, CA for our company that is based in SF. I plan to have Michael Neurt come out to evaluate the EMF situation at our home and at my shop too very soon.

# Thread: Optimal or bust...

**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal

### Mare — 2014-03-17T23:34:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-or-bust.10209/post-116042>

Hi Everyone, I, like all of you have been on the hunt to feel better/optimal. I'm diagnosed Hashi's and have been battling to get well for several years. I'm a single mom with a wicked 14 y/o boy that I love to death and I'm hoping that by finding my way on this journey, I can help him avoid some pain down the road...

Here's a nutshell of my progression thus far:

-   Diagnosed Hashi's 2007-synthroid prescribed, continued to decline, extreme muscle/joint pain, headaches, brain fog
-   August 2010- Found lil' article linking gluten to hashi's quit the stuff and within' 24 hrs, almost all pain resolved (found later that dairy in certain amounts causes' me fits too) Quittin the G was like hell on earth for this philly girl weaned on cheesesteaks, pizza and tastykakes!
-   I eat a fairly clean/paleoish diet, but I'm at a plateau...
-   So basically all the muscle pain is gone, but left with less than stellar energy/stamina and I feel a bit dumber than I once believed....things just take longer to sink in, it stinks
-   Chronic constipation, since childhood (Not breastfed?) Take tons of mag, but it's hit or miss on if I get a movement
-   Last year learned via a Genova labs stool test I have a level 4+ yeast. Spent the last year chasing that beast and after about 10k in alt docs and supplements I don't think it made a dent.
-   My other devilish symptom is cystic acne, it comes and goes and is less severe since altering my diet, but none the less a total downer. PMS, FIBROID at 25, Estrogen dominance?? have labs if anyone can help me interpret??
-   Mom diagnosed with BC at 55 now at 63 it's moved to bone, she's suffered her entire life with hormonal disorders, endo, fibroids, acne, now breast cancer that's taking her life. It's a horror to watch.

Tried to make it to the point! I'm fascinated by the EMF and CT connections. Dr. Jack Kruse is my hero even though he hurts my head daily!! I'm trying to make this very technical info work on a daily basis, would be most interested in the links that give specifics on CT duration, etc. This is the one I dread the most. My temp runs at 97 degrees, constantly bundled up, so the submerging in cold is tough for me, but I will do it to get well...any tips on getting started would be much appreciated

I look forward to joining you all on this path.
Mare

### Josh — 2014-03-18T03:50:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/redox-rx-podcast-with-ameer-rosic.10210/post-116107>

> **The Takeaway: The link between electromagnetic signals and water**
> **An electron-rich body charge encourages oxygen to penetrate cells, prevents junk molecules from sticking to cell membranes and DNA base pairs, vanquishes pathogens and powers our immune cells.** [It also decreases the need for glucose in your diet.](http://jackkruse.com/emf-4-why-might-you-need-carbs-for-performance/) The interchange between electromagnetic signals and water is where we need to focus for optimal health.

Otpimal water/EZ=best defense

More glucose metabolism increases oxidative stress, reduces redox potential, destroys EZ=methylation defects because other things bind to DNA and or methyl groups do not bind to properly inhibit/promote=altered phenotypic expression which may include mutagenesis. EZ water preserves optimal methylation and phenotypic expression, glucose metabolism often destroys it. All may be promoted by EMF as demonstrated by the studies of V, A and B.

> cause increase ROS at cytochrome one.... EMF 4

Sinclair: EZ water=optimal oxygen levels, he sees NAD as the key for signalling, I am not a good enough biochemist yet...NAD=Oxygen=EZ optimized by cold and ketosis

> As mammals age, says Sinclair, levels of NAD drop by 50%; with less of the compound, the communication between the cell and its mitochondrial energy source also falters, and the cell becomes vulnerable to common aging assaults — inflammation, muscle wasting and slower metabolism. By tricking the cell into thinking it’s young again, with adequate amounts of NAD, aging can theoretically be reversed. “When we give the molecule, the cells think oxygen levels are normal and everything revs back up again,” Sinclair says.
>
> Read more: [Reversing Aging: Scientists Say Yes, It May Be Possible | TIME.com](http://healthland.time.com/2013/12/19/reversing-aging-not-as-crazy-as-you-think/#ixzz2wH5nqs6A) [http://healthland.time.com/2013/12/19/reversing-aging-not-as-crazy-as-you-think/#ixzz2wH5nqs6A](http://healthland.time.com/2013/12/19/reversing-aging-not-as-crazy-as-you-think/#ixzz2wH5nqs6A)

# Thread: Sherpa in Action....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sherpa-in-action.10211/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-03-18T02:00:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sherpa-in-action.10211/post-116087>

I watched the movie "The Summit" over the weekend. In between all of the melodrama of Western climber bemoaning their fate and the loss of partners, one had to pay attention to notice Pemba. He did not sleep the night before the climb. He had to get out of his tent to rescue someone before climbing who almost blew off of K2 in the middle of the night at 25,000 feet. He climbed K2, and came down to camp (3,000 feet in each direction). He came down, did not sleep and then went back up part way to rescue a few people. Some friends died in front of him on this round. He came down, slept a few hours and went up again to rescue someone else. He did not sleep more than a few hours in 3-4 days and performed at this level above 25,000 feet. He was no doubt dehydrated and probably did not eat much. He climbed down with some rescued climbers and walked out from K2 base camp.

[http://www.pemba.com.np/The savior and the storm on K2.php](http://www.pemba.com.np/The%20savior%20and%20the%20storm%20on%20K2.php)

### nicld — 2014-03-18T03:13:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sherpa-in-action.10211/post-116103>

I LOVE watching shows on Everest. The little hair on my little toe would love to try that but then the rest of my body yells HELL NO. I know my limits.

We watched Everest: Beyond The Limit and was just amazed at the Sherpa's. They would not think twice about helping the climbers, they are machines.

### caroline — 2014-03-18T04:18:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sherpa-in-action.10211/post-116111>

When I was there we were ill prepared for the snow and unseasonably cold weather. Only a light jacket, no boots etc. I was doing C/T and didn't know it .....and thrived!

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-18T15:33:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sherpa-in-action.10211/post-116197>

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-18T15:57:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sherpa-in-action.10211/post-116200>

This is a great book on Summiting Everest. its quite chilling how very few of the people on this trek were not prepared. [http://bit.ly/In2thinAir](http://bit.ly/In2thinAir)

# Thread: Beyond CRON
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beyond-cron.10220/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-03-18T19:07:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beyond-cron.10220/post-116226>

> Dietary restriction (DR) famously extends lifespan and reduces fecundity across a diverse range of species. A prominent hypothesis suggests that these life-history responses evolved as a survival-enhancing strategy whereby resources are redirected from reproduction to somatic maintenance, enabling organisms to weather periods of resource scarcity. We argue that this hypothesis is inconsistent with recent evidence and at odds with the ecology of natural populations. We consider a wealth of molecular, medical, and evolutionary research, and conclude that the lifespan extension effect of DR is likely to be a laboratory artifact: in contrast with captivity, most animals living in natural environments may fail to achieve lifespan extension under DR. What, then, is the evolutionary significance of the suite of responses that extend lifespan in the laboratory? We suggest that these responses represent a highly conserved nutrient recycling mechanism that enables organisms to maximize immediate reproductive output under conditions of resource scarcity.

[http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/bies.201300165/abstract](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/bies.201300165/abstract)

[http://www.foodnavigator.com/Scienc...-low-nutrient-diet-may-offer-anti-aging-clues](http://www.foodnavigator.com/Science-Nutrition/Dietary-restriction-Very-low-nutrient-diet-may-offer-anti-aging-clues)

### Josh — 2014-04-03T05:07:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beyond-cron.10220/post-119552>

But they keep trying to sell CRON....

> Extreme calorie counting boosts lifespan in monkeys, according to new research.
>
> Until now, the rationale for following an ultra-low calorie diet to ward off ageing has been based on experiments in worms and mice.
>
> Studies reported in [Nature Communications](http://www.nature.com/ncomms/index.html) found primates also benefited from the regime.
>
> A nutritionist urged caution over severe calorie restriction in humans, saying more research was needed.
>
> Perhaps Benjamin Franklin was right when he said, 'To lengthen thy life lessen thy meals'”
>
> Dr Emma Williams British Nutrition Foundation
> Advocates of the Calorie Restriction (CR) diet claim that by severely restricting the number of calories they consume they will live longer, perhaps into their hundreds.
>
> They cite a wealth of scientific evidence dating back more than 75 years.
>
> Much of the research is based on experiments in animals such as mice and worms, with primate studies giving conflicting results.
>
> Now, a US team has published new evidence showing CR also shows benefits in primates.
>
> Less is more
>
> "CR works to delay ageing in primate species," Dr Rozalyn Anderson of the department of medicine at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, told BBC News. "Our study data is consistent with that."
>
> The study found CR boosted survival in a group of rhesus monkeys studied over the course of decades.

[http://www.bbc.com/news/health-26837211](http://www.bbc.com/news/health-26837211)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-03T07:42:57+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-17T21:31:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beyond-cron.10220/post-122210>

[http://blogs.scientificamerican.com...t-eating-a-lot-less-wont-let-you-live-longer/](http://blogs.scientificamerican.com/talking-back/2012/08/29/big-hint-that-eating-a-lot-less-wont-let-you-live-longer/)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-21T04:41:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beyond-cron.10220/post-122741>

A National Institute on Aging study published in Nature shows that more than 20 years of limiting calorie intake by 30 percent in a group of rhesus monkeys did not allow them to live longer than a control group of monkeys who were eating a normal, unrestricted diet. “I think the message is that a caloric restriction response, if it exists for monkeys and by implication humans, may occur only under the **narrowest conditions**,” says Steven N. Austad, a professor at University of Texas Health Science Center San Antonio, who wrote an accompanying commentary in Nature.

Just so you have some context, I am about 50-70 lbs over weight, 30 yr old male also I am a shift worker and am in a high emf environment. I know I know that is hardly optimal... but that is life for a little while until I get that country home on the range. Was over weight as a child, in late teens I evened out a bit. I had a year of heavy metal contact on my hands from oil paints in college.

Here is what I see:

High LDH, warburg effect? Had a tickle in my chest a week ago. Lets hope it was indigestion.
Hydration looks good but low D probably means I have low mag too.
Potassium is solid
Reverse T3 high means cytokines up somewhere in the body, CRP is down so is IL-6 high?
Cortisol is likely high due to my environment.
Hypothyroid with no signs of hashi's yet.
SHBG is high, could this be from insulin?
Testosterone, DHEA Pregnenlone need to be supplemented

Lets make some lemonade out of these lemons.

I eat off and on epi paleo, and keto. Do CT occasionally and supplement with some mitochondrial RX supps. And drink RO water strictly.

What do you all see?

### persistence — 2014-05-08T22:49:22+0300

>
> Your brain and peripheral system are not yoked........in other words your peripheral tank has adjusted before your brain........which tells me your issue might be a lack of iodine via the choroid plexus in the CSF. Cold around head or neck or breathing in colder air might help this.

Would dietary iodine supplementation address this? What would be a reasonable range of doses to experiment around? I would be using J. Crow's Lugol's Solution 2%, but I could use another if you prefer it. I have always put iodine off because of my food intolerance issues, and I figured iodine might amplify immune response \*if\* I have a leaky gut (not diagnosed yet). But I think I should test using iodine now. In terms of dosing, is there any reason to take this more than once a day or is once in morning sufficient?

What you said about my brain and body not being in synch strikes a deep chord with me. I think there is some kind of disconnect there. At the same time, going low carb did put my body into some kind of real distress, and even in ketosis with high ketone levels my body never seems to really adapt. It's like my brain is not able to reset correctly in that new metabolic environment.

As one example of that, the other day I did a very moderate workout (30 minutes walk / light jog and later that day 30 minutes lifting four sets in gym). That night I lost four pounds of body weight (calibrated against two different scales). I never went to the bathroom, so that body weight must be just water loss through my breathing. That's two liters of water being lost in less than nine hours, just by breathing. This is reinforced by my having a sense of thirst, having somewhat labored and deep breathing, etc. Yet I go to the doctor with those symptoms and they tell me my bun/creatine levels say I am not dehydrated. As I increase the level of carbs in diet, these symptoms lessen. At this point I am going to start increasing carb levels just to establish how the body responds to that and how much resolution I can get on symptoms.

If I go to neurologist about this, I guess he is just going to look at me in a funny way and not really be able to address the issue?

### persistence — 2014-05-09T23:47:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-to-creatinine-ratio.10225/post-126100>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=125839)
>
> Your brain and peripheral system are not yoked........in other words your peripheral tank has adjusted before your brain........which tells me your issue might be a lack of iodine via the choroid plexus in the CSF. Cold around head or neck or breathing in colder air might help this.

I have done some brief experiments with iodine and - wow - I think you are onto something. It appears to have some very calming and neuroprotective effect for me.

Since about the age of 18, I have had food intolerances that have neurological side effects. I normally feel this as either a brain fog or something outright toxic/irritating to my brain that makes it impossible to sit down and focus. I literally sometimes want to stick my hand into my brain and rip out the toxins.

The more recent problem with low carb diet has manifested with additional symptoms, like the constant fatigue, dehydration, deep breathing, rapid water weight loss, etc. Since those things are apparently not being explained by my electrolyte panel or my BUN/Creatine ratios, now you have me wondering if this is really some kind of neurological condition around CSF and the blood brain barrier. Alternately, does the Iodine suppress some kind of virus or bacteria, and what I experience is just temporary stopping of toxins produced by the bacteria...?

Both the long-term food-related neurological toxicity and the more recent symptoms of dehydration, deep breathing, rapid water loss appear to almost immediately calm down when I take a few drops of Lugol's 2% Solution. But this calming effect only appears to last about eight hours, then the symptoms come back. So far no allergic or bad reactions to iodine.... I could drop the dose to one drop and take three times a day I guess, to spread the effect out over the day.

Based on this, maybe I need to see a neurologist?

### nonchalant — 2014-05-13T19:26:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-to-creatinine-ratio.10225/post-126700>

Persistence, have you considered testing your iodine level?

### persistence — 2014-05-13T22:13:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-to-creatinine-ratio.10225/post-126728>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=126700)
>
> Persistence, have you considered testing your iodine level?

I went to my last major panel, and interesting there is no test for iodine there. Is it okay to just test serum iodine, or is there a special kind of test I need to order?

I am supplementing iodine now, so if I get this test how many day(s) do I need to stop the iodine before I run the test?

### nonchalant — 2014-05-13T22:24:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-to-creatinine-ratio.10225/post-126729>

I have taken a couple of mailorder urine iodine tests from Hakala Labs. IIRC, you need to stop supplementing 2 days before the test.

As far as I know, no PCP ever orders a test for iodine.

### persistence — 2014-05-14T01:42:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-to-creatinine-ratio.10225/post-126779>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=126729)
>
> I have taken a couple of mailorder urine iodine tests from Hakala Labs. IIRC, you need to stop supplementing 2 days before the test.
>
> As far as I know, no PCP ever orders a test for iodine.

So I did a little research and found one of the doctors in US - Dr Flechas - who builds an entire practice around iodine:

[http://cypress.he.net/~bigmacnc/drflechas/iodine.htm](http://cypress.he.net/~bigmacnc/drflechas/iodine.htm)

I think the other is Dr Brownstein, who has some great videos and books on why to not take certain types of prescription drugs.

Dr Flechas has a 24 hour urine collection for iodine that looks like a very good test. They get you off dietary iodine for 24 hours, then they challenge you with a single 50 mg iodine dose. They follow that with a 24 hour urine collection to see what part of that your body excretes. I suppose their theory is that if the body absorbs most of that 50 mg challenge you must be deficient. And I'm sure that must be a controversial premise by itself.

I'll also look at doing a one time iodine measurement when I am not supplementing.

### nicld — 2014-05-14T03:16:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-to-creatinine-ratio.10225/post-126799>

ZRT labs has a urine iodine test as long as you live in a state that you can get it.

### nonchalant — 2014-05-14T05:10:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-to-creatinine-ratio.10225/post-126819>

@[persistence](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/10062/), Dr Flechas' test sounds good. Just be sure you can tolerate that much iodine.
There is also a spot test, where you don't take any iodine, and it measures the iodine excreted in a single sample.

### persistence — 2014-05-14T06:12:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-to-creatinine-ratio.10225/post-126826>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=126819)
>
> @[persistence](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/10062/), Dr Flechas' test sounds good. Just be sure you can tolerate that much iodine.
> There is also a spot test, where you don't take any iodine, and it measures the iodine excreted in a single sample.

I am taking currently about nine drops a day of Lugol's 2% solution. If math is right, that is about 9 \* 2.5 = 22.5 mg / day. My body seems to love that dose, so far. So based on that result I guess I at least ruled out allergic reaction or some kind of really horrible bromine detox reaction?

I hate having to work with something that is so far outside of what the standard of care considers to be proper supplementation.

### nonchalant — 2014-05-14T12:55:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-to-creatinine-ratio.10225/post-126854>

I got my lovely bromoderma with only one drop of 2% Lugols, so you might be just fine, Persistence. I was ... seriously deficient in iodine. And I had a serious surplus of bromine, evidently.

### persistence — 2014-05-15T00:46:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-to-creatinine-ratio.10225/post-126976>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=126854)

### nonchalant — 2014-05-15T02:30:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-to-creatinine-ratio.10225/post-127001>

I was taking about 2 drops of 5% daily until recently, Now I take it only when I eat seafood, and only if I remember.

### thomasdoud@gmail.com — 2014-06-12T22:28:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-to-creatinine-ratio.10225/post-130676>

any recommendation on how often to check BUN/CR?

### persistence — 2014-06-12T22:40:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-to-creatinine-ratio.10225/post-130677>

> [thomasdoud@gmail.com said:](/goto/post?id=130676)
>
> any recommendation on how often to check BUN/CR?

It's a standard part of any basic metabolic panel. I think Labcorp has it on the CHEM8 Basic Metabolic Panel. You get BUN and Creatinine values and calculate the ratio yourself.

Assuming you have bad values and are actively trying to treat this by some therapy, maybe check once a month?

### Josh — 2014-03-20T06:37:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dr-patel-and-dr-kruse.10230/post-116613>

Towards calrification of the Gerson confusion:

1.  Doctor Gerson was a brilliant and iconoclastic doctor along the lines of Dr. Kruse.
2.  He fought the good fight until the end and may have been poisoned for his efforts.
3.  To understand the protocol, one must actually read his books, including his biography by his grandson.
4.  The "protocol" is actually quite similar conceptually and in implementation to much of what we are doing with the notable exception of a higher carb vegetarian diet.
5.  Understand that Dr. Gerson never did a coffee enema himself. He ate raw beef once per week and fish twice per week. He lived to 79 when he was mysteriously poisoned with arsenic. Simultaneously, his finished book was stolen from his desk. He recovered enough to rewrite the book and die a year later.
6.  The famous coffee enemas were an accident of WWI. The Germans had no analgesics at the end of the war. The only pain relief after surgery was from enemas. There was also a water shortage, so the nurses did not want to waste the leftover staff coffee. They put it in the enemas and noticed that it improved the analgesic effect. I have confirmed this in an acute episode of back pain. It is more work, but the end result is better than a few days on opiates and NSAIDS. It appears that there are references to coffee being used in this way much earlier in history. I am not really interested in that. Ayurveda and Unani (ancient Greek Medicine still taught and practiced in India) use enemas with herbs and oils extensively. Our distaste for the other end of the digestive tract does not diminish its effectiveness as a delivery system for a variety of compounds. There is a huge venous system going from the colon to the liver- it does not pass go or the stomach/small intestine and delivers stuff quickly in quantity to the liver....seems helpful with small children and the elderly from experience....
7.  Gerson decided to try them when he started treating cancer which happened after he became very successful treating TB with juices and nutrients. Someone said could we try this on my cancer? The rest became history.
8.  Much of "Functional Medicine" can be traced to Gerson and others of his generation.
9.  The Gerson therapy and its evolution since his death with doctors Kelly and Gozalez is more than juices and enemas. All of the pieces have some efficacy and the synergy is greater than the sum of the parts.
10.  Reading Gerson's " 50 Cases" , his daughter's "The Gerson Miracle"and his biography gave me clues into the clinical choices made and refinements routinely applied to the full protocol. Listening to Dr. Gonzalez talking with Dr. Mercola offers further insights into the evolution of the concepts and therapeutics. At that time, Dr. Gonzalez said he did one every morning to remediate the effects of living and working in NYC. He said he used the time to read journal articles. I have learned most of the Functional Medicine I know under similar circumstances....
11.  Consider Gerson's first challenge was being a Jew in Nazi Germany. He was the only Jew not removed from a train going to Vienna from Berlin. He was on the way to a conference and the soldiers asked him where he was going, but never asked if he was Jewish. It was early and the other Jews answered "yes". He moved through Europe to the UK and then to the US just before the start of the war.
12.  Gerson did use extensive lab testing so there is data to satisfy the skeptics...
13.  He identified low intracellular Potassium as the cause of the loss of metabolic function and the cause of disease. He may have understood Ling, but I do not know.

14.  I will say he had a pair.....

I have done a few thousand coffee enemas and they were mostly helpful based on my criteria. Of course in the absence of extensive lab testing, cynics could say something to the effect of "what, you are still breathing, you call that a result?" I have used them with many clients with good effect. Whether there are better and or different ways to skin the cat, I will not get into that. I will say, it is worth knowing whether one has sulfur and methylation issues as it makes demands on these systems. It would be helpful if our great leader would elaborate a little conceptually as to his reservations and concerns? I do not doubt that most of the claims are misunderstandings, yet I feel there is real efficacy in the method when used appropriately. Especially if the system is sub-optimal.

[http://doctordonato.com/wp-content/uploads/pdfs/Biological-Basis-Gerson-Therapy.pdf](http://doctordonato.com/wp-content/uploads/pdfs/Biological-Basis-Gerson-Therapy.pdf)

### caroline — 2014-03-20T07:21:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dr-patel-and-dr-kruse.10230/post-116616>

Thanks Josh..........

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dr-patel-and-dr-kruse.10230/post-116684>

### Tanya — 2014-03-20T18:51:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dr-patel-and-dr-kruse.10230/post-116731>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=116613)
>
> Towards calrification of the Gerson confusion:
>
>
> 1.  Doctor Gerson was a brilliant and iconoclastic doctor along the lines of Dr. Kruse.
> 2.  He fought the good fight until the end and may have been poisoned for his efforts.
> 3.  To understand the protocol, one must actually read his books, including his biography by his grandson.

Josh, we are so lucky to have you here - my hat’s off to you for everything that you post on this forum.

What I have to say about the Gerson therapy is not meant to be any kind of criticism at all. I just want to share my experience with it. There are many devoted followers of that protocol and stories after stories of people whose lives were saved by it.

That was the reason why I also tried it at one time in my struggle to get better. Unfortunately, it did not happen for me and I was actually getting worse - having a huge difficulty breathing and unbearable headaches among other things. In addition to the initial dx of CFS and FM, I got a few more added to it....

Even then, I was still committed to the protocol, and the only reason that I stopped it was because my test showed that I had a significant amount of arsenic in me, there was none in the water I was drinking, but it was known that apricot kernels that I was consuming as part of the diet would contain. I had another test done and sure enough was shocked to find out that there was cyanide in me as well, also thanks to apricot kernels. I am glad that it was caught in time..

### Josh — 2014-03-20T19:50:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dr-patel-and-dr-kruse.10230/post-116735>

As I mentioned, to really "understand" the protocol, one needs to look at its evolution under Gerson and its continuing evolution since then. AN integral part of the "Gerson Protocol" was Dr. Gerson himself , the context and his clinical skills....As we have reviewed many times, there is still some "art" left in "medicine"......What is being promoted now as "Gerson" may be true to some of the principals, but more typically is some version of Functional Medicine packaged as "Gerson" which I consider a disservice to all involved. 50 years after Dr. Kruse's death, does he want people to be practicing the "Kruse Protocol"? Will it bear any resemblance to what we are doing and be relevant to the context?

### Hope — 2014-03-21T01:09:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dr-patel-and-dr-kruse.10230/post-116814>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=116613)
>
> Towards calrification of the Gerson confusion:
>
>
> 1.  Doctor Gerson was a brilliant and iconoclastic doctor along the lines of Dr. Kruse.
> 2.  He fought the good fight until the end and may have been poisoned for his efforts.
> 3.  To understand the protocol, one must actually read his books, including his biography by his grandson.
> 4.  The "protocol" is actually quite similar conceptually and in implementation to much of what we are doing with the notable exception of a higher carb vegetarian diet.
> 5.  Understand that Dr. Gerson never did a coffee enema himself. He ate raw beef once per week and fish twice per week. He lived to 79 when he was mysteriously poisoned with arsenic. Simultaneously, his finished book was stolen from his desk. He recovered enough to rewrite the book and die a year later.
> 6.  The famous coffee enemas were an accident of WWI. The Germans had no analgesics at the end of the war. The only pain relief after surgery was from enemas. There was also a water shortage, so the nurses did not want to waste the leftover staff coffee. They put it in the enemas and noticed that it improved the analgesic effect. I have confirmed this in an acute episode of back pain. It is more work, but the end result is better than a few days on opiates and NSAIDS. It appears that there are references to coffee being used in this way much earlier in history. I am not really interested in that. Ayurveda and Unani (ancient Greek Medicine still taught and practiced in India) use enemas with herbs and oils extensively. Our distaste for the other end of the digestive tract does not diminish its effectiveness as a delivery system for a variety of compounds. There is a huge venous system going from the colon to the liver- it does not pass go or the stomach/small intestine and delivers stuff quickly in quantity to the liver....seems helpful with small children and the elderly from experience....
> 7.  Gerson decided to try them when he started treating cancer which happened after he became very successful treating TB with juices and nutrients. Someone said could we try this on
>
>
> [http://doctordonato.com/wp-content/uploads/pdfs/Biological-Basis-Gerson-Therapy.pdf](http://doctordonato.com/wp-content/uploads/pdfs/Biological-Basis-Gerson-Therapy.pdf)

Thanks Josh, indeed, for sharing this. I know Dr Nick Gonzalez, a prominent cancer fighter, recommends enemas as well, in addition to many other alternative practitioners or MDs,...

I have no doubt it works for some people....and for some it doesn't bring great results. Just like everything else in life. I also wonder why people often look to one magic bullet to be so important....when often many things are part of a healing process.

### Josh — 2014-03-21T02:27:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dr-patel-and-dr-kruse.10230/post-116837>

As I mentioned earlier, "Gerson Therapy" is being marketed which may or may not be close to what Dr. Gerson actually practiced in the first half of the 20th century. One huge difference is that Gerson used 16oz of fresh raw calf's liver juice daily in his regimen and last time I checked there are no safe sources. It is hard to replace the good raw liver part and it is not readily availble anymore in a safe form. Oysters would probably be a better substitute. Of course there was no EMF then and we have no idea of the nutrient density and relative toxicity of the vegetables they used. At its essence it is a "hyperalimentation" program, sound familiar...eat a lot of electrons/nutrient dense food and drink a lot of fresh vegetable juice-best source of highly structured water....low or no salt...lots of K+ in juices and they added a K+ salt mix to everyone's regimen as well. Lots of Iodine and Thyroid Hormone....beginning to sound familiar...time outside in the sun a la the old German/Swiss TB sanitarium model...read "The Magic Mountain" by Thomas Mann for an intimate portrait of that...our boy Hans and the whistling women....

So I have no idea what your cousin is doing and whether it bears any resemblance to what was done in the 50's. The principles are all the same so if you have access to the details, it should be pretty easy to assess the strengths and weaknesses. I wish him the best.

No water drinking allowed
No animal protein, used potatoes as a main source of protein
No seafood
No fats at all

### Josh — 2014-03-21T19:09:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dr-patel-and-dr-kruse.10230/post-116966>

> [Tanya said:](/goto/post?id=116959)
>
> I was just hoping for a quick answer from you regarding the 5 items I listed, because I doubt the possibility of reading this book in my near future. It's ok, though, Josh - that protocol is behind me now, anyway and I am hard at work reading all Jack's writings, including today's one.....

Drinking 13 juices a day: _if they are the right juices from quality produce, okay. Notice they stick mostly to high water content greens, not high sugar content stuff like carrots. Dilute green juices are basically highly structured water if they are uncontaminated._
No water drinking allowed: _this was probably because they did not understand the different types of water and Ling's work._
No animal protein, used potatoes as a main source of protein. _Serious mistake in my experience and understanding. Notice the Liver Juice in the original protocol after Gerson's experiences. He ate meat himself. Practically, if someone cannot digest food for all of the reasons that happens, concentrated animal protein can be rough, especially during rapid detoxification._
No seafood: _he ate seafood and the reasons for limiting animal protein is a misunderstanding of the role of electrons and the density of electrons in food and physiology. Practically, if someone cannot digest food for all of the reasons that happens, concentrated animal protein can be rough, especially during rapid detoxification._
No fats at all: _I thought they used a lot of Flax Oil for the Omega 3's, ultimately a misunderstanding based on Dr. Kruse's explanation of fats._

Dr. Gonzalez covers many of these and discusses how he and Dr. Kelly sought to resolve many of these issues and make the therapy more of a conceptual exercise with improved individuation for N=1. Kelly healed himself from pancreatic cancer with his early efforts.

There was no liver juice in my list, but if it would have been, I am not so sure of my compliance with it...lol but maybe if I did - my hemoglobin and hematocrit would not have been so low... No regrets here. I was just trying to see if what I was given was real McCoy or not. Thanks again.

### Hope — 2014-03-22T00:31:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dr-patel-and-dr-kruse.10230/post-117027>

being outside in the cold air even during the night has been a long, long therapy in Europe and Germany......hospitals in certain places, like in mountains or away from cities even in WW one and two recommended this....especially for people with TB and other issues. So amazing. Of course this is before the big bucks Pharma came along...which has been really bombing the world since around WW two.

### Jasmin — 2014-03-19T10:46:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-hypothyroidism.10233/post-116365>

Hi
Have any of you in this fantasic forum been able to heal hypothyroidism?
I have hypothyroidism and take 4 grain of Erfa Thyroid each day and would like to lower the dose - wish that some day I will be able skip it completely.
I tried with Lugol's Iodine but my hypothyroidism got worse - perhaps I upped the dose too fast? reached 7 dropps. I lowered the dose to 1 drop and got better. I have now been more cautious and have reached 4 drops. The reason I tarted Lugol's was that I experienced that my hypothyroidism got better eating seaweed every day so I thought that if taking Lugol's I would be sure to get an exactly amount of iodine every day. Perhaps my body need all the good things in the seaweed to be able to use the iodine.

I also have osteoporosis and have been told that the thyroid medication is bad for my bones - anyone know if that is right.

Several people has told me to eat many vegetables and only a small amount of meat, if any at all, becayse of the acid/alcaline thing. Personally I dont belive it but have a hard time convincing them an sometimes feel confused and uncertain.

### nonchalant — 2014-03-19T17:40:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-hypothyroidism.10233/post-116432>

I haven't cured my hypothyroidism yet, but it sounds like you are doing well so far. Seaweed is great, because it is a complete package with other nutrients. Seafood is another great source that I hope you eat plenty of. I also take a small amount of Lugols also.

Dr. Kruse has an osteoporosis blog you might want to read. Also the acid/alkaline thing - Dr. K has mentioned that somewhere also. They are not correct. Besides, I doubt many of those veggie eaters stay away from bread, which is very acid.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-24T19:52:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-hypothyroidism.10233/post-117640>

Iodine is critical for oxidation protection of [PUFA](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PUFA)’s utilized by the brain, such as [DHA](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Docosahexaenoic_acid). It also appears that iodine is a lot better protector of all [lipids](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lipids) in all cells and not just the brain. With iodine deficiency, the body loses the ability to handle [ROS](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reactive_oxygen_species) (oxidation) optimally, and other organ systems have to offset those losses to protect the cell from more oxidation. When this happens the cell is placed in a chronic survival mode, with its functionality severely compromised. A critical problem here is that Iodine is a constituent of the most powerful inhibitors of [lipid peroxidation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lipid_peroxidation) in humans, T2 and T1 thyroid hormones. These anti-oxidants are so powerful that they exceed the efficacy of vitamin E, glutathione, and vitamin C in humans. In fact, it appears the real reason vitamin C may have lost its role in humans, as a powerful antioxidant protector in cells, is because it is not strong enough to protect [AA](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arachidonic_acid) and DHA in human brain tissue nor the thyroid, particularly at the synapse of neurons.

With iodine deficiency, the body loses the ability to handle [ROS](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reactive_oxygen_species) (oxidation) optimally, and other organ systems have to offset those losses to protect the cell from more oxidation. When this happens the cell is placed in a chronic survival mode, with its functionality severely compromised. A critical problem here is that Iodine is a constituent of the most powerful inhibitors of [lipid peroxidation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lipid_peroxidation) in humans, T2 and T1 thyroid hormones. These anti-oxidants are so powerful that they exceed the efficacy of vitamin E, glutathione, and vitamin C in humans. In fact, it appears the real reason vitamin C may have lost its role in humans, as a powerful antioxidant protector in cells, is because it is not strong enough to protect [AA](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arachidonic_acid) and DHA in human brain tissue nor the thyroid, particularly at the synapse of neurons.

There would seem to be an argument here in favour of the use of desiccated thyroid, which contains T2 and T1, over the use of synthetic T4 with or without T3.

# Thread: New here - overwhelmed, but determined!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-here-overwhelmed-but-determined.10238/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 42

### GGinPG — 2014-03-19T16:35:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-here-overwhelmed-but-determined.10238/post-116409>

Hello everyone! Just discovered Dr. Jack Kruse last evening and spent several hours reading through blog posts and comments. I only got through a fraction of the information on this site, but I believe I have already found the answers to questions I didn't even know I had.

I am a 43-year old woman with Hashimoto's, Celiac and a _lifetime_ of horrible sleep. (I can count on one hand the number of nights of decent sleep I have had in at least the past twenty-five years. _Literal_ statement.) I have been "Paleo" since my Hashi's/Celiac diagnoses about 7 years ago, with some missteps along the way. I do reasearch into AI issues and the Paleo lifestyle virtually daily; I can't believe I haven't come across Dr. Kruse until now. I am beginning to understand that I need to do more than simply "fix" myself (which I certainly haven't managed to do yet), I need to start over from scratch.

I'm currently taking Natural Dessicated Thyroid plus (only very recently) a tiny amount of nature-identical T3, numerous supplements including Iodorol and iron, which have allowed me to become a functioning human - something that eluded me for several years - but only at the barest minimum level. This isn't _living_, however, at least not to me, and so now here I am.

Since you have been avoiding artificial oils in your diet, your skin will be in much better condition to use plenty of sunlight, and also for starting CT (cold thermogenesis). Do you also avoid fluoridated water? Hydration with good water is also of supreme importance.

I think once you start on the Leptin Reset your sleep will improve. Sounds like you deserve some!

I'll give you an example of someone in my practice.......total skeptic. I told the lady if you give me 12 months I can stop you from having a cervical fusion on your neck. That got her thinking. She had Hashi's. After going over all her stuff I realized casein, but specifically tyrosine was what was setting off her immune system. Her history revealed a constant gain of food intolerances to new substances that alternative practitioner's keep Rx an elimination diet. This is much like many paleo practitioner's tell people with Hashi's to do with iodine and seafood. I understand how the immune system is activated by the AI induction hypothesis and how it ties to water, K+ Iodine and seafood. When you are missing electrons or missing the potential energy bound to your protons in water what happens chronically? Your immune system seeks out new food substances to make antibodies to, then attacks the affected tissue. The small minded idea is to avoid the foods........the right play is to restore the redox potential by improving things that improve electrons and protons. I talk about this in Brain Gut 1.

Thes pathways use more energy from your cells than any other. So when you mark proteins for replacement with AB's what is the real problem? The AB's or the protein turnover? > A famous Soviet academic, Petya, is dying, and his friend and colleague, Vasya, comes to his
> bedside to pay his last respects to this great old man. He finds Petya Lying there with closed eyes
> and a sad look on his face.
> Slowly he opens his eyes, looks at his friend, and then says, ”Vasya, look at all those books on that
> wall over there – just look at them.”
> ”Yes, Petya,” replies his friend. ”Those are your most famous works. Everyone knows what an
> important and...”
> ”Yes, yes, yes!” interrupts Petya. ”But look over there at that wall – you see all those books?”
> ”Yes, my dear friend. Those are the translations of your works into all the languages of the...”
> ”Yes, but that is not the point. Look over there – do you see those books?”
> ”But of course. Those are the works of your students and followers. We all admire...”
> ”No, but you don’t understand, Vasya!” exclaims Petya. ”Look, do you remember when we were
> students, and we were sent off to work on the collective farm?”
> ”Of course I remember, my friend. How could I forget?”
> ”And do you remember, Vasya, walking down that country road one hot summer’s day?”
> ”Yes, Petya, yes I do.”
> ”And do you remember, we passed that cornfield, and there, sitting on the top of one of the
> haystacks, was that pretty young Masha?”
> ”Yes, yes, Petya, I do! You liked her, I remember!”
> ”Yes,” says Petya, sinking back onto his pillow with a blissful smile on his face. ”You know what I did,
> Vasya? I climbed up on top of the haystack and we were really starting to have a bloody good time.
> But you know, my friend, those bloody haystacks – just when you are really getting somewhere,
> whoosh!... they give way under you. I did everything I could, but every time that bloody haystack...
> So you see, I have just been thinking, Vasya” – Petya looked round the room – ”how it would have
> been if we had all these bloody books underneath us!”
> A dying old academic... at the last moment, what he is thinking about?

### Josh — 2014-03-21T00:23:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-here-overwhelmed-but-determined.10238/post-116808>

I looked up Tyrosine in Dr. Yasko's work and found these to orient myself, thought I would add them for perspective...

> The added ammonia that is generated due to the enhanced breakdown of methylation
> cycle intermediates will also burden the adjoining urea cycle, thereby
> depleting a key intermediate called BH4, which plays a critical role in regulating
> neurotransmitters and therefore mood. BH4 is needed for serotonin, dopamine,
> conversion of phenylalanine to tyrosine and language-related function. The
> A1298C mutation in the MTHFR gene may also impact levels of BH4.

> The amino acid tyrosine is a precursor for both thyroid hormones and dopamine
> synthesis. An enzyme activated during chronic bacterial infection can deplete
> tyrosine levels, which is why there is an association between low thyroid function
> and chronic sinus infections. Iodine also affects thyroid function which is why I
> recommend monitoring iodine and lithium on essential mineral tests. You can
> supplement if values are low. One option is an iodine-containing product called
> Iodoral, which both supports the thyroid and may be helpful with respect to
> mercury excretion. If you supplement with Iodoral, make sure to watch lithium
> levels.
> Thyroid/tyrosine (Metabolic Advantage) supplements can also help. The use of
> the herb guggul (Ayur-Guggulipid) may help to balance T3 and T4, two thyroid
> hormones. After four weeks of nutritional support, run a follow up thyroid test
> to confirm that thyroid hormone levels are in the normal range. An essential
> mineral test (or a topical iodine test) can confirm when you’ve achieved healthy
> iodine. The thyroid hormone iodination cycle is tied to glucose 6 phosphate dehydrogenase
> (G6PDH) levels which are also affected by sulfur groups. Addressing
> thyroid issues helps to clear chronic bacterial/sinus infections.

Autism: Pathways to Recovery by Dr. Amy Yasko

### Josh — 2014-03-21T03:15:06+0200

A Joke for Caroline in case she did not see it in the other place I posted it...

A famous Soviet academic, Petya, is dying, and his friend and colleague, Vasya, comes to his
bedside to pay his last respects to this great old man. He finds Petya Lying there with closed eyes
and a sad look on his face.
Slowly he opens his eyes, looks at his friend, and then says, ”Vasya, look at all those books on that
wall over there – just look at them.”
”Yes, Petya,” replies his friend. ”Those are your most famous works. Everyone knows what an
important and...”
”Yes, yes, yes!” interrupts Petya. ”But look over there at that wall – you see all those books?”
”Yes, my dear friend. Those are the translations of your works into all the languages of the...”
”Yes, but that is not the point. Look over there – do you see those books?”
”But of course. Those are the works of your students and followers. We all admire...”
”No, but you don’t understand, Vasya!” exclaims Petya. ”Look, do you remember when we were
students, and we were sent off to work on the collective farm?”
”Of course I remember, my friend. How could I forget?”
”And do you remember, Vasya, walking down that country road one hot summer’s day?”
”Yes, Petya, yes I do.”
”And do you remember, we passed that cornfield, and there, sitting on the top of one of the
haystacks, was that pretty young Masha?”
”Yes, yes, Petya, I do! You liked her, I remember!”
”Yes,” says Petya, sinking back onto his pillow with a blissful smile on his face. ”You know what I did,
Vasya? I climbed up on top of the haystack and we were really starting to have a bloody good time.
But you know, my friend, those bloody haystacks – just when you are really getting somewhere,
whoosh!... they give way under you. I did everything I could, but every time that bloody haystack...
So you see, I have just been thinking, Vasya” – Petya looked round the room –
_**”how it would have been if we had all these bloody books underneath us!”**_

### Steph Corby — 2014-03-19T17:41:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-caught-a-cold-do-i-adapt-anything-or-keep-going-as-is-in-lr.10240/post-116433>

Well, after avoiding it for 2 weeks, I finally caught the cold that my kids and co-workers were passing around. Its not bad, just annoying. Im a little tired and a bit of post-nasal drip.

I started my usual regimen (I usually only get sick once a year, this time of year during the weather change - lasts 1-3 days) of colloidal silver, Cold Care Tea with chunks of ginger, soup, and lots of water + lots of SLEEP.

But usually I eat a lot (i.e. often) when I have a cold to keep up my energy. I felt MUCH better after BAB and AM vitamins (6:30am), but now- at 11:30 am - Im starting to drag again. I caved and made myself a BPC. Wondering if I should do anything else???

Should I do anything differently? Im still early in LR so I generally only IF on weekends when I get up later.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-caught-a-cold-do-i-adapt-anything-or-keep-going-as-is-in-lr.10240/post-116459>

# Thread: Is there a dark side of withdrawal from modernism?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-a-dark-side-of-withdrawal-from-modernism.10242/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 66
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### NeilBB — 2014-03-19T18:52:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-a-dark-side-of-withdrawal-from-modernism.10242/post-116454>

Let me preface this by saying that I am as big an epi-paleo guy as possible and that this is not intended as any kind of criticism, but as an observation that I’d like to toss out there…

One thing that has “disappointed me” about my aggressive paleo lifestyle over the past several years is the fact that small incursions (like a carb-fest or a couple nights of lost sleep) seem to be more disruptive to my system now that I am really healthy than they would have been, years ago when I was a train wreck. Its often worse when I’ve been "particularly good" for a long time too! How frustrating. I had chalked this effect up to aging and prior abuses & wear and tear, but I’m not sure it’s really that simple anymore. Age-matched colleagues seem to be more resilient sometimes in these areas, although they seem to trade their resilience for chronic health problems in other ways, of course.

I guess I always hoped that strict adherence to ancestral health principles would make me so powerfully healthy as to be sort of invulnerable to brief exposures to the bad modern stuff. But its not the case that I have observed with myself or many others.

And Jack, your experience with the facial/abducens palsy with one cell phone call is a perfect example of this IMO. I mean, we all know people that stick the phone on their head all day, every day and don’t have acute reactions like that, and you happen to live in a place with NO cell phone reception, so WTF?

Here is my theory and my concern. What if with relatively strict avoidance of modern day stimuli, we are creating some sort of “reverse-hormetic” effect? What if we are weakening our defenses to acute modern stimuli because we have “tricked” our genes into thinking we live in pre-industrial civilization. So then when we suddenly get blasted with a modern device like that, we are less epigenetically-and molecularly-on-guard to respond than even a conventional human?

Analogy is this. A country that downgrades its milita to spend more capital on infrastructure may be a better more productive society, yet more vulnerable to sneak attacks by external enemies. In a similar way, our paleo and epi-paleo epigenetic manipulations that allow us to live more “ancestrally correct” and free us from “chronic modern disease” could theoretically leave us more vulnerable to acute doses of modern technology, for example. Maybe, we have "turned off" the genes and systems that might mitigate these effects somewhat, and so we sort-of become more-and-more dependent on the _absence of the technology_ to maintain our newfound health. After all we really don’t and never will live in pre-industrial civilization.

I guess my point and question is really this. I’m not sure that trying to achieve the “absolute minimum” of modernism is always the best road to optimal today. Perhaps some small controlled low-dose exposure to specific technology/carbs/toxins/etc can even be somewhat protective in this modern environment. Much in the same way that a vaccination would be, by priming defense mechanisms to remain to some extent "on guard" at all times. Perhaps it is possible to live “too much in a bubble” to the point that any excursion can be made more dangerous? Maybe it’s possible to have too much “yin” and not enough “yang” even when it comes to modernism. After all, we are in uncharted territory for humans these days and we are the most adaptable of all species. Just a thought.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-19T19:06:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-a-dark-side-of-withdrawal-from-modernism.10242/post-116457>

I think it is more that my CNS is now more responsive to my environment and not less.......that is the risk.......but here is the key point. If your redox is good you can handle more stress.......if not you're in deep shit either way. So which way is better? I think you know my answer........but I will stop the more extreme biohacks..........this one unnerved me quite a bit.

### NeilBB — 2014-03-19T19:30:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-a-dark-side-of-withdrawal-from-modernism.10242/post-116463>

### Josh — 2014-03-19T21:45:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-a-dark-side-of-withdrawal-from-modernism.10242/post-116504>

Extreme is extreme for a system that relies on balance. However, it is not always clear what is "extreme" if we do not know the conceptual framework for how it "actually works" and by virtue of this, all of the relevant factors. I can think of a dozen additional factors that may have contributed to the Bell's Palsy that may not be considered or even available to be measured with any ease or reliability. This is where there is still some "art" in medicine and science....The mystery of why, where and when a system as complex as the human body fails is not a casual exercise for anyone. Fixing the International Space Station in orbit is simpler....

My two cents...

### prAna303 — 2014-03-19T21:49:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-a-dark-side-of-withdrawal-from-modernism.10242/post-116505>

> Here is my theory and my concern. What if with relatively strict avoidance of modern day stimuli, we are creating some sort of “reverse-hormetic” effect? What if we are weakening our defenses to acute modern stimuli because we have “tricked” our genes into thinking we live in pre-industrial civilization. So then when we suddenly get blasted with a modern device like that, we are less epigenetically-and molecularly-on-guard to respond than even a conventional human?

I think, if i examine myself that it is like a warning signal that starts to ring...

Today, with a dear friend of mine, we are the only ones we know of (in our own little world) that has thrown the phone away, because we feel something weird is going on. The one thing is that we have been deep, way deeper than most into technology and we both know what it can do to living things.

I strongly belive that when you reach some critical point you start to see how weird the usage has become. You start to sense something is bad, not only physical but also mental and esthetical. We become more observant about things that is NOT natural. When we feel like this we get reactions inside us that starts some very old system to do its work.

I have this discussion quite often with people and they call me anti tech guy, but would i like to adapt to a world where you live, lying in bed, fed by tubes, watching endless movies of cats on skateboards, listening to music that google THINKS i like?

No, way... I take all this discomfort, all these hostile looks by others, because i still want to be HUMAN.

### NeilBB — 2014-03-19T21:57:15+0200

### Josh — 2014-03-19T23:15:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-a-dark-side-of-withdrawal-from-modernism.10242/post-116558>

Worm man says...I have tried almost everything here before the worms and I still had a lot more sensitivity to everything and reduced function as a result. The worms have removed about half of it and continue to do so coming up upon a year. What do the worms do? They modulate my immune system and the relationships between microorganisms in my body. They may have direct metabolic effects as well, but I have not found those in the literature yet. So it seems to come down to the old story found in the more traditional medical systems such as Oriental Medicine and Ayurveda:

Purgation/Detoxification-relatively easy, like demoing a house interior as long as the structure does not fall down

Tonification/Nourishing/Redox Stuff-putting the walls and stuff back in. Requires more skill and planning. Things must be staged better to fit together, etc..

Balancing/Modulating/Regulating-getting everything to work and to work together in a synchronized fashion. In a house, this is relatively easy conceptually and ask Martin how much works it takes by how many trades in practice. For a set of systems such as we have in the body, I would imagine it is easier to work on the Space Station in a Space suit floating on a Tether a la "Gravity"... In fact in many ways, that is what a practitioner of whatever modality is doing...

The Worms have had all of vertebrate evolution and a few years before that too, to learn and adapt for this. I have had 20 or so years of training and practice...who would you chose? I do not think the worms are smarter, they are just clearer about what is pro-survival and sustainable for them in the context of my body than I am. They have many fewer distractions and as I said they have been practicing a lot longer.

Consider that all medical practice included the worm effect in most if not all patients until about 80 years ago. The issue was always not whether you had worms, but rather whether you had too many-i.e. enough to create a clinical syndrome identifiable in the medical or survival context one lived in. I am in no way suggesting that the worms are the only factor such as this that may not be factored into the long view back.....

### Josh — 2014-03-20T00:06:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-a-dark-side-of-withdrawal-from-modernism.10242/post-116563>

Note the above is mostly based on subjective signs and symptoms. How much of what I have perceived as "improvement" will show up on the labwork is not clear. I will run some in the next month or so depending upon budget. Regardless, I am thrilled at how much better this year has gone without allergies and multiple infection episodes. I would say the two main factors are the worms and the refined understanding of the methylation pathways; the addition of the transition elements molybdenum, manganese and lithium; and finally a couple of weeks ago the L 5 MTHF. I am not as clear where my experiments with CT fit, but I am warmer than last year and my head works better along with my immune system based on observation and sensation. This does not mean that I do not have a brain tumor growing as yet undetected or a growing blockage in an artery that may explode. May bones may be detriorating daily and my thyroid may be falling apart from cyclical AI reactions...Even if I got all of the labtests recommended today, these things would probably not be apparent. Only weekly or even more frequent blood work or scans and thermography might give me clues about these. When they would become "symptomatic" at a "clincial horizon" based on someone paying attention, not clear.

I do not have the resources to track things with labtests in ways that might reveal everything I would like to know, and give me a number to validate my assertions/experiences. Looking at Functional Medicine and its reliance on labtests versus Traditonal systems with sophisticated signs and symptom maps correlated with pulse and palpation, I feel that everyone has room to learn and share. The problem is that no one can agree on the context and the science that underlies all of it. We are on the same planet, driving the same models in the same environment. At some level in some some system, there must be at least some objective verifiable and possibly repeatable data that everyone can use to track the efficacy of their system. This will then allow everyone to talk to each other with the highest good for all as the goal rather than the protection of one's territory and system ...income....ego.....family....country....culture....nation....religion...fill in the blank....

For what it is worth, I like the idea of Dr. Stewart's Sensory View System and the idea that coordinated neural function is a good marker for redox and infectious/inflammatory load. Matches all of theory we are learning here within the limit of my understanding. There are similar and often ignored s/sx's in OM correlated to syndromes and used irregularly in practice. In most OM, the brain and nervous system are actually a part of the kidneys-something to ponder. Dr. Kharrazian endorses a comprehensive neural exam as does Dr. Patel and Dr. Kruse in his book. To date in this life, I have never had one or been offered one by any doctor, even when I went off a motorcycle without a helmet....Maybe when I had a concussion from the car I wrapped around a tree at 17, but I was not fully conscious then....
