I guess if that's the worst that I've done so far, I should still be on track with the reset. ### caroline — 2014-03-21T03:42:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-minor-error-break-the-reset.10260/post-116870>

### Infinusss — 2014-04-04T05:01:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-minor-error-break-the-reset.10260/post-119703>

Starting this week, I'm not on week 4 of the reset. Hold pretty well for the most part. My only real slip-ups have been that some mornings I've been eating breakfast within an hour of waking up and not 30 min. Sometimes when I wake up too early (my internal clock is changing and sometimes I wake up really early now) I try and go back to bed, but most of the time I can't, and I get up after 20-30 minutes of trying to go back to sleep. I'm not sure when that 30 minute window starts. Is it when you wake up, or when you get up?

Also, sometimes I eat dinner late, and I try to get at least 4 hours in before I go to bed, but sometimes it's only 3 or 3.5. Does anyone think that either of those two things are setting me back in my reset?

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-04T05:16:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-minor-error-break-the-reset.10260/post-119707>

### Inger — 2014-04-04T08:15:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-minor-error-break-the-reset.10260/post-119725>

when do you wake up and when do you go to bed? I sometimes wake up early too.,.. and I just lie there and sometimes I sleep in a little and sometimes I just go up when it starts to get light, I do not stress about this.. and I eat breakfast around 6-7 AM, sometimes even later. I think if you wake up at 4 AM you should maybe not eat at 4;30 this sounds weird to me. because that is almost night still... I would wait until sun rises or it starts to get light outside (ok in winter here this does not work for sure..lol )
Same in the evening. I would not eat after 6 PM... better not after 5 PM. In the darkest winter I usually eat dinner at 3 or 4 PM.. In summer when sun is up so long and you might go to be at 11 PM at times.. when sun is still shining... I do not think it will kill you to eat at 7 PM.

Hope you see what I am trying to say here..  (you need to try to see the whole picture and then you will know what to do )

### caroline — 2014-04-04T09:13:21+0300

I tried it in the first few days and not only none of us liked it, especially me, but I did not feel that the benefits of doing that were out weighting the less positive side of it for me. I am having no problem with having it this way. It may take me, perhaps a little longer to get where I am going, but what really matters is for me to get there.... It is like playing chess - some can see 20 moves ahead, and some only 10, but they can still win....

# Thread: Cutting out grains-palpitations and agitation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cutting-out-grains-palpitations-and-agitation.10262/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### lubylouUK — 2014-03-21T11:41:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cutting-out-grains-palpitations-and-agitation.10262/post-116914>

Hello, I'm slowly starting to get into the Leptin reset. On Wednesday I cut out all grains from my diet and felt great. The next day and today I have been having palpitations and feeling jittery and anxious. I had to eat some rice last night. Has anyone else experienced this? I have adrenal fatigue but I'm getting better. My my blood sugar needs to be kept in check. It's very fragile. Any advice would be great. I seem to need carbs......

### kovita — 2014-03-21T12:22:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cutting-out-grains-palpitations-and-agitation.10262/post-116919>

I would suggest you take it slowly. As far as I understood the bigger part of success with leptin reset lies in timing. Why don't you start going just gluten free and keep rice if you tolerate it well. It takes some time for your body to get used to burn fat instead of carbs, specially if your diet was lifelong high carb. Considering ypur adrenal problems do not attamept to cut carbs from one day to another. This will put tremendous stress on your adrenalsa nd thyroid. You can eat heat,y and nutrient rich diet based in meats, fats and veggies and keeping some anount of carbs with each meal and lowering the amount slowly. as always listen to your inner docotor and go with your feelings. Our nutritional needs change, it is not a
status quo and you have to learn to understand and listen to your body. I do well on low carb, but my body does not like continuous ketosis. Each of us is unique and different and there is no universal recipe.

### sooperb — 2014-03-21T13:29:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cutting-out-grains-palpitations-and-agitation.10262/post-116926>

Well said Kovita

Do you have any specific health issues looby? Use of the word "fragile" suggests to me that you may be diabetic? Are you drinking enough water?

### Lahelada — 2014-03-21T15:46:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cutting-out-grains-palpitations-and-agitation.10262/post-116944>

I would say push through it as it sounds like detox symptoms ,what is known as carb flu on other forums. Up your protein intake slightly for the next 3 or 4 days and drink buckets of good water. The second day is not enough time to evaluate what ketosis is doing to your body. You very likely have just one foot on the ladder in that department anyway. True ketosis is a much longer process. It starts around a week in and takes a lot of time before your whole body adapts. You do not become a fatburner overnight.
From the Leptin RX blog
"
Will the Leptin Reset fix adrenal problems?

It depends on the severity of the condition, as most are tied to cortisol issues. High cortisol levels are harder to treat than low cortisol levels. If they are really bad, I treat both the same time. Most often though, with adaptogenic support and some good doctoring, you can fix most adrenal problems."

### Lahelada — 2014-03-21T16:15:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cutting-out-grains-palpitations-and-agitation.10262/post-116949>

### lubylouUK — 2014-03-22T17:16:15+0200

Kovita: I have been gluten free for a year. I have been eating pretty well-meat and veg and complex carbs and no sugar for at least 8 months. So cutting out grains was the next step. I had noticed they made me a bit jittery when I ate them on their own so I thought I'd give the leptin reset a go.
I don't fancy going into ketosis. I have lost enough weight with the adrenal exhaustion. I had high cortisol at one point (ASI) but now it's low ( I think). However I had a blood test the other day and the cortisol was in range! I'm not sure how accurate a blood test is.
Sooperb: sometimes I go through phases where I need to drink 2 litres a night I'm so thirsty. I was so sure I had a blood sugar problem/pre diabetic/hypoglycaemia. Again, my blood test the other day says glucose fasting test was fine! Though I am convinced I am becoming a diabetic when I go through this!
Lahelada: I am well on the way with fixing my adrenal problems thanks to nutritional balancing and energy healing. It was horrendous a few months ago. I'm scared about going into ketosis though as it's a major stress on the adrenals and it mucks up my sleep which is something I've only just recently got sorted thanks to cutting out fake light and getting rid of EMFs and detoxing heavy metals.

My question is-how do I fix my adrenals with the leptin reset if I have to go into ketosis? Which will kick my adrenals into gear and make me burn fat, neither of which I need at the moment?! ### Gracelind — 2014-03-22T01:07:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cpc-8-is-live-critical-for-healthcare-professionals-to-read.10264/post-117032>

The boy across the street from my childhood home had a brain tumor--I assume it was benign as he recovered. I was no longer living at home at the time so never knew the details. The house backed up to a hill on which stood a very large [high tension wire tower like this](https://www.google.com/search?q=high+tension+towers&client=firefox-a&hs=6fU&rls=org.mozilla:en-US:official&channel=sb&tbm=isch&tbo=u&source=univ&sa=X&ei=G8MsU5TfHZXioAS-84KgAw&ved=0CEsQ7Ak&biw=1291&bih=693#facrc=_&imgdii=_&imgrc=C1uomN4uwza_2M%253A%3BvnKXePkP408ySM%3Bhttp%253A%252F%252Fupload.wikimedia.org%252Fwikipedia%252Fcommons%252F9%252F97%252FPylon_ds.jpg%3Bhttp%253A%252F%252Fen.wikipedia.org%252Fwiki%252FTransmission_tower%3B1120%3B2270). The tower was maybe 150 yards away, but loomed over the backyard where the kids played. I'm sure it didn't have as many wires as in the photo. On foggy nights we could hear zapping, zinging sounds.

OMG I just remembered that the father of a family that lived directly under the lines died of brain cancer. Lots of heart disease (two early sudden deaths of young fathers) and rheumatoid arthritis in that small neighborhood. May have been other cases among the families that I didn't know of.

CPC #8 is going to take--and deserves--serious study.

> No one is studying this aspect of biology. What I am saying in thos blog is that proteins via the electronic induction control epigenetic signaling. This is not a DNA level event. DNA only makes the proteins. What organizes them. An important step in the Central Dogma of modern biology (DNA encodes RNA encodes protein-RL) is the folding of the peptide to form the protein, which can become biologically activated and contribute, as a component of the electrostatic attractor, and eventually lead to the phenotype of the organism. I don't believe that. The blog shows you the first two folds are determined by DNA. The last two folds are determined by the redox state of the cell. This is why so many proteins, like SHBG have so many varied actions. Their environment determines their folding and eventual function. This theory of mine breaks the central dogma paradigm of protein folding. This is also why all neuro degenerative diseases have protein folding abnormalties in the brain. Lewy body, beta amyloid, synaptophsin.....etc.
>
> An extreme example is the involvement of chaperone proteins, which provide an environment favoring a specific folding. Therefore, we have the possibility that a single amino acid sequence, as a peptide, dictated by a gene coding sequence, can fold into more than one protein and therefore perform more than one biological activity: THINK SHBG. This is why we still do not know its ultimate molecular formula. It is the ultimate chameleon chaperone protein for sex hormone binding.
>
> All of this leads to some physics terms. When you really understand the core of this blog..........you realize that Gilbert Ling's basic idea that electronic induction changes biochemistry........and this allows emergent physics to appear. What controls the emergent physical processes of quantum mechanics in biology? The electromagnetic forces.
>
> A quantum cell (think the blog I wrote about that issue) is a dissipative system. This means it has the role or purpose to break symmetry and create a metastable system to react to all environmental possibilities. While a cell is designed to break symmetries guess what it is filled with? WATER. Liquid water has perfect symmetry in that no matter from which direction you look at the molecules, the view is the same from a molecular standpoint. But can water lose its symmetry in nature? Yes.I f the water freezes to ice, the perfect symmetry is lost or broken and the property of rigidity emerges in its lattice. Same is true when it becomes a gas, a hydronium ion or an OH group. Symmetry is broken by a phase transition many times in biochemistry in cells, and as such all breakage of symmetry require a transfer of energy. Symmetry is also broken any time temperature rises or falls or when electrons or protons are moving in any chemical reaction. Any transfer of energy has the potential to break symmetry and therefore to give rise to emergent properties. Cancer is a state of excessive symmetry breakage for proteins that generate energy in the cytochromes. It has nothing to do with genes or DNA. What I am proposing is complete paradigm shifting. A new way of looking at everything in a text book of biology. The scientists heads will explode.

> Symmetry breakage is not how biochemists see biochemistry. It is how a physicist sees the natural world of QED unfold. So let me give you an example of how breaking symmetry leads to an emergent new chemical. We see this all the time in chemistry. If we take a mixture of the harmless and odorless gases, nitrogen and hydrogen, and heat them to a high temperature, exchange of electrons between the two molecules occurs (symmetry breaking) and ammonia is the product with the emergent properties of a noxious and pungent gas. If this reaction had never been performed, there would be no way to predict, from the physical properties of hydrogen and nitrogen, the properties of ammonia – its properties are emergent. Now think about Pollack's book. Water becomes emergent with IR. Think about the February webinar on the fourth phase of matter.......water collagen and proteins become a new phase of matter when it is electrified and protonated. BOOM

From Dr.K's Comments in Blog to CPC8

Good redox=SHBG behaves and hormones orchestrate proper handling of electrons and protons to optimize function and detoxification. Poor redox=**_SHBG binds the wrong stuff and is more sensitive to EMF. So it does things like bind up all of my testosterone instead of estrogen or just getting bound up itself in the tissues that are supposed to bind it and keep it out of the way_**.

> [complete **_quaternary structure of SHBG_** even today.](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12228253) The reason? They do not realize it changes with the electromagnetic force of the environment that the tissues are in. It also **varies with the redox potential of the tissues where it is made**.
>
> The **only thing that changes in their structures is rotational bond angles and their atomic positions** but the molecular structures printed are exactly the same. Estrogen, testosterone and SHBG do the exact same thing. When you get that it can and does happen in real biochemistry a lot more often than anyone realizes……**then I can explain to you how the sex steroid hormones do their magic in health and in cancer states**. They are not the cause of cancer, they act to help it when you understand the quantum molecular actions of how they change
>
> The blog shows you the first two folds are determined by DNA. **The last two folds are determined by the redox state of the cell.** This is why so many proteins, like SHBG have so many varied actions. Their**environment determines their folding and eventual function.** This theory of mine breaks the central dogma paradigm of protein folding. This is also why all neuro degenerative diseases have protein folding abnormalties in the brain. Lewy body, beta amyloid, synaptophsin.....etc.
>
> An extreme example is the involvement of **chaperone proteins, which provide an environment favoring a specific folding**. Therefore, we have the possibility that **a single amino acid sequence, as a peptide, dictated by a gene coding sequence, can fold into more than one protein and therefore perform more than one biological activity: THINK SHBG.** This is why we still do not know its ultimate molecular formula. It is the ultimate chameleon chaperone protein for sex hormone binding.
>
>
> regarding your email about SHBG. Here is the response: **It can act as both a key to open heaven (hormone health) or hell (fuel cancer growth) What determines it? The electronic and protonicty that induces the changes within the molecular structure of the protein to allow it to have multiple functions depending upon the environment it is in**. Why can it do this? Think back to EE 8. I talked to you there about transition metals and the non native EMF and the special qualities of D shell electrons. **In SHBG protein there are several atoms with D shell electrons that give it, its ability to transform rapidly.** Think about a Star.
>
> Now think about SHBG. S**ex hormone bonding globulin (SHBG) is the wild card action in these hormone sensitive cancers and in optimal hormone health states and is tied to its special molecular acrobatics. It explains why it walks the hermophrodite pathways between estrogen and testosterone levels in the face of a low redox potential**. How does it multitask? It too is loaded with transition metals and D Shell electrons.
>
> **It all comes down to a transition metal at a key binding site**. The primary and second structure of proteins are all controlled by nucleic acid codes. The tertiary and quaternary structure of proteins is the domain of epigenetic modifications of non coding DNA. This is where the action happens for SHBG. The primary structure of SHBG comprises tandem laminin G-like (LG) domains. The amino-terminal LG-domain includes the steroid-binding site and dimerization interface, and its tertiary structure, resolved in complex with natural and synthetic sex steroids, has revealed unanticipated mechanisms of steroid binding at the atomic level. This LG-domain interacts with fibulin-1D and fibulin- 2 in a ligand-specific manner, and this is attributed to the unique way estrogens reside within the steroid-binding site, and the ordering of an otherwise flexible loop structure covering the entrance of the steroid-binding pocket.**Estrogen is designed to work in with SHBG because it is associated with a lower level of iodine in women.** This changes the delocalization of electrons of zinc’s D shell electrons. **There are however two other transition metals binding sites (cation) The cation changes make SHBG resonant to different electromagnetic frequencies when different sex steroids bond to the loop ring. This mechanism enables estradiol to enhance the sequestration of plasma SHBG by the stroma of some tissues through binding to these extra-cellular matrix-associated proteins. Non native EMF cause changes to SHBG molecular function and this is why hormone sensitive cancer are observed. The hormones do not cause it........the non native EMF that changed the molecular action of SHBG did............BOOM**.​

​

We lose the water battery and then we revert to glucose metabolism first aerobic and then anaerobic. ### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-21T14:22:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-supplementation.10266/post-116938>

zinc oversupplementation can lead to big time issues but most people today need more zinc in their diet. Oysters are the best source. Excessive zinc cause massive copper issues.

### Aggie — 2014-03-21T17:08:03+0200

Very rarely will you find a US Doc who knows how to test for it or how to treat it. The only lab that can run the test is in Holland . Dr. Kampsteeg lab testing

Furthermore, you're in serious trouble to able to detox. Without a Doc helping treat KPU - you'll never rid yourself of total toxins. Which ties back into transitional metals - you're full of them having KPU. Even more bad news. KPU ties into Autism, leaky gut, ADHD, Lyme, mental disease, Rett syndrome and a bunch of other disorders.

### Ken23 — 2014-03-21T15:51:59+0200

Here in Nj it's beginning to warm up and I'm looking forward to adding fruit to my diet but not sure how to balance it within my meals. Is it ok to have a fruit smoothie with a banana, couple oranges and berries, if its balanced with eggs and plenty of fat? Is there an optimal range that its ok to stay in for carbs from fruit?

### Lahelada — 2014-03-21T16:20:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transitioning-to-spring.10268/post-116950>

Hmm, how is the fruit harvest going in NJ ?  What I mean is that you had better leave fruit for summer when they ripen where you are. Strawberries at the end of May will probably be your first fruit . I cannot think of any other fruit that ripens earlier.

### prAna303 — 2014-03-21T21:32:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transitioning-to-spring.10268/post-116997>

Just like Lahelada said, just because it is spring does not mean that anything has grown yet. Fruits and berries take a while to become able to eat.

For me, who is living in sweden, it means strawberries around middle of june, blueberries late august, if lucky.

Go with local things first, not things that grow in other countries. If you have any problems with inflammation i would just skip them until you are ready, one year or two years from now. If i look how the world looks today, hell, just keep your diet with fat, proteins and tons of water.

### Gracelind — 2014-03-24T08:04:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transitioning-to-spring.10268/post-117540>

Hi Ken,

There are genetic SNPs for Vitamin D receptors--two being VDR-Taq and VDR-Fok1--that can affect how your body metabolizes Vitamin D.
Have you done the 23 and Me test? I know that the V3 chip tested for VDR-Taq. Not sure whether the new V4 chip does or not.

Be sure Vitamin D supplements are D3 and in oil--not the dry form.

### Lahelada — 2014-03-24T09:12:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transitioning-to-spring.10268/post-117547>

Also Vit K2 prepares the vit D receptors so it may be a good idea as well to take it inits k2 form as you may not be metabolising it from vit K if you have gut issues.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transitioning-to-spring.10268/post-128099>

### cantweight — 2014-05-23T20:07:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transitioning-to-spring.10268/post-128102>

I have taken it out for 2 years thinking it may have been bad for blood sugar spikes, I hadnt had a single blood sugar issue before now. I plan to add it back

Juicing finally got him to give up drinking milk too - - he didn't have phlegm all the time. I haven't bought milk in a long long time.

He talks about doing this again, but hasn't done it yet.

### GGinPG — 2014-03-21T20:20:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/most-recent-labs-and-sleep-study-new-here.10270/post-116981>

These are routine labs, done every three months. Latest results from about 5 weeks ago.

Not much to report:

T4, TOTAL 6.8 MCG/DL (REFERENCE RANGE 4.5-12.0 U)
T3, TOTAL 120 NG/DL (REFERENCE RANGE 76-181 U)
T3, REVERSE, LC/MS/MS 13 NG/dL (REFERENCE RANGE 8-25 G)
FERRITIN 88 NG/ML (REFERENCE RANGE 10-232U)

I will have this testing (plus TPO ab & TG ab) again in about one month.

I have had Saliva testing done for Cortisol (Morning, Noon, Evening, Night), DHEAS, Testosterone, Estradiol, Progesterone, Ratio Pg/E2, however 18 months has passed, so I have no idea if these results are of any value. I had Saliva testing done (same things) about 5 years prior to that, and the results hadn't changed all that much.

Basically, LOW Estradiol, Progesterone, Ratio Pg/E2, Noon Cortisol. HIGH Evening Cortisol.

(The earlier testing had LOW Cortisol at Noon and Evening, HIGH at night)

I had a sleep study done some time ago (for dental purposes, as it were) and as my sleep quality hasn't really changed, I thought I'd share the numbers:

"THE PATIENT SLEPT FOR A TOTAL OF 258 MINUTES OUT OF 466 MINUTES FOR A SLEEP EFFICIENCY OF 55%, THIS CONSISTED OF 9% STAGE I, 72% STAGE 2, 0% STAGE III, 0% STAGE IV, AND 19% STAGE REM SLEEP. THE SLEEP ARCHITECTURE WAS ABNORMAL WITH AN ABSENCE OF DELTA SLEEP. THE ONSET TO SLEEP WAS AT 28 MINUTES AND THE REM LATENCY WAS NORMAL AT 90 MINUTES."

"THE DEGREE OF APNEA IS MILD DURING THIS STUDY. PATIENT IS ASYMTOMATIC FOR HYPOPNEA."

I had a more complete bloodwork panel done about a year ago, which seems to be MIA just now. Again, don't know how relevant old labs are but I'll keep looking and post later.

GG

So he thought "direct" hormones might not be the best way to go. We planned on revisting this in the future. When I find my (dang) labs I will post. You can get Naltextrone over internet, cost pennies.

\===========================
You said:
DHEA. I don't remember the results but my doctor wanted me to take a small amount (orally),
...
LOW Estradiol, Progesterone, Cortisol
\===========================
Since your menstrual cycles are hard to figure, at least you know when flow started, specially when you wait 2-4 days.
Get your estradiol and progesterone (serum) measured on day (2-4).
Make corrections accordingly.
Look at my post #4
[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/)
chart on estradiol and progesterone

day 2-4 ideal values are:
Estradiol=20-40 pg/mL
Progesterone=1.5 ng/ml

you want to establish proper levels there first
Start with progesterone

You should also measure pregnenolone, likely it is very also low.

I would start with this two type of pills:

150mg pregnenolone Micronized Lipid Matrix from Nutricology
50mg DHEA Micronized Lipid Matrix from Allergy Research

take one of each pills, make sure to take it long enough so yo have a one whole cycle behind you,
on day 2-4 draw blood
review test results
make adjustments to your protocol,
on day 2-4 draw blood
review test results
make adjustments to your protocol
etc
once you have idea when your max progesterone may be (usually day 19-21), add prog & E2 test there.
If you can figure day of ovulation (temperature), at first assume that you should have max progesterone 7 days after ovulation.

At some point you may reach top of range on pregnenolone, but still need to increase progesterone.
If that is so,
add progesterone, cream in day time, or Prometriol (100mg) (at bed time).

pregnenolone-MLM you may likely use 150-900mg (1-6 pills)
that should give you good progesterone levels for cycle days 1-17 or so,
then you may have about a week when you may have lagging prog,
for that about week you may consider Prometrium or cream, whichewer you like better.

Prometrium comes in 100 & 200mg gelcaps (cannot be divided)
\===================================
This tests at LabCorp are handy for all this:

Pregnenolone, Mass Spectrometry--Test Number: 140707 CPT Code: 84140
Progesterone--Test Number: 004317 CPT Code: 84144
Cortisol, Free, Serum, With CBG (Endocrine Sciences) Test Number: 500440 CPT Code: 82533; 84449
Cortisol, AM & PM Test Number: 104000 CPT Code: 82533(x2) (7:30AM, 12PM)
Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) Sulfate--Test Number: 004020 CPT Code: 82627
Estradiol, Sensitive Test Number: 140244 CPT Code: 82670
Estradiol Free, Equilibrium Dialysis, With Total Estradiol, HPLC/MS-MS (Endocrine Sciences) Test Number: 500649 CPT Code: 82670; 84999
Estrone, Serum Test Number: 004564 CPT Code: 82679

\================

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-26T00:02:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/most-recent-labs-and-sleep-study-new-here.10270/post-117916>

As your redox Rx improves you will see your hormones respond in kind. When it is rather mixed you wont get the hormone response you expect and often times it can be paradoxical and confusing the inexperienced clinician or the unsuspecting patient.

### GGinPG — 2014-03-26T14:06:56+0200

Finished the book last night (read a few paragraphs out loud to my husband each night so that he has some context for what I'm doing and about to do). I feel like I have a pretty good handle on what needs to happen and why; a little intimidated by the testing (not the blood draws, I'm used to that), but working through it all. But I have a path in front of me and that is working to settle my mind somewhat. Baby steps...

GG

# Thread: GG's Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 58
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### GGinPG — 2014-03-21T20:56:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-116988>

For anyone who has seen _Groundhog Day,_ you'll recognize this quote:

Anything different is good.

Of course that statement is not universally correct; all too often different is bad.

But not today. Today, I awoke around 3 am and I (1) was not sweating (or even too hot) (2) was not thrashing about trying to find a more comfortable position (3) was not frustrated or even panicking that I was going to take _forever_ to fall back asleep. True, I _was_ awake at 3 am. I am awake every day at 3 am and it is sheer misery.

But not today. I may be awake and miserable at 3 am tomorrow, so I must remember that I have made the following changes _so far_ which I choose to believe made my today possible:

\- Found Jack Kruse Tuesday

\- Ate B(er)AB breakfast (protein heavy, very low carb, decent amount of fat) within ONE HOUR of rising\* Wednesday and Thursday (today too, but that's after the change)

\- Walked the dog in the _winter_ morning air dressed for _spring (_still wore a hat, though)
\- Started taking cool showers
\- Ate 2 meals of seafood\*\*
\- Walked away from my laptop at 7 pm Wednesday, 6 pm Thursday and didn't look back
\- Walked away from the room where the TV is located at 7 pm Wednesday and Thursday
\- Stayed in rooms containing only orange light until bedtime, while wearing my blue-light blocking goggles Wednesday and Thursday

\- Took my (2x/wk) epsom salt bath Thursday in cool water (didn't measure temperature, but it wasn't much warmer than the ambient air, which was 65 degrees F.

\- **Turned off the Wifi network before going to bed Thursday\*\*\***

And today I awoke around 3 am and it took me a long time to fall back asleep. An hour at the least, probably closer to two (I did not look at the clock, which was placed too far from my bed for me to see it without rising)**_._** But. _**BUT.**_ The entire time I was awake I felt strangely peaceful and calm. And that has never happened before.

Today is tomorrow.

GG

\*I waited an hour because of the thyroid meds. Should I not be doing this?
\*\*I typically eat seafood once a week, if that (excluding canned Skipjack Tuna)
\*\*\*THIS IS UNHEARD OF IN MY HOUSE!

### caroline — 2014-03-22T00:23:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-117025>

Good job!!!! this is a great journey ..... remain focused and committed and positive and you will reap huge rewards!

### Gracelind — 2014-03-22T01:22:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-117034>

Hi GG,

Wonderful! Positive trending results in a short time is great sign of future benefit.

I hear you on waking up in the night. Happens to me, too, but with less consistency--maybe 4:30a for a couple of days then it moves to a different time for a few more days.

Have you been a night owl? What time did you go to sleep?

Last night I listened to a very interesting [podcast on sleep apnea](http://hwcdn.libsyn.com/p/4/1/0/41018912a79518a8/Borelli.mp3?c_id=5804777&expiration=1395370947&hwt=2873bdf09b8d42ae7240b9b6284d8b5c). The speaker, Dr Joseph Borelli, described waking with pounding heart and anxiety as a quick release of fight or flight neurotransmitters. He was able to resolve this as part of his therapy for apnea.

Sounds like you may already have increased endorphins with the changes you've made to feel calmer at that too-early awakening. An excellent change.

### GGinPG — 2014-03-22T02:30:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-117048>

I was a night owl until I suspected I had severe adrenal fatigue about 2 years ago - and then I forced myself into a routine and an early bedtime. So I've been consistent (with a few exceptions like holidays or rare special events) with being in bed by 10 and lights out at 10:30.

I had a sleep study (I posted results in the Optimal Labs section) a while back. "Mild apnea", but it didn't seem to warrant treatment. Mostly it showed I get virtually no deep sleep. Had cortisol tested (also posted there) - out of whack there, too.

I will be journaling more specific details in the morning - I promised myself no more computer at night

### GGinPG — 2014-03-22T14:31:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-117137>

Well it's Groundhog Day. Again.

Last night was a bad night. I've cried my tears of frustration over it and I will struggle to get through the day, which is Saturday, which means my zombie-like state will affect my husband's "day off", too. Not that it doesn't affect him during the work week, it does, it's just that on weekdays he's immersed in his work practically every waking hour so he doesn't "miss out" on as much. But anyway, this is what happened - and then I move on to figuring out why. I awoke at 3-ish as usual but could not fall back asleep at all. I was frustrated enough to check the clock after what seemed like an eternity had passed and it read 5:17. I stayed in bed until 5:48 when my cat pushed the bedroom door open - my sunrise alarm clock begins to go off at 6 anyway, so might as well get up. (It's not really even light here until 7 or 7:30 if the morning's cloudy, but this time of year I'm up before the sunrise anyway.) I took my morning thyroid meds, fed the cat, had cup one (of 2) of my morning green tea and sat down at the computer to document the night.

(Pausing for breakfast break - 7 to 7:40 - it takes a really long time to get this bigger, earlier breakfast down. A struggle, really.)

Some of Thursday's progress showed up last night. I didn't wake up in a sweat again (although I felt warmer than Thursday) and I felt calm again (at least until the frustration of not falling back asleep at all took hold). So that's something.

One thing I noticed both last night and Thursday: I had hunger pains when I awoke at 3. I don't remember ever having noticed hunger pains before, athough that may be because I'm usually hot, sweaty, and thrashing around in the bed trying to find a comfortable position. (I also noticed my bladder was uncomfortably full, but that's always the case.)

The only thing I did differently between Thursday and last night was the cool epsom salt bath. Well, technically that's not the only thing, but I believe it to be the thing of importance.

At this point I could go in so many different directions about sleep history, breakfast history, yada, yada, yada. But for some reason today two words are stuck in my head: temperature and light. First, temperature. Two things are absolutely dead certain in my sleep history: If I am too cold or my skin is exposed (even in hot weather) I cannot fall asleep; and, I cannot sleep if I am too hot (or the environment around me is). What really stinks is that my sleep, which is already horrible while it's still winter, will get even worse with spring and summer.

As for light, I didn't always recognize that it affected my sleep. This is embarrasing, considering. Now I know that it makes a huge difference. I've had a few nights in pitch darkness - it matters. Unfortunately, I have been unable to make any environment in my home (viable for sleeping, that is) pitch black, and it's not from a lack of trying. I do the best I can, but there are drawbacks to even the positive changes. I will save the whys and hows for another time; suffice it to say, the best I can do for now (and I hope this won't always be the case) is use blackout goggles (like swim goggles). Which work well (sleep masks? tried them all, none work for me) until that point in the middle of the night (usually in the middle of my three o'clock thrashing) when I absolutely cannot stand to have them on. It is sudden, because until that point they don't bother me at all, consciously anyway.

Another thing (unrelated to temperature or light) is that despite knowing that it is causing me problems, I absolutely cannot fall asleep if I am not tightly cocooned in the fetal position. I have tried to break this habit so many times I have lost count. I feel so much better when lying on my back, but I cannot fall asleep on my back. I have no idea why but I have utterly failed to make this happen. This is significant somehow, but I don't know if or how it is related to my waking at virtually the same time every single night, so for now....temperature and light.

I think I'll try a cool bath tonight (sans epsom salts).

An afterthought - I sleep earthed. I use an Earthing Sheet (18 months, give or take, except on nights when lightening is expected or likely). I know it has helped my circulation (a positive). Maybe I should reconsider this?

### SeaHorse — 2014-03-22T16:03:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-117145>

Is your earthing sheet plugged into a grounding rod outside in the dirt? or is it in your outlets…sorry if this has been asked before, I'm just dropping and this struck me. In the outlets can be a big problem with dirty electricity…in other words you are becoming the ground for bad wiring etc.
Also, again, I apologize if this is going over old territory, but have you played around with your timing of the NDT? When I was supplementing, I couldn't dose anything after about 2:00 or I wouldn't sleep.

Any luck with Phosphatidylserine for cortisol build up at night?

I had a number of years with your kind of sleep patterns and now I love my sleep so much. Lots of things were changed up, but really minding the light cycles, magnesium, CT, the MAGNETICO, and all the other protocols have really made sleep a great thing for me….doesn't mean it's all perfect, but this one element has definitely improved, so don't give up….

Also….I used Buteyko breathing techniques when I would wake up to try and activate the parasympathetic nervous system…

The Chillow that Dr. K talked about a few years ago really helped keep my head cool during the temp. rising at night. I would keep it by the bed and just grab it when I felt my head get hot. I often started the night cold and ended sweaty….now I'm keeping a more on an even keel. Remember your brain has a lot of rewiring and re signaling to organize and it will take time.

Your apnea is why you don't like to stay on your back…that is the classic position for apnea to happen.

### GGinPG — 2014-03-22T17:31:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-117160>

Seahorse - wow - lots of things I haven't thought of.

My earthing sheet is plugged into an outlet (as a matter of convenience as there's "weather" for most of the year in Western Pennsylvania. Honestly, I hadn't given any thought to "dirty" electricity. Even after having read through a good bit of this website, I didn't make a connection. I can see I have some research to do in this area. Better to not to ground at all then?

Haven't used Phosphatidylserine - can't say I know what that is. More research

As for my NDT, for six years I took it once a day, when I woke up. I had a change in doctors (not my choice, but it worked out for the better) and my new doctor upped my dosage from 90mg once a day to 150mg split in two, 90mg at rise, 60mg roughly 12 hours later. ( About 5 weeks ago, my doctor asked me to try 5mg Cytomel once or twice a day, as well.) I try to take the evening NDT dose consistently at 7. Pills and supplements and timing is tricky. I take Ferritin, which needs to be four hours away from any thyroid meds and the thyroid meds need (from what I've always heard) to be kept away from food. So day goes like this: rise 6-6:30, 90mg NDT + 5mg T3 , right before breakfast I take a probiotic, high dose Vitamin C, Ashwaghanda, and 5000 IU vitamin d3, breakfast between 9 and 10 (that changed this week when I discovered this site, now breakfast is 60 minutes after my am thyroid meds), more high dose C before "lunch" (if I have anything to eat) around noon, at 2:30 I take my ferritin with some Betaine HCL and some magnesium citrate, dinner as close to 4 pm as possible, 60mg NDT at 7pm, shower or bath between 7 and 8, and at 8 (no later because of the amount of liquid required to get it all down): more high dose C, my main dose of magnesium citrate, and 3mg melatonin. I try to stay in orange-bulb lighted rooms as much as possible after 7 (I have been lax here in the past, but I'm correcting that) or else wear my orange goggles. Sometimes both. I'm avoiding computer and TV light after 7, too. Very light activity from 7 on. Mostly I read. In bed no later than 10, where I try to read something heavy to make me sleepy and - lights out by 10:30.

I used to have problems falling asleep and staying asleep. With that routine I fall asleep fairly easily (of course I'm not perfect, but I do my darndest because I know I must). Now the bigger problem is the waking in the middle of the night. The timing is so consistent I know it's telling me something. Because I wake up so hot, it's telling me "temperature", but because I cannot fall asleep if I'm too cold, I'm not sure how to manage it, because I don't have an automated blanket removal system to prepare me for that rise in body temperature. So any "solution" is disruptive. Even before finding Dr. Kruse I have made several attempts at falling asleep with fewer blankets (I keep the house as cool as I can keep it and still keep the other househould members comfortable (those being my husband, cat and elderly dog) and it's the same thing with the back sleeping - I just cannot seem to make it happen.

I thank you for your questions and insights - sometimes I think I've covered everything only to discover something THAT SHOULD HAVE BEEN REALLY OBVIOUS (like the dirty electricity you mention).

A thought to the evening NDT dose...my sleep didn't get any worse (or change from recent patterns) when I started taking it, so I assumed it wasn't having a negative impact.

GG

I forgot the Iodorol, which I take 3x/wk, on Monday, Wednesday and Friday after breakfast.

### sooperb — 2014-03-22T17:56:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-117168>

I've been using an earthing sheet for around two years. When I first plugged it in to the domestic socket I struggled to use the sheet, however it was explained to me that you need to work up gradually. I think dirty electricity applies more if you live in an old house with possibly no ground to the domestic wiring, if your home is modern it should be fine. I started using a rod out of curiousity, I experienced the same tingling as I had using the domestic socket. I wouldn't want to give up using the sheet as I know I have benefitted from it.

I also wake up during the night, often after the same interval, 1.5 hours after falling asleep, almost always hot and sweaty with my heart racing. Sometimes I've been dreaming sometimes not. I generally just lie there till my pulse rate drops and/or I fall asleep. I've no idea why this happens and I don't know how to prevent it. One day maybe I'll have some answers.

### GGinPG — 2014-03-23T00:59:16+0200

### GGinPG — 2014-03-28T18:48:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-118520>

Ten days in. I continue to read, read, read, although not so much online as I'm trying to limit my exposure to those nasty things coming out of my laptop. Just received two of Dr. Kruse's recommended titles, Cross Currents and Zapped so this may be my last blog post ever. (Kidding.) (Sort of.) Also just received a fresh supply of orange light bulbs so that I can have low blue light in all my living spaces at night. Planning on getting filters for TV, monitors, etc.

What I have been doing faithfully: 50 grams protein in the morning (60 minutes after rising and taking my thyroid meds.) This is becoming tougher, not easier. I'm thoroughly sick of my breakfast before I'm halfway done. Please tell me this gets easier? I start with a can of sardines, this gets me 20+ grams of protein right off the bat, and if I'm unable to finish my plate, I figure I've gotten the healthiest part of breakfast down. Then I have either leftover protein from dinner the night before, or some eggs, plus a veggie of some sort and a small amount of carbs (sweet potato cubes sauteed in duck fat, lard or beef fat - also helps to increase the fat grams). Roughly every other meal is fish or seafood (in addition to the sardines at breakfast), but I'm finding that at breakfast virtually nothing tastes good to me. For instance, I love scallops. Had some for breakfast and had to choke them down. I'm not complaining, I know I am doing my best, but am wondering if this is typical. And short term. Strangely, I am not full after breakfast, just sick of it. After breakfast I have a cup of DECAF with a tablespoon of coconut oil. The coffee is new. I'm only drinking it to get the coconut oil down. I can't stand the taste of it in food and I can't take it plain, so I figure it's a solution to getting it down. By noon, I'm a little hungry.

"Lunch" is a cup of bone broth with a tablespoon of grassfed gelatin added and a small handful of soaked and dehydrated macadamia nuts. I'm already spending a lot of time in the kitchen with breakfast and dinner so I don't want to have to prepare anything. This is a reasonably healthful way to get me through until dinner. I realize with autoimmune issues I should ditch the macadamias and I will; right now it's just a solution until I can stock up on better choices (I do my food shopping at the beginning of the month). Since having gone Paleo, lunch hasn't really been much of a meal for me anyway.

Dinner between 4 and 4:30. Smaller than breakfast, heavy on protein and veggies. Every other meal has seafood. By dinner time I am usually fairly hungry but I don't need to eat much to feel satisfied. I may have a piece of fruit afterwards, but I haven't "needed" it (I've always had a sweet tooth).

I walk away from my computer and the TV no later than 7 pm (it is still daylight here) and take a cool epsom salt bath (3X/wk) or a cool shower. I know this isn't the true CT protocol, but I'm hoping to work my way up to that. If I'm a little active (say warm from washing the dishes in hot water) prior to my bath/shower it's much easier to tolerate the temperatures, which are roughly that of the ambient air, right now it's still cool enough for the furnace which I have set to 65. I was already taking an epsom salt bath 2X/week (in warm water), and I feel better afterward, so I added the third. I can't do it every night -- too expensive. After bathing, I put on my blue light blocking goggles, do a few light evening chores (15 minutes) and then go sit in orange light to read away from television and computers. I had been on a schedule of in bed by 10, lights out 10:30, but I'm not making it that long. Sometimes lights out at 9:30 now. This is fine with me, but my husband resents the heck out of it as we have a very limited amount of time to spend together (he works until fairly late in the evening most days). We are working on it - but there's some added stress because of it and I try to avoid stress at all costs.

My sleep has been consistent this past week. I'm still falling asleep fairly easily, but waking at roughly 3 am every night. Thanks to a link shared by sooperb, I believe I at least know why it's happening (liver, estrogen), but nothing I've changed these last 10 days has reversed the trend. My husband has been cutting off the power to my bedroom each night (he sleeps in another room) and turning off the WiFi, and I think that's been helping in that I am calmer overall when I wake. But the nights he's forgotten to turn the WiFi off weren't noticeably worse.

I have noticed that I'm queasy in the evenings after dinner and also in the morning when I rise. I'm definitely hungry when I get up, although that quickly turns to revulsion once I've had a few bites of food (no matter what that food is). I know I have to work on getting more breakfast down. Most days I do manage to finish most of it, but it's taking increasingly longer to get it down. This morning it took me an hour and twenty minutes to eat roughly 2/3 of my breakfast.

Onward and upward!

### sooperb — 2014-03-28T19:32:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-118533>

The thing that strikes me is that if you don't like or enjoy what you're eating you're going to give up sooner or later. You have to eat food you like and enjoy, if you don't you're making it really tough on yourself. I'd suggest you vary it so that at least some times you are eating without wanting to barf. Eggs can be eaten in lots of ways, are pretty cheap and a reasonable source of protein, you could add avocado, smoked salmon or I guess even a little cheese. If you don't like CO, use butter  Poached eggs over wilted spinach if it's available. Ring the changes and make sure to include what you like rather than food that you probably wouldn't eat. I would have said at one time that I couldn't possibly eat "xxx" for breakfast ever, but I have changed substantially in that respect and now I wouldn't rule out hamburger or even steak lol.

I'm impressed with your dedication and determination, especially in the face of opposition from your husband, that's a big sticking point for me, he's not enthusiastic at all.

### Inger — 2014-03-29T07:54:14+0200

I think Jack is right. I eat stuff I do not enjoy too much... and you get used to it. Taste changes. I think we need to listen to our body's needs not our taste buds too much because they are manipulated through years of eating artificial foods.

I do give my self some threats once in a while... but I take care it is very clean, natural stuff like 100% organic chocolate. Or enjoy taste boosters like raw onion slices + ACV and EVOO on my chevice maybe a tiny tiny amount of raw wild honey in my egg yolk foam..

### caroline — 2014-03-29T08:34:15+0200

### sooperb — 2014-03-30T12:53:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-118808>

" think Jack is right. I eat stuff I do not enjoy too much... and you get used to it."

But GG isn't liking it! That was my point, there are a wide variety of optimal foods to choose from, you don't have to force down things you don't like. I agree though Inger, you can change your taste buds and if you've spent years of eating mass produced pap then they are probably just dying for a day out out on real food and real flavours. ### GGinPG — 2014-03-31T16:18:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-119053>

Actually, I see a lot of valid points on all sides, here. I think what's going on with me right now (it's still very early in the process, which I expect to be a long one) is more an issue of timing and quantiy, than about a reaction to a specific food. I'm having problems getting ANYTHING down, even foods I've loved all of my life. So yes, while it's true that my body isn't used to scallops or sardines for breakfast I think my mind and stomach are rebelling because I'm forcing myself into a new habit after years of waiting several hours after rising to eat a "gentler" breakfast. Yes, I have been Paleo for several years so my breakfast (and lunch and dinner) choices were already miles better than a typical SAD meal. Plus, I've never eaten so much protein in one meal before in my life (let alone quite so much fat). I have confidence that this food revulsion will correct itself (I have to hold to that hope or else I wouldn't be subjecting myself to this much misery!); I only questioned whether it was indeed, temporary. In truth, even in the few days since I posted the update, the situation has worsened. Today, about 1/3 of the way through breakfast I had to get up from the table - the nausea hit me like a lightning bolt. I waited about 10 minutes for it to subside and then (slowly) finished as much as I could. Now I have to mention that I had leftover slow-cooked pastured pork shoulder on my plate (about 6 ounces, which is a lot) and that I am almost always a little queasy some time after eating pork shoulder or Boston Butt (I think it's because they're such fatty cuts, even though I remove as much of that fat as possible). Also, I had already had my sardines. So, it's entirely possible that my extreme reaction today was because of a specific food, but I've never before reacted to the pork while I'm eating it. So I guess what I'm saying is that I fully accept that necessary change can and will be painful; I'd just like to know that I don't have months of this pain ahead of me, that it will eventually become easier.

And while I'm on that topic, I will mention that my sleep, despite all of my positive changes so far (which are, I must admit, still very limited), is right back where I started - horrible. No need to rehash the whens and whys and hows here, that's all documented in earlier posts. What's important at this point is that I've learned that my sleep can be improved through small changes, but ultimately, until I fix THE thing (if it is indeed just ONE thing) that is causing it, I will always have this problem. sooperb's article pointed my issue originating in the liver - too much estrogen in my system? This is another post entirely, but suffice it to say that with what I know about my hormone history, this rings very true. So I know that until my hormones are in proper balance, I will not have proper sleep. Of course there's more to it than that, but at the same time it is a basic truth. Simple. But not so simple to achieve and not without (probably) going through some "have to get worse before I get better" changes - such as what I'm going through with breakfast.

So I will continue to do what I'm doing for as long as I can until something "clicks", and if it never does then I'll need to consider trying something else. In the meantime, I'll keep reading, reading, reading to find even more connections and possible avenues of approach.

Thanks everybody for your thoughts!

GG

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-31T17:19:59+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-31T17:37:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-119077>

If you feel fatigued consider dehydration as a top issue. Few do.

You should never rely on thirst to dictate water consumption, because it lags the real effect. Calculating your [total body water deficit](http://www.ccmtutorials.com/problems/explore/name/hypernatremia.htm) is another way to try to measure how badly your engine is working. We covered this in the [April 2013 webinar](http://www.jackkruse.com/april-webinar-2013/). By the time thirst kicks in your serum osmolarity is already impaired. Dehydration is a major cause of day time fatigue as well, and dehydration slows your metabolism by 2-3%. As time goes on this imbalance can steepen dramatically in a person in an altered environment. In fact, just a 2% drop in total body water can cause neurologic changes to show up. How do I know this? I am a neurosurgeon, and we see these swings all the time in trauma cases and and brain tumors that are associated with syndromes called SIADH, cerebral salt wasting syndrome, and diabetes insipidus. Now think about what I mentioned in the [April 2013 webinar](http://www.jackkruse.com/april-webinar-2013/) for our members.

Sooperb do not give up after eating raw oysters just once... it took me about half a year (!) eating 12 raw oysters every week to really start liking them! I can tell you I did not care for them in the beginning at all... I just took them as medicine..lol
I would never ever cook my oysters.. that would be such a waste  I think they are way more healing when fresh and raw

GG, maybe some dehydrated raw meat slices (home made jerky) with coconut oil would be easier for breakfast? That way you might be able to get in lots of protein without even feeling full at all! Or some raw salmon chevice or sashimi? I love eating raw meat for breakfast.. it is so fresh and really - delicious too! Make yourself carpaccio, or tartar... whatever.

### caroline — 2014-04-04T09:43:25+0300

I have spoke before about how I eat raw meat ..... but in case you haven't seen it......

I buy GF steak with as much fat as possible and then put it on a WOODEN cutting board .....I grind some black pepper on it and some chili flakes and stick it in the fridge. After about a week it dries a bit and then I slice it thinly I love it and have no problem at all with digestion .... unlike with cooked steak.

### GGinPG — 2014-04-11T21:49:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-121026>

A little over 3 weeks in and....status quo. There's very little difference (yet!) in how I feel day to day and my routine (posted above) is pretty darn consistent. I'm still having some trouble with the BAB, but (!) I've been grounding myself while I eat (Earthing mat) and that's been helping. There still comes a point where I know I've had enough and I put my fork down (it's not really a feeling of fullness, just a strong signal to stop), but the aversion to all foods at breakfast is all but gone. I am still leaving food on my plate regularly, but I get most of it down. I've also upped my seafood intake, I'm having seafood two out of every three meals instead of every other meal. I can't say that I'm craving seafood, but I will say that I have a lot less interest in beef, pork and chicken. Could be a moon phase thing  or a sign that something's about to shift. While I am not obese, I do have twenty pounds that came with my hashi's diagnosis and pretty much never went away\*, and so I have been pretty strict about the 50g/day carb limit. Some days I'll have a few grams over 50; many days I have much less. I haven't snacked at all and my fruit consumption is way down (1X-2X/wk tops). My husband requests a (Paleo) dessert once a week, and I have indulged a bit especially when chocolate is involved. For a healthy person, my few bites of dessert once a week are no biggie. I am not a healthy person, so I recognize that this habit has to go (if you can call two fork/spoonfuls a habit!), and soon. It would be easier if the hubs decided to forgo his weekly need for a treat - if it isn't around I can't sample, right? - but I can't blame my own weaknesses on him. I plan to report in my next update that no "dessert", Paleo or not, touched my lips. It's a goal.

Bedtime habits and sleep are roughly the same. Still waking around 3 (and a few more times after that), but still mostly calm and relatively cool(er). I've had exactly 2 naps in 3+ weeks, both of which I felt I "needed" at the time, one of which negatively affected my sleep that night. This is progress; I've needed 2 or 3 naps/week for many years. My baths and showers are consistently cool and becoming cooler. I haven't taken the face plunge yet, not out of fear, but because I think I'm not ready for that step. Even though my current diet ratios of Omega 3/6 are good (and have been so for a while now), I haven't tested my levels yet - I suspect they are not where they should be, and so I'm holding off for now.

Other (hormone) testing is on hold; I am having a period roughly every 10 days (bad) and it sneaks up on me every time (good! no PMS symptoms to alert me!) This pattern predates my Leptin Rx somewhat, but I believe the daily low carb counts are having an impact. Overall, I think this is a good thing and I'm not really concerned (yet) as it's probably a necessary side effect of "normalizing". It's a bit inconvenient, and I'm sure it's not helping my ferritin levels at all, but overall it's something I can deal with.

Still reading everything I can get my hands on, online and off. I posted my labs some time back and received a recommendation to add resistant starch to my diet. I knew it was the latest big thing in Paleo circles, but I didn't really know where to start with it, so I did my research and ordered some (green banana flour, no nightshades for me) as well as some soil-based probiotics (Prescript Assist, also new to me). Haven't taken either yet, plan to start the PA first, probably Monday. Here's hoping!

My (facial) skin is looking better. I have good skin, really (other than extreme paleness), but even at 43 I get the occasional blemish or unevenness. Been pretty clear since starting the Leptin Rx, so I'm counting that as my first VICTORY on the road to optimal!

GG

\*When I had my NDT increased from 60 mg (1 grain) to 90 mg (1 1/2 grains) I lost that twenty pounds fast and kept it off for several months. My weight eventually crept back up. My diet/exercise habits were nearly identical before and after the NDT increase, so I can't credit anything I did for the loss or the re-gain. Nothing I've done since has made that 20 pounds budge beyond a pound or two here and there. I accept that the weight won't come off until all the little duckies are lined up in a row, and that won't happen tomorrow!

\*\*\*Edited to add: I've been experiencing heart palpitations (not unusual with thyroid meds) for about a week now. First few days only at night, since then day and night. They are mild and I attribute them to the Leptin Rx as I haven't had a change in my NDT dosage lately. My next scheduled bloodwork is in about 2 weeks; next doctors appointment toward the end of May. Here's hoping the reset is decreasing my need for medication!

### GGinPG — 2014-04-12T01:41:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-121060>

More thoughts for a Friday.

I was a skinny kid, pre-adolescence. Not too tall, but miles of bony legs. I've been told my entire life that I was a cry baby as an infant - cried constantly. I learned from my paternal grandmother (never a word from my mother's lips) that my mother was advised by my doctor that I wasn't getting enough nourishment from her breast milk and that she should feed me formula. My grandmother claims my mother resented this remark and refused to comply. I really have no way of knowing for sure, my mother won't talk about it, even now, but it certainly makes sense. I ate pretty well as a kid, as far as it goes, my mother didn't keep many sweets in the house. I wasn't much of a fruit eater, but I adored blueberries (still do). Spent a lot of time (every weekend for years) with my maternal grandparents. Upon picking me up for the weekend, my grandfather would stop at the Dolly Madison shop and fill a cart will all manner of goodies: brownies, fruit pies, donuts. None of this was off limits at my grandparents' - it was offered cheerfully, and I ate up, happily. I developed quite a sweet tooth. Stayed skinny.

Until I turned twelve and then I went from skinny to, not really chubby or heavy, but soft. I was an early bloomer (needed my first training bra at 9; menarche at 11) and I can't say for sure if the "softness" came from the change in hormones or the need to "disguise" the fact that I was developing a womanly curvature - in the third and fourth grade! By the fifth grade, I was still the only girl that had rounded out without the use of Kleenex - and believe me the boys noticed and teased. I was extremely self-conscious, so it's possible I subconsciously ate more (junk) so that my chest would stand out less. Nothing more obvious than boobs on a skinny girl! But I don't know. There's no "naturally" skinny members of my family on either side, so it's possibe I was just settling in to my destiny. I never became "heavy", just always had a softness about me that wasn't alleviated by activity. I was a reader, not a softball player or swimmer. As a teen I walked a lot, and enjoyed it. (I still love to walk.) But I didn't do it rigorously enough to change my shape much.

I was mostly healthy, as a kid. The usual colds, the flu once or twice. Chicken pox (mild case), strep throat, tonsilitis once each. My younger brother (the "happy, never-crying" baby) was the sick one. Frequent tonsilitis, strep, Epstein Barr. He was diagnosed with Graves Disease at 16 and had his thyroid removed with radioactive iodine treatment. Synthroid for the rest of his life. He's also had vitiligo from a very young age.

Right before I got married at 22 I lost all of my "baby fat" (I believe it was the stress of planning a wedding, I wasn't trying to lose the weight). I weighed 115 when I went to have my blood test done prior to the wedding. I was very thin (I'm 5' 4") and my wedding dress was two sizes too big when I walked down the aisle (it's obvious in the photos, I was too nervous to notice it at the time, I think). I wanted to keep the weight off and thought that "low fat" was the way to do it (it was the mid 1990's, low fat was all the rage). As a newly married couple, we didn't have a lot of money. I ate a lot of spaghetti with no-fat sauce. A LOT. Also plenty of sugar. I also started a running and weightlifting routine. The running was a bad idea, but I felt fantastic when the runner's high kicked in. The weightlifting was fine (I'm sure I didn't have the greatest form) and even though I gained about 10 pounds doing it, I looked fitter than I had at 115, and my clothes still fit great, so I kept it up.

I had always had "bad periods", since practically the first one. Every PMS symptom you can think of was a 10 on the 10 scale, and my god, the cramps! In my twenties all of this got worse. At thirty I made my first gynecological appointment, and I was handed a sample of birth control pills to "manage" my PMS symptoms. Even though I desperately wanted relief, something made me hesitate and I never took a pill. I suffered on. In my early thirties I noticed that I worked backwards. Never had a huge sex drive, but I grew to see that I had a strong aversion to sex leading up to ovulation. I wasn't at the point of making connections yet, not by a long shot, but I remember speaking the words "I don't think I want to have children" and not really understanding where that was coming from. In high school, I could have been voted "most likely to become a mommy", not because I was in any way promiscuous (quite the opposite), but because I had a "way" with children. I loved children! I loved babysitting. I loved babies! If you asked me at 18 if I would be childless at 43 my answer would have been "not a chance". And yet. And yet now I believe my body was telling me in my thirties that my becoming pregnant was a bad idea, hence the constant aversion to sex at the time in my cycle when I should have been biologically driven to have sex.

When I was thirty five I came down (in the middle of the summer) with a fever, a high one. I spent four days on the couch insisting it would come down before my husband made me go to the emergency room. My white cell count had dropped to virtually nothing; I was told I came in the nick of time. I was tested for everything (my only symptom was a fever) and I mean everything. I was poked with needles so many times I lost count. Eventually, diagnosed with "viral infection". I was attached to an IV for four days (laced with doxycycline) and released to a long recovery. I did eventually improve but I wasn't the same. I was tired all of the time, needed naps to get through the day, and my vision was "fuzzy". I ate very little and continued to run, but I was gaining weight. I suspected my thyroid was out of sorts and asked my doctor for tests. Not knowing much, there wasn't a whole lot to say when my TSH came back "normal" and I was told I was fine. I knew I wasn't fine, but didn't have the energy or knowledge to argue the point. So I went home, spent several more months with extreme fatigue and eventually found a naturopath who supplemented me to within an inch of my life. When nothing worked, she admitted it was probably my thyroid and gave me a referral to a doctor who would treat me with Armour, rather than Synthroid, which she felt would be harmful to me.

My new doctor ran a more thorough workup and I was diagnosed with Hashimoto's. In the course of doing tons of research on the hashi's I came to believe that I had a problem with gluten and so I had myself tested for Celiac. That test came back negative, but I knew that wasn't uncommon. I eliminated gluten (eventually all grains, in fairly short order) and felt light years better almost immediately. I had further testing done (on my own) including a stool test and genetic testing, which confirmed what I already knew. It wasn't long before "gluten free" became "Paleo". Since my diagnosis, I've had ups and downs, but I'm certainly miles away from where I started. I still feel less than optimal - like I still have miles to walk before I reach my destination, but I'm constantly learning and tweaking. And now having found Jack Kruse and this community, I have hope that that road terminates eventually,

GG

### caroline — 2014-04-12T02:11:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-121063>

An amazing story of self discovery and finally listening to the Doctor in you head!

I am so glad you are here and sharing your story with us...

### GGinPG — 2014-05-09T17:59:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-126039>

This is me just shy of 2 months into the reset: The Good, The Bad, The Ugly.

I have an appointment with my doctor in 6 days, during which I will hopefully convince him to order me a plethora of exotic tests. Meanwhile my thyroid labs are due in my mailbox any day now. But for now...

The GOOD:

I am having a much easier time with my BAB. Some days are easier than others, but I don't have to force myself much and I'm (mostly) finishing everything on my plate. I'm still follwing a strict ketogenic diet; still roughly the same pattern - every other meal is seafood. (Sometimes five meals in a row will be seafood, followed by two or three meals straight of beef or duck or pork, but it's all evening out in the end.) I had a few bites of (Paleo) "dessert" for Easter (and only because it was a treat made especially for me by my mother); other than that I have been as clean and pure as the driven snow.

The Bad:

Perhaps I should rename this category "The NEUTRAL". There, I just did.

The Neutral:

I am still waking in the night. It is no longer consistently 3:00, though. Whenever I am too hot (which is a lot) I wake up. This is a deceptively simple problem. It goes something like this: If I am even the slightest bit cold, I cannot fall asleep. If I am even the slightest bit hot, I cannot stay asleep. And for 43 years my biggest challenge has been to find the sweet spot, and I haven't found it yet. I sleep in a room by myself now so that I can control my environment as much as possible. I sleep with earplugs and a sleep mask and the door closed because my husband stays up later than I (and is noisy), also because an open door is an invitation to a cat on the bed and further sleep disruptions. I try to keep the room as cool as possible (easy in the winter, virtually impossible in the summer, so my summer sleep is much worse than my already lousy winter sleep. It is essentially summer here now...) I hate fans. If I run the A/C even a degree or two lower than typical, the electric bill - already high - skyrockets. Plus my husband complains he's freezing. If it's cool enough (and there's enough of a nighttime breeze to circulate air) and I keep the windows open, the noise from the street below keeps me up, so I'm seldom able to keep the windows open. Basically, I'm temperature-challenged. Ultra-sensitive. (More on this topic in "The Ugly".)

I've been taking resistant starch, green banana flour, (alongside soil-based probiotics) for nearly a month now. Because any change I make takes what seems like forever to manifest itself in me, I held off documenting what I've noticed. Mostly because neither thing seemed to be doing anything dramatic for me. For a while my digestion slowed down somewhat and I experienced a smidgen of gas in the evenings, roughly 12 hours after I took the RS; timing was very consistent. Digestion (meaning transit time) seems to be mostly back to normal. Two changes of note (which have only happened in the last few days): One, I am MUCH sleepier in the evenings, even before I get up to prepare for bed. As in, can't even keep my eyes open. This hasn't translated into my falling asleep any easier but I feel like my sleep, albeit more disrupted lately, is deeper in the periods in which I am actually sleeping. I am having more specific dreams, easily explainable (I tend to be a cryptic, nonlinear dreamer), mundane, almost, but very clear and I usually remember all the details come morning. I almost feel as if I COULD get a decent (full) night's sleep if the conditions were right - they're clearly not, yet.

The second change leads directly to The UGLY.

The UGLY:

I am constantly hot. Uncomfortably hot. Miserably hot. Virtually the only time I am not too hot is when I am sitting in a cool tub or taking a cool shower. And once I'm out of the water, I'm almost instantly hot again.

One of my defining characteristics is that I am truly miserable when I'm hot. Probably because I don't sweat properly (never have) and so that's not a cooling mechanism for me. (I hate to sweat, too, even if my perspiration isn't quite up to standards.) Also, the wonky thyroid doesn't help, I'm sure. I know that one of the signs (as mentioned by Dr. Kruse) of a reset is that my body will be hotter but I will feel cooler (poor paraphrasing). I'm MORE than ok with that. But I'm hotter and feeling hotter, so I'm not sure what to make of it. I almost feel as if I'm burning fuel, strange as that sounds, and I'm kind of surprised that I haven't lost any weight. Not that I'm expecting weight loss at this stage, I know myself better than that. But hot = irritable, so basically I'm snapping at my husband a bit and finding myself avoiding activity because it only contributes to more HOT. I'm Jonesing for some cold adaptation.

Everything takes longer with me. If the standard time frame (for a change to show benefits) is one month, rest assured that five, six months out, I'm still waiting. So I've learned to be patient. Meanwhile, I'm staying away from the bad EMFs, I've put filters on my shower and drop Vitamin C into my tub. I'm having my water delivered from Crystal Springs and my seafood delivered in bulk (or nearly) - although I haven't eaten it raw yet. (By the way, anybody else out there have some issues with Crystal Springs' service? I've only been a customer for a couple of weeks and I've had to call with problems three times already.)

Onward,
GG

### fitness@home — 2014-05-09T20:25:42+0300

> But hot = irritable, so basically I'm snapping at my husband a bit and finding myself avoiding activity because it only contributes to more HOT.
