### Gagnrad — 2014-03-23T21:00:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117429>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=117420)
>
> One last option from my list of favorites. Thomas Hanna was one of Feldenkreis's student and his guided work is my favorite...

Moishe Feldenkrais apparently knew martial arts, particularly judo. That went into his system, and he also drew on F. M. Alexander's work and a number of other disciplines. I have read that he would use manipulation on people and had a very keen sense of touch, so that as soon as he felt resistance he'd go back the other way. It's been suggested that there's something rather judo-like about that refusal to force things.

I think with some therapies where there's manipulation it can be rather forceful and not always particularly helpful. I can remember a long while back listening somewhat aghast to a podcast as Rob Wolf explained how a physical therapist had _stood_ on his pelvis and left him in agony. I guess more forceful techniques may have their place, but …

Feldenkrais seems more to have tried to re-awaken functionality than to put people's body parts where he thought they should go. And here's another theme, common to meditational practice and to several of these systems -- _minimal effort_. It's also there in Taoism (and Tai Chi which is grounded in Taoist thought, of course) in the form of the notion of _wu wei_ (effortless effort). But you will know more about this than me, having done more Tai Chi and in Hong Kong at that.

There's an interesting lecture by John Nicholls, an AT teacher in New York, online where he attempts to relate some of these disciplines. The diagram (Appendix 1) right at the end is quite fun. He's got all these different practices joined by lines, according to how they relate to each other, and what concepts and practices they share.

[http://www.johnnichollsatnyc.com/wp-content/uploads/2010/10/ATLargerCnxt-STAT-Mem-1.pdf](http://www.johnnichollsatnyc.com/wp-content/uploads/2010/10/ATLargerCnxt-STAT-Mem-1.pdf)

So you _can_ trace certain links between "depth psychology" and "bodywork' and "eastern religion" -- and also western esotericism, such as Rudolf Steiner and your old friend Gurdjieff. And this isn't always "influence" but I think sometimes simply because it's the same human reality being dealt with.

But, of course, the nice thing, particularly for modern Westerners who shy away from some of that, is that you don't have to be religious or esoteric -- or believe in the doctrines of particular psychologists.

### Josh — 2014-03-23T22:37:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117461>

My wife is in the 2nd year of a 4 year Feldenkreis Guild training. Moishe's Judo work was rare and he discusses it in one of his books and how the tradition has fallen apart, lowered its standards. How he was literally recruited by the Japanese in Paris is an amazing story. Apparently Moishe was not unfamiliar with Gurdjieff. I found this a little while ago and thought I would put it here:

> **Einstein: When Language Interferes**
> “The words of the language as they are written or spoken do not seem to play any role in my mechanism of thought. The psychical entities which seem to serve as elements of thought are certain signs and more or less clear images which . . . are in my case of visual and some of muscular type. \[These elements take part in\] a rather vague play… in which they can be voluntarily reproduced and combined… This combinatory play seems to be the essential feature in productive thought, before there is any connection with logical construction in words or other kinds of sign which can be communicated to others . . . In a stage where words intervene at all, they are, in my case, purely auditive, but they interfere only in a secondary stage.” [Hare Brain](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/0060955414/ref=as_li_ss_tl?ie=UTF8&tag=thefeldemetho-20&linkCode=as2&camp=217145&creative=399349&creativeASIN=0060955414), p. 56.
>
> **Feldenkrais: I make a special effort not to think in words.**
> “When I am presented with a trouble in function, I make a special effort not to think in words. I try not to think logically and in correctly formed sentences. It has become a habit with me to imagine the relevant nervous structures by seeing them with my mind’s eye. I imagine a part which produces a flow of fluid. Part of the travel of the fluid is electrical, then becomes chemical, and again electrical. After many transformations the flow will end in a muscular contraction, and the muscular play will result in some apparent outside action involving the body, or parts of it, that will affect or transform the immediate environment. Sometimes I am stuck at a point where I cannot imagine the pattern of the flow, nor the possible obstacles in its way. Then I ask, is the obstacle a diffusion, damping, deviation, loss of impetus, break of continuity, or an impossibility of one of the transformations.
>
> I have found this way of imagining so fruitful that I cannot do without it. It often shows me where my knowledge is insufficient so that I know exactly what I am after and therefore in which books I am likely to find the information. I form a working theory and change it in the light of new observations I must add to make the theory work. This mode of thinking is often successful in situations where specialists with greater knowledge than mine have failed. Nobody is omniscient enough to think mechanically.” [Body Awareness](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/1883319080/ref=as_li_ss_tl?ie=UTF8&tag=thefeldemetho-20&linkCode=as2&camp=217145&creative=399349&creativeASIN=1883319080), p. 16

I had only an introductory training af 3 weeks, 7 days a week with about 6 hours on the floor per day doing FI. I have used Hannah's sessions to continue my explorations when I get inspired and have taught them to clients occasionally. I have had about 25 FI sessions over the years and look forward to my wife being able to do them. Most martial arts schools and yoga studios miss this level of "self sensing" and awareness. They start with too much doing and effort. I spent some times learning Pilates as well and it teaches self-sensing from a different angle. It is really thrilling as it comes together.

As far as strong manipulation goes, if it is done really well with skill, nothing touches it. I have experienced things that others have worked on with everything from acupuncture to FI and rolfing resolve in a minute. However truly skilled and courageous practitioners are now few and far between. Also, after a big structural shift, one needs to maintain one's redox and potentially have other people balance the more subtle energetic, facial, muscular and structural imbalances to achieve the desired result.

One of the first bodyworkers who worked on me was from the Alexander world and I appreciated his work a great deal when I was a teenager. He has now become an Oriental Medicine practitioner.

The whole issue of "engramic" information "held" in the body is an interesting subject approached differently by different traditions. I feel that the key is the holgraphic nature of memory as it is help in the 3D collagen matrix throughout the body. One can access things from many places and they can move around. There is a separate question of that which may be "connected" to a given body rather than "in" the body. One can skin this cat many different ways in my experience and I now feel that the highest good is empowering people to do their own work in a conscious state. This creates conscious evolution or learning, rather than just moving the furniture around so to speak....

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-24T10:45:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117556>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=117461)
>
> As far as strong manipulation goes, if it is done really well with skill, nothing touches it.....

Well, maybe. There's a lot of related issues here. One could again say that the "soft martial arts" are not all softness. Indeed, one very famous Scottish Tai Chi teacher over here likes to make that point:

> The essential combat theory of Tai Chi Chuan is to use softness or Yin to overcome hardness or Yang and to use hardness or Yang to overcome softness or Yin. So rather than blocking the opponent's attacks we divert or redirect them using evasion and/or footwork at the same time. This is using softness to overcome hardness. The attack has then become 'dead' force and has changed from Yang to Yin. At this point we must also change from Yin to Yang by striking (Yang) the vital points of our opponent (Yin). This is using hardness against softness. In order to train this evasion it is necessary to do a lot of practise on the pushing hands exercises I mentioned earlier. ...

[http://www.taichichuan.co.uk/information/articles/tai\_chi\_gladiator.html](http://www.taichichuan.co.uk/information/articles/tai_chi_gladiator.html)

So it's both ways round and hard and soft. Partly, I guess it's that Tai Chi is more a health activity nowadays and less something used for combat, although it was originally used by the Emperor's guards. Docherty himself won an all-comers South East Asian Martial Arts Championship with Tai Chi.

But the wider issue of using minimum effort for what you want to do interests me. All the time all round us people use far more effort for things than they need to. You see them, for example, huffing and puffing and contracting all over the place just to get out of a chair ... yet if you do it in a coordinated, balanced and smooth way it takes _almost no_ effort at all to stand up from a chair.

It turns out that as soon as most people merely _think_ of doing some action they start to tighten in various muscle-groups unecessarily. Fascinating!

They "get in their own way". It seems to be exacerbated by modern educational systems that emphasize effort. The truth is that much of the time one needs not to "try harder" (a familiar school report comment) but to work smarter. I recall a lecture by Alan Watts (whom I don't actually care much for) in which he mentions a boy in his class at school who would clench his teeth and grunt every time he tried to read. Wasted effort.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-24T11:51:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117560>

Here's a Zen saying that also pulls in some of these themes:

> When standing, just stand. When sitting, just sit. Above all, don't wobble.

There you've got "being in the present" -- just doing whatever it is you're doing. We tend always to be thinking about the next thing and anticipating outrselves. And I think the notion of "minimum effort" is in there, too: this is when everything comes right.

Zen in Japanese is the equiavalent of Ch'an in Chinese. There's a view that the Ch'an form of Buddhism in China was actually much influenced by Taoist thought.

### siajanai — 2014-03-24T14:54:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117574>

> [Sammy said:](/goto/post?id=117328)
>
> I am interested in transcendetal meditation to help me destress and handle stress better.
>
> Anyone here practice this on a daily basis? How does it work for you? Are there other types of meditation or activities that you routinely do to help de-stress?
>
> How do I get started??
>
> All types of meditation courses come up on a quick search in google.
> Is it necessary to enrol in a course to learn meditation?
> Is there more to it than breathing in and out and going "ommmm........."
>
> Thanks you guys!

I learned TM 40+ years about and I've been pretty regular with it ever since.

The thing about TM is that it is meant to be learned from a trained teacher, not from a book. It is meant to be a single practice that distills all the wisdom of the teacher of the founder of TM, Maharishi Mahesh Yogi, into a single technique that can be easily mastered. You can read more about maharishi's teacher here: [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brahmananda\_Saraswati](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brahmananda_Saraswati) -he was the first person to hold the position of "Shankaracharya of Jyotirmath" \[head swami of northern India\] in over 150 years. The entire TM organization is dedicated to his memory.

TM is taught over a period of 4 days because that is about the fastest time that Maharishi could devise to teach it as he traveled from village to village in India back in the mid 1950's.

TM teachers undergo training that teaches them how to teach TM just as Maharishi did.

the basic outline of the course is:

you learn to meditate then go home and practice.
Then you come back the next day and, based on your first day's experience meditating, the teacher tells you things. Then you go home and practice.
Then you come back the next day and, based on your two day's experience meditating, the teacher tells you things. Then you go home and practice.
Then you come back the next day and, based on your three day's experience meditating, the teacher tells you things. By this time, you should have a "fairly clear" idea what it means to "do" TM and what the long-term outcome might be. Then you go home and practice.

The formal TM program has been expanded a bit since 1955. These days, with local teachers in most large towns, they recommend that you return to your local center once-a-week for the first month, and once-a-month for the first year in order to get your meditation checked. The 3 days instruction I mentioned above is sometimes called the "Three Nights of Checking." When you come back to your local center, you get a simple procedure simply called "checking" which is kinda a mini version of the above. In most countries, the first year of checking is free. In the USA, it is free for the rest of your life.

That is a very simplified explanation of the TM teaching process. In fact, the TM teacher spends the entire 5 months, in-residence, rehearsing the exact words, hand-gestures, body language and tone of voice he or she will use when teaching and answering questions, in accordance with how Maharishi Mahesh Yogi set up the training. When people (such as Deepak Chopra) claim they have devised their own teaching method for meditation, recall that they were never trained as TM teachers, and it is the difference between someone taking a play they have seen and trying to reproduce it from memory (or make up a new one they think is better, just because), versus someone rehearsing for 5 months, 7 hours a day, 7 days a week, with the playwright (or someone he trained) in order to present the original play as the author intended.

The "technique" of TM is so simple, that many people think that they can learn it from a book or over the internet, and no-doubt, a few do. However, when the American Heart Association reviewed all the research on how meditation or relaxation effects high blood pressure, they found that despite there being huge numbers of studies published each month on mindfulness and other practices, compared to only one or two studies published each month on TM, the difference in quality of research, and consistency of outcome made it impossible to recommend other meditation practices for the clinical treatment of high blood pressure. As it stands right now (and they just updated their report without changing the conclusions on meditation/relaxation), they say "The overall evidence supports that TM modestly lowers BP." ... "TM may be used in clinical practice to treat hypertension" \[as a secondary treatment\]... \[due to the poor quality of research and inconsistent results\] "other meditation techniques are not recommended in clinical practice to lower BP at this time." See Summary and Clinical Recommendations \[for meditation/relaxation\] in: [http://hyper.ahajournals.org/content/61/6/1360.full?sid=a3b8b00a-919b-4027-8ac5-15317c283df1](http://hyper.ahajournals.org/content/61/6/1360.full?sid=a3b8b00a-919b-4027-8ac5-15317c283df1)

TM's effects can be noted literally over night, or it might take months or even a year or two for YOU to notice a change in yourself. A few months before I learned (during teh summer between high school and college), a couple of my worst enemies in high school, who were constantly tormenting me and bullying me, happened to learn TM. The effect on them was crazy. The Friday before they learned, they were in full-on bully mode. By the next Monday, something had changed: I said or did something in my usual defensive-obnoxious way that would normally have triggered a violent reaction, even a beating, and they just looked at me and laughed and walked off. Very, VERY strange.

On the other hand, I learned TM a few months later and didn't notice any change in myself. The TM center was simply a safe place for a lonely kid to hang, so I continued to meditate regularly, just because. And then one day, I was chatting on the phone with this cute high school girl I was madly in love with. She hadn't spoken to me since school let out. She suddenly interrupted me and said "Whatever you have been doing different for the past few months, it has changed you. You are suddenly years older. It is very attractive." Needless to say, I had a new reason to continue doing TM, but to be honest, I think it was as long as another year (or more) before I actually noticed changes in myself.

There's a saying amongst TM teachers: the more stressed the student is, the faster teh changes tend to show up.

Recently, researchers went to Uganda and taught TM to a bunch of war refugees. These were people who had fled into one of the poorest countries in the world because where they were born was even worse. They were victims of the most horrible violence: watching their husbands murdered, then being gang-raped while their children watched, etc. -you know: the stuff that war movies leave out. They were living in a country where they couldn't speak the language, and couldn't find a job. Sleeping on the floors of churches, or in rude mud huts ("temporary rental shelters") or under bushes. They were completely in survival mode, barely able to procure food from teh relief agencies. In fact, researchers paid them to show up for the initial pre-test by giving away bags of cooked beans. You get the idea....

After working out details (30% of the people weren't really interested in learning TM, they just wanted food), researchers managed to devise a "delayed start control group" study with 21 people who learned TM immediately matched with 21 controls who would learn TM at the end of the study.

The results were overwhelmingly good:

**Reduction in posttraumatic stress symptoms in Congolese refugees practicing transcendental meditation.**
[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23568415](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23568415)

90 percent of the TM group tested non-symptomatic for PTSD after only 30 days practice. None of the control group improved that much, and the average score continued to get worse.

A second study tested a new group of TMers.

**Significant Reductions in Posttraumatic Stress Symptoms in Congolese Refugees Within 10 days of Transcendental Meditation Practice.**
[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24515537](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24515537)

They showed almost the same levels of improvement after 3o-days of TM but researchers found that 2/3 of the reduction in PTSD symptoms took place after only 10 days of TM.

It's pilot studies but already the United Nations has contacted the David Lynch Foundation (which sponsored the study) to explore how fast the program could be expanded to meet the needs of teh 100 million Africans thought to be suffering from PTSD or other stress-related illnesses. See the last minute or so of the top video: [http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2014/02/21/meditation-ptsd\_n\_4825609.html](http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2014/02/21/meditation-ptsd_n_4825609.html)

Anyway, no guarantees that the above research will be verified, it is very possible/probable that it is an "outlier" for the effects TM will have on PTSD, but the advantage for TM is that mindfulness practices take 2 months to teach, and then researchers do testing on the effects of mindfulness after 3 months practice. TM takes 4 days to teach, and researchers have found striking results within 10 days of practice. No-one even attempts to test the effects of mindfulness 14 days after the first lesson.

TM is taught by a not-for-profit organization and the current fee has been lowered a quite a bit from its high. Right now, TM is $960 to learn for adults and $360 for 10-18 year olds and full-time college students. The David Lynch Foundation gets celebrities like Ringo Star and Paul McCartney to do fundraising concerts

and then they hire TM teachers for a set rate to go and teach disadvantaged people for free. Browse the videos at the DLF website for more info: [http://www.davidlynchfoundation.com](http://www.davidlynchfoundation.com)

Hope this gives you a taste of what TM is about and what is going on with it. You can find your local TM center via the international website: [http://www.tm.org](http://www.tm.org)

Hope this helps (without being Too Much Info).

### siajanai — 2014-03-24T15:06:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117577>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=117332)
>
> I tried TM when I was 8 I think...It did not click for me. I have no idea how it is being taught these days. It is not necessary to enroll in a course from my experience and the support of other people who have the "knack" in a technique is helpful at the beginning for sure. I started with the meditations of Osho which do not really require anyone to teach. He suggested that most people are now a little wound up to just sit and watch, so he developed meditations to take people from movement and expression to stillness. After a few years of these, I found that I could sit and watch for long periods and I still sit every day after 24 years. It is the center of my life. Anything that gets you into your body is a good preparation including yoga, dancing, exercise, etc..All of the people I know who meditate regularly had something or someone that introduced or seduced them into exploring their inner world. It was an accident for me as I experienced it. I was in a new martial arts school and they would sit zazen after training. I had never been able to sit comfortably and they had zen benches for kneeling. As soon as I sat on one, I disappeared and was shocked when I opened my eyes and people told me that an hour had passed. I practiced irregularly after that. However, slowly it became easier to get to that "space" and I explored it from many directions with many techniques both active and passive. Meditation is a great adventure...worth the price of admission which is free or everything you have depending on how you look at it....off to the my midnight weekend sit....

The adult form of TM is never taught to 8-year-olds. In fact, it is almost unheard of for anyone under 10 to be taught the adult technique, and that is people who stalked up to Maharishi Mahesh Yogi at the age of 9 and insisted that they were ready to learn, so he taught them himself.

There's a kid's technique for 4-10 year-olds, but it isn't the adult technique.

And TM has been taught the same way since the TM teacher training course that the Beatles walked out of in 1967 and no accredited TM teacher in the past 40+ years has taught TM differently since all TM teachers have made a promise to only teach TM as authorized by the international accreditation organization that the old monk set up.

It may sound a bit obsessive-compulsive, but the strict attention to detail pays off. Recently, the federal government of Brazil announced that they were setting aside funds to train 48,000 TM teachers, one for each public school in Brazil, so that all 45 million school age kids could learn TM for free.

### siajanai — 2014-03-24T15:24:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117581>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=117560)
>
> Here's a Zen saying that also pulls in some of these themes:
>
>
>
> There you've got "being in the present" -- just doing whatever it is you're doing. We tend always to be thinking about the next thing and anticipating outrselves. And I think the notion of "minimum effort" is in there, too: this is when everything comes right.
>
> Zen in Japanese is the equiavalent of Ch'an in Chinese. There's a view that the Ch'an form of Buddhism in China was actually much influenced by Taoist thought.

Dhyan Buddhism entered China and became Ch'an Buddhism. Ch'an Buddhism entered Japan and became Zen Buddhism.

Maharishi Mahesh Yogi referred to Transcendental Meditation as "the simplest and most important of the dhyan techniques." Outside of the West and Tibet, Buddhists generally don't insist that there's a "Zen"-only way or a "Ch'an"-only way to meditate, and there's plenty of practicing Buddhists who learn TM. In fact, there is a TM teacher who specializes in teaching Buddhist priests and monks to meditate, being a Buddhist priest himself: [http://www.tm.org/blog/meditation/buddhist-monks/](http://www.tm.org/blog/meditation/buddhist-monks/)

In Thailand, the founder and principal of the only Buddhist all-girls boarding school, is a Buddhist nun who decided that TM might be good for her students. Based on watching the results in her students, she eventually learned TM herself, and finally became an accredited TM teacher:

[http://www.tm-women.org/blog/helping-at-risk-girls-in-thailand-succeed.html](http://www.tm-women.org/blog/helping-at-risk-girls-in-thailand-succeed.html)
[http://www.tm-women.org/blog/the-personal-journey-of-acharn-yai-buddhist-nun.html](http://www.tm-women.org/blog/the-personal-journey-of-acharn-yai-buddhist-nun.html)

.

Recently, the federal government of Brazil announced that they were setting aside funds to train 48,000 TM teachers, one for each public school in Brazil, so that all 45 million school age kids could learn TM for free.

I learned TM from my father and his friends who were all in their 30's. so I guess it was the adult version. In some ways I have never been a child and in some ways I still am....I was not ready for meditation at 8 and it all just pissed me off. I wanted to go fishing....

I already gave a positive perspective on the Maharishi, traditions send the appropriate people out to publicize the work and get people started. This I know from personal experience. I never met him personally and I know several people who knew him have even before he left India. I also know people who spent time with his teacher. I know a number of followers in the West who spent time with him.

For me, if one is blessed to become close to a teacher and a tradition that opens ones heart and grows one's consciousness, it will be embodied in one's being and needs no lengthy justifications and defense of whatever has or has not happened in public. While TM has been successful, you guys have not touched The Church of Scientology or the Catholic Church. Your response embodies what I have always experienced from long time TM practitioners and adherents, a rigidity and lack of playfulness/sense of humor. It is a stage in every spiritual journey and whatever happens longer-term with TM, seems to create these conditions. Most of the people I know who started with TM have moved on to other traditions or practices. It is all a "take what you like and leave the rest" or the "truth is what works" on the path for an individual's N=1. no matter how big the organization, we all walk alone......

Here was an innocent inquiry from a young woman enthusiastic to explore herself and she ends with a sales pitch that includes the defense of the entire history of the organization and university data, etc.. It does say "your experience". If you have participated in all that was listed, then I would love to know how you felt and how it "grew your consciousness", if not, perhaps this was the wrong thread. For me, I listed what I have actually explored myself where I felt my heart was touched. From my experience, note the word "my" versus everyone's or university study or whatever, TM seems to be as good of a starting technique as any and takes people as far as it takes them, N=1 stuff.

Finally, the mystery of a teacher or master is beyond the organization and people around him. As one notorious mystic said "there is no bad publicity".........

Namaste

Swami Brahmananda Saraswati died in 1953, and there are very few people alive who studied with him. The youngest would be about 80 now, and most would be far, far older. Maharishi Mahesh Yogi was one of his younger students, and he died 5 years ago at age 90.

And just who do you know that knew Maharishi before he left India in 1958? I'm gathering you think Maharishi isn't a "best and brightest" in his line, and yet, I know someone who spoke with the hand-picked successor to Maharishi's teacher who asked him "What about this Maharishi who is with teh Beatles? Is he legitimate?" and the response of the Shankaracharya was "let me put it to you this way: he would be my first choice as my successor but they won't allow it due to the caste laws."

Anoop Chandola is the name of my friend. [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anoop\_Chandola](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anoop_Chandola) he mentions his own attitude towards Maharishi whom he never met (never learned TM either, as he learned to meditate from teh Shankaracharya he was quoting to me) in his book _In the Himalayan Nights [http://www.amazon.com/In-Himalayan-Nights-Anoop-Chandola/dp/0982998708](http://www.amazon.com/In-Himalayan-Nights-Anoop-Chandola/dp/0982998708)
_

> For me, if one is blessedto become close to a teacher and a tradition that opens ones heart and grows one's consciousness, it will be embodied in one's being and needs no lengthy justifications and defense of whatever has or has not happened in public. Most of the people I know who started with TM have moved on to other traditions or practices. It is all a "take what you like and leave the rest" or the "truth is what works" on the path for an individual's N=1. no matter how big the organization, we all walk alone......

Being willing to "toe the line" with teaching TM often means being less adventuresome, but not always. And most people who leave TM do so because they fail to see the significance of an open-ended effortless practice. And I sense a certain need to bring personality into this discussion. If I did so first, I apologize.

For me, I listed what I have actually explored myself where I felt my heart was touched. From my experience, note the word "my" versus everyone's or university study or whatever, TM seems to be as good of a starting technique as any and takes people as far as it takes them, N=1 stuff.

Since you learned to meditate from your father, who (it sounds very much like) was not a trained TM teacher, I'd say that it's really N=0 stuff here. You never learned TM in the first place as your father had not a clue how to teach it. As I pointed out elsewhere TM teaching isn't the same as having gone through the 4-day course.

> Finally, the mystery of a teacher or master is beyond the organization and people around him. As one notorious mystic said "there is no bad publicity".........

### Josh — 2014-03-24T19:22:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117630>

All selling of spirituality is a great seduction and the reality is that each person has to face different things when they "go in". What seduces or forces one to "go in" is an N=1 thing. Most authentic teachers sooner or later admit that it tends to become rough at a certain point and that help and support is useful at this stage, "dark night of the soul" stuff. Most authentic teachers also admit somewhere that it is a miracle that anyone starts and that the real starting point is when an individual comes to the point of suicide or utter despair....Many suggest that people "start with the body" meaning to stabilize their physiology, think yoga asanas, tai qi and qi gong, sitting, routine life, routine diet, etc...Sounds a bit like what we are doing here.....staying playful can be tough at times....

### Josh — 2014-03-25T19:45:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117886>

Perhaps for objectivity, TM is "mantra practice" and there are many subtleties to mantra practice as well as stages. This is my favorite piece on mantra practice:

> DISEASE, LANGUOR, DOUBT, CARELESSNESS, LAZINESS, SENSUALITY, DELUSION,
> IMPOTENCY AND INSTABILITY ARE THE OBSTACLES THAT DISTRACT THE MIND.
> ANGUISH, DESPAIR, TREMORS AND IRREGULAR BREATHING ARE THE SYMPTOMS OF A
> DISTRACTED MIND.
> TO REMOVE THESE, MEDITATE ON ONE PRINCIPLE.
>
> PATANJALI BELIEVES – and not only believes, he knows also – that sound is the basic element of
> existence. Just as physicists – say that electricity is the basic element, yogis say that sound is the
> basic element. They agree with each other in a subtle way.

From: Osho, Yoga, The Alpha and the Omega: Vol2, #7

[http://www.oshoworld.com/e-books/eng\_discourses.asp?page\_id=16](http://www.oshoworld.com/e-books/eng_discourses.asp?page_id=16)

Further mysteries can be explored in the books about Swami Rama:

[http://www.amazon.com/Living-Himala...NZ2\_1\_2?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1395769317&sr=1-2](http://www.amazon.com/Living-Himalayan-Masters-Swami-Rama/dp/0893891568/ref=la_B002882NZ2_1_2?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1395769317&sr=1-2)

[http://www.amazon.com/Walking-Himalayan-Master-Americans-Odyssey/dp/0936663375/ref=sr\_1\_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1395769375&sr=1-1&keywords=walking with a himalayan master](http://www.amazon.com/Walking-Himalayan-Master-Americans-Odyssey/dp/0936663375/ref=sr_1_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1395769375&sr=1-1&keywords=walking+with+a+himalayan+master)

[http://www.amazon.com/At-Eleventh-Hour-biography-Swami/dp/0893892122/ref=sr\_1\_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1395769416&sr=1-1&keywords=swami rama biography](http://www.amazon.com/At-Eleventh-Hour-biography-Swami/dp/0893892122/ref=sr_1_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1395769416&sr=1-1&keywords=swami+rama+biography)

These are the ones I have read and experienced. I ahve not read any of Maharishi Mahesh Yogis books as I have not been drawn to them. Each of us seem to have our "preferences" and "tunings".....

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-25T20:35:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117887>

Well … not to maintain the controversy, _but_ …

"Osho" was an alias of the Baghwan Shree Ragneesh, another figure who was surrounded by a certain amount of controversy:

> In mid-1981, Rajneesh relocated to the United States, where his followers established an intentional community (later known as Rajneeshpuram) in Oregon. Almost immediately, the commune's leadership became embroiled in conflicts with local residents (primarily over land use), which were marked by hostility on both sides. The large number of Rolls-Royce cars purchased for Rajneesh's use by his followers also attracted criticism. The Oregon commune collapsed in 1985 when Rajneesh revealed that the commune leadership had committed a number of serious crimes, including a bioterror attack (food contamination) on the citizens of The Dalles.\[2\] He was arrested shortly afterwards, and charged with immigration violations. Rajneesh was deported from the United States in accordance with a plea bargain.

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rajneesh](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rajneesh)

Hmmm … Rolls Royces again.

Doesn't exactly sounds like Saint Francis -- even if this patchwork of rags wasn't actually his:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

[http://uk.reuters.com/article/2007/09/05/oukoe-uk-italy-saint-idUKL0586082220070905](http://uk.reuters.com/article/2007/09/05/oukoe-uk-italy-saint-idUKL0586082220070905)

Nor like Tsung Tsai, the Ch'an monk befriended by the poet George Crane, who gave away the boots and coats given to him by Crane:

[http://www.amazon.com/Bones-Master-Journey-Secret-Mongolia/dp/0553379089/](http://www.amazon.com/Bones-Master-Journey-Secret-Mongolia/dp/0553379089/)

In short, there's a heck of a lot of questionable stuff going on in the whole arena of "spiritual search".

Mick Brown's _The Dance of 17 Lives_ is very revealing on this. Mick is something of a searcher in rehab:

[http://www.amazon.com/The-Dance-17-Lives-Incredible/dp/0747568715/](http://www.amazon.com/The-Dance-17-Lives-Incredible/dp/0747568715/)

I probably should not have provoked our visitor -- who seemed to have joined the board for a very specific purpose on that day, made a handful of comments in one specific thread, and has now, perhaps, vanished. (But, if he hasn't, good -- there's lots of interesting information for any visitor to read on all Jack's boards.)

However, wasn't his response interesting?

I'm not quite sure what people coming from this mindset mean by "spiritual" or "very spiritual". It's an alien kind of discourse to me. It seems to confuse advice on particular practices that seem to reduce "stress" -- _which was what was asked for by the original poster_ -- with … what? … morality or religion or something?

And notice his peculiar accusation came even though I had earlier said:

> For most people nowadays it's just a way of quietening down.

And then:

> But, of course, the nice thing, particularly for modern Westerners who shy away from some of that, is that you don't have to be religious or esoteric -- or believe in the doctrines of particular psychologists.

I'm also somewhat sceptical of the view that religion boils down to mysticism and all mysticisms are much the same. I'm something of a religious sceptic, but I'm no less wary of mysticism. C. S. Lewis almost convinces me in _Letters to Malcolm_ when he says:

> … one thing common to all mysticisms is the temporary shattering of our ordinary spatial and temporal consciousness and of our discursive intellect. The value of this negative experience must depend on the nature of that positive, whatever it is, for which that makes room …

He adds:

> I shouldn't be at all disturbed if it could be shown that a diabolical mysticism, or drugs, produced experiences indistinguishable (by introspection) from those of the Great Christian mystics. Departures are all alike, it is the landfall that crowns the voyage.

Al Ghazali was a mystic -- I think he's included in Evelyn Underhill's book -- yet Al Ghazali said that the good Moslem should go on _razzia_ (raiding against unbelievers) every year, and that killing people and burning their crops was a meritorious act.

I'd sooner the extravagant "spiritual" claims were toned down, and people just said: do something like this if it helps you. Maybe try a guided meditation tape. Or you might find "other activities" (also asked about by the O.P) -- say Feldenkrais, or just walking in the woods or listening to music -- could be helpful.

### Josh — 2014-03-25T21:44:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117896>

Respectfully, "faith without acts..." intellectual exploration of mysticism and criticism without experience is relatively futile. It is similar to the contrast between working what Dr. Kruse proposes and thinking and reading about it.

"There is no bad publicity", consider how Jesus, Buddha, Mahavira, Zarathustra, Junaid, Heraclitus, Bayazid, Gurdjieff, J. Krishanmurti, Bal Shem, Al-Mansoor, Thomas, John the Batist were received in their times....."take what you like and leave the rest"...Read Osho/Bhagwhan/Rajneesh's 300 plus books in English, learn Hindi and read the other 200 or so and then go find the people who lived with him; all of which is available...then make a judgement based on real data and experience.....prematurely judging a being such as him has been one of the great personal tragedies in this life for me. I lost a lot of time.......

This is not stuff that can be experienced from an armchair by most people....

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-26T19:51:15+0200

Hmmm … I'd have expected better from you, since you do say some interesting things elsewhere in the thread. There's a lot of meat in what I wrote, and frankly you're not even trying to address any of it. Perhaps some of it went too close to home. Be that as it may, I'll leave it now.

### Josh — 2014-03-26T21:24:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-118115>

I have respect for your perspective and sources and I have experienced their limits. I am someone who has lived half of my life on each side of the "fence" so to speak. There is a place where intellectual discourse about this does not serve anyone. I agree that anything that gets a person more in harmony with themselves and their surroundings and decreases the stress response is a big win. The question of what is "meditation", "mysticism" and a "valid" or "authentic" "tradition" is a vaster topic even than what we are exploring here regarding the body. Some people make academic careers out of investigating this without taking the first step themselves. I have met people in this position and others who started there and then actually entered the work and saw things radically differently. I have no idea where you are and it is not my business. And yes, intellectual inquiry is one version of the "path" in every "tradition" and it is called "Ghyan" in Sanskrit...I decided not to be a professor of East Asian History and Culture and entered a different path. My teacher's response to my intellect was "teaching you is like trying to teach a brick wall". He is not an uneducated man and has a degree in mathematics and was the assistant to the governor of his state in India before he became and wandering saddhu and other explorations. Many mystics have started as exceptional intellectuals and then have gone on from there. Take a look at Bose who worked with Einstein and his relationship to Tagore for example or Bohm and J. Krishnamurti.

Nothing personal, I respect your perspective and I was much more outspoken in this regard until the age of 26 when I "had to eat crow" so to speak....On we go....

# Thread: Ancient clam gardens
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-clam-gardens.10292/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-23T16:15:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-clam-gardens.10292/post-117360>

> A three-year study of ancient clam gardens in the Pacific Northwest has led researchers, including three from Simon Fraser University, to make a discovery that could benefit coastal communities' food production. _PLOS ONE_, a peer-reviewed science journal, has just published their study.

[http://www.eurekalert.org/pub\_releases/2014-03/sfu-acg032014.php](http://www.eurekalert.org/pub_releases/2014-03/sfu-acg032014.php)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-23T16:46:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-clam-gardens.10292/post-117371>

You should look into the story about NYC and Long Island. 120 yrs ago they had the biggest oyster beds in the world. And we destroyed it in 50 yrs. This is why a city in Long Island had the name Oyster Bay. There is a great bar in Grand Central Station too Called the Oyster Bar. I do not think they even serve them any longer.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-23T17:57:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-clam-gardens.10292/post-117383>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=117371)
>
> You should look into the story about NYC and Long Island. 120 yrs ago they had the biggest oyster beds in the world. And we destroyed it in 50 yrs. This is why a city in Long Island had the name Oyster Bay. There is a great bar in Grand Central Station too Called the Oyster Bar. I do not think they even serve them any longer.

So I see:

> Originally much of the west shore of [Upper New York Bay](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Upper_New_York_Bay) consisted of large tidal flats which hosted vast [oyster banks](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oyster), a major source of food for the [Lenape](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lenape) \[Delaware\] population who lived in the area prior to the arrival of Dutch settlers. There were several islands which were not completely submerged at high tide, although most of them were submerged. Three of them (later to be known as [Liberty](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liberty_Island), [Black Tom](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black_Tom_explosion#Black_Tom_Island) and Ellis) were given the name Oyster Islands by the settlers of [New Netherland](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Netherland), the first European colony in the [Mid-Atlantic](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_states) states. The oyster beds would remain a major source of food for nearly three centuries.[\[14\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ellis_Island#cite_note-14)[\[15\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ellis_Island#cite_note-15)[\[16\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ellis_Island#cite_note-16)[Landfilling](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Land_reclamation) to build the railyards of the [Lehigh Valley Railroad](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lehigh_Valley_Railroad) and the [Central Railroad of New Jersey](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_Railroad_of_New_Jersey) would eventually obliterate the beds, engulf one island and bring the shoreline much closer to the others.[\[17\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ellis_Island#cite_note-supreme.justia.com-17)

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ellis\_Island#Ownership](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ellis_Island#Ownership)

With hindsight one could see the discovery of the New World as an ecological disaster. The proximate cause was the railroad, but the ultimate cause the incursion of a civilisation that was moving towards that technology.

Not to glamourise pre-contact cultures in the Americas. Powhatan's people were not the "willowy vegans" of Walt Disney imaginings, as one commentator caustically observed when the Disney Pocahontas came out. What Powhatan most wanted off Europeans was cannon for prosecuting tribal wars, as the film critic correctly observed. What he didn't add, but could have, is that anyone who annoyed Powhatan was liable to find himself being flayed alive with sharp sea-shells.

But Native Americans lacked the power, the technological means, to really trash their own environment. Modern society's problem is that it has that power and is too short-sighted to see that it's short-term decisions come back on it.

### Josh — 2014-03-23T18:01:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-clam-gardens.10292/post-117386>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=117371)
>
> You should look into the story about NYC and Long Island. 120 yrs ago they had the biggest oyster beds in the world. And we destroyed it in 50 yrs. This is why a city in Long Island had the name Oyster Bay. There is a great bar in Grand Central Station too Called the Oyster Bar. I do not think they even serve them any longer.

I grew up on a beach in New Rochelle Harbor and there were no oysters, only muscles, clams-mostly Cohogs and snails. A few people would still find oysters in the Sound at that time.

[http://www.oysterguide.com/maps/new-york-and-connecticut/](http://www.oysterguide.com/maps/new-york-and-connecticut/)

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-23T16:17:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/humans-drive-evolution-of-conch-size.10293/post-117361>

Another Epi-paleo story from the Smithsonian!

> The first humans to pluck a Caribbean fighting conch from the shallow lagoons of Panama’s Bocas del Toro were in for a good meal. Smithsonian scientists found that 7,000 years ago, this common marine shellfish contained 66 percent more meat than its descendants do today. Because of persistent harvesting of the largest conchs, it became advantageous for the animal to mature at a smaller size, resulting in evolutionary change. ...

[http://newsdesk.si.edu/releases/humans-drive-evolution-conch-size](http://newsdesk.si.edu/releases/humans-drive-evolution-conch-size)

# Thread: Benfotiamine in Pentose Phosphate Pathway
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/benfotiamine-in-pentose-phosphate-pathway.10297/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-03-24T09:37:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/benfotiamine-in-pentose-phosphate-pathway.10297/post-117553>

The form of thiamine known as benfotiamine apparently works in part by stimulating the pentose phosphate pathway:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12882930](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12882930)

I don't see this substance mentioned in Jack's blogs. Normally benfotiamine is used to treat diabetic neuropathy, and one of the possible mechanisms for this is that it shunts potential advanced glycation end products into the PPP.

Curious if Jack or others have any opinion on use for benfotiamine.

I saw Rosedale pushing this as a substitute for dietary D-Ribose, and that just perplexed me.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-24T19:47:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/benfotiamine-in-pentose-phosphate-pathway.10297/post-117638>

Benfotiamine requires concomitant use of high dose B vitamins especially B 1. to get D ribose to work. I don't buy Rosedale's argument. Many studies have shown D Ribose gets into the cytosol and into mitochondria in the cardiac literature on heart failure cases. These cases are when cardiomyocytes are failing and the proximate cause of the AHA grade 3 and 4 heart failure cases. Mushrooms are the best B vitamin supplement out there. In T2D with neuropathy and nephropathy, high-dose thiamine and benfotiamine therapy increased transketolase expression in the kidney. This helped to increased the conversion of triosephosphates to ribose-5-phosphate. This action was shown to strongly inhibited the development of microalbuminuria in nephropathy.

### persistence — 2014-03-24T20:34:56+0200

In T2D with neuropathy and nephropathy, high-dose thiamine and benfotiamine therapy increased transketolase expression in the kidney. This helped to increased the conversion of triosephosphates to ribose-5-phosphate. This action was shown to strongly inhibited the development of microalbuminuria in nephropathy.

So the follow on that many will ask is does taking benfotiamine help a non diabetic make better use of the PPP?

### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-29T03:19:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/benfotiamine-in-pentose-phosphate-pathway.10297/post-162703>

Mushrooms are a B vitamin story and tied to thiamine story. Those with poor redox potential have very low thiamine and folate levels and this is classically seen in T2D, neurodegeneration, and alcoholics. It can lead to Wernicke's encephalopathy and confabulation at its nth degree. The reason is because NAD+ and NADP+ are chronically low and cells are anaerobic and are catabolic and not able to build proteins. Catabolic and anaerobic issues are synonyms for cell with very high ubiquitination levels. The more this cycle is increased the more of the PPP you need to help restore NADPH which is the major reducing element in all of biochemistry........this is how we increase our redox power. Raising NAD+ is the other key issue.........B vitamins are intermediates in this coupling and that is why mushrooms are key. They provide a lot folate and thiamin which are key in elevated ubiquitin states. The higher ubi goes up the more oxidation in cells happens their is a huge lack of electrons and a net positive charge that develops with inflammation.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-29T03:24:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/benfotiamine-in-pentose-phosphate-pathway.10297/post-162704>

lack of reduction = lack of electrons = no DHA in cell membranes and in the SCN.........= too much blue light = too much nnEMF when you think you're mitigating well. When someone tells me seafood hurts them I know their environment is odic beyond belief. They need an EMF engineer because they don't know enough where to look for the problem

It can lead to Wernicke's encephalopathy and confabulation at its nth degree. The reason is because NAD+ and NADP+ are chronically low and cells are anaerobic and are catabolic and not able to build proteins. Catabolic and anaerobic issues are synonyms for cell with very high ubiquitination levels. The more this cycle is increased the more of the PPP you need to help restore NADPH which is the major reducing element in all of biochemistry........this is how we increase our redox power. Raising NAD+ is the other key issue.........B vitamins are intermediates in this coupling and that is why mushrooms are key. They provide a lot folate and thiamin which are key in elevated ubiquitin states. The higher ubi goes up the more oxidation in cells happens their is a huge lack of electrons and a net positive charge that develops with inflammation.

What are your favorite mushrooms for health purposes? And is this story of mushrooms giving candida a bunch of BS?

# Thread: Need Suggestions - Huge Weight Gain
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Huck — 2014-03-24T15:33:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/post-117588>

After starting the Leptin RX 2+ years ago, I finally broke through 260 lbs. Mid summer 2012, I started spending a minimum of 2 hours a day outside with just shorts and tennis shoes. I saw some fantastic improvements in weight loss and hormone levels. I got down to 230, but as Fall started my weight started increasing.

Over Fall and Winter of 2012/2013 I gained about 15 lbs, but starting in late Spring it started coming back off. During Spring and Summer of 2013, I spent as much time as possible outside in the full sun with just shorts and shoes. Mid summer I started intermittent fasting (IF), eating breakfast around 10 am and dinner around 4 pm, resulting in about 17-18 hours without eating. I also started walking a minimum 30 miles a week.

The weight started flying off and by early Fall I was down to 205 lbs, the lowest weight in over 30 years. I thought I had things under control.

My doctor thought I might have prostate cancer based on positive dre. Saw a urologist who thought it was cancer. This all happened early to mid summer. The IF and walking was my attempt to starve the cancer of glucose if in fact it was cancer.

I schedule a MRI for early January. I weighed 230 lbs the morning of the MRI. So from early October when I weighed 205, I had gained 25 lbs in less than 3 months.

The MRI was negative for cancer and I allowed myself a couple days of eating whatever I wanted. I couldn't stop. Within a month, I had gained another 25 lbs. So now I have gone from 205 in October to 255 in February, 50 lbs in 4 months.

I can't seem to go more than 3 or 4 days before I have overwhelming cravings. This is the same problem I had before I started the Leptin Rx. When my body is working right, I don't have cravings, so something is definitely not right. I have been drinking spring water for over a year, I eat a ketogenic diet, I don't know of any increase in emf, most of the supplements I take I have been taking for a long period of time, etc.

I am stumped. Every nice day I get out in the sun, but we sure haven't had many nice days so far this year. Maybe all I need is a week of sun. The only other thing I can think to do is increase fats.

I don't understand all this pathway stuff, but it seems the path that uses photons works fine and the other path(s) doesn't. In low light seasons, I seem to need a lot of carbs.

Is the problem that I have such a huge electron deficit that I eat a ton of food during low light seasons to try to get enough electrons from food?

I may have a partial solution. I recently bought a small travel trailer and plan on spending most of the winters (from now on) farther South. It's hard to get far enough South within the US to get great sun in the winter, but hopefully I can at least find some beaches where I can use the water and grounding to compensate.

Help me figure this out. What can I do? And I would appreciate it if you don't just say reread x, y, and z. If I understood x, y, and z, I probably wouldn't have to ask for help.

### cantweight — 2014-03-24T16:08:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/post-117591>

I wish I had the answer. I have struggled with periods of easy losses and rapid gains myself. Can not put my finger on the answers. There is still imbalance in places in my body but it does not seem extreme to me. I do the right things to the best of my ability and myself and one of my children struggles along while the rest of the family are fit as fiddles following the principles we have learned here. I put together all the pieces I can like you and am coming up short. At least we are not alone

Huck. I discovered how sensitive I am to sunlight this winter. I didn't have the drive for carbs - yet I gained weight, due to lack of electrons. Sensitivity to light or lack of light directly impacts your vitamin A cycle, as well as thyroid production... and cortisol production. coupled with dehydration - and non-native EMF, your body pretty much has only one choice - gain weight to conserve the limited energy it has. So I've come to the conclusion that Sunlight and Water are key especially in a non-native EMF environment....

I need sun like there's no tomorrow... this also explains why I was my healthiest in Guam 12hrs of sunlight year round at the equator... I was banging with energy even though I was totally out of sync with eating and circadian cycles.... the sun is my key...

>
> maybe I am all wrong.. and rambling stupid stuff.. but this is all because of my own personal experiences I have come to these conclusions.. Might all be a N=1

I don't really understand all this stuff in any detail, but here's what I am wondering.

We need electrons and/or photons. For some of us, our bodies are well adapted to using photons. By getting plenty of sun in high light seasons, our energy levels increase - we lose weight, hormones normalize, brain works better, etc.

But once the sun gets too low in the sky to give us all the photons we need for energy, we have to rely on electrons. And for some of us, the pathway(s) to gather and use electrons for energy is inefficient at best. There is a mismatch between photon processing and electron processing. What I am currently trying to do is to extend the high light season for at least a month in the Fall and start it at least a month earlier in the Spring. That still leaves at least 4 months of low light I have to survive.

For years, I have turned to sugars and starches for energy during the low light season. So now that's what my brain knows - low energy, eat 6 doughnuts. Or snack on a big bag of potato chips all afternoon. But we know glucose is not as good as ketones, especially for brain functioning, so there is an almost constant need for more glucose. For those of us who have very low energy in the winter, the cravings for glucose generating foods are often overwhelming. For example, doing my taxes or writing a college paper usually was accompanied by diet coke and a big bag of potato chips. I knew eventually I would have a big crash, but it was the only way I knew to ramp up my brain.

Just exposing myself to electrons doesn't seem to be enough. I need to know what I need to do to get my body to efficiently absorb and use electrons. While a ketogenic diet, good water, and grounding provide some stability, they don't produce even close to the same energy level as photons do (for me).

So as I see my options:

1.  Move to where I can get plenty of photons year round
2.  Use a light source to generate photons
3.  Improve body function to better absorb and utilize electrons

Option 1 is fairly expensive, but right now is the best option.

Option 2 is a bit of unknown. What is available that works?

I have a Philips light that I use for about 15 minutes first thing in the morning during low light season. I don't know if it helps or not. I know there has been some discussion on this board about lights, but I don't remember anything definitive.

Option 3 is probably the best option, IF I understood what it is that I need to do. This is where I need some suggestions.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-25T14:27:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/post-117847>

I don't know how much this is really about winter.

Huck says:

> For those of us who have very low energy in the winter, the cravings for glucose generating foods are often overwhelming.

So here winter appears as the basic problem, _causing_ "very low energy" -- and sugary or starchy foods appear as the solution to the low energy (although they're also conceived in terms of what the body is demanding).

But those foods are no solution, since they simply don't supply that much energy -- and can't be held on hand to supply it as needed. Accordingly, they have to be eaten and re-eaten -- which Huck also refers to:

> ... almost constant need for more glucose ...

What he _doesn't_ say is that that cycle also helps to push insulin up and down-regulate his fat-burning enzymes. And there's where his real energy stores are: in his fat. It's like money in a safe-deposit box to which he's lost the key.

Is winter the problem, or is that he can't use his fat stores?

Couldn't we suggest ways to help Huck transition to more efficient fat-burning?

For example, how about coconut oil (or MCT oil)? Personally, I dislike the taste of coconut oil (even Nutiva) and don't use it much, but aren't I right in saying that the medium chain triglycerides in it can be burnt quite easily?

How about adding some of that in?

### cantweight — 2014-03-25T14:53:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/post-117848>

Huck do you do bulletproof coffee? I have very little issue with needing carbs yet I am still in a similar situation as you which only lends to the argument that food is a tiny part of the problem.

What is your cortisol like? I know my uncontrollable carb cravings were a problem when my cortisol was way off.
Can you calm those cravings with seasonal carbs? Root veggies, citrus, and the like? The inflammation caused by one donut let alone 6 makes me shudder
If that carb craving is just driving you too hard just give it carbs that are not deadly...the body will get used to it. Surprisingly quickly.

You are absolutely right...we are beings with completely inefficient use of electrons. I have not yet figured out how to battle that beast and I have been at it for some time. And most of the science behind it baffles me.

### Huck — 2014-03-25T14:59:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/post-117849>

I just recently started (2 days ago) to make sure I got plenty of fats in my diet - coconut oil, organic butter, olive oil). Last summer, I found that adding fats was really slowing down my weight loss, so I cut way back on fats. When Fall started, I should have started increasing fats, but didn't think of it because I was feeling so good.

### Huck — 2014-03-25T15:16:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/post-117850>

> [cantweight said:](/goto/post?id=117848)
>
> What is your cortisol like? I know my uncontrollable carb cravings were a problem when my cortisol was way off.
> Can you calm those cravings with seasonal carbs? Root veggies, citrus, and the like? The inflammation caused by one donut let alone 6 makes me shudder

I tested hormones for about 2 years and then stopped most testing. In the summer I had fairly good levels and then very low levels in the winter. I decided for now I would just use how I feel as my gauge.

One idea I have is that the cravings may be toxin related. As we lose fat, toxins are released into the blood stream. Is it possible that dumping toxins to quickly for the liver to process results in the body's attempt to protect itself by holding on to or even increasing fat? And the easiest way to increase fat is to eat starches and sugars. There have been several weight points that always seemed to coincide with cravings. For years, I knew that when I got to 270 the cravings would kick in. Then it was 250 and later 230. But I had to hit each weight point many, many times before I could get below it without cravings.

I have several mercury fillings and wonder if this might be related. What's the best way to test for mercury?

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-25T15:49:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/post-117856>

> [Huck said:](/goto/post?id=117849)
>
> I just recently started (2 days ago) to make sure I got plenty of fats in my diet - coconut oil, organic butter, olive oil). Last summer, I found that adding fats was really slowing down my weight loss, so I cut way back on fats. When Fall started, I should have started increasing fats, but didn't think of it because I was feeling so good.

It was especially the medium-chain triglycerides that I was thinking might be helpful. I just checked:

> MCTs passively diffuse from the GI tract to the portal system (longer fatty acids are absorbed into the lymphatic system) without requirement for modification like long-chain fatty acids or very-long-chain fatty acids. In addition, MCTs do not require bile salts for digestion. Patients that have malnutrition or malabsorption syndromes are treated with MCTs because they do not require energy for absorption, utilization, or storage.

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medium-chain\_triglyceride](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medium-chain_triglyceride)

IOW, it's maybe easier to get energy out of them than from some other fats, so perhaps they'd be a nice option while transitioning. Maybe a way to get a burst of energy without the downside of carbs.

I've used coconut oil a little, but never MCT oil. That may be more effective. Dave Asprey seems to be a great believer in it. Dave sells it, too, although I don't know I'd buy Dave's own, since his stuff is pretty pricey.

The other thing that might be worth a try is ketone-esthers. Try googling that. It's not something I've tried or know much about, but apparently they're becoming available. Apparently, the military have been experimenting with them for some time, for special forces soldiers that need to get into ketosis quickly. But as I say, I've no direct experience there and don't know if there are downsides -- others here may know more.

Some people recommend L-glutamine for sugar-cravings. I recall that Nora Gedgaudas does. But I don't know Dr. Kruse's opinion on that one. It may be an option temporarily.

### nonchalant — 2014-03-25T16:16:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/post-117862>

Huck, in winter when it was a cloudy day (without precipitation) I'd really notice the loss in energy. You said:

> I have a Philips light that I use for about 15 minutes first thing in the morning during low light season. I don't know if it helps or not. I know there has been some discussion on this board about lights, but I don't remember anything definitive.

Did you notice if you skipped the light session that the rest of that day was adversely affected? I doubt anything would be as good as the sun, but I think you should feel the absence of the light session.

Lately I've noticed that a cloudy day doesn't affect me as much. I started to pat myself on the back, but then I realized that the sun is stronger now, and the days aren't as cold. I don't spend much energy staying warm throughout the day. So I'm not becoming a superstar, my environment is. ;-) Still, I think someday I'll have more energy reserves available so that a cloudy day or two won't set me back.

Also, on cloudy days this winter I used a red led light in the nose for a few minutes. No, it didn't seem to help, but red light doesn't have much strength. Plus I know I have poor redox, so the local energy in the nose wouldn't be transferred far.

### BJK77 — 2014-03-25T16:38:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/post-117864>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=117594)
>
> inflammation = low redox = loss of energy = weight gain.

> [Huck said:](/goto/post?id=117833)
>
> I don't really understand all this stuff in any detail, but here's what I am wondering.
>
> We need electrons and/or photons. For some of us, our bodies are well adapted to using photons. By getting plenty of sun in high light seasons, our energy levels increase - we lose weight, hormones normalize, brain works better, etc.
>
> But once the sun gets too low in the sky to give us all the photons we need for energy, we have to rely on electrons. And for some of us, the pathway(s) to gather and use electrons for energy is inefficient at best. There is a mismatch between photon processing and electron processing. What I am currently trying to do is to extend the high light season for at least a month in the Fall and start it at least a month earlier in the Spring. That still leaves at least 4 months of low light I have to survive.
>
> **Just exposing myself to electrons doesn't seem to be enough. I need to know what I need to do to get my body to efficiently absorb and use electrons. While a ketogenic diet, good water, and grounding provide some stability, they don't produce even close to the same energy level as photons do (for me).**
>
> So as I see my options:
>
> 1.  Move to where I can get plenty of photons year round
> 2.  Use a light source to generate photons
> 3.  Improve body function to better absorb and utilize electrons
>
> Option 1 is fairly expensive, but right now is the best option.
>
> Option 2 is a bit of unknown. What is available that works?
>
> I have a Philips light that I use for about 15 minutes first thing in the morning during low light season. I don't know if it helps or not. I know there has been some discussion on this board about lights, but I don't remember anything definitive.
>
> Option 3 is probably the best option, IF I understood what it is that I need to do. This is where I need some suggestions.

I could have written your post Huck . . . EXCEPT that I lose weight instead of gain which I have yet to understand. I wonder if it isn't due to my extremely inflamed gut that just cannot absorb any nutrients and I'm basically starving over the winter months despite how much and the quality of the foods that I eat.

I do believe I am much more sensitive to the effects of photons than electrons like you said. Whether this is due to me being unable to "access" the electron pathway or something else, I don't know, but despite all I do with drinking tons of good quality water, eating ketogenic, earthing, etc, etc I find myself getting worse and worse. I made great progress this past summer when I was sure to get plenty of sunlight and of course as I mentioned many times on the forum, I CRASHED and crashed hard as soon as summer ended even though my diet was still very keto and I was doing everything else.

It's getting clearer and clearer to me that I need to make the leap and take option #1 that you mentioned above. We've been actively working toward that for a while now, but then I wonder if I need a deep winter to truly heal, how do I do that somewhere far enough south where I'm getting plenty of sunshine? I know there's always an ice machine, of course, but there's no "natural" cold. I don't know if that makes a difference or not. Would getting the year-round photons just be a band-aid over my problem or will it actualy heal my body? I don't know.

### Huck — 2014-03-25T16:41:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-suggestions-huge-weight-gain.10300/post-117865>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=117856)

I use NOW MCT oil. I add it to my protein shake.

Apparently, the military have been experimenting with them for some time, for special forces soldiers that need to get into ketosis quickly. Haven't tried it. Will look into it.

> Some people recommend L-glutamine for sugar-cravings. I recall that Nora Gedgaudas does. But I don't know Dr. Kruse's opinion on that one. It may be an option temporarily.

I've tried it in past with no success. Stopped because I read that cancer likes it almost as much as glucose.

Thanks.

# Thread: Am I ready for the ice?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/am-i-ready-for-the-ice.10304/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Infinusss — 2014-03-24T20:17:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/am-i-ready-for-the-ice.10304/post-117644>

Today was my first day of CT. I ate my BAB, waited an hour and a half, then had 24oz of water from the fridge. I have one of those temperature guns that Jack suggested. I filled up the sink, the water was already 53.7F straight out of the faucet. Right off the bat I was able to hold my face under water for as long as I could hold my breath. I did this 6-8 times right in a row, and my skin temp wouldn't drop below 64F. Does this mean that I do well with the cold and that I'm already good to start with 20-40lbs of ice on my chest?

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-24T21:04:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/am-i-ready-for-the-ice.10304/post-117668>

Nice. Step it up now.

### nonchalant — 2014-03-24T21:24:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/am-i-ready-for-the-ice.10304/post-117675>

Hi Infinusss, congrats! I know when I finally put my face in ice water, I had to wonder what all my previous fuss was about! It isn't difficult like I thought it would be. I dunked my face for a week before taking it further, but I'm a wimp!

### Infinusss — 2014-03-27T04:20:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/am-i-ready-for-the-ice.10304/post-118208>

I'm a size large, and I just bought a size small compression fit Under Armour shirt. It is uncomfortably tight, hahah. Anything larger, though, and I didn't feel as thought it really compressed my skin much. First day of ice on chest tomorrow! Going to be checking the temp of my skin every five minutes and checking color to make sure that it stays cherry red/pink and doesn't turn white.

### Infinusss — 2014-03-27T18:47:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/am-i-ready-for-the-ice.10304/post-118317>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=117668)
>
> Nice. Step it up now.

Step it up, I did! I put 20lbs of ice on my chest (with really tight Under Armour) this morning and I was able to go the whole hour on my first day! Pink/cherry red chest the whole time and my skin temp never dropped below 50F. Should I continue this for a week or so, or do you think I can jump right over to no shirt and ice?

I'm in my 5th week of my Lepting RX, and I'm thrilled that I've made it this far without major slip-ups (i.e. eating an entire box of cookies on my own) but it seems that my hypoglycemia ins't improving. The first few weeks it was good, I only need 2 meals a day and I was fine. Now I eat breakfast around 8 and by 1 I get the shaky/panicky type hunger that can't be ignored. Could it be that I just need 3 meals with the days growing longer? Could it have something to do with PMS, which I am very much in the midst of? Thoughts?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/night-time-waking-for.10306/post-117671>

peeing.

Ok, I know that is was mentioned that getting up in the middle of the night to use the bathroom meant a sign that something was off but for the life of me I cannot remember what or even what webinar it was in.

The DH is getting up more and more to do this and I am wondering why. I want to give his a good reason why this is happening so maybe he will start to change some of the crap that he is doing.

### Huck — 2014-03-24T21:52:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/night-time-waking-for.10306/post-117684>

One possible reason is an enlarged prostate. The prostate sits right under the bladder, so when the prostate gets bigger it squeezes the bladder. The urethra also runs through the prostate, so it might be "pinched".
