### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-15T00:00:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/march-2014-webinar-promo.10335/post-121506>

And look what shows up today............LOL Tensegrity 1 get a huge boost.

A big piece of a long-standing puzzle—how cells maintain osmotic pressure via volume-regulated anion channels (VRACs)—appears to have been solved, as researchers reported in [_Cell_](http://www.cell.com/cell/abstract/S0092-8674\(14\)00358-4) today (April 10) having identified a key VRAC protein. [Ardem Patapoutian](http://www.scripps.edu/research/faculty/patapoutian) of The Scripps Research Institute in La Jolla, California, and his colleagues found that this protein, which they’ve dubbed “SWELL1,” is a critical component of the osmotic channels that help keep cells from swelling until they explode.

“So far, nobody has been able to identify the proteins involved in mediating this widely-expressed current,” said [Jorg Grandl](http://icru.duhs.duke.edu/grandl/) of Duke University in Durham, North Carolina, who was not involved in this work. “To have a first report about one molecule that’s likely forming an ion channel that is mediating this current is a major step forward.”

Study coauthor [Zhaozhu Qiu](http://www.scripps.edu/patapoutian/people.html), a postdoctoral researcher in Patapoutian’s lab, first encountered VRACs while attending a conference talk that described how, for three decades, researchers have struggled to identify the proteins involved in the channels. VRACs keep the osmotic pressure of cells in check, using anions like chloride or iodide to generate an electrophysiological pulse when they’re open. These channels are ubiquitously expressed, making them difficult to isolate for study. Scientists have suggested several proteins as possible VRAC components, but one after another, each candidate dropped away as it became clear they were not actually involved.

So Qiu and his colleagues devised an assay to identify the proteins involved. They transfected cells with small interfering RNAs (siRNAs) and adapted a yellow fluorescent protein (YFP) to see which genes knocked were down by the siRNAs. Among the 51 genes the researchers homed in on, “there was only one gene that completely abolished the VRAC activity,” said Qiu. “Previously, very little was known of this gene.”

The researchers named the gene _SWELL1_. The same gene had been discovered a decade ago, and linked to B cell development. But little research had been done _SWELL1_ (once known as _LRRC8A_) since then. In 2012, it was found to be related to a class of proteins pannexins.

Using cells in which _SWELL1_ had been knocked down, Qiu and his colleagues found the SWELL1 is required for volume regulation. They also found that the protein was embedded in the cellular membrane in an unexpected way. “People have been thinking that the leucine-rich repeats were on the outside,” said Qiu, “However, the topology we found \[indicated\] they were on the inside.”

SWELL1 appears to form a pore-like structure, although it’s unlikely that the protein is responsible for channel function on its own. And overexpressing _SWELL1_ didn’t increase VRAC activity in the cell, the researchers found.

The identification of _SWELL1_ could impact biomedical research, said [Geoffrey Abbott](http://www.faculty.uci.edu/profile.cfm?faculty_id=5869) of the University of California, Irvine, who was not involved in the work. “Now that the gene is known, it opens the floodgate for geneticists and people interested in certain diseases to say ‘OK, let us start searching for human mutations in this gene that might be associated with disease states,’” he said.

Given the history of VRAC protein research, some ion channel researchers are cautious. “While very exciting, this excitement is tempered by the knowledge that several other protein candidates have been identified over the last 25 years,” [Peter Brown](http://www.ls.manchester.ac.uk/people/profile/?personid=305) of Manchester University in the U.K. told _The Scientist_ in an e-mail. However, he added, “this study seems more robust than those supporting the claims of the previous pretenders.”

And questions remain as to the molecular mechanisms at play. “It’s not clear how this channel is able to sense osmotic stimuli and then react to them,” said Grandl. “This is very interesting, very novel, but it will still require a lot of work” to fully characterize SWELL1’s role, he added.

Qiu, Patapoutian, and their colleagues now plan to purify the protein and insert it into a lipid bilayer membrane to assess its activities on its own, and with the help of other proteins and activator molecules.

**Z. Qiu et al., “SWELL1, a plasma membrane protein, is an essential component of volume-regulated anion channel,” _Cell,_ 157_:_447–58, 2014.**

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-27T12:22:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/social-botany-project.10339/post-118245>

I just stumbled upon this, looking for something completely different. It sounds like an interesting idea:

> Social Botany is the brain child of Eva Evangelakou, whose dynamism and drive are fast making the project come to life. The basic idea is to fund social botany field trips for marginalised and socially excluded children from inner city London to the olive groves of the Greek island of Lesvos by selling organic extra virgin olive oil from that island. ...
>
> Eva’s vision is wider than simply providing one-off visits to Lesvos. The Social Botany programme will include the involvement of both Greek and British schools in environmental studies, an introduction to the Mediterranean Diet with a donation of olive oil to the schools taking part, “edible” schoolyards growing suitable plants, an annual Olive Tree Festival and plantings, by local children, of olive trees in three inner London parks and there is much more to come. ...

[http://www.judyridgway-oliveoil.org...aden-the-lives-of-london-inner-city-children/](http://www.judyridgway-oliveoil.org.uk/exciting-new-project-using-olive-oil-to-help-broaden-the-lives-of-london-inner-city-children/)

I just thought it was encouraging to know that people were doing something like this.

### sooperb — 2014-03-28T19:51:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/social-botany-project.10339/post-118536>

I love the idea but .......... it harks back a little to what I was chatting to you about earlier, they go, they experience and then they go back to their home environment. Probably full of joy and enthusiasm but I don't think children are able to change much in their home lives. If they are deprived it will be even more difficult. What then will they learn from the trip? There is better out there, olive oil is good for you, Greek Islands are fab. They will struggle to maintain that when they get home and might even cause themselves a problem by talking about it and they might also resent not being able to access that kind of lifestyle. Result frustration, sadness, who knows.

I have real life experience of trying to help a child from a deprived background, or rather my daughter does. She wanted to help a child that had been friends with her daughters and whose homelife was pretty grim so she worked at it and managed to get her a place in the same school her girls go to. Initially the girl was thrilled, less than a year later though it's all gone belly up, the girl has driven a wedge between my two granddaughters, behaves badly and is on a very slippery slope to expulsion. On top of that she is dragging another girl down with her, they both go out drinking and messing with boys, both are about 14/15. A sad end for her and a lesson learned by my daughter. I think the problem is that the girl cannot handle the two distinct social settings she has to try to exist in, her home with a dreadful mother and 4 siblings where bettering yourself and doing well is not on the agenda, and mixing at school with children, a lot of whose parents are loaded and who have a better set of morals and values. I don't blame the girl, I feel terribly sad that something she said she wanted more than anything has turned out to be such a trial for her.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-29T10:40:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/social-botany-project.10339/post-118640>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=118536)
>
> I love the idea but .......... it harks back a little to what I was chatting to you about earlier, they go, they experience and then they go back to their home environment. Probably full of joy and enthusiasm but I don't think children are able to change much in their home lives. If they are deprived it will be even more difficult. What then will they learn from the trip? There is better out there, olive oil is good for you, Greek Islands are fab. They will struggle to maintain that when they get home and might even cause themselves a problem by talking about it and they might also resent not being able to access that kind of lifestyle. Result frustration, sadness, who knows. ...

Yes, of course, Sue. All that. And I'm not surprised that someone's attempt to help could backfire on them, as you relate later.

But the second paragraph I quoted there does mention quite a few other activities besides the trip.

> ... involvement of both Greek and British schools in environmental studies, an introduction to the Mediterranean Diet ... “edible” schoolyards growing suitable plants, an annual Olive Tree Festival and plantings ... and much more to come ...

It seems to me that if all of this leaves some awareness of farming and gardening and cooking, and that bears fruit at some point in even a few children, then it will have been worth doing.

I heard somewhere -- probably on a podcast -- that an art teacher in the U.S. used to set as an assignment every year "draw a cooking pot". Because everyone would have a cooking pot. They would be asked to bring one in and draw in it the classroom. Year by year there began to be children who didn't bring one, didn't _have_ one at home. According to what the interviewee said to her interlocutor it got to the point where _no_ child brought one in. Maybe she was exaggerting, maybe she wasn't but it was rather that the kids just weren't bothered, but that's how the story went.

The teacher asked them, "Well, what do you cook in at home?" The children looked blank and eventually someone said, "We put the food in the microwave."

I think we have to push back. Anything anyone can do, if it looks to be no more than carrying a grain of sand.

If we wait on Whitehall or the NHS or the USDA ... well, forget that. Not only is there layer upon layer of stupidity -- I bet most of them still beleive that saturated fat "clogs your arteries" -- but as you pointed out recently, a chief government scientific advisor seems to have been bought by Coca-Cola.

### caroline — 2014-03-29T10:48:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/social-botany-project.10339/post-118641>

When my daughter started working - she would bring in food that she had made ....no one seemed to have a clue how to make anything. It seemed so sad to me ....imagine not smelling a pot roast cooking or an apple pie in the oven or bread baking .....it seems to me that used to be the fabric of our life - and a microwave replaced all that wonderful hands on stuff we all did together.

### sooperb — 2014-03-29T11:34:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/social-botany-project.10339/post-118646>

I agree totally Mike with the notion that we should be turning the clock back and bring back the teaching of real life necessities in the classroom. Do they need to learn about different cultures and religions in primary school, couldn't that wait until they are of an age when they could understand it better? The curriculum is way too broad, it should focus on learning the important stuff, literacy, numeracy and how to look after yourself, so cookery, woodwork, growing things (anyone remember growing a broad bean in a glass jar with paper and water?) and lots more.

Since they stopped teaching these things in school so there are more and more families who are simply deficient in life skills. You could theoretically link the lack of such elements in educational settings with the number of families with two working parents, that leaves no one to pass on life skills to the children unless there are grandparents with enough time to fill in. Parents don't have the time or are too tired so there's been a slow, steady drift to convenience food and a trip to the doctors for every issue like nappy rash and simple coughs and colds that we used to deal with at home.

The precursor to today when folk have no idea whether they're ill or just off colour and who never open a book to try to solve problems for themselves. I'm not sure if we can ever turn the tide, there's no willingness by governments and little interest by parents. Which brings me back to one of my previous assertions, that even if you try to improve yourself by turning back the clock, you become the one who's odd, whacky and peculiar.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-29T16:10:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/social-botany-project.10339/post-118655>

> [prAna303 said:](/goto/post?id=118652)
>
> All that surrounds me is trees...

Conifers are actually quite controversial in this country. The Forestry Commission planted a lot between the wars. I believe it was partly because the experience of the Great War led them to think that they might need them for pit-props (coal being seen as vital to any subsequent war that might come upon the nation).

Environmentalists tend to dislike these plantations. Apart from the fact that real woodland isn't planted -- it grows -- they're sometimes called "dead forests". They don't support a lot of wildlife and the needles are quite acidic. People tend to prefer the deciduous woodland, not just for the trees, although those are attractive, but for the plants among it and the insects, birds, small mammals, and so on it supports.

Although the county I live in is fairly populous it's also one of the most wooded in the country. There are a lot of little copses around, many of them probably having served for coppicing in the past -- one can see that the "trees" are actually coppiced stools that have grown out.

Oliver Rackham is our premier writer on woodland and the development of the British landscape:

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/s/ref=ntt\_a...kham&search-alias=books-uk&sort=relevancerank](http://www.amazon.co.uk/s/ref=ntt_athr_dp_sr_1/279-6523773-1452045?_encoding=UTF8&field-author=Oliver%20Rackham&search-alias=books-uk&sort=relevancerank)

I'm afraid I haven't read any of his books, though. Too much to do, too little time.

# Thread: EMF on Wildlife
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-on-wildlife.10341/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-27T16:01:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-on-wildlife.10341/post-118280>

**Dept. of Interior Attacks FCC regarding Adverse Impact of Cell Tower Radiation on Wildlife**

****Monday, March 24, 2014**

**_The Department of Interior charges that the FCC standards for cell phone radiation are outmoded and no longer applicable as they do not adequately protect wildlife._**
The Director of the Office of Environmental Policy and Compliance of the United States Department of the Interior sent a letterto the National Telecommunications and Information Administration in the Department of Commerce which addresses the Interior Department's concern that cell tower radiation has had negative impacts on the health of migratory birds and other wildlife.
The Interior Department accused the Federal government of employing outdated radiation standards set by the Federal Communications Commission (FCC), a federal agency with no expertise in health. The standards are no longer applicable because they control only for overheating and do not protect organisms from the adverse effects of exposure to the low-intensity radiation produced by cell phones and cell towers:
"the electromagnetic radiation standards used by the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) continue to be based on thermal heating, a criterion now nearly 30 years out of date and inapplicable today." The Department criticized the Federal government's proposed procedures for placement and operation of communication towers, andcalled for "independent, third-party peer-reviewed studies" in the U.S. to examine the effects of cell tower radiation on "migratory birds and other trust species."
Following are excerpts from the letter, dated Feb 7, 2014:
"The Department believes that some of the proposed procedures are not consistent with Executive Order 13186 Responsibilities of Federal Agencies to Protect Migratory Birds, which specifically requires federal agencies to develop and use principles, standards, and practices that will lessen the amount of unintentional take reasonably attributed to agency actions. The Department, through the Fish and Wildlife Service (FWS), finds that the proposals lack provisions necessary to conserve migratory bird resources, including eagles. The proposals also do not reflect current information regarding the effects of communication towers to birds. Our comments are intended to further clarify specific issues and address provisions in the proposals.The Department recommends revisions to the proposed procedures to better reflect the impacts to resources under our jurisdiction from communication towers. The placement and operation of communication towers, including un-guyed, unlit, monopole or lattice-designed structures, impact protected migratory birds in two significant ways. The first is by injury, crippling loss, and death from collisions with towers and their supporting guy-wire infrastructure, where present. The second significant issue associated with communication towers involves impacts from non-ionizing electromagnetic radiation emitted by them (See Attachment)."Enclosure A

"The second significant issue associated with communication towers involves impacts from nonionizing electromagnetic radiation emitted by these structures. Radiation studies at cellular communication towers were begun circa 2000 in Europe and continue today on wild nesting birds. Study results have documented nest and site abandonment, plumage deterioration, locomotion problems, reduced survivorship, and death (e.g., Balmori 2005, Balmori and Hallberg 2007, and Everaert and Bauwens 2007). Nesting migratory birds and their offspring have apparently been affected by the radiation from cellular phone towers in the 900 and 1800 MHz frequency ranges- 915 MHz is the standard cellular phone frequency used in the United States. However, **the electromagnetic radiation standards used by the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) continue to be based on thermal heating, a criterion now nearly 30 years out of date and inapplicable today. This is primarily due to the lower levels of radiation output from microwave-powered communication devices such as cellular telephones and other sources of point-to-point communications; levels typically lower than from microwave ovens. The problem, however, appears to focus on very low levels of non-ionizing electromagnetic radiation.** For example, in laboratory studies, T. Litovitz (personal communication) and DiCarlo et al. (2002) raised concerns about impacts of low-level, non-thermal electromagnetic radiation from the standard 915 MHz cell phone frequency on domestic chicken embryos- with some lethal results (Manville 2009, 2013a). Radiation at extremely low levels (0.0001 the level emitted by the average digital cellular telephone) caused heart attacks and the deaths of some chicken embryos subjected to hypoxic conditions in the laboratory while controls subjected to hypoxia were unaffected (DiCarlo et al. 2002). To date, no independent, third-party field studies have been conducted in North America on impacts of tower electromagnetic radiation on migratory birds. With the European field and U.S. laboratory evidence already available, independent, third-party peer-reviewed studies need to be conducted in the U.S. to begin examining the effects from radiation on migratory birds and other trust species."

Radiation Impacts and Categorical Exclusions

"There is a growing level of anecdotal evidence linking effects of non-thermal, non-ionizing electromagnetic radiation from communication towers on nesting and roosting wild birds and other wildlife in the U.S. Independent, third-party studies have yet to be conducted in the U.S. or Canada, although a peer-reviewed research protocol developed for the U.S. Forest Service by the Service's Division of Migratory Bird Management is available to study both collision and radiation impacts (Manville 2002). As previously mentioned, Balmori (2005) found strong negative correlations between levels of tower-emitted microwave radiation and bird breeding, nesting, and roosting in the vicinity of electromagnetic fields in Spain. He documented nest and site abandonment, plumage deterioration, locomotion problems, reduced survivorship, and death in House Sparrows, White Storks, Rock Doves, Magpies, Collared Doves, and other species. Though these species had historically been documented to roost and nest in these areas, Balmori (2005) did not observe these symptoms prior to construction and operation of the cellular phone towers. Balmori and Hallberg (2007) and Everaert and Bauwens (2007) found similar strong negative correlations among male House Sparrows. Under laboratory 'conditions, DiCarlo et al. (2002) raised troubling concerns about impacts of low-level, non-thermal electromagnetic radiation from the standard 915 MHz cell phone frequency on domestic chicken embryos- with some lethal results (Manville 2009). **Given the findings of the studies mentioned above, field studies should be conducted in North America to validate potential impacts of communication tower radiation both direct and indirect - to migratory birds and other trust wildlife species."**The full text of the letter, the addendum and citations are available at: [http://1.usa.gov/1jn3CZg](http://1.usa.gov/1jn3CZg)

​

**

# Thread: Do raw foods improve redox potential through hydride ions?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-raw-foods-improve-redox-potential-through-hydride-ions.10343/>
**Subforum:** Redox Rx
**Posts:** 51

### Melanie Procter — 2014-03-27T16:24:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-raw-foods-improve-redox-potential-through-hydride-ions.10343/post-118286>

Hi,

There is a product called Mega Hydrate created by Dr. Patrick Flanagan. The product is called hydride ions which are supposed to have an additional unpaired electron, making them potent anti oxidants. I am guessing these would improve redox potential.

Dr. Flanagan stated raw foods and fresh cold pressed raw juices are full of hydride ions and thus raw food and juices would improve redox potential. I wonder if this is accurate.

Mel

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-27T21:45:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-raw-foods-improve-redox-potential-through-hydride-ions.10343/post-118353>

Hydride? If you understand Ling and Pollack's work on water and charge separation you will understand that Flanagan is a farce. He clearly is assuming the reader knows nothing about physics and about water chemistry.
Water becomes a hydride only in an alloy with a metal. In life water is charge separates into -OH and hydronium ions which are also called protons.

A **hydride** is the [anion](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anion) of [hydrogen](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrogen), H−.

I was understanding it from Flanagan's concept of "hydride ions". He stated that raw food contained these "hydride ions" which had additional "unpaired electrons". ### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-30T07:41:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-raw-foods-improve-redox-potential-through-hydride-ions.10343/post-118769>

Eating raw seafood like oysters and shrimp and organ meat (like Inger does) contains the most prebiotic glycans. Organ meat is particularly good because it has the most prebiotics because it has the most collagen in it. It also has massive amounts of CoEnQ10. When the meat is freshly killed like would be the case in human history prior to agriculture, or what takes place in the African plains today with carnivores allows for optimal gut function. This is why in my opinion zoo animals get obese........it has zero to do with them being contained........it has to do with the lack of raw carnivorous substrate to keep the gut microbiome natural and not weighted to the obesogenic template that allows us to loses electrons constantly from food. When you marry this to a the confinement and lack of sun and good water and city living it should be no wonder why zoo animals can not outlive their wild cohorts. It is common sense when you have my perspective.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-30T07:47:21+0300

This is why modern cooking has downsides. It is even why bone broths and seafood exoskeleton broths have downsides. We need to realize eating the tough sinew and tendons of fresh animals allows for the glycans and GAG's to go undigested and make it to our distal gut to feed the critters there to keep the colon healthy by changing these things to butyrate. Eating butter is a modern Rx around what we should be doing. We need to eat that collagen and the April 2014 webinar will explain why.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-30T07:54:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-raw-foods-improve-redox-potential-through-hydride-ions.10343/post-118771>

Mastication and chewing in carnivores is not favored by evolution for a deep reason (depletes deuterium from brain).........think about great white's, lions, hyena's and crocodiles and gators.........they all swallow their food whole with little chewing. they all eat a deuterium free diet. The Evolutionary Rx might be for those predators who rely on their sensory systems for life that they need an electron dense diet that requires Less mastication to process the food. Humans have herbivore teeth, a short gut, a stomach with low pH and this makes them to need to chew more to pound the deuterium of of them.

This is why I eat oysters raw and do not chew them. I believe we need to eat them whole to deliver more food less digested to the distal gut which is underdeveloped in us for a deep reason.........in our colon is where SCFA are made and where massive amounts of water is resorbed and deuterium is depleted more. This is the mark of energy conservation in humans in my belief paradigm. this explains why our teeth are not like other carnivores too.........they are less developed for tearing and cutting and makes mastication mandatory so we deliver more undigested pieces of food to stomach low pH and our colon for processing. Having been a dentist too I always tried to make sense of the difference in human teeth from other carnivores. Deuterium fractionation is why It exists in my opinion.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-30T08:04:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-raw-foods-improve-redox-potential-through-hydride-ions.10343/post-118774>

Humans possess grinding teeth like herbivorous animals who also have longer intestines than carnivorous animals. Many clueless vegans and vegetarians believe this finding shows that humans are best adapted for eating like a rabbit or engaging in vegetarianism. This argument fails to note several human physiological features which clearly indicate a design for animal product consumption. First and foremost is our stomach's production of hydrochloric acid, something not found in herbivores. HCL activates protein-splitting enzymes. Further, the human pancreas manufactures a full range of digestive enzymes to handle a wide variety of foods, both animal and vegetable based.

But the occlusal surfaces of our human teeth appear to be a big mismatch until you realize why the gut shrunk in humans from chimps........if you digest your food less well you deliver more prebiotics to the smaller colon to extract as many electrons from food and absorb all the water you can. In fact, the human colon major function is water resorption and not SCFA production. The reason? Protons carry potential energy that can do massive amounts of cellular work. This is why mitochondria constantly make protons in all life forms.

### Josh — 2014-03-30T19:35:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-raw-foods-improve-redox-potential-through-hydride-ions.10343/post-118836>

Some fun for Sunday....

> It was on the thirteenth day of a tiring journey, during which we spent the nights in the huts of Persian or Kurd shepherds or in small settlements, that we finally reached the village where this dervish lived.
> We were directed to his house, which was some distance beyond the village. We immediately made our way there and found him near his house in the shade of some trees, where he usually spoke with the people who came to him.
> We saw a man of fairly advanced age, dressed in rags, barefoot and seated cross-legged on the ground. Near him sat a number of young Persians who, as we discovered later, were his pupils. We approached, asked his benediction, and also seated ourselves on the ground, forming a half-circle round him. Our conversation began.
> We put questions and he answered us, and in his turn he asked us questions.
> At first he received us rather coldly and showed little inclination to talk, but later, when he found that we had come a considerable distance especially to talk with him, he became more cordial. He expressed himself very simply, in unpolished language, and at the beginning gave the impression, at least to me personally, of being an ignorant man, that is to say, uneducated in the European sense of the word.
> The conversation with the dervish was conducted in Persian, but in a particular dialect which none of our company knew except myself, Dr. Sari-Ogli and one other who was not very fluent in it. Consequently Sari-Ogli and I asked the questions, immediately translating all that was said for the benefit of the others.
> It was dinner-time. A pupil came bringing the dervish his food—rice in a bowl made out of a gourd. Continuing the conversation, the dervish began to eat. As we had eaten nothing
> since we had risen and started on our way early in the morning, we opened our knapsacks and began to eat also.
> I must remind you that at that time I was an ardent follower of the famous Indian yogis and carried out very exactly all the indications of what is called Hatha Yoga, and when eating I tried to masticate my food as thoroughly as possible. So, long after everyone, including the old man, had finished their simple meal, I continued slowly eating, trying not to swallow a single morsel without masticating it according to all the rules.
> Seeing this, the dervish asked me: 'Tell me, young stranger, why are you eating like that?'
> I was so sincerely astonished by this question—which seemed to me very strange and to say not very much for his knowledge—that I even had no desire to reply to him, and thought that we had made such a long detour in vain, to meet a man who was not worth talking with seriously. Looking into his eyes, I felt not only pity but also ashamed for him, and replied with self-assurance that I chewed my food carefully so that it might be better assimilated in the intestines, and, referring to the wellknown fact that properly digested food gives the organism a larger quantity of calories necessary for all our functions, I repeated all that I had extracted from various books on the subject.
> Shaking his head, the old man slowly and with conviction uttered the following saying which is known throughout Persia:
> Let God kill him who himself does not know and yet presumes to show
> others the way to the doors of His Kingdom.
> After that Sari-Ogli put a question to the dervish which he answered briefly. He then turned again to me and asked:
> 'Tell me, young stranger, you probably also do gymnastics?'
> I was in fact working very hard at gymnastics at that time and although I knew all the methods recommended by the Indian Yogis, I kept to the system of the Swede, Mueller. I told the dervish that I did work at gymnastics and considered it necessary to practise twice a day, morning and evening, and I explained briefly the kind of exercises I was doing.
> 'This is only for the development of arms, legs and in general
> the external muscles,' said the old man, 'but you have also inner muscles which are not affected at all by your mechanical movements.'
> 'Yes, certainly,' said I.
> 'Good. Let us now return to your way of chewing your food,' the old man continued. 'If you chew in this way as a means to health or for the sake of other attainments, then I shall have to say, if you would like to know my sincere opinion, that you have chosen the worst possible way. By chewing your food so carefully you reduce the work of your stomach. Now you are young and everything is all right, but you are accustoming your stomach to do nothing; and when you are older, owing to the lack of normal work, your muscles will be to a certain extent atrophied. And that is bound to occur if you continue this system of chewing. You know that our muscles and body get weaker in old age. Now, in addition to the natural weaknesses of old age, you will have another brought on by yourself, because you are accustoming your stomach not to work. Can you imagine how it will be then?
> 'On the contrary, it is not at all necessary to masticate carefully. At your age it is better not to chew at all, but to swallow whole pieces, even bones if possible, to give work to your stomach. I can see that those who have advised you to practise this mastication, and also those who write books about it, have, as is said, "heard a bell without knowing where the sound came from".'
> These simple, obvious and consistent words of the old man made me completely change my first opinion of him. Until then I had put questions to him out of curiosity, but from that moment I felt a serious interest in him, and began to listen with the greatest attention to everything he said.
> Suddenly I understood with the whole of my being that ideas I had hitherto accepted as indisputable truths were incorrect. I realized that up till then I had seen things only from one side. Now many things appeared in quite a new light. Hundreds of new questions arose in my mind concerning this subject.

Gurdjieff, "Meetings With Remarkable Men"

### Tanya — 2014-04-03T23:10:18+0300

At the risk of completely getting pulverized here, I need to ask this: while discussion here is primarily about raw food, I have a different concern regarding what Jack said about the vegetables. To quote the master himself:

> Veggies are just not that critical to humans.....

Here is what he wrote about the importance of potassium in his Redox Rx blog:

> 1.  **Potassium levels tell you about the relationships to ATP.** For every 0.3 mEq below 3.8 mEq that potassium is on a standard blood lab draw, means there is 100 mEq deficit inside a cell. The atomic size and its redox potential is huge for potassium “gluing of water” for it to function as the optimal electrical adapter to transfer energy throughout the cell coherently. ATP is designed to unfold proteins fully to open their carbonyl and imino side chain groups on all amino acids to intracellular water. This action allows binding and polarization to separate water into subatomic particles that are positively and negatively charged. **This action is called building or expanding the exclusion zone (EZ) of water.**

So, how else without taking a supplement can I have enough, potassium or vitamin c, for example, without consuming the vegetables?

> I am happy to hear there is a benefit to less chewing, which I am usually too tired for.
>
> And I am so jealous of all the folks who can do raw - especially meats...my GI tract would not be happy with that...but I hope some day.

Have you tried raw good quality meat and what happened Hope? I believe raw is easier to digest.. Even better is when you let the meat rot or mature a bit (high meat or air dried) that way i is a bit pre digested.

Do not be sad about not being able to do gardening.... I like wild edibles way better than the cultivated ones.. and they are way more nutritious too! Who need veggies..lol I do gardening here for my mom and family primarely. I myself like the wild stuff way better and it is way less work too
Now you have more time to go out in the woods and forage nettles and stuff - you should try to make nettle soup. I prefer nettle smoothies or just raw and plain but my family loves when I make them nettle soup! Just pick lots of nettles, use the leaves, fry up a onion in butter and add water + nettles, cook in water until soft. Add some nutmeg, HWC, salt, fresh ground black pepper.. butter... puree the soup. Cook eggs hard boiled. Serve egg halves together with the soup. yum (if you want it even way more healthier do just nettle smoothie instead, like I do  )

>
> Wild edibles...! They have huge amounts of potassium, and so much other good stuff too! You should eat some every day all summer long, seriously... you need no veggies!

THIS IS ALL FOR FREE    They are so potent you need just a tiny bit! Say... a handful a day or so - or even less, sure you can eat more too. I just pick and eat a few leaves here and there
> They have vit C too. Wild berries have lots of them, picked directly from bush. The best thing is... you got to be out in the woods too
>
> Chlorella have lots of potassium too... and should help detox metals and stuff. Dr Bonlie from Magnetico recommends it when you start sleeping on the magnetico - to help the detox. I figure it cannot hurt? If you get a good quality one. Seaweed and wild edibles would do too... i bet!

Feel a bit out of place interrupting this bike conversation

Hi Inger, I read what you said and thought to myself how lucky she is to be able to do things she does. It is not the only time that I felt this way about you and many other people here.

What you suggested earlier sounds really great( and I thank you for doing that, my friend) until I start looking at it from a personal and practical point of view. Considering my situation, it is really a judgement call for me - how much risk am I willing to take with something that might affect me in adverse way and still be able to bounce back. If the cons outweigh the pros, it becomes out of the question for me.

With the exception of nettles (btw, I loved your recipe for them) and dandelions (which I can purchase), my preliminary research leads me to believe that I can not have any other wild edibles in this area.

Yes, I also heard Dr. Bonlie talk about chlorella, but I know for a fact that not everyone can have that either. I am one of those people. It is because its cell membrane contains lipopolysaccharide that can (and in my case it actually did so) increase inflammation and stimulate Th1 cells.

I don’t take any potassium supplement, never had and don’t intend to. I was getting all of it from the organic vegetables that are available to me here. However, I still take many other supplement (even though not as many as I used to prior to Epi-Paleo) and my goal is to eventually to have even lee so.

For now, I just envy you and the others who don’t need to be as careful as I must be, and hope that one day will come that I could do the same.

I hope though that it will not stop you from keeping to share all your great tips - you are such a wonderful role model for me to follow. (I wish we had a real heart emoticon here)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-27T18:36:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insulin-makes-you-fat-or-is-it.10344/post-118307>

It is insulin:glucagon ratio you need to consider, not the insulin level alone. Think of food not as metabolic fuel but as hormone information. I think you have all heard me say that on occasion.

Many can lose weight drinking tons of artificial whey protein; in fact, it is one of the most insulinogenic protein concoctions there is. Guess what the paleo guys forget to tell you about their fake whey scoops? You lose weight because it increases glucagon. What does glucagon do? It resorbs energy in the form of protons. It stops energy loss generated from water.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15181098](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15181098)

If you pull a Jaminet move of adding "safe starches", like adding a bowl of rice to that whey drink, you'll get fatter than a house. Brown rice makes no difference either. Food is not metabolic fuel.......it is hormone information. Become aware of how clueless some advice really is.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-27T19:47:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jockey-regrows-finger-after-horse-bites-it-off.10347/post-118334>

It turns out to be just the tip of the finger. Still, impressive enough.

I heard of this with children in Robert O. Becker's book, but this is in an adult:

> When a horse bit off the tip of his finger, Paul Halpern thought he would have to go under the knife for a full amputation.
>
> But Dr. Eugenio Rodriguez from Delray Beach, Fla., who treated the 33-year-old, had a different plan. He made a mold of Halpern’s finger using a pig’s bladder tissue and attached it to his hand. Over time, the finger started to regenerate ...

[http://www.ibtimes.com/paul-halpern...r-regenerates-jockeys-finger-using-pig-tissue](http://www.ibtimes.com/paul-halpern-regrows-finger-after-horse-bite-doctor-regenerates-jockeys-finger-using-pig-tissue)

### Josh — 2014-03-28T05:20:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-chernobyl-wildlife-film-radiation-adaptation.10346/post-118417>

From the CPC 8 Blog Comments:

> Actually Mark..........modern conifers evolved after the KT event. They have not been around a long time. They first evolved 300-256 million years ago. The first exploded in the "Great Dying" extinction but they really blossomed to what they are today after the KT event. The KT event was the key to their explosion because of the interaction of water chemistry and their sensitivity to fire to cause their explosive expansion. The infra red heat causes them to spread rapidly. The asteroid caused their spread around our globe in the Northern hemisphere because this hemisphere was affected by the fires and put into a climatologic winter for many decades that fueled their evolution and natural selection because they were able to return entropy back to the altered environment after the KT event. I tend to know a lot about these trees because I happen to live in the pine belt because these trees mitigate EMF best of any life form on the planet today. This is ironically why you think they helped at Chernobyl. Which occurred on March 26th in 1986 ironically. I have actually biohacked this product and found it to be ineffective. I used three different types several years ago. I have found other mTOR polyphenols that work with SIRT 6 to be far better. So I do not share your enthusiasm for this particular product. I do however, share your enthusiasm for conifers. They are great to have around us in our modern world. They are the dominant plants over huge areas of land; especially in the boreal forests of the northern hemisphere where they create huge amounts of oxygen in the tundra that surrounds the planet. They are also present in similar cool climates in mountains further south but since the southern hemisphere was more devastated in the KT event they are not as common there. I covered this in my book ironically. Boreal conifers have many wintertime adaptations because they embraced thermoplastic adaptations that are all based in Gerald Pollack's work on water. The narrow conical shape of northern conifers, and their downward-drooping limbs, help them shed heavy snowfall. Many of them seasonally alter their biochemistry to make them more resistant to freezing, called "hardening". Tropical rainforests have more biodiversity and turnover of conifer's but they are not the main tree in these areas. The immense conifer forests of the world represent the largest terrestrial carbon sink on this planet where carbon from atmospheric CO2 is bound as organic compounds. The biggest carbon sink is found in the oceans in methane clathrates. In closing........the trees alive are far better than any supplement.

### Andrea — 2014-03-28T19:44:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-chernobyl-wildlife-film-radiation-adaptation.10346/post-118535>

Food for thought...
The disaster at Chernobyl-4 occured on April 26, 1986.
Chernobyl-1 was in operation until 1996. Chernobyl-2 was shut down in 1991. Chernobyl-3 was shut down in 2000.

Obviously, people have been working on the site, with "normal operations" of the other reactors, as if nothing had happened all along...

[http://www.prisonplanet.com/america...ocking-extent-of-electropollution-damage.html](http://www.prisonplanet.com/americans-brains-being-fried-by-cell-towers-new-scientific-evidence-reveals-shocking-extent-of-electropollution-damage.html)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-28T16:59:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uggg-these-power-lines-are-killing-me.10352/post-118478>

(NaturalNews) Exposure to cell phone towers alters brain function in alarming ways, causing a lack of concentration, irritability, difficulty sleeping and lack of appetite. That's the conclusion of [a new study just published by the British Medical Journal](http://bmjopen.bmj.com/content/3/12/e003836.full).(1)

The study, authored by Professor Enrique A Navarro, concluded that **the severity of such symptoms directly correlated to cell tower exposure levels**. In other words, the closer a person lives to a cell tower, the greater the severity of their symptoms. This was true regardless of race, income level and other demographics.

Cell towers, of course, broadcast and receive electromagnetic switching signals. Human biology -- and the brain in particular -- relies on electro-biochemical pathways for healthy function. Many scientists have long suspected that chronic exposure to low levels of EMF pollution (electropollution) may interfere with healthy functioning of the brain and body. This latest research adds yet more support to that alarming idea.

**It's not your imagination: Electromagnetic hypersensitivity is real**

Electromagnetic hypersensitivity has long been dismissed as non-existent by some doctors and industry-funded scientists. After all, if EMF pollution from cell towers really does harm public health, then the implications are truly massive, both economically and in terms of human suffering.

But **electromagnetic hypersensitivity is a genuine phenomenon**. People are not "inventing" side effects or symptoms. As [Navarro writes in the study](http://bmjopen.bmj.com/content/3/12/e003836.full):

_The term electromagnetic hypersensitivity has been recently introduced in discussions attributing symptoms to exposure to EMFs. A review of this topic in 2010 found that 8 of the 10 studies evaluated through PubMed had reported increased prevalence of adverse neurobehavioral symptoms or cancer in populations living at distances < 500 m from \[cell phone towers\]._

Importantly, all these symptoms were recorded in people living near [cell phone](http://www.naturalnews.com/cell_phone.html) towers whose broadcast signal strength **meets current safety guidelines**. As the study author points out, this most likely means current government guidelines on cell phone towers are **inadequate to protect the public**. Revising such guidelines could have drastic implications for the nationwide telecommunications infrastructure.

By the way, people who live fewer than 500 meters from cell phone towers appear to be especially at risk of electromagnetic interference with brain function. Because [electropollution](http://www.naturalnews.com/electropollution.html) strength is determined by the inverse square of the distance, a person who moves twice as close to a cell tower experiences **four times the radiation**.

**190,000 cell phone towers and growing**
There are currently over 190,000 cell phone towers across the United States.(2)

Their typical "maximum range" is over 21 miles, meaning their electromagnetic pollution extends in a sphere with a radius of over 21 miles. (In reality, this pollution extends indefinitely, but the intensity of it drops off with the square of the distance.)

The following map shows AT&T coverage areas in orange. **If you live inside an orange area, you are currently exposed to cell tower radiation**.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

People who live within range of two or more [cell phone towers](http://www.naturalnews.com/cell_phone_towers.html) experience electropollution from **all the towers within a range of 21 miles**. This electropollution effect is cumulative.

It is not known how many Americans live within 21 miles of at least one cell tower, but given that over half the U.S. population lives in urban areas, it's safe to assume that at least 150 million -- and more likely close to 300 million -- Americans are exposed to EMF electropollution from cell towers.

**Modern society increasingly confused, irritable and sleepless**
Have you noticed how the mass public seems increasingly confused and irritable? A society that once operated with some degree of sanity and politeness has become largely demented and rude. Mathematical abilities are nearly lost across the population, as very few people under the age of 40 can even calculate 15% waiter's tips at a restaurant. The ability of voters to understand laws, liberties, freedom and even the structure of government is almost entirely lost in nations where cell phone towers are ubiquitous.

Given this recent research revealing the negative impact of cell phone radiation of human brain function, it would be incredibly irresponsible to fail to consider how cell tower radiation alters healthy brain function and promotes confusion and irritability. As more scientists look into this issue, we may indeed find that the **fall of American civilization** is being accelerated by electromagnetic pollution that leads to disastrous cognitive consequences across the population.

### sjoshua — 2014-03-28T17:43:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/okay-tell-me-this.10360/post-118502>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=118485)
>
> I really need to figure out how to compress my updated pics so I can post it here.... you know the one from my site - with me laughing....

I usually just use windows paint to do this. If you open your pic in paint, and click "resize" on the menu bar a new box pops up offering resize/skew. You want to resize by pixels. If you only modify one of the numbers (horizontal OR vertical), then the program will automacially scale the other variable to "maintain aspect ratio" as long as that is selected. This means that it wont make your picture all distorted.

JK forums suggests an avatar "at least 200 x 200 pixels", so try to scale your image close to that size.

If you use a mac instead of a PC there is likely a similar program but I don't know it. You could try a site like this to make it easy for you: [http://www.picresize.com/](http://www.picresize.com/)

### caroline — 2014-03-29T00:31:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/okay-tell-me-this.10360/post-118564>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=118450)
>
> I think it looks a little like Audrey's waiting for something. I'm not sure why -- maybe because her mouth is slightly open.
>
> Perhaps she's actually waiting for the shutter-release to go. But it does give her an antipatory air.
>
> It's a nice picture, Audrey. The vibrant colours are appealing. The sky's tending almost to violet, and the skin tones are caught well.

you are right Mike ..... I was waiting for the shutter to go! but I do think I am full of anticipation too...... I hadn't really noticed the sky so much...... you are a very observant man! I should lose the sunglasses I guess - I don't normally wear them - well - for reading on my laptop. I put them on there because I was squinting too much!

To me - you do look like a tourist ..... but more in the sense ...... an observer of life. I think you take everything in - all the detail that a lot of us miss.

### caroline — 2014-03-29T00:42:01+0200

Sue - you pretty much nailed that too ..... I am very shy. In a crowd of people I don't know - I fade into the wall paper ...... I hate that! It isn't exactly shy I don't think - I have been thinking about this lately. I have a soft personality and people sometimes take that for .... no one is home/nothing going on. I really shrink around very aggressive personalities or negative people or very loud people. I suppose I don't have much presence?
my DH was quiet .... but had a great deal of presence! Our kids would never have crossed the line with him .... but walk all over me.
How did this turn into a confessional .... things always go weird for me in a thread ......WTH

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-29T10:14:52+0200

> [cantweight said:](/goto/post?id=118489)
>
> Mike I thought tourist too! Like you were on safari!

That's it! The second comment.

You'd actually not find anything larger than a badger where that was taken. But when the friend who took it sent it to me, I immediately thought, "_Looks like I'm on Safari._"

It amused me so much I've used it as my address card on the Mac ever since. I'm nothing like Allan Quartermain in real life, of course.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-29T10:22:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/okay-tell-me-this.10360/post-118639>

> [sjoshua said:](/goto/post?id=118502)
>
> If you use a mac instead of a PC there is likely a similar program but I don't know it.

I generally use an old version of Adobe Lightroom, but iPhoto should do that. Just drop the original photo onto its icon in the Dock. It's finding where they put the options to export from iPhoto that might take a minute. IIRC, it's not "share" (that's for exporting to particular sites) but "export" which is under one of the menu items on the left -- "File" perhaps.

You can open an image in Preview.app and save out of that in a number of formats, too.

# Thread: March 26, 2014 update
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/march-26-2014-update.10364/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-28T17:00:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/march-26-2014-update.10364/post-118480>

(NaturalNews) Exposure to cell phone towers alters brain function in alarming ways, causing a lack of concentration, irritability, difficulty sleeping and lack of appetite. That's the conclusion of [a new study just published by the British Medical Journal](http://bmjopen.bmj.com/content/3/12/e003836.full).(1)

The study, authored by Professor Enrique A Navarro, concluded that **the severity of such symptoms directly correlated to cell tower exposure levels**. In other words, the closer a person lives to a cell tower, the greater the severity of their symptoms. This was true regardless of race, income level and other demographics.

Cell towers, of course, broadcast and receive electromagnetic switching signals. Human biology -- and the brain in particular -- relies on electro-biochemical pathways for healthy function. Many scientists have long suspected that chronic exposure to low levels of EMF pollution (electropollution) may interfere with healthy functioning of the brain and body. This latest research adds yet more support to that alarming idea.

**It's not your imagination: Electromagnetic hypersensitivity is real**

Electromagnetic hypersensitivity has long been dismissed as non-existent by some doctors and industry-funded scientists. After all, if EMF pollution from cell towers really does harm public health, then the implications are truly massive, both economically and in terms of human suffering.

But **electromagnetic hypersensitivity is a genuine phenomenon**. People are not "inventing" side effects or symptoms. As [Navarro writes in the study](http://bmjopen.bmj.com/content/3/12/e003836.full):

_The term electromagnetic hypersensitivity has been recently introduced in discussions attributing symptoms to exposure to EMFs. A review of this topic in 2010 found that 8 of the 10 studies evaluated through PubMed had reported increased prevalence of adverse neurobehavioral symptoms or cancer in populations living at distances < 500 m from \[cell phone towers\]._

Importantly, all these symptoms were recorded in people living near [cell phone](http://www.naturalnews.com/cell_phone.html) towers whose broadcast signal strength **meets current safety guidelines**. As the study author points out, this most likely means current government guidelines on cell phone towers are **inadequate to protect the public**. Revising such guidelines could have drastic implications for the nationwide telecommunications infrastructure.

By the way, people who live fewer than 500 meters from cell phone towers appear to be especially at risk of electromagnetic interference with brain function. Because [electropollution](http://www.naturalnews.com/electropollution.html) strength is determined by the inverse square of the distance, a person who moves twice as close to a cell tower experiences **four times the radiation**.

**190,000 cell phone towers and growing**
There are currently over 190,000 cell phone towers across the United States.(2)

Their typical "maximum range" is over 21 miles, meaning their electromagnetic pollution extends in a sphere with a radius of over 21 miles. (In reality, this pollution extends indefinitely, but the intensity of it drops off with the square of the distance.)

The following map shows AT&T coverage areas in orange. **If you live inside an orange area, you are currently exposed to cell tower radiation**.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

People who live within range of two or more [cell phone towers](http://www.naturalnews.com/cell_phone_towers.html) experience electropollution from **all the towers within a range of 21 miles**. This electropollution effect is cumulative.

It is not known how many Americans live within 21 miles of at least one cell tower, but given that over half the U.S. population lives in urban areas, it's safe to assume that at least 150 million -- and more likely close to 300 million -- Americans are exposed to EMF electropollution from cell towers.

**Modern society increasingly confused, irritable and sleepless**
Have you noticed how the mass public seems increasingly confused and irritable? A society that once operated with some degree of sanity and politeness has become largely demented and rude. Mathematical abilities are nearly lost across the population, as very few people under the age of 40 can even calculate 15% waiter's tips at a restaurant. The ability of voters to understand laws, liberties, freedom and even the structure of government is almost entirely lost in nations where cell phone towers are ubiquitous.

Given this recent research revealing the negative impact of cell phone radiation of human brain function, it would be incredibly irresponsible to fail to consider how cell tower radiation alters healthy brain function and promotes confusion and irritability. As more scientists look into this issue, we may indeed find that the **fall of American civilization** is being accelerated by electromagnetic pollution that leads to disastrous cognitive consequences across the population.

**Sources for this article include:**
1\. [http://bmjopen.bmj.com/content/3/12/e003836....](http://bmjopen.bmj.com/content/3/12/e003836.full)
2\. [http://www.statisticbrain.com/cell-phone-tow...](http://www.statisticbrain.com/cell-phone-tower-statistics/)

Learn more: [http://www.naturalnews.com/044464\_cell\_towers\_emf\_pollution\_mental\_confusion.html#ixzz2xGmhfX58](http://www.naturalnews.com/044464_cell_towers_emf_pollution_mental_confusion.html#ixzz2xGmhfX58)

### seanb4 — 2014-03-28T21:45:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-creatinine.10373/post-118545>

Hi guys,

I dug up some labs from a year and a half ago that contained my serum urea level, serum creatinine level, and serum potassium level. I have told with these three labs you can get a decent idea on your cellular hydration but when I try to work out my BUN to Creatinine ratio I get a ridiculously high number (**181.4 : 1).** I will list the results here and if anyone can tell me the actual ratio I would be greatful.

Serum potassium level 4.0 mmol/L (3.5 - 5.3)
Serum urea level 7.1 mmol/L (2.5 - 7.8)
Serum creatinine level 68 umol/L (62.0 - 106.0)

Can anyone help?

### seanb4 — 2014-03-28T21:49:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-creatinine.10373/post-118547>

Just realised that I have got serum urea level and not urea nitrogen level. Thats why the results are so wrong.

### seanb4 — 2014-03-28T22:05:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-creatinine.10373/post-118551>

Okay I tried it again using the following links and conversions

[http://www.endmemo.com/medical/unitconvert/Urea\_nitrogen.php](http://www.endmemo.com/medical/unitconvert/Urea_nitrogen.php)

Came up with a BUN/Creatine ratio of 28.3:1
Is this right, or do I need urea nitrogen to get actual results?

I listened to the webinar yesterday on methylation. I found out that Jack and Tim Jackson did a podcast a long time ago on this subject. After much searching, I found this gem of a podcast.

[http://www.blogtalkradio.com/mthfrs...k-kruse-neurosurgeon-and-optimal-health-coach](http://www.blogtalkradio.com/mthfrsupport/2013/02/21/dr-jack-kruse-neurosurgeon-and-optimal-health-coach)

A friend of mine with essential tremors went into the ice bath last night and said her tremors stopped for a bit after the bath. Mel

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-10T06:14:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-methylation-and-super-conductivity.10374/post-120728>

All thermodynamics.......................Some of the nutrient depletions that I worry most about are vitamin B6, BH4, tyrosine, and iodine. BH4 is part of the methylation pathways for neurotransmitters, like dopamine. COMT pathways are quite important for those with sleep disorders and low DHEA levels. BH4 is needed to synthesize serotonin, dopamine, conversion of phenylalanine to tyrosine and language-related function (autism connection). The A1298C mutation in the MTHFR gene may also impact levels of BH4. Remember BH4 is a B like vitamin so exogenous estrogens deplete BH4 too!!! Vitamin B6 is required for the production of serotonin and melatonin. Serotonin gets converted to melatonin in the pineal gland, which is why a B6 deficiency may lead to sleep problems. Simultaneously IL −6 rises in the brain to destroy melatonin and DHEA signaling. Here the B vitamins of the MTHFR pathways overlap with COMT pathways. Sleep problems in these women are often diagnosed and treated with Rx drugs, when a B6 supplement may be effective. Tyrosine is a precursor for the production of dopamine and norepinephrine, which are involved in a vast array of brain functions, including motivation, stress, cognition, and arousal.................all mass equivalence issues....................

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-10T14:23:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-methylation-and-super-conductivity.10374/post-120778>

Melanie essential tremor is a thermodynamic problem related to a loss of symmetry. It is a kinetic tremor. All physics is motion. And when symmetry is broken motion is also altered. When symmetry is broken something causes it. I personally think ET is due to a loss of stromal control in the cerebellum. The pathophysiology, clinical, physiological and MRI imaging studies all point to an involvement of the cerebellum and/or cerebellothalamocortical circuits in the hind brain. the hind brain is one of the last parts of the brain to develop. I believe if estrogen is in higher amounts in the stroma of the developing brain in this area we see the development of ET. This is why ET is seen with alcohol use. ETOH increase stromal levels of estrogen. Estrogen is a symmetry breaking molecule. This is why I look at puberty as a phase transition and thermodynamic process. Testosterone has the same effect on men but it orders the stroma. Estrogen disorders the stroma. Hence phenotype and function of the tissues change. Changes in the cerebellum are mediated by alcohol consumption. We see cerebellar degeneration in alcoholic via this same mechanism, but the trigger is different. In ET the trigger is transgenerational and epigenetic from a mother with a bad PG/E2 ratio. [Purkinje cells](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Purkinje_cell), which make up the primary outflow of the cerebellum are especially susceptible to ethanol excitotoxicity and high estrogen levels in development. Impairment of Purkinje synapses is a component of cerebellar degradation that could underlie essential tremor.

### Marie — 2014-04-10T19:38:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-methylation-and-super-conductivity.10374/post-120819>

Wow! This is sooo interesting to me. (And I a little pat my back... I made the Vit B connection a few years ago, which is why I stopped being a vegetarian.)

So now I'm tracing back MD dogma... "Depression" seems to predispose one for later neurological disorders, and specifically PD or tremor related disorders. This "depression" is precipitated by a traumatic event. (Picture being 50ish and losing your job, or your spouse.) Duh, you deplete your stress supporting Vit Bs, go into adrenal fatigue, and if treated incorrectly (with psych meds), come out the other end with a visible tremor +10 years later.

In Chinese Medicine this is called:
_
"**Liver Wind Moving Internally:** This is an internal condition of the liver that can result from a long-term imbalance; the usual chronic patterns are liver yin deficiency or _blood deficiency_. Signs of this condition are various abnormal body movements, such as twitching, shaking, convulsions, and spasms. The liver is in charge of the smooth movement of qi and blood in the body as well as harmonious movement within the body. An imbalance in the liver impairs this function, producing abnormal movement, and the influence of wind stirs this movement at unpredictable times."_

Each time I look at the details of how TCM practitioners look at the body, I am awed by how incredibly detailed this system of health KNEW the patterns leading to disease.

__"liver yin deficiency" ->__ Yin corresponds to "Female__"__ \-> Estrogen
__"_blood deficiency" ->___ Anemia_
"The liver is in charge of the smooth movement"_ -> Hormonal communication

Josh, could you add your 2 cents from a TCM perspective?

Marie

1.  Without the Weapons of Mass Distraction that plague modern life, people had a lot of time to observe each other and doctors typically lived intimate to their patients in the same environment and social milleu. Thus, most well-developed traditional systems reflect this. Consider Yi Tian Ni, who was the 49th generation in her family, unfortunately she died in a car accident shortly after this talk on "symmetry" in Qi flow among other things:

[http://www.pacificcollege.edu/pcom\_static/publications/audio/pop\_up.php](http://www.pacificcollege.edu/pcom_static/publications/audio/pop_up.php)

1.  Wind=irregular nervous system activity manifesting in physiological movement disorders and mental/cognitive function derangement...
2.  Yin=hormones, myelin, lymph, material precursors for collagen, collagen=soft wet stuff
3.  Blood=Yin stuff with Yang energy and elements, anemia is blood deficiency
4.  Blood and Yin are required to maintain tissues and their development. The deficiency of one leads to the deficiency of the other over time and or they decline simultaneously depending upon causes. EMF=heat that dries up wet stuff like yin and blood leading to "wind"....
5.  I like the "smooth and even flow of energy"=hormonal communication idea and the "Liver" is responsible for a good deal more depending upon which school one looks at. The Japanese feel that the "Liver" maintains "structure" while Chinese TCM attributes it more to "Kidney". I consider the Liver's vital role in metabolism as well. There are Liver-Yin, Liver-Blood and Liver-Yang with issues related to each and combinations.
6.  Translating between the two systems can be a fruitful cross-pollination of ideas. For me, the question is what is different now with regard to COE's that makes traditional prescriptions unreliable. I have been on this since I started school in 1995.
7.  The Liver controls the "tendons" that may include Collagen...

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-30T07:19:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vortices-and-energy-efficiency-fluoro-plane.10381/post-118765>

**[Mohirah Hall](https://www.facebook.com/mhall5) asked me on FB**
[57 mins](https://www.facebook.com/groups/260386650793783/permalink/289870157845432/?stream_ref=3) · [Los Angeles, CA](https://www.facebook.com/pages/Los-Angeles-California/110970792260960)
Hey [Jack Kruse](https://www.facebook.com/jack.kruse) and everyone else Thanks for all your information and posts, its so helpful! Ive been practicing CT for the past month and seem to be cold adapted now. Lately I have been doing more research about the benefits of the cold, and am wondering if same benefits extend to cold exposure via air. I'm trying to figure out what the optimal temperature is for general cold air exposure to become cold adapted, and receive CT benefits w/o danger of hypothermia, frostbite similar to your CT protocol. I've come across various articles saying different things but so far nothing definitive. wondering if you had take. Thanks again for everything you've done!

[Like](https://www.facebook.com/john.sorrentino.31/posts/10201979844557481?comment_id=54005949&offset=0&total_comments=19&ref=notif&notif_t=share_reply#) ·

-   [
    Jack Kruse](https://www.facebook.com/jack.kruse?fref=ufi) I will have a lot more to say on this later this yr........when we breath we create air driven vortices of air in our lungs that has direct effects on our SNS and PNS. I will give you a preview of where I am headed.........google the fluoro flyer. This fluoro flyer is a model for an aircraft called X-48 C prototype that is being developed by our military, NASA, and Boeing that runs on a superfluid called liquid helium. Liquid helium like water is a superfluid. It creates massive vortices to power this plane to unreal performance. It uses a blended wing-body technology. This technology is used in the human respiratory tract. When the technology is engaged the entire body of the plane creates uplift and not just the wing. This is why our nostrils can be point downward but we still have zero issue breathing from 8% O2 to 21% O2 atmospheres. When you see the optics of airflow on the undersurface of this plane you see the fractal design in you...............BOOM!!!

# Thread: Iodine -
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine.10395/>
**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 26
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Aggie — 2014-03-31T01:42:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine.10395/post-118882>

iodine in supplement form is tied to a salt. If I remember correctly Jack said don't take anything attached to a salt - it alters EZ. Furthermore, most people are unable to break the double bond & be able to utilize supplemental iodine. In addition, you must make sure to have the two vitamins required for organification of iodine on board. I would it would seem as though it's better to take atomic iodine bound to glycerin? I've only been able to find one source from Germany to provide this.

Dr Flechas, MD has a great presentation on iodine.

Did you know most iodine supplements come from old oil wells in Texas? ick!

### Gracelind — 2014-04-01T22:04:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine.10395/post-119301>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=118936)
>
> You are human........you are designed to get iodine from the sea. If you do it any other way........new emergent problems in you will manifest.

Two years ago I started taking Lugol's with the protocol including selenium and Vit C (at a different time of day) as for several decades we had removed most salt from table and cooking. Worked up to about 10 mg/day for about a week. Shortly I discovered a marble-sized lump on my neck--stopped the iodine. Thyroid scan showed multi-nodular goiter, with about 6 nodules, most small but several good sized.

Started eating more seafood a'la Epi-paleo. The lump was getting smaller but still there. One weekend was especially shrimp/seafood intensive. Not for any reason--just happened that way. On Monday the lump was completely gone and hasn't returned. Can I attribute that to natural sources of iodine in the shrimp and fish? Not wanting extra exposure to radiation, I haven't had a follow-up scan but clearly the nodules have reduced in size if they still exist at all.

One experience of non-natural supplementation vs natural source seafood.

### persistence — 2014-05-10T04:18:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine.10395/post-126135>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=119327)
>
> This reminds me of a question that I asked earlier this year, which still isn't totally answered in my mind today -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/combining-supplements-with-natural-food.9313/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/combining-supplements-with-natural-food.9313/)
>
> Basically, what's the implication of doing concurrent natural source seafood with non-natural supplementation?
>
> For example, what would happen to the body's ability to utilise iodine if I say put 10 drops of Lugols into a seaweed soup? Would the iodine become more bio-available and cause less problematic side-effects? My gut sense says "Yes, but only to a small degree".

Phenomenal question, but how can anyone answer that? It requires double blind studies to quantify any effects. For some nutrients, you would think the nutrient needs to be in some ratio to the other chemicals that might support its bioavailability.

Common sense tells you nutrients from food probably have more bioavailability than supplements in isolation, because the foods probably have hundreds or thousands of other chemicals not documented or understood by our primitive understanding of nutrition. "The Wahls Protocol" by Dr Terry Wahls documents how supplements alone did little to improve her health, but only when she researched which foods contained the required supplements and added those in large quantities did "the magic start to happen."

The problem with iodine specifically is that you don't have any reliable measure for quantity contained in - for example - seaweed. The amounts contained can vary enormously. Seaweed also binds to many toxic minerals including radioactive iodine. After the Japanese disaster that becomes a non trivial consideration.... Iodine is one of the few supplements that Perfect Health Diet recommends getting as a supplement, but they recommend fairly small doses (around 1mg / day).

### PaulG — 2014-03-31T04:39:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breadcrumbs.10396/post-118903>

Jack is infamous for leaving breadcrumbs (hints) to where he is heading.

Who remembers the 159 page [What the H is Factor X! thread?](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/) ah fun times I think my life got put on hold for two weeks following that massive !!!!! (insert annotomical word) tease.

I enjoy thinking about the future undisclosed implications of Jack's thinking coupled with the collective thoughts and intuition of this mighty community. Also I believe the greater your redox, the better your brain is working, the greater your ability to be intuitive and sense/anticipate the future.

So this thread I thought could be used for us to try and predict and discuss such breadcrumbs

I will get the ball rolling with summary of his current breadcrumbs for the next three webinars (Three amigos) though I have probably missed loads

1.  First four minutes+ [of Pink Floyds “Wish you were here” album](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AV4BW2NJKE0)
2.  We are [seasonal mammals](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0018506X14000506)
3.  [Stress induces increased permeability of the gut](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306453012000935)
4.  Ability to [regrow pineal gland](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/shruken-pineal-gland-aka-the-third-eye.10380/#post-118752)
5.  “I will also be throwing bread crumbs in the next few blogs. But you wont see what is coming...........its one of the turns in the road I warned you to expect in the Quilt document”

In relation to the Pink Floyd this sentence from [recent Mae-Wan Ho article](http://www.mdpi.com/2079-8954/1/3/30/pdf) struck a chord

“My guess was that living fractals are associated with irrational numbers such as the golden mean, and cannot enter into resonance; which is why the activities can remain distinct. However, the fractals are also close to harmonics that do resonate, so phase coupling and energy transfer through resonance is readily achieved by sliding from fractals to harmonics. Sliding from fractals to harmonics may coincide with the threshold for percolation in which local connects with global.”
