### NeilBB — 2014-04-04T16:39:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breadcrumbs.10396/post-119776>

> [patgrif@hotmail.com said:](/goto/post?id=119622)
>
> Neil - I like your contributions some 'intellectual sobriety' is always useful! However not that is so important I do not like Ayn Rand or much of any of that kind of thinking. Her modern day 'acolytes' like Paul Ryan and Alan Greenberg to me at least kind of symbolize so much of what is wrong with society. To me we are going pell mell into disaster, just yesterday the Supreme Court MORE money in politics, and the world is hurtling towards disaster especially through global warming. As much as I like Dr Kruse even he I believe has to kind of turn away from the full implications of that. Though he has talked about the oceans dying and so on. As the oceen goes then so will the land.............
>
> I grew up in Ireland and it is very interesting to me.................in those days the '50's in Ireland, we were a 'poor' country but there was a lot of security, we had free health care, free education even to University level, I got a Philosophy degree and my sister become a doctor without costing my parents a penny and also we came out having no debts. Everything big was 'socialized' transportation, medicine, education etc. So to me this is a much better model than what I consider the kind of free market madness as enunciated by Ayn Rand and her followers. I know there is another side to that but I for one think the only hope is if society pulls together as a unit with a common purpose the alternative seems to be 'free markets' and endless wars

Thanks Pat. We could have a long debate about history, philosophy and economic systems, but that isn't the purpose of this site, and the last thing I want to do is hijack what Jack is doing here because I think he's doing important revolutionary work in scientific integration. I don't want to detract from his message.

Let me just say a few things. There are no truly "free markets" today. A half-free, half-government controlled economy as we have had in Western nations in the 20th century is called a "mixed-economy" and is not true capitalism because it is "for sale" and controlled by political favors and influence rather than economic merit on a level playing field. This was not Ayn Rand's objective. She actually addressed this extensively although she seemed to forget it sometimes in practice, and many of her followers certainly do today. She also had a very unfortunate "blind worship" of technology and disdain for anything primitive (see her opinion of "primitive cultures" like the American Indians for example), which I think was a mistake.

But blaming "free-markets" for the failures of the 20th century "mixed economies" is inaccurate and misplaced in my opinion. What we have is only superficially-free and in-fact heavily manipulated. It is a straw man.

People always think of "brutal raging capitalism" when they think of Rand, but her most important contributions to philosophy were actually in epistemology in my opinion. (Radical and revolutionarily important theory of concept-formation, see [Introduction to Objectivist Epistemology](http://www.amazon.com/Introduction-Objectivist-Epistemology-Expanded-Second/dp/0452010306/ref=sr_1_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1396618862&sr=1-1&keywords=objectivist+epistemology)).

I'll grant you this though. I used to think that laissez-faire capitalism would solve everything as Rand thought. I believed this for many years actually. But I no longer do, largely because of what I learned from Jack. I believe a new scientific/philosophical synthesis is now needed and I have a lot of thoughts on that and that is the kind of stuff I currently scribble on notepads in my spare time.

### NeilBB — 2014-04-04T17:56:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breadcrumbs.10396/post-119786>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=119755)
>
> There is never going to be a time when society all works towards a common goal, there are too many variables in what people desire, people are also very different in their beliefs and behaviours. The best you can do is make yourself optimal and hope that others will be inspired to emulate you.
>
> I'm joining Gretchen on the Neil admiration step

Sooperb, I think you and I have a tendency towards pessimism, which can be an asset because it allows us to analyze the world clearly and objectively and anticipate potential roadblocks ahead. But it can also be a drawback in that we can become discouraged and that can negatively impact our health if not controlled. I believe pessimists are usually just "frustrated idealists." We want things to be to "right" today and when they are not, we tend to "catastrophize." But we have to be careful and not get too upset about things outside our control.

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2014-04-04T18:07:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breadcrumbs.10396/post-119787>

Sooperb - I am aware I am sort of indulging in 'nostalgia' though I do sort of think in a general way that "everything is getting worse". What I mean is what I remember and gleaned from the really old Irish culture growing up was almost by definition far closer to 'ancestral wisdom' and even going to much more modern times say '50's and '60's in Ireland or England too for that matter at least you had a 'society' that held together and worked together more. Even on the simplest level I would feel more comfortable in a world of say "British Rail" than all the 'privatisation' schemes we have to deal with now. The way I see it there is a gradual increase in 'atomization' and 'separation'..............of course the internet or for example this group provided a way for connection but in a different (more virtual, less real) way

Neil - you are correct and I don't mean to distract.................it's just I noticed quite a few references to Ayn Rand and felt like putting in my 2 cents worth. The little I know about her she was heavily traumatized by her parents losing all their wealth and business to the Bolsheviks/Communists in Russia and was on an almost personal crusade/jihad to right that wrong. As an individual it seems she had a very sad and alienated life and died alone and not even having friends. To me she is not a great example we should be following in terms of any real solutions to our myriad problems. I would not want to belong to a club that had Paul Ryan and Alan Greenspan as members.......................just sayin'

### NeilBB — 2014-04-04T18:30:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breadcrumbs.10396/post-119788>

> she was heavily traumatized by her parents losing all their wealth and business to the Bolsheviks/Communists in Russia and was on an almost personal crusade/jihad to right that wrong

Maybe, Pat. But those who are motivated to change the world usually begin with some powerful experience. That does not make the theory itself right or wrong. Does a powerful personal "trauma" early in life "bias" a person? Sure, it can. But epistemology is "the theory of how we validate human knowledge." It is in essence the science of eliminating bias and error from human thinking. It was her field and few understand what she really said about it. It is worth reading.

> As an individual it seems she had a very sad and alienated life and died alone and not even having friends

Maybe. But idealists who take on the dogma of the entire world are in a very dangerous business. That's why most people wouldn't do it. (That's why I worry a little about Jack--I care about him and worry about the ridiculously powerful forces he is up against to achieve his dream.)

> I would not want to belong to a club that had Paul Ryan and Alan Greenspan as members.......................just sayin'

Me either man. I'm with you.

### NeilBB — 2014-04-04T19:57:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breadcrumbs.10396/post-119803>

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=119786)
>
> Sooperb, I think you and I have a tendency towards pessimism, which can be an asset because it allows us to analyze the world clearly and objectively and anticipate potential roadblocks ahead. But it can also be a drawback in that we can become discouraged and that can negatively impact our health if not controlled. I believe pessimists are usually just "frustrated idealists." We want things to be to "right" today and when they are not, we tend to "catastrophize." But we have to be careful and not get too upset about things outside our control.

You know, I don't want to always be the "analytical but negative" guy around here. I know in most of my posts it probably comes off that way, but that's not really what I want. I do believe that the best way to control pessimism is likely practicing meditation and "living in the moment" more. Focusing on the "next step" only instead of everything at once. I plan to follow my own prescription and do more of that this year. At least part of the time. More communing with nature. Maybe climbing a rock. A small one. Nowhere near Venezuela.

Its also harder when you try to help other people for a living and most of those people have truly no hope and no chance. It tends to darken your daily outlook. That's my excuse. But I need to overcome that. Excuses are for losers.

I actually think Eckhart Tolle's stuff can be quite helpful in this regard. Learning how to "turn off the mind" and live just in "experience" part of the time. And actively "cogitate" only when you want to. I like reading J Krishnamurti a lot as well. Very calming. What you think, Josh?

### Josh — 2014-04-04T20:28:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breadcrumbs.10396/post-119810>

"You are the world..." Understand and experience this and one is free....my teacher told me I would not be ready for advanced work until I could feel and experience everything happening on the planet in real time without reacting to it...gulp...I asked him if that is how it is for him and he said "yes". Living with him for a few years and seeing the grace that he handled it and us "students" with was a gift. He was personally close to J. Krishnamurti and had many personal meetings with him.

> **You are the World**
> **J. Krishnamurti You Are The World Chapter 1 18th October 1968 1st Public Talk at Brandeis University**
>
> AS ONE TRAVELS one is very much aware that human problems everywhere, though apparently dissimilar, are really more or less similar; the problems of violence and the problem of freedom; the problem of how to bring about a real and better relationship between man and man, so that he may live at peace, with some decency and not be constantly in conflict, not only within himself but also with his neighbour. Also there is the problem, as in the whole of Asia, of poverty, starvation and the utter despair of the poor. And there is the problem, as in this country and in Western Europe, of prosperity; where there is prosperity without austerity there is violence, there is every form of unethical luxury - the society which is utterly corrupt and immoral.
>
> There is the problem of organized religion - which man, throughout the world is rejecting, more or less - and the question of what a religious mind is and what meditation is - which are not monopolies of the East. There is the question of love and death - so many interrelated problems. The speaker does not represent any system of conceptual thinking or ideology, Indian or otherwise. If we can talk over together these many problems, not as with an expert or a specialist - because the speaker is neither - then possibly we can establish right communication; but bear in mind that the word is not the thing, and that the descrip- tion, however detailed, however intricate, however well-reasoned out and beautiful, is not the thing described.
>
> There are the whole separate worlds, the ideological divisions of the Hindu, the Muslim, the Christian and the Communist, which have brought about such incalculable harm, such hatred and antagonism. All ideologies are idiotic, whether religious or political, for it is conceptual thinking, the conceptual word, which has so unfortunately divided man.
>
> These ideologies have brought about wars; although there may be religious tolerance, it is up to a certain point only; after that, destruction, intolerance, brutality, violence - the religious wars. Similarly there are the national and tribal divisions caused by ideologies, the black nationalism and the various tribal expressions.

[http://www.jkrishnamurti.org/krishnamurti-teachings/view-text.php?s=books&tid=19&chid=68560](http://www.jkrishnamurti.org/krishnamurti-teachings/view-text.php?s=books&tid=19&chid=68560)

On another note, my father went to see J. Krishnamurti at the Felt Forum (part of Madison Square Garden in NYC) in 1978. My father said that JK walked out, sat down in his wooden straight-backed chair and said "Sir, the mind of man has not changed a bit in 4 million years!" The JK work that pierced my heart the first time was the "On Education" where first you see him talking to teachers at one of his schools and then to the students. I felt his love and compassion in an overwhelming way and cried for a few days....

[http://www.jkrishnamurti.org/krishnamurti-teachings/view-text.php?tid=43&chid=297](http://www.jkrishnamurti.org/krishnamurti-teachings/view-text.php?tid=43&chid=297)

[http://www.amazon.com/Krishnamurti-Education-Jiddu/dp/0060647949](http://www.amazon.com/Krishnamurti-Education-Jiddu/dp/0060647949)

Suzuki had a pair too...."freedom without form is anarchy"

### Josh — 2014-04-04T20:56:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breadcrumbs.10396/post-119813>

I remember one more thing from J. Krishnamurti's life. Someone visited his school teacher and asked him about JK. The teacher said that he was the dumbest boy in the class and that he would routinely put him out on the veranda to sit alone. The teacher could not understand how that boy had become the eloquent being that JK became as a teacher.

The story of his basically rejecting "messiahship" by dissolving the "Order of the Star" is really fascinating to me.

[http://www.jkrishnamurti.org/about-krishnamurti/dissolution-speech.php](http://www.jkrishnamurti.org/about-krishnamurti/dissolution-speech.php)

"Truth cannot be brought down, rather the individual must make the effort to ascend to it. You cannot bring the mountain-top to the valley. If you would attain to the mountain-top you must pass through the valley, climb the steeps, unafraid of the dangerous precipices."

"If an organization be created for this purpose, it becomes a crutch, a weakness, a bondage, and must cripple the individual, and prevent him from growing, from establishing his uniqueness, which lies in the discovery for himself of that absolute, unconditioned Truth. So that is another reason why I have decided, as I happen to be the Head of the Order, to dissolve it. No one has persuaded me to this decision. This is no magnificent deed, because I do not want followers, and I mean this. The moment you follow someone you cease to follow Truth."

"For two years I have been thinking about this, slowly, carefully, patiently, and I have now decided to disband the Order, as I happen to be its Head. You can form other organizations and expect someone else. With that I am not concerned, nor with creating new cages, new decorations for those cages. My only concern is to set men absolutely, unconditionally free."

> **Primary symptoms\[[edit](http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Gerstmann_syndrome&action=edit&section=1)\]**
> Gerstmann syndrome is characterized by four primary symptoms:
>
>
> 1.  [Dysgraphia](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dysgraphia)/agraphia: deficiency in the ability to write[\[2\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gerstmann_syndrome#cite_note-Vallar_2007-2)[\[3\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gerstmann_syndrome#cite_note-Carota.2C_2004-3)
> 2.  [Dyscalculia](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dyscalculia)/acalculia: difficulty in learning or comprehending mathematics[\[2\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gerstmann_syndrome#cite_note-Vallar_2007-2)[\[3\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gerstmann_syndrome#cite_note-Carota.2C_2004-3)
> 3.  [Finger agnosia](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Finger_agnosia): inability to distinguish the fingers on the hand[\[2\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gerstmann_syndrome#cite_note-Vallar_2007-2)[\[3\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gerstmann_syndrome#cite_note-Carota.2C_2004-3)
> 4.  Left-right disorientation[\[2\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gerstmann_syndrome#cite_note-Vallar_2007-2)[\[3\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gerstmann_syndrome#cite_note-Carota.2C_2004-3)
> 5.  contralateral [hemianopia](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hemianopia)
> 6.  lower [quadrantanopia](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quadrantanopia)

### NeilBB — 2014-04-05T18:03:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breadcrumbs.10396/post-119913>

Ok here goes my take, Jack. People think that the brain processes information serially and sequentually through synaptic transmission. And that nerves carry information through action potentials, which are actually painfully slow. Basically like a digital computer. But this is only part of the story.

In actuality, the brain can also function as a quantum computer, which means that each cognitive unit can process multiple pieces of information at the same time. In other words in parallel rather than serial processing, to use computer lingo. This is due to quantum superposition of states as explained by QED. The difference in speed of processing is enormous and revolutionary.

Furthermore, impulses do not wait for action potential transmission. They propagate through the collagen-water fabric of the body through quantum waves, which involve electron orbitals and spins, using a network created by the quantum cells acting in unison. Which means virtually instantaneous and unlimited information transfer is possible. Aka, superconductivity.

When this all works, organisms are metastable, not at equilibrium exactly, but instead in a unique biological state that provides protection from the second law's move towards disorder. That is the zero entropy state, and the definition of optimal.

This quantum supercomputing superconducting system evolved in an environment that was relatively constant for eons, but has now been rapidly altered by modern man. The alterations that have advanced civilization and creature comforts also happen to virtually all interfere in one way or another with the infrastructure of this quantum mechanism rendering it faulty in one way or another.

All that we do to make ourselves healthy supports the proper operation of this system. The quantum physician's job is to figure out in an individual patient, which specific aspect of this system is broken and how to most efficiently biohack it in our artificial environment. Quantum neurology will be the basis for the future of medicine.

> Patanjali can become more and more significant. As this century will come to its closure, techniques

> interested to find again how humanity in the past existed with such beatitude, such bliss. With so
> poor conditions, how such rich men existed in the past, and we, with such a rich situation, why we
> are so poor?
> **_This is a paradox, the modern paradox. For the first time on the earth we have created rich, scientific
> societies, and they are the most ugly and most unhappy. And in the past there was no scientific
> technology, no affluence, nothing of comfort, but humanity was existing in such a deep, peaceful
> milieu – happy, thankful. What has happened? We can be more happy than anyone, but we have
> lost contact with existence._**
> And that existence is here and now, and an impatient mind cannot be in contact with it. Impatience
> is like a feverish, mad state of mind; you go on running. Even if the goal comes, you cannot stand
> there because the running has become just the habit. Even if you reach the goal you will miss it,
> you will pass it because you cannot stop. If you can stop, the goal is not to be searched.
> Zen Master Hui-Hai, has said that, ”Seek, and you will lose; don’t seek, and you can get it
> immediately. Stop, and it is here. Run, it is nowhere.”

Yoga: The Alpha and the Omega, Vol 1 Osho
CHAPTER 8. STOP, AND IT IS HERE!

Meher Baba said that "Comfort is the hardest habit to break and that once one enters it, it is a long journey to come out..."

### NeilBB — 2014-04-05T21:28:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breadcrumbs.10396/post-119925>

Well Jack, I am currently studying the concept of T-Symmetry in theoretical physics because I think it ties into where you may be headed. The idea exists that time is symmetrical, meaning that it can theoretically "go in reverse" in the quantum world, but is still thought to be unidirectional in the macroscopic world, mostly due to the 2nd law of thermodynamics. I'm already convinced that "zero entropy" is possible for organisms and that that is what produces biological metastability. But true time symmetry at the macroscopic level would require more than just zero-entropy. It would actually require true "negative-entropy," which has generally been thought to be impossible because of the 2nd law. When Schrodinger coined the term "negative entropy" I'm not sure if he really meant zero-entropy or near zero-entropy. Or if he really actually meant negative-entropy?

So the question is, are biological Maxwell Demons efficient enough to not only slow or temporarily stop entropy but to actually reverse entropy, or to actually add order to the system. You have hinted that you believe that this is possible. Of course, this seems unfathomable at the macroscopic level. It implies not only slowing or even stopping aging, as we have discussed, but literally being able to actually reverse aging by adding back order to a biological system. ### Josh — 2014-04-05T22:33:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breadcrumbs.10396/post-119946>

Hah! Jeremy is a yogi in a coffin walking around. We are all optimal as yogi's in boxes or eutherian animals underground after the KT event. Cellular respiration is optimal in hypoxia because our mitochondrial DNA is optimized for cold, dark and hypoxia-KT again. The basement membrane defect/issue in CF may actually drive the system toward optimal oxygen utilization and signalling with the right environmental messages. Traditionally, these would have come from intimate contact with the environment that would run electrons and possibly other information carrying particles through the system. The microbiota would get these signals as well and do their part. As a species, we are now not only local environment electron/particle deficient, but also non visual/audio sensing deficient and getting more so by the day.....Jeremy's disability forced him to have a much more intimate multisensorial experience of life and environment to survive and created a more "traditional/optimal" balance between his senses and thus more optimal sensing of the environment and regulation of his metabolism. It is a system that allows him to create a very powerful metastability and it is one that is very sensitive to crashing when things go out of range. Thus, if he is not careful, he ends up in the hospital with breathing issues.

It suggests that the extracellular matrix system of water and collagen may be our primary energy system by design with appropriate support from intracellular/other organ systems. If optimal function of these systems is based on a hypoxic state such as experienced by a eutherian animal in hibernation or living underground permanently, then we are obviously overpriming and stimulating the intracellular system most of the time. It even suggests that buried in there may be a way to really slow things down if need be to maintain stability. Perhaps reversing electron flow across the mitochondrial membrane and or some other key processes that can absorb extra energy/entropy to maintain the Second Law of Thermodynamics. I guess the 64, 000 dollar question is what would be the signal for this...hypoxia, cold, darkness, a flow of electrons from the environment via the skin and extracellular collagen matrix with particular information....?

In the context of "practices" that increase resonance/qi/prana/etc., this explains how and why so many work with breath and touch/movement to emphasize the development of gross and subtle neural and muscular function. The great risk and danger is always that the metastability will shift too quickly blasting the nervous system with a great deal of extra energy and producing "uncomfortable" "phenomena" that classically have included pretty much everything in the lexicon of epilepsy and psychiatry and beyond....Mystics have always said that these experiences can be attained by falling below "human" or rising above "human" function...I am guessing that this is above or below a familiar metastability with familiar nervous system function and phenomena to a point. Another example being the commonality between "mystical experiences" and "drug experiences". The later being used as an introduction to the possibility of the former in some traditions....Hence, the caution to go slow and steady as well as to be meticulous with all "practices" because shifting the metastability of the system will have profound effects on the nervous system and immune system as they sit at the top of the energy tree and use new additional sources of energy for new work and even possibly "evolution" of sorts.....

He says that he did not defecate for 11 months while in there sitting. So he is at altitude in a tiny cave in the cold in contact with the earth with almost no ventilation=hypoxia with cold and almost no light=KT event adaptation. He says that yogis come out rejuvenated. You can read this in his biography:

[http://shop.himalayaninstitute.org/products/at-the-eleventh-hour](http://shop.himalayaninstitute.org/products/at-the-eleventh-hour)

Reich built his "orgone" chambers as sealed units as well. I never sat in one, but I do not think they have any air flow either. They are dark for sure. He used these to "rejuvenate" people who suffered from chronic illness. I believe their construction would shield one from EMF's as well....

### NeilBB — 2014-04-06T06:30:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breadcrumbs.10396/post-120007>

OK Jack, here goes... Water chemistry is drastically altered in CF due to the CFTR dysfunction. Resultant extracellular dehydration contributes to the thick mucous of course, but more importantly interferes with ability to form exclusion zone and maintain good redox potential. When protons are pumped out of mitochondria, they will tend to accumulate because water is not available to neutralize and store protons as hydronium ions. This would typically lead to metabolic acidosis and inflammation.

How would Jeremy combat this? Well, through his physical training he has built an extraordinary network of collagen to apparently maximize his ability to semiconduct despite his constant battle with dehydration and low redox potential. He then systematically collects the maximal possible number of electrons from the earth/rocks and energizes them constantly with photons from the sun. He prefers to live in mild hypoxia chronically, controlled partially by yoga/breathing at sea level and partially by seeking out lower O2-concentration air on the mountaintop. He does this to limit his mitochondria from producing too many protons because he lacks the ability to fully absorb and store them in excess due to chronic low redox potential. He prefers carbs because fatty acid oxidation requires a lot of oxygen and would also produce more protons than he could handle--again he would end up with metabolic acidosis otherwise, like other CF patients do. Hypoxic high-altitude air also allows him to ventilate more without necessarily increasing oxygenation which also helps to dissipate excess protons as needed.

So his life requires an extraordinary metabolic precision to walk the tightrope of just enough oxygen and ATP production in mitochondria to unfold his proteins that work with his collagen in dehydration to support his semiconductors to optimize his own N=1. He has somehow learned to synesthetically "modulate his doses" of all these parameters by sensing his internal environment in some extraordinary way. His is so leptin sensitive that he is literally able to somehow "count his electrons" although he feels it in a way he can't really fully explain. What he "feels" out in nature on the rocks apparently includes an accurate subconscious representation of his internal "energy status" which he is apparently able to not only accurately sense but also has learned to modulate by instantaneously altering his breathing pace and his pressure on the rocks to always maintain maximal energy efficiency. That's what he means when he says he "breathes through touch."

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-06T19:23:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breadcrumbs.10396/post-120071>

> Canaima National Park is where Angel Falls is located in Venezuela. It formed when South America and Africa were one............electron spin up and down. The most famous tepuis in the park are [Mount Roraima](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monte_Roraima), the tallest and easiest to climb, and [Auyantepui](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Auyantepui), the site of Angel Falls. All the tepuis are made of sandstone. Rock formations that are primarily composed of sandstone usually allow [percolation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Percolation) of water and other fluids and are [porous](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Porosity) enough to store large quantities, making them valuable [aquifers](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aquifer). Interesting huh? you climb rock to increase your collagen matrix and the rocks happen to be ancient sea beds that function as aquifers for water. Water than binds to collagen.........
> Fine-grained aquifers, such as sandstones, are more apt to filter out pollutants from the surface than are rocks with cracks and crevices, such as [limestone](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Limestone) or other rocks fractured by [seismic activity](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake).
>
> Quartz-bearing sandstone is converted into [quartzite](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quartzite) through heating and pressure usually related to tectonic compression......makes interesting crystals.....

### Jack Kruse — 2017-10-15T19:43:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breadcrumbs.10396/post-225926>

Are you still haunted by bad cholesterol science? You shouldn't be. Cholesterol is not something you should waste your time with any longer. Any type of cellular stress lowers our stores of ATP and of magnesium (Mg) because they are coupled together by our ATPase enzyme in mitochondria. This is classically seen in diabetes development. Another interesting thing also occurs to the cell, that we clinicians can follow on our patient's regular labs like a Chem 7 or BMP. Their carbon dioxide (CO2) levels also fall, while cells become dehydrated because mitochondria actively transfer tons of electrons to O2 to make more ATP to replace the deficit from the stress. This causes a simultaneous drop in CO2. The normal cellular response to ANY cellular stress however is rather telling to review for most lay people and MDs because they may have forgotten how that biochemistry works via its connection to the environment. The simultaneous fall in ATP and Mg2+ stimulates the cell to make LDL cholesterol! Yes, you read that correctly folks, the supposedly bad LDL stuff, LOL! It is obviously not bad if the cell is making it for some reason. Let us look at why the cell does this. You may want to reread this blog on cholesterol.

The cell responds to all stress by increasing cholesterol production. If you go into an ICU with a patient with an acute infection and draw their lipids (not often done except by a tool like me) you will find sky-high LDL cholesterol. And that is both LDL and HDL, but the LDL fraction is much higher. Higher LDL cholesterol stabilizes the inner mitochondrial membrane function during heavy oxidative phosphorylation (cellular energy production). So a raised LDL actually helps mitochondria retain its electric charge to condense H+ inside the matrix to make energy using metallic light hydrogen. Making CO2 and non-D20 water is the reason our cells stole bacteria 600 million years ago. And since the cell is trying to recover from a stressor, it needs a good inner mitochondrial membrane in which to transfer its electrons to make ATP. That is the reason why this occurs. You can review this at your leisure.

But here is why the cholesterol story tends to trip clinicians up. Cholesterol is needed by the cell for many uses. To be used properly, cholesterol is made to stabilize membranes and make all our hormones. To do this several co-factors are also required. If the cells have a lot of toxins around or the use of the cholesterol is too slow it can oxidize and become the real bad stuff called the sdLDL that is oxidized easily (sdLDLox). This is the stuff that can hurt you. How does this happen? Well, to make steroids we need an adequate amount of thyroid hormone and vitamin A around to convert the cholesterol to pregnenolone and then to DHEA. T3 is made from solar exposure from sunrise until UVA light appears. Vitamin A is made properly when a normal photoperiod is followed from sunrise to sunset. Any circadian disruption causes a LOWERED vitamin A cycle as my January 2017 webinar shows. Remember the Hormone 101 blog post that spoke of pregnenolone steal syndrome? So if someone is hypothyroid and/or has low vitamin A levels they can accumulate higher sdLDLox and this lowers the HDL level. Remember from the VAP blog post that a lower HDL means less filtering ability (endocytosis clearing by the liver) in the liver's portal circulation for endotoxins. This process breaks the long loop of the Bazan effect and we lose the ability to replace DHA and recycle Vitamin A in our cells. That means that the liver is no longer as good a sieve to filter any toxins from our guts to keep the antioxidant burden in our general circulation low. If DHA and Vitamin A are not replaced in cell membranes this is how leptin resistance develops as laid out in the Reality 4 blog post. Continuing on, the lowered HDL causes a "leakier" gut too to proteins and the same is true of the blood-brain barrier. This means both the gut and brain are more easily exposed to toxins and unwanted toxins that lower the redox potential in cells to further oxidize the blood plasma and lower the exclusion zone further. [https://www.nhs.uk/news/heart-and-l...o-link-between-cholesterol-and-heart-disease/](https://www.nhs.uk/news/heart-and-lungs/study-says-theres-no-link-between-cholesterol-and-heart-disease/)

At life's genesis chaos has to gain order. Dissipative structure theory really aims to solve this problem for biology by using physics.

A biological cell is a dissipative system by its very nature. You heard this in the podcast when Rick said, "I don't know what that means." I said I will tell you. Now I am retelling it to you here to crystallize the lesson. I think I did a bad job answering Mr. Rubin's question because I was trying to get it all out.
A dissipative structure is built atomically to capture energy in some format and store it. When this occurs order emerges directly from chaos. Things get more ordered in a cell as energy continues to be pumped in. This is essentially what melanin and water are doing for cells. Water, created by mitochondria and powered by visible light gives flexibility to proteins, reduces the energy barrier between reactants and products, and increases the probability of quantum tunneling by a transient compression of the energy barrier. This is my idea I call Ling 2.0

Time is critical issue to dissipative systems. While most current thermodynamical analyses used in biology completely ignore space-time structure, the “thermodynamics of organized complexity” applying to living systems depends WHOLLY on space-time heterogeneity, which allows ‘free’ variation of microscopic states within macroscopic constraints. THIS DEFINES WHAT A MITOCHONDRIA WAS DESIGNED TO DO AND TO BE IN A VARYING EMF FIELD CREATED ON THIS PLANET by the sun.

Cholesterol really is a key semiconductor in a microscopic state in cells that has macroscopic constraints in membranes. These contraints can be freed when choleterol is sculpt by light to change into a hormone or neurosteroid and can move throughout the system.

All steroid receptors possess a nongenomic modality of action, particularly at plasma membrane or mitochondrial membrane sites, and the VDR is no exception. In fact, the rapid, nongenomic effects of vitamin D appear to be mediated by the VDR. Many steroid receptors and nuclear transcription factors enter the mitochondrial compartment, where they either exert transcriptional regulation of mitochondrial DNA or control mitochondrial biogenesis and metabolism. This means Vitamin D can modulate the biophotons mitochondria make and this light can turn on or off many genes.

### Silencing the VDR enhances mitochondrial respiration and this is why obesity, diabetes, and cancer are all linked. What is the link? A lack of sunlight from 290-320nm silences the mitochondrial VDR.​

This is essentially what hormones are. Hormones have to follow a strict timing pattern set forth by the sun and circadian cycle. Few antiaging or longevity experts have this perspective. in life that bridges the environment of the skin and acts as an insulator to hold a solar charge and then it is transformed into hormones by metabolism to allow the light to be ferried to other tissues that need a solar infusion of light to be buried at the electronic & vibration level in cells. This is how charge transfer happens in us.

One of the hallmarks of the living system in cells is that they are is exquisitely sensitivity to specific, weak signals. Most of these signals are electromagnetic in nature. The human eye can detect single photons falling on the retina, which pass information directly to the SCN and all its mitochondria. This retina is where the light-sensitive photoreceptors in cells send out an action potential that represents a million-fold amplification of the energy in the photon. This defines what a non-linear stimulus is. Some of you know it by the butterfly effect.

It is not just a property of the central retinal pathways. The entire system is a plasma or syncytium of excitable matter ready to react to the environment's EMF signals. Similarly, a few molecules of pheromones in the air is sufficient to attract male insects to their mates. This sensitivity is characteristic of all parts of the system and is a consequence of the energy stored at the electronic level = related to the AMO physics of organization of the cell. Without that detail all of it falls apart.

When AMO physics is maintained by the melanin mitochondrial system of energy transformation, no part of the system has to be pushed or pulled into action, nor be subjected to mechanical regulation and control. Instead, coordinated action of all the parts depends on rapid intercommunication throughout the system because of how it is built to react to light. The organism is a system of “excitable media” (syncytium) — excitable cells and tissues poised to respond specifically and disproportionately to weak signals because a large amount of energy stored everywhere automatically amplifies weak signals. These signals often direct things in cells into macroscopic actions. This is how order is built from chaos in a dissipative structure described by **[Prigogine's theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prigogine%27s_theorem).**

Centralized science has to answer some key questions to go from a biochemical level of understanding to get to the quantum level of the cell. Namely, what is it that enabled the tens of thousands of different kinds of molecules in the organism to recognize their specific targets in biochemistry? Living processes depend on selective interactions between particular molecules, and that is true for basic metabolism to the subtlest nuances of emotion. It's like trying to find a friend in a very big very crowded ballroom in the dark.
The conventional picture of a cell even now is that of a bag of molecules dissolved in water. That is what was in my biochemistry text 40 years ago and that story is copied and pasted for first-year med students in 2023. It is crazy.

And through bumping into one another by chance -- random collisions -- those molecules that have complementary shapes lock onto each other so the appropriate biochemical reactions can take place. This 'lock and key' model has been refined to a more flexible (and realistic) 'induced fit' hypothesis that allows each molecule to change shape slightly to fit the other better after they get in touch, but the main idea remains the same.
It is supposed to explain how enzymes can recognize their respective substrates, and how antibodies in the immune system can grab onto specific foreign invaders and disarm them. By extension, that's how proteins can 'dock' with different partner proteins, latch onto specific nucleic acids to control gene expression, or assemble into ribosomes for translating proteins, or other multi-molecular complexes that modify the genetic messages in various ways. But with thousands -- or even hundreds of thousands of reactions happening each second in just one cell this seems pushing the "mechanical" concept a bit too far.

What has been proposed is that somehow each molecule sends out a unique electromagnetic field that can "sense" the field of the complimentary molecule. It's as if there is a "dance" in the cellular medium and the molecules move to the rhythm. The music is supplied by the biophoton made by metabolism and the wide band-gapped semiconductors inside of us.

Veljkovic and Cosic proposed that molecular interactions are electrical in nature, and they take place over distances that are large compared with the size of molecules. Cosic later introduced the idea of dynamic electromagnetic field interactions, that molecules recognize their particular targets and vice versa by electromagnetic resonance. Cosic was the researcher I mentioned in the Huberman podcast who used blue light to cure Crigler-Najjar Syndrome via a molecular resonance model. [https://epjnonlinearbiomedphys.springeropen.com/articles/10.1140/epjnbp/s40366-016-0036-6](https://epjnonlinearbiomedphys.springeropen.com/articles/10.1140/epjnbp/s40366-016-0036-6)

In other words, the molecules emit electromagnetic signals at very specific frequencies which not only enable them to 'see' and 'hear' each other, as both photon and phonon modes exist for electromagnetic waves, but also to influence each other at a distance and become ineluctably drawn to each other if vibrating out of phase (in a complementary way).

### PaulG — 2014-03-31T04:54:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/suggestion-for-a-kruse-recommendations-online-questionnaire-kroq-not-grok.10397/post-118909>

Would be good if there was an online multiple choice questionnaire that encapsulates some of the kruse recommendations so that you end up with an overall score out of 100 and an action plan at the end.

I know everyone's n=1 is different but I think some recommendations are good for everyone e.g. epi-paleo diet, emf reduction, good water

**Example questions**
How many times do you do full immersion CT?
How often do you eat shellfish?
How much sunshine do you get?

you get the gist. Questions could be grouped into categories such as Testing, Diet, Lifestyle, Exercise, EMF Reduction

I think many of the recommendations are scattered so this project would be a good place to draw them together although due to context may pose problems.

Would be a good tool for new people and older forum members to gauge where they are at and to give an honest reality check. Personally I know I delude myself that I am doing well but have issues putting theory into action, for example have only just bought a cast iron bath for outside CTing and still using 3G (though not wifi)

What do y'all reckon? Don't think it would take much time to get up and running as there are tools online to do this sort of thing (and indeed it may be on Misty's radar already)

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-31T20:40:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/suggestion-for-a-kruse-recommendations-online-questionnaire-kroq-not-grok.10397/post-119101>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=119027)
>
> this reminds me of the story in the bible (thanks daddy ) of the 7 good and the 7 lazy bridesmaids.. was it 7 BTW? I might remember it wrong.... but I do remember the message of the story.. we all will have to gather the oil for our lamp ourselves.
> I thought as a kid it was kind of a cruel story... why could they not share some.... I just felt so bad for the lazy bridesmaids that were left out in the night because they were too late...

The parable of the wise and foolish virgins, Inger.

You have to remember it's a parable. That's to say what it means comes in the form of a story. You might ask why, and that's what I always wondered as a kid. I thought, "If there's an explanation, why not give that, too? Or why not _just_ give that?"

I think the answer to that is that our culture makes us think in that way -- and that's a whole topic in itself. We think if we have an "explanation" to hold, then we've really got something. That's debatable.

You may recall that Jesus' disciples ask him, after hearing the parable of the sower:

> “Why do you speak to them in parables?”

And he offers a reason:

[http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matthew 13&version=RSV](http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matthew+13&version=RSV)

The answer's rather cryptic, too. What I _**don't**_ think is that he's practising some form of hiding of doctrine (_ketman_) -- like the [Druze](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Druze), for example, do. (And like the the (Leo) [Straussian](http://plato.stanford.edu/entries/strauss-leo/) political philosophers in the U.S. have been accused of doing.) It's not that there are two doctrines: an _esoteric_ doctrine for an inner circle and an _exoteric_ doctrine, which is totally different, for everyone else.

The truth would seem to be this. Stories are easier to remember and make an immediate impact on our consciousness. Everything that's in the explanation is already implicit in the story. It's not one doctrine for one lot of people and another for another lot. The explanation can actually be misunderstood, despite what we think, and those who hear it and _think_ they know what it's about may, so to speak, hurt themselves with it. But the parable can't really hurt you if you don't see what it's about.

The traditional interpretation of the parable going back, I think, to the Fathers of the Church, is that the oil in the lamp represents "charity" -- love in the sense of disinterested love of one's neighbour, a positive outpouring of goodwill (_caritas_ in the Latin, _agape_ in the Greek). Basically, it's no good turning up without that. It's not that you'll get a "reward" for doing so, and don't think of heaven as a place (a location in space). It's just that you must have love.

> Though I speak with the tongues of men and of angels, and have not charity, I am become as sounding brass, or a tinkling cymbal.
>
> And though I have the gift of prophecy, and understand all mysteries, and all knowledge; and though I have all faith, so that I could remove mountains, and have not charity, I am nothing. ...

[http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=1 corinthians 13&version=KJV](http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=1+corinthians+13&version=KJV)

I like this interpretation of the ten virgins

"Thus, to be wise is to know what to do, know how to do it, and then actually to do it"

[http://books.google.com.au/books?id...age&q=wise and foolish virgins nicoll&f=false](http://books.google.com.au/books?id=dLFdnHzUJCcC&pg=PA98&lpg=PA98&dq=wise+and+foolish+virgins+nicoll&source=bl&ots=5HEMixvN4V&sig=VBBP7wDWDi_5uFIYyoUmiZQ2f1o&hl=en&sa=X&ei=yPU5U9nvE6HtiAe8hAE&ved=0CCsQ6AEwAA#v=onepage&q=wise%20and%20foolish%20virgins%20nicoll&f=false)

Jungian psychologist Maurice Nicoll wrote of the psychological meaning of this parable in his book "The New Man."

"Wisdom" itself must be understood. It is applied understanding: practical application of intelligence plus a 'new heart' or feeling--to know how to do and actually to do the right thing at the right time. (People who imagine that believing sentimentally in Jesus is all that's necessary are "foolish," the opposite of wise.) In the gospels, it is used to mean the right action of an intelligent person seeking a higher level (the 'bridegroom') of himself or herself through inner evolution, and that's what this parable is about.

The wise virgins here are not sentimental but practical; they realized what is necessary to reach this higher level called here the bridegroom. This is represented by their having oil in their lamps. A lamp gives light...here, not physical light but the light of understanding. The lamp here is intellectual knowledge, but it can't give light alone. "Oil" is the understanding that comes from actually realizing and living the new knowledge, from practically applying it to oneself ("Everyone that heareth these words of mind and DOETH them, shall be likened unto a WISE man...and everyone that heareth these words of mine and doeth them not, shall be likened unto a FOOLISH man.")

Obviously people can hear a higher Truth yet continue to act only from their habitual level of life, not following a new meaning but continuing to follow the outer ways of the world. These have lamps but no oil. They must go to the sellers (those churches, counselors, or social authorities who tell you what is meritorious) and buy it (get their understanding from meritorious actions in life, which is the only kind of Good they value). They remain basically the same people they were before, but just obey the rules. This is their problem: their actual lives are not governed by their knowledge; they know one thing and will do another. It is not that the door is shut on them--they shut the door on themselves.

Nicoll: "In this parable, the wise virgins are those who actually seek to live from their own understanding of what they have been taught and seek the Good of what they have been taught by practicing, by applying, from their own wills, the knowledge to themselves. The foolish, on the other hand, knowing the teaching, continue to seek their Good from life, from rewards, from reputation, from being first, getting to higher and higher positions, having better morals than others, being thought well of, from outwardly conforming to laws and social standards, when internally they are quite different and are only restrained by fear. This is the only Good they know and so they must follow it."

### caroline — 2014-03-31T05:58:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-feet-and-i.10398/post-118934>

My feet and I need your help please ...

They are stiff and feel as if they are retaining fluid...this has been an on going problem for a couple of years. I think they were better - but seem to be worse lately.

Dr. K. Suggest I am having issues with NGF. Tied to low BDNF and low PG/E2 ratio

I will do a saliva ASI this week ....... I would bet big money that pg is low...has been low in the past

I need a game plan...
Walking in the surf everyday
Stay off this stupid laptop
Soaking feet in Epsom salts
Lots of water and seafood

Any other ideas?

I am really, really worried about flying ...

### Inger — 2014-03-31T09:49:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-feet-and-i.10398/post-118974>

Are you walking barefoot Audrey, everywhere outside where is grass, earth stones and sand? My feet always get way prettier when I do this... ok they are super dirty all summer long haha, I try to soak them in natural soap water to get the dirt off - it is almost impossible! But at least they are healthy... lol

I think your plan is great

I think flying might be not so good idea  If you do, take care to live super great before and do anything you can to prevent damage. Do not use electronic devices at the beach.. and try to walk further along the shore - away from the beach with all the guys in speedos  (because probably there are many using cellphones etc. so going somewhere where no one else is would be clever I guess)

Could some cycling in the air with your feet help? Or laying on the floor with feet along the wall, like an L..? Just for some time every day? After the soak you could also massage your feet with some EVOO or coconut oil...
You could try to put wet sea salt / epsom salt soaked cotton socks + dry wool socks on your feet in the night. No idea if it would help this problem.. but in Germany they have this great Meine Base-Salz from Dr Jentschura, you put some in water, wet socks with it and use them over night. Makes pretty feet  the Jentschura salt and mineral water has a PH around 8,5 I think. It is all natural, with minerals and sea salt in it.

[http://www.amazon.de/Jentschura-Mei...ostenlose-Zugabe/dp/B001XO2MEE#productDetails](http://www.amazon.de/Jentschura-MeineBase%C2%AE-Portionen-Kr%C3%A4utertee-kostenlose-Zugabe/dp/B001XO2MEE#productDetails)

### sooperb — 2014-03-31T14:30:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-feet-and-i.10398/post-119041>

Audrey, are your feet swollen all the time or do they go down overnight? Swollen feet have a few causes, heart problems (probably unlikely in your case) or vascular issues. I currently have some swelling in my right foot. I'm pretty convinced that it's to do with a developing varicose vein in my right leg, all the signs are there, bumps under the skin, a cluster of tiny veins on my ankle and some pain behind my knee. As it isn't causing problems I haven't bothered about it until the foot started swelling so I guess it's diy vein stripping (I'd have to be very drunk lol) or going to see a doctor or waiting until it's a problem because that's what they will probably tell me. It's apparently considered a cosmetic issue by the NHS so in some areas they don't do anything!

### Inger — 2014-04-04T08:45:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-feet-and-i.10398/post-119728>

Yay about the labs Audrey

I wonder if it helps hammer toes to walk in naturally bumpy terrain barefoot, like in the woods or on a stony shore? I think I have read somewhere foot gymnastics helps for hammer toes too but I am not sure.. would not hurt to try?

> n the earlier stages of hammer toe, when the toes can still be manually straightened, then conservative treatment is appropriate. This means wearing shoes which are a half size bigger than normal and which are not narrow around the toes. Exercises to stretch the toes out and strengthen the muscles under the foot which balances the tightness of the top tendons are important. Padding or corn plasters can be used to ease the discomfort of any associated corns and calluses.

[http://www.sportsinjuryclinic.net/sport-injuries/foot-heel-pain/hammer-toe-1](http://www.sportsinjuryclinic.net/sport-injuries/foot-heel-pain/hammer-toe-1)

[http://www.sportsinjuryclinic.net/sport-injuries/foot-heel-pain/hammer-toe-1](http://www.sportsinjuryclinic.net/sport-injuries/foot-heel-pain/hammer-toe-1)

> [](http://www.sportsinjuryclinic.net/sport-injuries/foot-heel-pain/hammer-toe-1)
>
> [
>
> If you notice the beginning signs of hammertoe, you may be able to prevent the tendons from tightening by wearing toe-friendly shoes, by flattening your toes regularly, and by soaking your feet every day in warm water, then stretching your toes and ankles by pointing your toes.
>
> Foot exercises also can help to maintain or restore the flexibility of the tendons. One simple exercise is to place a small towel on the floor and then pick it up using only your toes. You also can grasp at carpet with your toes or curl your toes up and down repeatedly.
>
> ](http://www.sportsinjuryclinic.net/sport-injuries/foot-heel-pain/hammer-toe-1)

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=119041)

> Audrey, are your feet swollen all the time or do they go down overnight? Swollen feet have a few causes, heart problems (probably unlikely in your case) or vascular issues. I currently have some swelling in my right foot. I'm pretty convinced that it's to do with a developing varicose vein in my right leg, all the signs are there, bumps under the skin, a cluster of tiny veins on my ankle and some pain behind my knee. As it isn't causing problems I haven't bothered about it until the foot started swelling so I guess it's diy vein stripping (I'd have to be very drunk lol) or going to see a doctor or waiting until it's a problem because that's what they will probably tell me. It's apparently considered a cosmetic issue by the NHS so in some areas they don't do anything!

I have a couple of varicose veins too but mostly spider veins. I have a family history of deep vein thrombosis so not sure if that's relevant. My sister-in-law works in a doctor's office and she has varicose vein treatment all the time (twice a year). She tells me that we have too many veins and we should prune them like a tree but this doesn't seem logical to me. Has anyone here read of any harm that comes from varicose vein removal or laser treatment? @ [sooperb](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?members/sooperb.8412/) [@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/)

Barefoot walking is great advice.
It worked for me.
But I had a pain dancing.
For mere $500 for orthotics, insurance paid for doctors visit, and I had monstrous orthotics for which I had to buy different dancing shoes. Another $150.
Eventually I was able to find $19 nice little orthotics that fit any shoe.
My problem was a metatarsal pain, two bones that are to close, rubbing.
Eventually after 3-4 years of use all went back into place and I do not need anything.
(But on my weekly chiropractic adjustments, he also works on my feet.)

So, possibly chiropractor may be worth trying.

The $19 was just final price.
looking at my ($500) orthotics I was guessing and buying orthotics at amazon.
The third $19 worked.
Mine were totally made of plastic.
This is closest I can locate now that looks similar to what I have been using.

....
Pedag 2879 Holiday 3/4 Leather Ultra Light, Thin, Semi-Rigid Orthotic with Metatarsal Pad, Arch Support and Padding at the Heel, Black, Women's 8 EU 38

# Thread: High histamine / heartburn
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-histamine-heartburn.10407/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2014-03-31T16:31:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-histamine-heartburn.10407/post-119055>

I'm in a tailspin lately (stress of quitting my job is probably making things worse, I'm sure). I've suddenly developed major histamine problems where everything I eat (since it's all high histamine - organ meats, seafood, etc) is causing me to burn up like crazy or shiver like mad!

It seems I start out shivering in my chest in the mornings after my BAB and sometimes my neck and face will be burning up at the same time. As the day goes on, I just ROAST. My face and ears turn bright red and are literally hot to the touch. I read on Amy Yasko's site that high histamine is associated with high levels of stomach acid, which I suppose could explain my sudden heartburn.

I know this is all related to poor redox. I'm working on that, but does anyone have any great tips for trying to manage these issues in the meantime? I'm literally getting about 2 hours of sleep at night because I am just HOT and can't get comfortable. I keep the window open and last night it dropped to 27 degrees and I was still burning up. I'm putting ice packs on my carotid arteries at night. I take 500 mg Quercetin with each meal, but none of this is helping and the heartburn is getting worse and more painful.

I actually had heartburn this morning at 5 AM and I hadn't even eaten since 5 PM the night before.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-31T17:18:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-histamine-heartburn.10407/post-119060>

you could consider betaine HCl in a situation like this. it can be bought over-the-counter

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-31T19:12:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-histamine-heartburn.10407/post-119093>

I would stay away from all fermented foods while you have the histamine reaction. They contain both histamines and tyramines. Even though they are advocated by many for probiotic effect they may no tbe good for your context

### BJK77 — 2014-03-31T19:41:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-histamine-heartburn.10407/post-119098>

Thanks Jack. I appreciate the help. At this point I haven't touched anything fermented for a few months. I'm on a strict meat, seafood and fat diet with the occasional avocado. It's all I can get down even though I know all of those foods are high histamine. I'm not sure what else I can even attempt to eat these days.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-31T20:36:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-histamine-heartburn.10407/post-119100>

try to go more raw like Inger does......on everything and I bet it improves. Betaine is not a bad idea.

### Josh — 2014-03-31T21:00:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-histamine-heartburn.10407/post-119107>

Thoughts:

1.  Giardia is famous for epigastric discomfort, but it is usually burping and distension.
2.  Heartburn is often low stomach acid as the food rots in the stomach producing acid.
3.  Low vagal tone, stuff stays in the stomach
4.  trauma along the stomach meridian and head trauma may "sedate" stomach function causing the above. Acupuncture, mudpacking, structural work may help
5.  All of the above may create enough stress to throw the SCN off and create chronic stress and take down the adrenals creating underpowered hypersensitive dysregulated immune responses

### BJK77 — 2014-04-01T18:36:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-histamine-heartburn.10407/post-119255>

Tried 500 mg HCL this morning with my BAB, but I didn't notice much difference yet. The heartburn seems to be worse at the end of the day though. I only get very minimal pain in the morning after BAB. I'll try upping to 1,000 mg tomorrow morning. And I'll try 500 mg tonight with my dinner, which is usually pretty small - just a few scallops or a couple ounces of sashimi.

My lunch is only 6 raw oysters, so I'm hesitant to take any with that small amount of food.

I'll also work on getting in more raw foods. Like I said, my lunch is always 6 raw oysters and I've been eating tons of rare liver lately - not raw, but rare. I almost wonder at times if I could be overdoing the liver, but I seem to be craving it like crazy. I'm eating at least 4 ounces per day. Should I be concerned about getting too much?

### BJK77 — 2014-04-01T21:09:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-histamine-heartburn.10407/post-119285>

Aah! 6 hours after taking the 500 mg HCL and eating some very rare liver and my heart is racing uncontrollably, I am so nauseous I can't even think about lunch and I'm shivering like crazy although my head and face are burning up. Could this all be related?

I had just gone to the store this morning and got some fresh sashimi and oysters for lunch. Now I'm scared to eat. I first noticed the heart racing in the store, but it was pretty mild there. Now it's very bad.

### BJK77 — 2014-04-01T21:26:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-histamine-heartburn.10407/post-119289>

Just read some scary info about ketosis caising all of these issues and leading to heart problems. I have to say I'm getting awfully scared with the heart racing. Thoughts anyone?

### Jude — 2014-04-02T01:31:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-histamine-heartburn.10407/post-119322>

It took me so long to work out tea and coffee were a major part of the problem for me.....I was always concerned that it was emf exposure but it was no worse in saturated areas......I hope you can find some relief soon

### Inger — 2014-04-02T08:49:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-histamine-heartburn.10407/post-119371>

can you try to be outside barefoot and drink lots of water to help the heart racing? I think even better if you sit on the ground / grass your bare feet planted on the ground and your both hands too. Like resting on your hands. I do this and it makes me feel very good. Maybe it would be helpful if you list all your supplements and what you eat/drink too... oh man I wish I could help..  it does not sound very fun, the heart racing...
The liver you are eating, is it great quality? and fresh.. as long as you have the histamine issue you really should try to eat everything super fresh... but I know that is not always easy. Frozen fish eaten directly after thawing might even be better than fresh bought because they mostly are some days old.. the frozen ones usually are frozen immediately after caught.
Have you tried to eat veggies? I really have no idea... but maybe, if I was in your situation.. I would try them for a while to see if I do not react to them working on fixing the other issues.. and believe me I am not a veggie fan at all
Are there wild edibles coming up? like dandelion, nettles, chickweed, goutweed, linden leaves.. etc? I would do lots of wild greens picked and eaten directly from the ground, you could be out barefoot picking them in the fresh air  I have done this already this year many times! It always is such a joy

Frozen fish eaten directly after thawing might even be better than fresh bought because they mostly are some days old.. the frozen ones usually are frozen immediately after caught.

It always is such a joy

Good thoughts Inger! Earthing outside is the only thing that stops the heart racing. I spent most of yesterday afternoon out there to try to calm things down and it did help. This morning it's raining and I spent a good hour sitting under a tree reading. It was wonderful, but I can't do that all day every day. I need to figure out what's causing this.

Fresh is hard. I try to thaw my meats and cook them right away saving in the fridge only what I'll eat for the next two days and freezing the rest. It is really tough with meats and fish though.

I'm really stuck with veggies too because I seem to be so limited - no high oxalate veggies, no sulfur veggies. That doesn't leave me with much. That's why I had been relying on lots of avocado, but now I see that is high histamine! I did try a little butternut squash this morning with my breakfast, but I'm still getting the heart racing. Unfortunately we only just got rid of all the snow covering the ground, so I don't have anything wild coming up yet

I will post what I'm eating/supplementing and see if anyone has ideas as to what's going on. Like I said, this seems to be the WORST after my BAB.

**BAB**
\- usually 50 gm+ protein from pork roast
\- pork fat, lard

**Lunch**
\- 6 raw oysters
\- 1/2 - 1 avocado

**Dinner**
\- scallops

These have been my "go-to" meals for the last couple of weeks. I honestly don't know what else to eat with my limitations! Everytime I try to go higher fat I crash pretty hard. My legs get weak and shakey and I just don't feel well.

**Supplements**
Naturethroid: 3/4 mcg 2x/day

**With BAB:**
\- Vitamin D - 12,000 IU
\- Vitamin K - 15 mg
\- D-Ribose - 15 mg
\- CoQ10 - 300 mg
\- Quercetin - 500 mg
\- Yucca - 1000 mg
\- Welchol - 2 tablets
\- Molybdenum - 150 mcg

**Lunch**
\- 500 mg Quercetin
\- 2 Welchol tablets

**Dinner**
\- 500 mg Quercetin
\- 2 Welchol tablets

Any thoughts on whether any of these supplements could be a problem?

# Thread: Nassim Haramein, the Einstein of our generation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nassim-haramein-the-einstein-of-our-generation.10411/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BradTheBreather — 2014-03-31T20:52:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nassim-haramein-the-einstein-of-our-generation.10411/post-119104>

I recent stumbled across some incredible ideas about the fundamental nature of the universe. Nassim Haramein offers a framework for a unified theory that solves Einstein's field equations at the scale of a proton, eliminating the need for things like dark matter, dark energy, and the strong and weak nuclear force.

I have only a basic understanding of physics but as I have been exploring, it has really been blowing my mind so I felt the need to share it with some people that might actually listen...

The tip of the iceberg of implications: free energy, extraterrestrial involvement in human evolution, telepathy, teleportation....

The video that introduced me to it:

The website of the Nassim's research center in Hawaii:
[http://resonance.is/](http://resonance.is/)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-31T21:44:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nassim-haramein-the-einstein-of-our-generation.10411/post-119111>

[http://thrivedebunked.wordpress.com/2012/03/09/who-is-nassim-haramein/](http://thrivedebunked.wordpress.com/2012/03/09/who-is-nassim-haramein/)

I have gotten at least 20 emails from people about him.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-31T21:44:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nassim-haramein-the-einstein-of-our-generation.10411/post-119112>

metaphysics does not = QED

### BradTheBreather — 2014-04-02T17:35:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nassim-haramein-the-einstein-of-our-generation.10411/post-119413>

Its easy to find reactions to extraordinary ideas. Does this eliminate the need to maintain an open mind and really explore them? I know this critique of you is mostly name calling and ridiculous but I post it to raise a point. Anyone (not just Nassim) who will present the radical shift in viewpoint that is required to create and understand a potential unification theory is going to have plenty of dissenters, and the presence of opposing arguments shouldn't make us ignore what could be a new way of looking at things.

You say it yourself all the time, "the truth will set you free, but the first time you hear it, it will piss you off".

This Bob-a-thon guy attacks Haramein, saying he doesn't have any physics credentials, while not presenting any reason to believe that Bob-a-tho knows anything about physics. I believe a lot can be done in the world of theoretical physics and mathematics outside the mainstream institutions, but I actually read Bob's critique and I get the sense that he is just a sexually-frustrated physics grad student in his late 20s. Even with my modest 6-8 hours of investigation into Haramein's work, I can tell that Bob-a-thon is misunderstanding some of the basic concepts and still very attached to his comfortable way of thinking in the standard model. When he tries to debunk the actual physics of Haramein, I'm not convinced. Most of the text is name calling and when he actually does bring in some reasoning, its something like "this doesn't agree with anything before" or "this is way off from the established laws"...

If it were aligned with what has been done, we would have already solved unification. Its easy to get caught up in the idea that we already know everything (or that we can _know_ anything for that matter), but really at the end of the day, even the things that appear most solid in the world are really made up of mostly empty space.

Haramein responded to Bob-a-thons arguments and the usual critiques of his work at great length here: [http://web.archive.org/web/20111110...nceproject.org/schwarzschild-proton-manifesto](http://web.archive.org/web/20111110193243/http://theresonanceproject.org/schwarzschild-proton-manifesto)
Please devote at least the same amount of time to this article as you did to Bob-a-thons and then I will hear what you have to say.

Jack, I have an incredible amount of respect for you and your ability to do what you know is right, even when it goes against everything that has gone before, and a lot of your recommendations have very greatly helped me on my personal health journey and my understanding of my own health. That said, things are different now than when you first started. You are not fighting as much to have people listen to you. Instead you have over 9000 people just waiting to read every word you put on this forum, and any dissenters are immediately quieted by your disciples. If I were in your position, it would be easy to use my own incredibly vast and complex way of thinking to justify anything I want as well as disprove anything I want. Part of what makes you great is how much you build and interact with your community and answer every question you can, but part of what _made_ you great in the first place was questioning what you yourself had thought all your life...

Personally, science is not my only means of finding truth. I respect it for sure, but I acknowledge its limitations in affecting my subjective experience (which is all that really matters when you come down to it). Spend a couple hours in a flotation tank or take a trip down to Peru to participate in an Ayahuasca ritual and you will see there is much more out there than can be measured with a lab test. When I first learned about the more philosophical side of QED, it really resonated with me and explained a lot of things in my life. That's why I love your work with applying it to health. It simply makes sense on many levels.

When I first heard Haramein's arguments, they just made everything click. A unification theory won't be a complex physics equation, it will be a simple idea that not only unifies QED with relativistic physics, but unifies all branches of science and unifies science with philosophy, spirituality, art, music, history, archaeology and everything else there is. Haramein's model is in the early stages of development obviously, but for me that was enough to see unification of a lot of things in my own life.

I will conclude with this quote from chapter 12 of the Tao Te Ching (Stephen Mitchell translation)

"Colors blind the eye.
Sounds deafen the ear.
Flavors numb the taste.
Thoughts weaken the mind.
Desires wither the heart.

The Master observes the world
but trusts his inner vision.
He allows things to come and go.
His heart is as open as the sky."

### BradTheBreather — 2014-04-02T18:25:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nassim-haramein-the-einstein-of-our-generation.10411/post-119423>

Besides, you're the one who talks about crystals and vortex energies... Maybe there is a QED basis for some metaphysical concepts...

### Josh — 2014-04-02T18:44:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nassim-haramein-the-einstein-of-our-generation.10411/post-119427>

From the bridge, we are children in playing in a sandbox....consider....

> And the way it has been written is such that those who are not ready for it will turn their back
> automatically. A similar case has happened in this century with Gurdjieff. For thirty years
> continuously, he was preparing one book. A man of the caliber of Gurdjieff can do that work in
> three days. Even three days may be more than enough. Lao Tzu had done it: in three days the
> whole TAO TEH CHING was written. Gurdjieff can do it in three days; there is no difficulty. But for
> thirty years he was writing his first book. And what he was doing? He will write one chapter, and then
> he will allow it to be read before his disciples. Disciples will be listening to the chapter, and he will
> be looking to the disciples. If they can understand, he will change it. That was the condition: if they
> can understand, he will change it. If he sees that they are following it, then it is wrong. Continuously,
> for thirty years, every chapter was read a thousand and one times, and every time he was watching.
> When the book became completely impossible, that no one can read and understand it...
> Even a very intelligent person will have to read it at least seven times; then glimpses of meaning will
> start coming. And that too will be just glimpses. If he wants to penetrate more, then he will have to
> practice whatsoever he has said, and through practice meanings will become clear. And it will take
> at least one’s whole life to come to the total understanding what he has written.
> This type of book cannot be interpolated. Really, his first book, it is said that very few people have
> read it completely. It is difficult – one thousand pages. So when the first edition was published, he
> published it with a condition: only hundred pages, the introductory part, were cut. All other pages
> were not cut; they were uncut. Only hundred pages were cut, and a note was given on the book
> that ”If you can read the first hundred pages and still think to read ahead, then cut other pages.
> Otherwise, return the book to the publisher and take your money back.”
> It is said that there are very few people alive who have read the whole book completely. It is written
> in such a way that you will get fed up. Reading twenty, twenty-five pages, it is enough; and this man
> seems to be mad.

Osho, Yoga The Alpha and the Omega Vol 1, #6

It took me 5 times to penetrate into Gurdjieff and then it broke my heart open, I do not buy easy as a path to anything beyond a glimpse....

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-02T19:18:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nassim-haramein-the-einstein-of-our-generation.10411/post-119432>

I question everything..........because my curiosity requires it. But I also know when the boundaries have been stretched with out foundation.........and I call BS on things I dont buy. You can not know good without knowing bad.........balance in knowledge and wisdom are the key.

### Shijin13 — 2014-04-01T17:23:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lab-review-help.10412/post-119243>

HDL is low, and Trigs are High you need to get them flipped around. you want HDL above 60 and Trigs below 60. Your LDL is high b/c of pregnenolone steal & b/c your vitamin A cycle is off - which is causing the low pregnenolone levels. Your thyroid isn't working well even with the Levothyroxine. FWIW some of the generics of Levothyroxine contains gluten. I'd bet you're making Reverse T3 instead of Free T3 - which also impacts your conversion of LDL to Pregnenolone... which is why your testosterone is in the tank (and is also why your DHEA will be in the tank)

Next time ask your doc for a TSH, Free T4, Free T3, and Reverse T3. Make sure you also get a Vit D3 - 25 hydroxy run too...

Start working on the 3 legged stool, and cut all carbs out to begin working on the HDL and Trigs. you probably also need to consider supplementation with Pregnenolone. As JanSz - I'm not a doctor! However I am a biohacker and I can read labs...

### JNV — 2014-04-01T17:42:43+0300
