### Phil Escott — 2014-04-09T12:40:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-to-arthritis-and-so-much-more%E2%80%A6.10457/post-120568>

By the way, can I give out a blatant plug here, in case anybody is around the north of England and looking for hands-on help in any of the areas I've discussed? Two amazing ladies with very open minds, tons of wisdom and superb senses of humour – Gabrielle Heyes, a wonderful naturopath who helped me so much early on and was so uncannily accurate with diagnoses, and Meidi Goodson, who knows all there is to know about emotional rebalancing and opened up a whole new world to me. They both have a good knowledge of paleo principles and GAPS too, and are very well aware of Dr Kruse's work. If you follow the link in my sig and look on the resources page, you can see links to them both. Thanks, Gabi and Meidi!!  xx

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2014-04-09T13:37:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-to-arthritis-and-so-much-more%E2%80%A6.10457/post-120572>

_A lot of people (me included) miss the point with these techniques at first and root around in their pasts trying to uncover every little trauma, but it's far simpler than that, and I can't stress it enough – whatever the mind and body is throwing up at any given time is the perfect thing to work on._

Phil - I really like that way of putting things and I echo that statement big time! I was/am involved in 'primal therapy' and that (rooting around in the past etc) is a definite danger. Someone said it can be like discovering dental floss, it seems great at first but it's like the dental floss then gets caught in your teeth and THAT becomes a problem (this is an image only I am not specifically talking about dental floss)

And this thing about working on whatever the body and mind throws up is sort of exactly how I think of it. Before Kruse (could we shorten that to BK as opposed to BC ) it's like I had 'only' the mind to 'work' on and it's like it gets played out! I also agree with you though that just working on the body can also get played out, it becomes 'reductionist' and kind of a cul-de-sac of it's own. There is something about ANY approach no matter how good it is or seems to be at first over time can and very often does become a kind of 'tunnel vision' of it's own

Now it's like I am never 'bored' something is 'happening' always and for me at least or the way I think of it kind of breaks down to 'mind' or 'body' and then the specifics of that. Healing is always specifics! Devil is in the details. But what I like now is I do not have to 'force' anything, let whatever comes up come up. I think your notions about auto-immune and anger or anger at the self is very interesting but I agree very much with you it is that kind of combination of mind AND body that can to seem to really help (at least for me). Or sometimes I think I was SO effed up it takes more than one approach or one great mind to help me solve my problems.

My physical problem is diabetes type 2 which seems now to be basically OK. I am Irish living in LA but I have a brother lives in Yorkshire UK and he has auto immune. Something called sarcoidosis which is very unpleasant. I have mentioned to him JK's site but have not heard back. He is a Chemical Engineer and quite knowledable about chemisty etc but weirdly seems to have so little interest in HIMSELF. Like he 'intellectually' knows a lot but never applies it to himself, I feel bad for him and wish I could help but my family has always seen me as a bit of a 'hippy weirdo'. My sister is a medical doctor and kind of the same syndrome there, she does not seem to think how could she help her brother.

That was one aspect of Dr K's recent work with Jeremy I really admire.............like he meets this guy with this problem and he basically thinks 'let me work this out or figure this out' To me that's the way docs should be, they have the 'knowledge' now let's really USE it, use it to discover and HELP people. I wish I was a doctor I have a smattering of knowledge but if I had the whole picture (and qualifications) I would aim to be a kind of doctor like Dr K. There seems to be a great lack of 'curiosity' so to speak, like I come more from the 'mental health' field psychology etc but there to me there is a lot of 'low curiosity'. Like that field could be really energised and 'irrigated' by the work of say Dr K but they rather stick to what is comfortable for them, and what they 'know' (if they do 'know' lol)

Anyway Phil just to say I think it is very 'cool' what you are doing and let us know when the book comes out. What kind of music do you play? I picture you in a Judas Priest type band. Forgive me if I am way off!

### sooperb — 2014-04-09T15:59:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-to-arthritis-and-so-much-more%E2%80%A6.10457/post-120582>

"my family has always seen me as a bit of a 'hippy weirdo'."

I can relate to that, not so much the hippy (although my husband often asks where my kaftan and sandals are lol, especially the time I had all my hair cropped short). Why is anything you try to do for yourself outside of a GP consultation considered weird? He's type ll, he went for his check up today, all well apparently, even his good cholesterol is up, nothing to do with the way we eat now at my instigation, apparently he's still taking his statins now and again, that must be it eh! So she is complimenting him on doing well and mentions it's nearly time for his foot check up and "you don't walk around barefoot I hope"? Oh no, says he, but my wife does all the time. Well says she, I agree with your wife, but not for you to do it, diabetics mustn't walk around barefoot, it's dangerous. Do you walk around barefoot at all Patrick?

Interesting theories Phil, I am often curious about what comes first, feeling better and then looking over your shoulder and attributing how you think it worked out, or doing the thinking first, following your ideas and then finding that you started to heal? I am really pleased for you and all the others that have turned their health around no matter how it came about, it must be a relief to get your life back.

### Phil Escott — 2014-04-09T16:13:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-to-arthritis-and-so-much-more%E2%80%A6.10457/post-120588>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=120582)
>
> Apparently he's still taking his statins now and again
>
> "you don't walk around barefoot I hope"?
>
> I am often curious about what comes first, feeling better and then looking over your shoulder and attributing how you think it worked out, or doing the thinking first, following your ideas and then finding that you started to heal? .

Aaargh, statins!!

Dangerous to walk barefoot. That's really funny. Those poor cavemen must have really stressed out, roaming to the ends of the Earth, searching in vain for a shoe shop…

I think the magic happens when you don't worry what comes first – just pay attention to exactly what life is throwing up in the present and deal with it, which might be physical, and might be emotional, or might be spiritual. It's all the same game – all part of a beautiful whole! Life throws up exactly what we need whenever we need it, and most of the time we reject those gifts, criticise them and mistrust them. Life is much simpler than we make it…

### sooperb — 2014-04-09T16:27:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-to-arthritis-and-so-much-more%E2%80%A6.10457/post-120592>

"Life is much simpler than we make it…"

It used to be, these days we're so tied up in what we can't live without that it's hard to imagine going backwards. How do you choose what's best to give up for example, how do you know if what you gave up was the right choice? What if you give up pretty much everything and nothing changes? It's got me stumped!

### Phil Escott — 2014-04-09T16:36:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-to-arthritis-and-so-much-more%E2%80%A6.10457/post-120594>

> [patgrif@hotmail.com said:](/goto/post?id=120572)
>
> Phil - I really like that way of putting things and I echo that statement big time! I was/am involved in 'primal therapy' and that (rooting around in the past etc) is a definite danger. Someone said it can be like discovering dental floss, it seems great at first but it's like the dental floss then gets caught in your teeth and THAT becomes a problem (this is an image only I am not specifically talking about dental floss) I have a brother lives in Yorkshire UK and he has auto immune. Something called sarcoidosis which is very unpleasant. I have mentioned to him JK's site but have not heard back. He is a Chemical Engineer and quite knowledable about chemisty etc but weirdly seems to have so little interest in HIMSELF. Like he 'intellectually' knows a lot but never applies it to himself, I feel bad for him and wish I could help but my family has always seen me as a bit of a 'hippy weirdo'. My sister is a medical doctor and kind of the same syndrome there, she does not seem to think how could she help her brother.
>
> Anyway Phil just to say I think it is very 'cool' what you are doing and let us know when the book comes out. What kind of music do you play? I picture you in a Judas Priest type band. Forgive me if I am way off!

Hi patgrif. Thank you for your kind words. I love the dental floss analogy. That certainly rings true. there is a lot of power in healing the emotions, but if we come at it from the wrong angle, it can be a great big tangle, and people can easily be put off before they find the magic. We always seem to overcomplicate things.

As for your brother, I feel for him. Nasty business. Don't let it upset you if he won't listen though. I realised what a huge strain it was when I worried about everyone. I became a bit of an evangelist and it hurt me and annoyed others. I just did a blog on that on my site actually!  Suggest, then let go. If he asks more, then you can give more. We all have our own paths, and we can't interrupt that path however much we care. After all, look how many blessing illness brings! Are you sure you want to deprive anybody of that? He will heal when he needs to heal.

My music? Judas Priest eh? Haa ha!  No, not at all. Was it the "wife-beater" vest in my pic that made you think that? The last two things I have done… A band called e-god – very strange and pretentious but very creative. A bit prog, a bit space rock… unclassifiable really. Lots of visuals too – videos and virtual vocalists on screens, MIDI light show and lots of electronic instruments. I often wonder if being in the middle of all that technology was one of the triggers of my illness. If you go to the "Shop" page of my website (in my signature) there is a link to the e-god site where there is still some footage of one concert. Yes, the big fatty on the drums with the long dreadlocks is me, a few months before I got really ill.

Now I'm playing with a blues band – also linked to on that Shop page. It's the other end of the scale – acoustic drums, spontaneous stuff, lots of improvisation, no rehearsal… lovely. Far less EMF flying around. Fantastic guitarists too. I'm having a lot fun being "just the drummer". I'm half the size too.  Most vids on the internet of me playing with them though are from last summer when I was still in my very skinny phase before I started to rebuild. I think in e-god I'm about 200lbs, in the Wayne Carrick vids I'm an emaciated 130lbs (scary!) and now a nice, balanced 155lbs at 5ft 10ins.. The stages of healing in action!

### Phil Escott — 2014-04-09T16:44:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-to-arthritis-and-so-much-more%E2%80%A6.10457/post-120597>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=120592)
>
> "Life is much simpler than we make it…"
>
> It used to be, these days we're so tied up in what we can't live without that it's hard to imagine going backwards. How do you choose what's best to give up for example, how do you know if what you gave up was the right choice? What if you give up pretty much everything and nothing changes? It's got me stumped!

The trick is not really giving things up, but letting them go. Also, letting go of agonising over what you need to give up. Follow your heart. There is this old idea that it's somehow bad to have possessions and wealth for example. It's not; it's only damaging if you are attached to them. Just be in the present and feel what you need, and things will give you up rather than the other way around. Stressing over what to give up causes far more stress than the things themselves I reckon. And if you give up stressing, you get a clearer mind, your intuition grows and you know exactly what's good and what isn't.

Also, be careful what you force yourself to give up, and which dogmas you keep. There is a lovely story about that…

“A new monk arrives at the monastery. He is assigned to help the other monks in copying the old texts by hand. He notices, however, that they are copying copies, not the original books. So, the new monk goes to the head monk to ask him about this. He points out that if there were an error in the first copy, that error would be continued in all of the other copies.

The head monk says, “We have been copying from the copies for centuries, but you make a good point, my son.” So, he goes down into the cellar with one of the copies to check it against the original.

Hours later, nobody has seen him. So, one of the monks goes downstairs to look for him. He hears a sobbing coming from the back of the cellar, and finds the old monk leaning over one of the original books and crying. He asks what’s wrong.

“The word is ‘celebrate’,” says the old monk.”

### sooperb — 2014-04-09T16:50:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-to-arthritis-and-so-much-more%E2%80%A6.10457/post-120602>

That's made me smile, thank you.

So I followed my heart, it said this site was the place to find the holy grail. I have read and followed the wisdom. I have given up what seemed and felt that it was right to do so. Nothing has changed, am in the right place or not, I no longer feel quite so certain.

### Phil Escott — 2014-04-09T16:59:02+0300

There's only one surefire way to tell. If you're in that place – it's the right place for you, so enjoy!

### nonchalant — 2014-04-09T17:09:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-to-arthritis-and-so-much-more%E2%80%A6.10457/post-120610>

> [Phil Escott said:](/goto/post?id=120526)
>
> Hi Nonchalant. So good to "meet" you. I have enjoyed many of your posts over the years…  Now, I absolutely agree, and this is the area where it gets really interesting and it's what I really want to write about more than anything else given some very profound awakenings just before and during this "illness" of "mine".  I truly believe that our perception of ourselves as individual entities is fundamentally flawed and that we are just aspects of the same field of consciousness brought about to enjoy itself. Whatever happens to us is exactly the right thing that we need for our evolution, or rather for the evolution of pure consciousness in its journey back to itself. There is no other view that I have found that explains absolutely all of life's mysteries when they are observed in that light. I am a little reluctant to go into it too much though for fear of being labelled a crank, but that's where my book will really focus in the end after I've trawled through the other layers of illusion that we go through in healing when we still think that there is actually a body to heal! Right, I'll shut up now before the woowoo police arrive…

Oh, we like woo here. But you just have to be able to prove it scientifically.

### Phil Escott — 2014-04-09T17:18:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-to-arthritis-and-so-much-more%E2%80%A6.10457/post-120616>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=120610)
>
> Oh, we like woo here. But you just have to be able to prove it scientifically.

Haa ha!  Well, I will leave the science to Jack. I think he's close to putting God under the microscope anyway. (Not that the microscope is anything other than God). Physics is pretty much there anyway isn't it? At a quantum level everything is the same – very little difference if any between objects and space. It's all made of consciousness, and we keep looking for peace/God/happiness desperately. It's comical when you see what it's all really made of – it makes the eternal human quest seem like fish swimming around looking for the ocean.

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2014-04-09T17:52:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-to-arthritis-and-so-much-more%E2%80%A6.10457/post-120628>

Sooperb - you asked if I walk around with no shoes. Funny you should ask yesterday I went for a pretty good hike in the Santa Monica mountains and did it all barefoot! I didn't or don't even think like that about diabetes (maybe I should well no I don't think so) I LOVE that and was telling the friend I was with all about the 'pizo-electric' effect. In fact now that I think of it here is what I wrote to another friend yesterday before I went

_"Today I plan to not even take my shoes so all the way up and down the hill in bare feet..............I can FEEL the 'pizo-electric' effect.............pressure on the bottom of the feet goes all around the body, this was also the idea behind 'acu-pressure' I think"_

I also am at the beach a lot barefoot all the time. Yeah it's weird I don't even know exactly why the docs have this thing about not being barefoot. I used to hike with shoes until about a month ago and being barefoot has really enhanced it for me. As Dr K says the 'pizo-electric' effect is very real and the thing is as he explains that 'pressure' is referred ALL over the body via collagen and water. So it FEELS like an all over body massage even though it is just the feet. I have a little bit of numbness on the big toe of one foot and it seems to help that too.

Phil - you are a bit of a mind reader too! about the Judas Priest thing I guess I have to plead 'guilty' (Not that there is anything wrong with Judas Priest) Your music sounds very interesting, I am always amazed how much great music continues to come out of England. Like every few years a whole new thing. The States is more 'conservative' we still have Led Zepp on the radio all day long (Not that there is anything wrong with Led Zepp)

### SeaHorse — 2014-04-09T18:09:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-to-arthritis-and-so-much-more%E2%80%A6.10457/post-120630>

> [Phil Escott said:](/goto/post?id=120626)
>
> Looking forward to it… I think I've always suspected where you're going with all this… I just haven't quite had the IQ to follow you the whole way "overland". I took the cop out way and booked a flight because I don't have such a roomy vehicle as you to be able to stop and generously pick up so many fortunate hitchhikers. What I have managed to see of it has been a fascinating and scenic journey…

Hi Phil…I'm with you…while you've been up in your plane covering new territory, my metaphor of choice is surfing the waves getting all the good stuff and letting the rest just roll on by. Translating the information here into living a new life has been a balance for me….too much time in my head reading, reading, reading isn't good and yet the ideas and concepts that I've learned here as I've activated them are life changing. It's such a great group of people here you don't want to miss one minute of the party ….but the party is inside and we all need to go _out_ don't we? Can you tell this has been a long winter? Anyway, I just read your whole thread and wanted to give you a cyber high five.

### Phil Escott — 2014-04-09T21:43:01+0300

It's seldom on the radio apart from 2am sometimes… Ahem… not that I'm ever up then, Jack… Well, if I am, I'm driving back from a gig with my Blublockers on.

### Phil Escott — 2014-04-09T21:55:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-to-arthritis-and-so-much-more%E2%80%A6.10457/post-120665>

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=120630)
>
> Hi Phil…I'm with you…while you've been up in your plane covering new territory, my metaphor of choice is surfing the waves getting all the good stuff and letting the rest just roll on by. Translating the information here into living a new life has been a balance for me….too much time in my head reading, reading, reading isn't good and yet the ideas and concepts that I've learned here as I've activated them are life changing. It's such a great group of people here you don't want to miss one minute of the party ….but the party is inside and we all need to go _out_ don't we? Can you tell this has been a long winter? Anyway, I just read your whole thread and wanted to give you a cyber high five.

Hi Seahorse… Thank you and high five back.  It's great to "meet" you too, as I've read many of your posts while I've been lurking.

Actually, I posted that message about the plane hurriedly and then shot out of the front door for a walk around a few new lakes that I'm scoping out for a bit of carp fishing this summer (a bizarre English addiction), and I have to admit that while I've been walking I've been cringing in embarrassment at posting it. It seems like I'm saying that I've flown ahead of everyone, and it sounds really arrogant. That's not what I meant at all… What I meant by it was that Jack takes everyone systematically through all the levels of this knowledge in a logical order, whereas I've spent most of my life being either a hippy or a yogi and paying attention only to the "spiritual" side of things, ignoring the foundations. In truth, ignoring all the essential steps on the way was a big reason why I got ill, so I'm very grateful for the info on this site in particular as it has joined innumerable dots that I missed out… So, apologies to anyone who thought I was a bit too full of myself… Just a bad choice of words…

### Phil Escott — 2014-04-13T12:22:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-to-arthritis-and-so-much-more%E2%80%A6.10457/post-121232>

Hi Inger… Ooh, well I'm honoured you ask, but I'm no expert really – only with my own body. It's just that I've found a huge connection between emotions and the various body parts I've had affected. Oddly, never the shoulders – they are the best, strongest joints on my body, perhaps because I'm such an irresponsible, selfish old hippy (and NEVER watch the news) I never take the weight of the world on them.

The first thing that occurs is that it's easy to say, "Oh, it's just the pressure…" but I've come to believe that if you don't have the genetics to hurt the body from within with an illness, accidents/strains/sprains etc can fulfil the same purpose. It took me a long time to believe that, but if everything's the same at a quantum level, what's the difference between an arthritic knee and a busted cruciate ligament? I mention that because I know my son and I both have had issues with "moving forward in life", which relates to the knees. My left knee blew up with arthritis, but my son, who doesn't have arthritis, snapped both AC ligaments in separate footballing and mountain biking accidents. Coincidence? I think not. Fascinating stuff!

So, all I can really do is have a look in the Louise Hay and Inna Segal reference books and steal the following…

Louise Hay – "\[Shoulders\] represent our ability to carry our experiences in life joyously. We make life a burden by our attitude."

Inna Segal – (Yep…) "Carrying the weight of the world. Holding onto too much strain, stress or worry. Feeling insecure, unsure, frightened, overwhelmed, sad, rejected, distrustful and discouraged. Easily hurt. Droopy shoulders indicate a lack of joy and fun. Seriousness. Focusing on problems rather than solutions."

Also, anything that is inflammatory in nature, i.e. ending in "-itis" also relates to repressed anger and frustration wherever it manifests in the body – arthritis, bursitis etc – so that's worth looking into. However, my intuition says with you there's less of a component of that. I don't know why.

I find that these things have to be sat with for a while, as some subtle variation of their interpretations is what is usually ailing us. An exception has been for the eyes and my bouts of uveitis. Even the distinctions between left and right eyes has been absolutely utterly accurate as if it was written for me. Have I confused you even more?

Well that's had me snivelling into my hanky! I can recognise some of that. Fixing it is the tricky bit.

### sooperb — 2014-04-13T14:27:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-to-arthritis-and-so-much-more%E2%80%A6.10457/post-121248>

One knee has been a problem for years. Gives way occasionally. During yoga or any time I try to take weight on it, there are crunching and cracking noises. The side is very tender to the touch as is around the back of it and I reckon it's swollen. I can walk around fine though. It's the same leg that I can see a varicose vein developing in.

Shoulder blades rather than shoulders, sometimes have quite bad pain there, especially overnight and on waking. It can ease or continue during the day, it goes away for quite sizeable chunks of time though.

Interesting about the droopy shoulders, I find mine are often jammed under my ears and I have to consciously relax them.

Apart from that, no problem lol.

### Phil Escott — 2014-04-13T15:03:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-to-arthritis-and-so-much-more%E2%80%A6.10457/post-121253>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=121248)
>
> One knee has been a problem for years. Gives way occasionally. During yoga or any time I try to take weight on it, there are crunching and cracking noises. The side is very tender to the touch as is around the back of it and I reckon it's swollen. I can walk around fine though. It's the same leg that I can see a varicose vein developing in.
>
> Shoulder blades rather than shoulders, sometimes have quite bad pain there, especially overnight and on waking. It can ease or continue during the day, it goes away for quite sizeable chunks of time though.

Sounds more like an osteoarthritic issue than full-on inflammatory to me, but I know even that has inflammatory roots. Maybe look at the "moving forward in life" issue. It's what I'm working on now. So many other joints have totally sorted themselves, I feel it must be that issue for me that the knee hasn't quite returned to normal. Also I have a little tenderness at the shoulder blade level of the thoracic spine which can be money issues/worries – not supporting oneself enough etc. Certainly for me those are my two remaining issues now, and so closely linked, that it can't be coincidence. I mean, the symptoms are practically nothing now with all the other stuff I've done, but I know the root issue isn't totally cleared like it is with other joints. Ankles for example are to do with trying NOT to run away from something, and yes, at the time I felt really trapped by having to look after my aging and unappreciative mother. Now I have dealt with that issue I have no ankle problems at all. And no, she's still alive – I didn't chop her up and bury her in the garden…

Try telling a rheumatologist that it's connected to emotions! I knew I'd find some kind of answer like that right from the start – it just took me a lot of groping around before I did.

Early on, an exasperated rheumatologist asked me why I wouldn't take methotrexate. Without really thinking, I blurted out, "Because then there wouldn't be any magic in the world." He looked at me like I was nuts.

Thank you Phil

yes you are right about that I have had less repressed anger or frustration and more stress and worry.. sad too.. because I have been mourning a bit, bad girl. I have had to pull me up! So... yeah I have listened to my body and changed things..
guess what, almost no pain anymore in shoulders    in just 2 days - and I dropped coffee and each and every supplement. Was not much but I have been testing D Ribose lately, i do not think it was good for me, I have enough energy and it felt like I was just "pushed", or nothing.... but I loved the taste..hehe Then the Chlorella, I thought could not hurt, I had a lot! But darn, might not have been good for me.. and the Magnesium Malate too. Have been eating it for a while every night. just because I though I should... lol I never was a big supplementer. But here was so much talk about all those supps and I thought heck lets try.... No idea if they have been bad but my gut says just to quit them all.
Noooo this girl needs it natural!
For me, to make my food my medicine is my only way, that becomes clearer and clearer to me - makes me less confused, and more focused

I slept in the tent one night and it was awesome! Just so bumpy...lol I have to dig a bit to make it nice. then I will carry my magnetico out there too. I wake up surrounded by the nature...  looking right onto the river  I am so close I can almost put my foot in the water from my tent!

### Phil Escott — 2014-04-14T10:22:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-to-arthritis-and-so-much-more%E2%80%A6.10457/post-121407>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=121390)
>
> Thank you Phil
>
> For me, to make my food my medicine is my only way, that becomes clearer and clearer to me - makes me less confused, and more focused
>
> I slept in the tent one night and it was awesome! Just so bumpy...lol I have to dig a bit to make it nice. then I will carry my magnetico out there too. I wake up surrounded by the nature...  looking right onto the river  I am so close I can almost put my foot in the water from my tent!

Ah yes, mag malate. I had a lot of palpitations about a year or so ago, and it used to kill them dead, so I got addicted and swallowed a LOT. I'm pretty sure it's best to be off all supplements as soon as you can. If I had all the money back that I've spent on unnecessary supplements over the past four years due to bad guesswork and no labs, I'd buy myself a car – an expensive one.

Now, sleeping outside. That is wonderful. It's the reason I go carp fishing. People think it's to do with catching fish, but it's not really. I mean, some of these carp are 40 years old and very difficult to catch, and some of the lakes are big and only have a handful of them in. So, if you are lucky you only catch a few a year if you go for a night or two a week. This is great as it means beautiful peaceful sleep in nature! To wake up at 3am, look out and see a swan gliding across the lake in the moonlight is wonderful. And people think shooting stars are rare. I see them all the time! There is so much that people never see during their whole lives that I see every time I go. It's like my Himalayan cave. Actually I did the same with the novel I wrote – when the main character isn't in India or the 15th century Middle East in a past life, he's carp fishing, not that the fishing is important – it's what happens while you're there, how your consciousness unfolds. I despair of people who take televisions with them!!

Actually, I'm supposed to be finishing a screenplay for a possible film of it and I'm procrastinating by messing around here, so I'd better get on with it, 'cos if it's not blowing a gale, I'm off to the lake later…

### sooperb — 2014-04-14T16:52:27+0300

I know I'm right, at our weekend place in Cumbria there are several fishing spots among the trees above the shore line. There is an unending stream of discarded litter by most of them, corn kernel tins (bait no doubt), many beer cans, lager bottles, coke cans, cigarette packets and sandwich wrappers and the carrier bags they arrive in, just discarded around some areas of natural beauty. Fishing? Maybe, lads night out drinking, more likely. Any help on this?

Oh dear… Yes, there is always that element in the more accessible places. Well the places I go are usually private club or syndicate lakes – no members of the public generally get to see them. No litter… well, if you get caught you get banned, and quite right too. I spent the night out last night and feel absolutely amazing this morning. It was a full moon, or almost, and the night was so bright. It was beautiful. To think, we pay hundreds of thousands for a house, but my bivvy (glorified tent), which cost about £400 and my bedchair (more comfortable than my bed at home) that cost about £300 is way better for my health than my house!! We certainly have got things the wrong way round!

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-15T17:55:08+0300

arthritis is a collagen/metal/MMP issue in the extracellular matrix..........when you lose electrons it breaks down.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-15T17:56:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-to-arthritis-and-so-much-more%E2%80%A6.10457/post-121656>

why re transition metals and EMF so bad? They lead to a phase transition which is where energy must be transferred. when one carbon based semiconductor meets a metal one we begin to see electron loss. Why?
Electron Depletion
When doped biologic semiconductors are [joined to metals](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metal-semiconductor_junction), [to different semiconductors](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heterojunction), and [to the same semiconductor with different doping](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/P-n_junction), the resulting junction often strips the electron excess or deficiency out from the semiconductor near the junction. This [depletion region](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Depletion_region) is [rectifying](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rectifying) (only allowing current to flow in one direction), and used to further shape electrical currents in semiconductor devices. In collagen we use copper. If your tissues collecting transition metals then you basically just created a "hole" in you electron flower pot" This is why many people on this site lose a lot of electrons even while they are doing a lot of things correctly. They have no idea how their environment is creating holes in their semiconductors. OA and RA are both electron steal syndrome diseases of the ECM in collagen

I had some time when I was very thin during this process of healing. I'd be interested to hear what Jack says about this, but what happened to me seemed to go along with what Natasha Campbell McBride said. She said that quite often on GAPS you can get very thin, but at some stage, when the gut heals, the body just balances at its ideal weight. That is certainly what happened to me. There was a time when I just couldn't put on weight, and for an ex-fatty it was a bit alarming to be that lean and skinny. I even wondered if I had cancer or something, but it all came good in the end. Suddenly the weight picked up with no real change in diet. I added some high fat dairy back in, but 30lbs gained back in three months can't have been down to just that. I just felt the whole energy of my body change and it just filled out again. Not very scientific, I know, but …

> Electron Depletion
> When doped biologic semiconductors are [joined to metals](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metal-semiconductor_junction), [to different semiconductors](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heterojunction), and [to the same semiconductor with different doping](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/P-n_junction), the resulting junction often strips the electron excess or deficiency out from the semiconductor near the junction. This [depletion region](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Depletion_region) is [rectifying](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rectifying) (only allowing current to flow in one direction), and used to further shape electrical currents in semiconductor devices. In collagen we use copper. # Thread: Greetings from Corpus Christi, TX.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-corpus-christi-tx.10460/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 17

### Orlando De La Cruz — 2014-04-06T18:30:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-corpus-christi-tx.10460/post-120067>

Hello everyone! I am very excited to have came across Jack's blog and forum. There is so much info here and it really feels like I've stubbled upon a rare gem or something.
My wife and I have been trying to stick to the "Bullet Proof Diet" as much as possible for the past 8 months or so, and have struggled a little bit with our own personal vices, mine being alcohol. I have been diagnosed with an autoimmune disease, psoriatic arthritis and my wife suffers from an irregular thyroid and anxiety. It's only been the past couple of months or so that I've quit drinking, started working out with kettle bells, and started taking liposomal glutathione. So far from everything, I've lost 35 lbs but I'm still overweight. Even though I kicked the alcohol and tightened up my diet I was stuck at around 165 lbs (I'm 5'6'') but didn't think too much about it since I was also gaining a significant amount of muscle from my workouts.
My wife was feeling a little discouraged though because of her cravings and stagnant weight loss as well, so I turned to the bullet proof forums for some info and a lot of people were suggesting this "leptin reset" thing. That of course led me here and it looks like we're both back on track! In less than a week, I've already dropped 5 more lbs. and my wife is losing about 2 lbs a day as well! I am surprised at how quickly this protocol started to work for us!
I plan on starting the Cold Thermogenesis Protocol today but I know it's gonna be hard since I live in a hot climate and not used to cold water or cold weather at all. We'll see how it goes but I am definitely determined!
I hope to remain active on this forum, learn from you all, and also offer and receive encouragement as well!

P.S. Quick question: Is taking supplements throughout the day considered snacking on the leptin reset?

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-06T18:52:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-corpus-christi-tx.10460/post-120069>

Hi.

You reminded me of Álvar Núñez Cabeza de Vaca's narrative _Naufragios_. The Karankawas apparently weren't averse to a cold bath:

> The Karankawas inhabited the Gulf coast from Galveston down to Corpus Christi … \[According to Cabeza de Vaca\] in winter they go out in early dawn to take a bath ... breaking the ice with their body...

[http://www.indigenouspeople.net/karankaw.htm](http://www.indigenouspeople.net/karankaw.htm)

[http://www.pbs.org/weta/thewest/resources/archives/one/cabeza.htm](http://www.pbs.org/weta/thewest/resources/archives/one/cabeza.htm)

Supplements? What are they? If it's just some vitamin D or CoQ10 or whatnot wouldn't you take them with your meals?

Or maybe you mean whey protein or something? If it's some kind of reconstituted food like that, then AFAIK there's no need to take it immediately before or after working out, as some people at gyms seem to assume. There are sports people here who even train fasted:

[http://www.bengreenfieldfitness.com/2014/02/combining-fasting-and-exercise/](http://www.bengreenfieldfitness.com/2014/02/combining-fasting-and-exercise/)

These, BTW, are supplements that might be of help for some people on the Leptin ℞

[http://jackkruse.com/catalog/leptin-store/#supplements](http://jackkruse.com/catalog/leptin-store/#supplements)

Also, since I'm only having breakfast and dinner, there's a big stretch of time that seems like a good opportunity to take some activated charcoal caps but I'm just not sure if my body will "see" that as a snack or something. ### Gagnrad — 2014-04-06T20:43:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-corpus-christi-tx.10460/post-120080>

So two meals on most days, but on the ones when you work out you do have lunch. Sounds fine to me. I think Jack himself usually just eats breakfast and dinner, but not always, and I don't think he's ever said that the Leptin Reset requires that.

As for these various pills and so on that you've mentioned, I doubt any would spike your blood-sugar, or even really affect it at all -- which is, I guess the point of not snacking. If there's anything you're in any doubt about, you could check your blood with a portable glucose meter (they're cheap enough) after eating it. But if everything seems to be working ...

# Thread: Feet
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feet.10463/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-06T21:19:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feet.10463/post-120087>

This is an article about feet with the catchy name "Footloose in Archaeology" that the British magazine _Current Archaeology_ published some years ago now. It's by a chiropodist. Interesting stuff.

There are quite definite local differences in feet.

> As a chiropodist, I spent half a century looking at feet. During this time, I gradually came to realise that pain was more frequently caused not so much by wearing unsuitable footwear as by anomalies in the bone structure. Practising as I did for a quarter of a century in Herefordshire and another long period in a remote part of the Cotswolds, I was able to observe the degree to which in bygone days, repeated inter-marriage caused by the isolation of these rural areas, had turned these slight deformities into a recognisably typical and strongly hereditary shape of foot. ...

Besides this more local and idiosyncratic pattern there's this:

> Alongside these regional shapes, I became aware of something much more fundamental, in that the entire foot structure differed from that which is generally regarded as the traditional English foot, – for which all shoe manufacturers in this country design their footwear. The drawing to the right shows what I mean: the right hand foot is the typical English foot: most of the shoes sold in this country will be based on this, although probably the shoe will be more pointed. ...

However, the foot is often far slimmer in Scotland, Ireland, Wales and the English West-Country and the toes "slope" less:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

She adds:

> Other types of foot (not shown) include the Italian foot, with a very small little toe. This is often seen on Roman statues, suggesting that it is a type that has persisted for two millennia. ...

This lady says that she can even tell whether a person had worn shoes or not in life just from the bones:

> One structure that is common to the long barrows at both Nutbane and Hazleton is the slight deviation of the first toe toward the mid-line of the body, which must surely indicate that if footwear was worn, it did not constrict the feet in any way.
>
> Mention of this first toe alignment reminds me of the photogrammetric contour plot of the Mesolithic footprints fossilised in the estuarine mud at the confluence of the Rivers Usk and Severn in Gwent. This indicates that the body weight passed from the heel, along the outer border of the foot, transversely across to the 1st toe where the final thrusting-off point shows clearly that this toe was very much inclined to the mid-line of the body – I would think undoubtedly bare-footed; the smaller toes would be curled under. ...

More here:

[http://www.archaeology.co.uk/articles/features/footloose-in-archaeology.htm](http://www.archaeology.co.uk/articles/features/footloose-in-archaeology.htm)

### nonchalant — 2014-04-09T04:45:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feet.10463/post-120515>

This part gave me shivers:
"the apparent splaying of the metatarsals in these illustrations is caused by the fact that they are no longer bound together as in life by ligaments and tendons."

I have experienced that, on a reduced scale, when recovering from long-term plantar faciitis. My feet finally got healthy enough to be able to painlessly walk without shoes or supportive sandals, but they had an unnerving splaying action going on... But now my feet are tough and useful. Adhering to Jack's protocols can rehabilitate some badly damaged collagen.

### Gracelind — 2014-04-09T07:47:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feet.10463/post-120524>

Interesting. My feet are very slim and narrow . . . possibly due to my Scottish g-g-grandparents?

But my toes are steeply sloped. Where did that come from?

About hands, at a huge family association meeting I sat next to a distant cousin and noticed how much the shape of her hands matched my mother's, and my mother's first cousin's. Their most recent common ancestor was born in 1592. So, is there a constellation of genes that can produce hands so similar in a unique shape, down to the slight curve in the thumb that can be so persistent as to last through 10 generations?

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-09T08:28:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feet.10463/post-120528>

> [Gracelind said:](/goto/post?id=120524)
>
> Interesting. My feet are very slim and narrow . . . possibly due to my Scottish g-g-grandparents?
>
> But my toes are steeply sloped. Where did that come from?

It is interesting, isn't it? It raises some issues about the standard lasts that are used. Aside from the problem that passing fashions -- raised heels, pointed toe-boxes, etc. -- cause problems, whose foot is a "standard" size and shape anyway?

### nonchalant — 2014-04-09T16:33:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feet.10463/post-120593>

My feet aren't narrow, but the toes are almost all the same length. I never could figure out those pointy women's shoes, when I was growing up.

### LaurenR — 2014-04-09T16:45:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feet.10463/post-120598>

She adds:

Other types of foot (not shown) include the Italian foot, with a very small little toe. This is often seen on Roman statues, suggesting that it is a type that has persisted for two millennia. ...

This is me: Half Italian and my little toe is so small it looks like half a toe! And the toe nail on it is about 2mm wide x 2mm length!!! (1/12th inch!)
About 1/3 of a grain of rice in size!

### sjoshua — 2014-04-10T01:28:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feet.10463/post-120690>

> [Gracelind said:](/goto/post?id=120524)
>
> Interesting. My feet are very slim and narrow . . . possibly due to my Scottish g-g-grandparents?
>
> But my toes are steeply sloped. Where did that come from?
>
> About hands, at a huge family association meeting I sat next to a distant cousin and noticed how much the shape of her hands matched my mother's, and my mother's first cousin's. Their most recent common ancestor was born in 1592. So, is there a constellation of genes that can produce hands so similar in a unique shape, down to the slight curve in the thumb that can be so persistent as to last through 10 generations?

Regarding your feet: My guess would be that the slim and narrow design is genetic, but your environmental inputs (i.e. wearing shoes your whole life) would cause the slope if your feet have been stuck in that type of shape during their entire development... Or that slope came from a different genetic source mixed in over generations.

Hands I'd think would track similarily, and possibly even moreso to a noticeable amount due to the fact that we don't wear shoes on our hands.

I've made personal observations about my feet over the last ~5 years. Prior to then, I had always worn shoes/sneakers and my toes did little but exist. There was not a significant noticable gap between them (other than my big toe), I couldn't do much muscular "spreading" of them, and I could not control their functioning much beyond a basic bend. I believe my body had learned to rely on the shoe to provide the support that would normally be demanded from the toes. In 2009, I started wearing vibram five fingers in the summer, and as of the last ~6 months I now try to be barefoot as often as possible. The vibrams definitely started to change the form of my feet somewhat, but it wasn't until my feet were touching the ground directly that I really started to see the changes. I now have notable gaps between all but my smallest two toes, and also have much more range of motion and strength in them, and this is continuing to improve.

### Gracelind — 2014-04-10T07:16:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feet.10463/post-120736>

The slope along the tops of my toes from big toe to pinkie is definitely genetic, too. Many in my family have a similar shape, along with a curved 4th toe. I was sure each baby boy was mine, at his birth, because they had that same curved toe. I've tended to wear round-toe shoes even when they weren't in style.

Plus, I've been barefoot in the house for nearly my entire life. My mother used to despair and yell at me to put on socks. My feet would get freezing cold on hardwood floors in the winter but there was just this compulsion to go barefoot despite the cold. As much as possible I then wore sandals outside, even in winter if I could get away with it. Now, with an autoimmune disease that is stealing strength from quads, going barefoot allows me to feel the floor and maintain a bit more balance. Under JK influence I'm venturing out in the yard barefoot more often. Have to be careful of pebbles and pine cone bits and such that can cause a fall.

Long time ago there was a program called "You Asked For It" that once featured a young woman with no arms (maybe due to prenatal thalidomide). After seeing her use her feet to pick up everything from a ball off the floor to the handle of a pan on the stove, I worked on foot and leg function. Wasn't long before I could turn the knob and open a door with my foot and pick up all manner of things. Can't do that any more due to the muscle disease.

It's pretty cool how much flexibility and strength we can develop in our feet when they are healthy.

### caroline — 2014-04-10T09:02:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feet.10463/post-120751>

Gracelind - you would benefit from doing some of the things that Jeremy does?
like touching rocks? could you rock climb? in those rock climbing places indoors - you are in a harness right?

Could you walk on rocks with your Husband's assistance?

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-04-10T09:47:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feet.10463/post-120761>

a deeper web space between the 1st and second toes - resulting from short 1st metatarsal, is known as a greek foot (rothbart's foot) it is always associated with hyperpronation as the mechanoreceptors are most dense in the ball of the great toe. effectively pressure to this area in midstance/standing still gives rise to ascending postural pattern. because the great toe metatarsal is shorter (resulting from incomplete unwinding of the limb bud in week 10 in the womb) the foot must hyperpronate to touch the met head on the ground for adequate postural mechanoreception.
a longer 1st metatarsal, then gradually sloping down to 5th pinky is known as an egyptian foot and is the archetypal functional foot that gives rise to the least problems.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-10T15:01:30+0300

# Thread: Does life emerge as a consequence of phase transition and breaking of symmetry
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-life-emerge-as-a-consequence-of-phase-transition-and-breaking-of-symmetry.10466/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaulG — 2014-04-07T03:11:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-life-emerge-as-a-consequence-of-phase-transition-and-breaking-of-symmetry.10466/post-120134>

I will be honest much of the QED physics goes over my head but when I read the article below it made a lot of sense especially after listening to the feb webinar that partially explained how CT had non-surface quantum effects at room temperature and the recent discussion in the [breadcrumbs](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/breadcrumbs.10396/page-12#post-120127) thread (though I haven't yet read Jeremy's Blog)

[http://www.mdpi.com/1422-0067/10/5/2084/pdf](http://www.mdpi.com/1422-0067/10/5/2084/pdf)

> Here we propose that the biochemical reactions selected by evolution are controlled by [Feshbach resonances](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Feshbach_resonance). These quantum resonances have been recently observed in gas phase chemical reactions. This important advance in the field of chemical reaction \[105-112\] dynamics has identified resonances, that are transient intermediate states produced during the reaction, occurring near the top of a reaction barrier (the "transition state"), where one would not normally expect to find metastable states

> Quantum coherence relevant to biology always appears in the normal ambient at the normal temperatures in conjunction with a well defined energy flow. The scenario presented here show that the dynamical biological order in the living cell requires the presence of constant energy flow in fact the Feshbach resonance is related with a high energy quasi bound state degenerate with an high energy continuum of free particles. A lot of open problems have to be faced to answer the Erwin Schrödinger question: “What is life?” \[118\] at the end of the first decade of the XXI century but now the crucial issue is well defined and the development of interdisciplinary research, getting the benefit of cross-fertilization between different fields, is driving us to unexpected surprises.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-07T03:36:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-life-emerge-as-a-consequence-of-phase-transition-and-breaking-of-symmetry.10466/post-120139>

patrick you listened! you're learning like a scientist and not a metaphysicists. Great sign..........And yes you are quite right. When I get to Organiztional Structure 3.........I got you covered. In it.........I go after the biggest dog. Jason called me out last yr for going after Mitchell............Well get ready.........I am going for Mr Darwin.

# Thread: Needing accountability
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/needing-accountability.10467/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 43
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Teri Pitman — 2014-04-07T03:22:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/needing-accountability.10467/post-120136>

Hello-
I recently heard Dr. Kruse on a podcast I listen to often and was blown away. I feel like I've been on a roller coaster of feeling bad then better more times than I can count and part of it is my own undoing so I'm wondering if some unbiased new support and some feedback will help me a bit. I love learning and have an Exercise physiology degree so this site is amazing however I think I need to be pulled back a bit as I don't really know where to start. So here is my background...

I am a 33 year old female, and I am in fairly good health but as me and my very supportive doctor have discussed I do not feel I'm at my best and right now I'm battling and trying to correct the stupidity I put my body through from middle school until my late 20's. We can start with eating disorders from middle school to my mid to late 20s. Starting somewhat anorexic, but mostly bulimia type behaviors, never throwing up, but laxatives and excessive exercising, and a selective (and now I know unhealthy and highly processed) vegetarian diet. All of this seemed to lead me to competing in ultra-endurance events like Ironman triathlon, ultra running and biking. As I got better and focused more on performance since I was getting quite good the eating disorders slowly went away but it's still a struggle in my brain (I suspect leptin and leaky gut issues). In about 2001 I was diagnosed hypothyroid and just thrown on some synthetic Levo which I didn't feel any difference but the doc said to take them. Another chink to the chain is birth control, I started on pills at about 18, tried some various alternatives and settled on the lovely DepoProvera shot which at the time was awesome, no period, no symptoms, I thought it was awesome and my doc at the time agreed, but all during this time still felt off.

Fast forward about 2 years ago I meet my awesome current doctor who slowly convinced me maybe it wasn't so great to be on DepoProvera and my thyroid numbers didn't seem to be awesome on synthetics so he put me on Armour, 90mcg and I finally felt a bit of a difference. Also blood tests revealed Vit D deficiency. So we worked on trying to get my body back to a "normal". I didn't get my period back for about a year but when I did get it back, it was hell. Maybe I just wasn't used to the hormonal fluctuations but geesh, my weight will fluctuate wildly and my periods can be painful. On a recent blood test it seemed my progesterone was on the low side but honestly we have tested those hormones like we should yet. I know what to do with my diet to be successful it's implementing it that is the hard part since I have crazy intense sugar cravings daily. I am able to stick to "Paleo" about 80% of the time and I realize I probably need to tighten it up for a good few months to see better results. Sometimes I feel like I can't eat anything without getting bloated, which stink because I am naturally fairly tiny and workout hard but will have this puffy little pooch after I eat.

I still compete in endurance events but have backed off the really long stuff to let my body rest. Like I said trying to keep eating better but just need some accountability or inspiration. So for whatever angel sat through reading this blurb, where do you suggest I start, and do I need to provide more info?

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-07T03:34:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/needing-accountability.10467/post-120138>

Best place to start is here on the forum but my book is an easy cut thru the first two yrs.......begin there and keep in mind where you are now. Then focus on this as you genesis point. The book is on amazon called the epi paleo rx

### Teri Pitman — 2014-04-07T03:40:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/needing-accountability.10467/post-120142>

Ok, I will admit I am a little star struck since I wasn't expecting a response from the man himself but I am in the process of ordering the book. I am on the Optimal Reset list so I am hoping to learn a lot. Thank you so much!

### nicld — 2014-04-07T04:12:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/needing-accountability.10467/post-120145>

Hi Teri and welcome.

Having abused my body for years by over exercising and restrictive eating, my suggestion is that you may need to stop all exercising. Chronic exercise causes major inflammation in the body and as Jack has said, inflammation is at the root at most all diseases. You are already showing signs on inflammation by the bloat and my guess is that there is some leaky gut going on too.

Just take it one say at a time and remember that this is not a quick fix and may take up to 2 years to start feeling better but it can be done.

Good luck

# Thread: Improving Colonic Health
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improving-colonic-health.10469/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 128
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### seanb4 — 2014-04-07T12:38:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improving-colonic-health.10469/post-120219>

Hi guys,

I'm back on the thought path that my poor digestion time could be a big part on my health problems. I do not think it's the main cause, but it can't hurt to improve it.

Since I ate pretty much zero vegetables and mostly cooked food, I suspect my colon isn't getting the substances it needs to be healthy. My transit time is roughly 5 days but the weird thing is, if I take some food that will increase my transit time (dark chocolate for example) it will still take days to come out but it will look as though it has rushed through my digestive system, lighter in colour, etc. I suspect that this could be because it is rushing through my small intestines but then getting stuck in the large intestines because my large intestine health is not up to scratch and thus takes a while to extract what it needs.

So to increase my colonic / large intestine health, I need to feed it the correct pre and probiotics (chitin, glycans), and enough water so it can convert the probiotics to SCFA (butyrate) at optimal speed. Without vegetables I need to eat raw to do this as cooking destroys the probiotics, also minimal chewing helps in allowing certain food to reach the colon undigested.

I can get my hands on frozen raw shrimp and raw salmon however I wonder if I can speed the process up by supplementing chitin, glycans or butyrate...

### nicld — 2014-04-07T17:07:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improving-colonic-health.10469/post-120242>

I would highly recommend getting a GI stool test done if you have not already. Mine showed that I have bad stuff in there that has been causing alot of my issues. Between that and a blood test it showed that I have a bunch of biofilms in there and with the help of Dr. Tim I am on treatments for that and it has helped.

I have felt I had the same issues int he past that once food got to the large intestines they would just stop and it felt horrible. Also start eating a bunch of fermented veggies, that has been helping me.

Hang in there, you'll figure it out.

### seanb4 — 2014-04-07T18:53:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improving-colonic-health.10469/post-120247>

I do plan on getting the GI test but I don't have much money and am prioritizing other things first.

I have done fermented vegetables in the past but only difference I noticed was better hair with really high doses. I have since found out that was due to the structured water content and not the bacteria. Hoping raw seafood will be different.

Yea biofilms sound like a bitch.

### Melanie Procter — 2014-04-25T06:24:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improving-colonic-health.10469/post-123380>

Hi, I don't know if this will help you. But this blog is unstoppable in its own way. At first, it is painful and then all of a sudden something clicks and it seems to take a life on its own. If you are working on your gut health, I suggest yo read it. [http://jackkruse.com/organizational-structural-failure-1-gutcollagen-link/](http://jackkruse.com/organizational-structural-failure-1-gutcollagen-link/)

I was looking for sources for butyrate recently for a funny reason. Dave Asprey interviewed this guy who heads up the Flow state project which helps people to get in a peak cognitive/creative state called flow. They were discussing nutritional precursors that could support flow. Dave suggested amino acids but I don't know which ones. I also heard butyrate. I found butyrate is high in butter. My guess butyrate supports the flow state as it is a precursor of gabba.

I am not really answering your question. But here I will give you a little tip. Dave Asprey has a recipe online called Get Some Ice Cream. Give that a try. It does have butter in it but people find it magical. Take a look at it and try it.

Be well!

### Inger — 2014-04-25T08:59:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improving-colonic-health.10469/post-123393>

High heart, fermented liver ( i let my raw grassfed beef liver stay on a wooden plate in the fridge, uncovered. It dries on the surface and gets a bit fermented on the inside, quite yummy!) do not chew much

Eat animal parts like skin and other tough stuff you can read about in the blog Melanie mentions, less chewed

Raw seafood! Chew as little as you can.

I wonder if that is why I do so well on a animal food diet because I have done all this for years - and it makes me feel great! I see what others eat.. and I think I would not do well with that... even if it is paleo

Jack has said again and again - do not supplement, eat the real thing. Let your food me your medicine

I see the ones that struggle the most tend to eat many supplements  and say, they cannot eat "weird stuff". But we all can. I close my nose and just swallow if it tastes too bad, period. It is a very good trick

I never eat anything else than grass fed or game... i would not do high meat with grain fed. I would not eat it at all.I think really not healthy

It is super easy, can you get heart? Lambs heart would do too... cut into cubes and put in a glass jar and let sit in the fridge or room temp and air it once in a while. ### Gagnrad — 2014-05-01T15:26:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improving-colonic-health.10469/post-124273>

It'll dry out if left open like that. People sometimes do that briefly as a way of drying poultry skin, so that it will crisp nicely.

Much game is traditionally hung till high. Venison would be an example. Just googled to find something for you. As he says:

> Meat is made up of long muscle cells connected by a fairly tough substance called collagen (the same stuff plastic surgeons inject into the lips of supermodels to make those lips full and "pouty"). Collagen causes most meat toughness. Young animals have little of it between their muscle cells, but as an animal gets older, more develops. Natural enzymes break down this intercellular collagen as meat is aged, so the longer it hangs, the more tender it becomes.

[http://www.fieldandstream.com/articles/other/recipes/2006/01/deer-hang-time](http://www.fieldandstream.com/articles/other/recipes/2006/01/deer-hang-time)

Of course, if your aim in eating the raw meat was to consume collagen that wasn't much broken down then perhaps well-aged vension, though you can buy it easily enough, wouldn't be the way to go. But perhaps you want tenderer meat or prefer the taste.

Bacteria are actually involved here -- similar to the ones in blue cheese, I believe.

At home, if you don't know what you're doing, you could come unstuck. Look up something on Icelandic specialities: you can see there are a lot of processes involved:

[http://www.guidetoiceland.is/about-iceland/article/the-worlds-most-disgusting-icelandic-food](http://www.guidetoiceland.is/about-iceland/article/the-worlds-most-disgusting-icelandic-food)

Sometimes whey is used to help the ferment, or there's smoking, or salting. Introducing bacteria, with the whey, would be a way of making sure the _right_ ones predominate. Again, salt inhibits gram-negative bacteria. The shark is buried in gravel ... but then they have to. It's a way of leaching out what would otherwise harm you.

I'd be wary if you don't understand the processes well and how to control them.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-01T20:20:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improving-colonic-health.10469/post-124384>

Best way to colon health is eat things with DHA. As far as our knowledge goes, DHA has been the dominant fatty acid in the membrane phosphoglycerides of the photore- ceptors, neurons in the head and the gut and synapses for all 600 million years of animal evolution. Even today, the composition of the photoreceptor and brain varies little between species despite large scale species variation in the lipid composition of the diet, liver and muscle. Remember that your gut has more nerves in it that the cerebral cortex. When you are human you need DHA to collect electrons in every tissue. In fact, DHA is the evolutionary conductor for DNA since the beginning of all animal evolution. Proteins are selected to function with the constancy of DHA and not the other way around for 600 million years. This is extraordinary natural selection. As humans moved away from the shore and began civilization and moved inland they slowly began to consume mostly agricultural-based processed foods, and we got what we should have expected........ a global evolutionary crises beginning in the last two evolutionary systems, namely neurogenesis and autoimmunity. The further you get away from seafood the worse we will de evolve in all respects.

### seanb4 — 2014-05-02T18:11:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improving-colonic-health.10469/post-124621>

Just to clear, it's not just about money, it's about what makes my symptoms worse. For example, the couple of months last year I was doing gaps intro diet stage 2, which is just broth and meat/fish with something like carrots. I was getting a lot of calories as well, like 3000+. These few months were really bad for me, palpitations, bloating / stomach pain, etc where all at there worst and so was tiredness, I was sleeping after every meal. I suspect this is because the meal volume was high. Contrast that to when I was eating egg yolk, multi vitamin, whey isolate, orange juice, and coconut meat. This was lower volume and my symptoms where much less servere. I was more able to get about and do something. Now it's obvious which one is the better diet, GAPS, but for how my body was reacting the other one was far better. It's not a case of, if I eat perfect epi-paleo I will be fine, at all.
Example today as I type this I feel very sick/full in my stomach and all I have drunk is water. I think I took too much at once or something but it happens all the time.

Anyways getting back on track. I have found an asian stall at the market that sells raw oysters and beef for relatively cheap. Which is good.

I have noticed that when I try to use magnesium to increase motility, it doesn't quite work right. Like I have to take real high doses, 4g x2 mag malate, and then I still won't have a BM for like 2 days but when I do it will be very loose. It's almost as if the magnesium is waiting around in my stomach/small intestines for far longer than it should...

@ caroline What were your gut and bowel like before you started swallowing the shellfish whole? Is this the only change youve made since then?

BTW, I would love to move to Norway...

### prAna303 — 2014-05-02T23:50:20+0300

Ok that sounds rather sexual but who cares. But it is one of the most sublime pleasures of life - wine that is.

From the movie: A good year

[Uncle Henry Skinner](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0001215/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): Max, have I told you why I enjoy making wine so much?

[Young Max](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0383603/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): You don't make the wine, Uncle Henry - that guy Duflot does.

[Uncle Henry Skinner](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0001215/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): \[Reproachfully\] In France it's always the landowner who makes the wine, even though he does nothing more than supervise with binoculars from the comfort of his study. No, I enjoy making wine, because this sublime nectar is quite simply incapable of lying. Picked too early, picked too late, it matters not - the wine will always whisper into your mouth with complete, unabashed honesty every time you take a sip.

This movie, like my own lifestory... Give it a try with a mind wide open and then you stop making excuses...

[Fanny Chenal](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0182839/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): \[Fanny approaches a customer who is behind a raised newspaper\] Bonjour. Vous avez choisi?

[Max Skinner](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0000128/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): \[lowers newspaper\] I think so.

[Fanny Chenal](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0182839/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): \[recognizing him\] You sure you don't need more time?

[Max Skinner](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0000128/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): No, I know what I want.

[Fanny Chenal](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0182839/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): You're sure?

[Max Skinner](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0000128/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): Absolutely.

[Fanny Chenal](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0182839/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): So, what is it to be?

[Max Skinner](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0000128/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): How's the soup?

[Fanny Chenal](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0182839/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): The soup is finished.

[Max Skinner](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0000128/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): Like my job... The fish?

[Fanny Chenal](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0182839/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): We've run out.

[Max Skinner](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0000128/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): That's like me with excuses.

[Fanny Chenal](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0182839/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): Don't waste my time. Choose something we have.

[Max Skinner](http://www.imdb.com/name/nm0000128/?ref_=tt_trv_qu): I would like a lifetime spent with an irrational and suspicious goddess, some short-tempered jealousy on the side, and a bottle of wine that tastes like you, a glass that's never empty.

\[they kiss\]

Food poisoning?

Had a really rough night. I finished work around 2 and took a swig of my water, got a feeling in my stomach of an iron ball sitting in there and didn't want to eat or drink anything. This happens from time to time with water and it usually goes after a few hours. Anyway I forced down some some raw scallops, raw mackerel, and salmon. The feeling didn't go and just got worse and worse. Palpitations, shortness of breath, iron ball, feeling very very sick, shivering. Managed to get to sleep around 8 and had an awful nights sleep. My hearts still painfully pounding this morning and I've still got shortness of breath and headache...

Is this food poisoning? I really don't want this to happen again...

Coincidentally I have been pretty ill the last 2 nights which coincides with when I upgraded to 20 Gauss. Don't think it is anything to do with the magnets though.

### seanb4 — 2014-05-04T12:02:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improving-colonic-health.10469/post-124961>

> I swear I started to crash hard after doing the same thing

Could you tell me what you mean by crash hard. How where you before and after the 20 guass.

I hope it is the increased electron flow causing this but tbh I still think it is food poisoning because in both nights I started coming on with gastrointestinal symptoms before laying on the pad. Plus I didn't experience any symptoms when moving from nothing to 10 gauss.

Still feel like crap today.

Found a post on cure zone ([http://curezone.org/forums/am.asp?i=1833429](http://curezone.org/forums/am.asp?i=1833429)) that got me thinking. One of the posters there had some of the same symptoms I do, he was eating gluten which was causing his intestines to swell up and slow down food moving through them, this meant stuff like water would stay in his stomach for far longer than it should, until his intestines cleared out.

Something similar to this could be happening to me. It could help explain why I have the symptoms of gasteroparesis yet my stomach emptying test came back fine. If the problem is in my small intestines, whenever something goes through my duodenum it gets irritated and swells. This would also make sense as when I originally got an ulcer that started this mess, it had the symptoms of a duodenal ulcer. Although having said that, come to think of it, this still wouldn't really explain why I had gasteroparesis symptoms when doing the water fast. The water should be absorbed straight away inside the small intestines regardless of swelling...
